title: The Price of Basecamp's Water
summary: Basecamp co-founder Jason Fried recounts how a competitive analysis of 25 similar products found Basecamp was the cheapest offering, which worried the team since it suggested customers were buying on price rather than value, and made business owners skeptical of trusting a cheap $29/month tool with critical operations. In response, Basecamp scrapped its tiered pricing ($29/$79/$129-159) and moved to a single flat $99/month plan, applied only to new customers while existing customers kept their old pricing. Fried reports the change worked: new signups dipped only slightly, while average ticket price and conversion rates both rose, and the team judged it a success tied to repositioning Basecamp as a full business-operations platform rather than a cheap tool.
what_they_built: A flat, single-tier $99/month pricing plan for new Basecamp customers, replacing a multi-tier structure ($29-$159), while grandfathering existing customers at their old prices.
evidence: Qualitative and directional quantitative: signups "fell... but only by a little bit," while "average ticket price per sale went up significantly, and conversion rates went up as well" — no exact percentages given, but Fried calls the change "a huge success."
why_it_matters: Shows a first-hand example of a PM/founder using competitive price benchmarking and value-perception signals to justify collapsing pricing tiers and raising price, then tracking signup, ticket-price, and conversion metrics to validate the bet.

title: Bringing Back Free (REWORK podcast, with transcript)
summary: Basecamp had dropped its free tier in 2015 in favor of a flat $99 pricing plan, but a conversation with a new head of marketing (ex-MailChimp) prompted the cofounders to bring back a free plan in 2025 — one project with the ability to invite dozens of collaborators, designed to spread exposure to the product through invited users rather than just signups. Jason Fried and David Heinemeier Hansson discuss the mechanism, their reasoning about support costs versus infrastructure costs, and report early results: weekly signups more than doubled and early conversion was tracking about 3% ahead of baseline, though they caution that figure may not yet be statistically significant. They also note they review analytics weekly rather than daily to avoid overreacting, and are separately testing the order in which pricing plans are displayed on the site.
what_they_built: A reinstated free Basecamp tier limited to one project but allowing unlimited invited (non-paying) users, intended to widen exposure and lower the barrier to trial.
evidence: Founders state weekly signups "more than doubled" after reintroducing the free plan, and early paid-conversion rate was running about 3% ahead of the prior baseline — self-described as too early to call statistically significant.
why_it_matters: Shows how a mature SaaS company reversed a decade-old pricing decision using a narrow, deliberately-scoped free tier (one project, unlimited invited users) as a growth lever, and how they hedge on reading early metrics before declaring a win.

title: HEY Bubble Up: From kickoff to launch
summary: A senior product designer at 37signals narrates, week by week, how the HEY email team shaped and built "Bubble Up" (a snooze-like feature to resurface emails later) inside a single six-week Shape Up cycle. It covers scoping the pitch into V1 essentials versus later polish, collaborative naming and accessibility discussions with the developer, the web build, handing off to the mobile team for iOS/Android in weeks 5-6, QA, an internal staff-only cooldown rollout, and the eventual public launch. The account is notable for showing how a shaped-but-not-fully-specified pitch gets progressively concretized through daily collaboration rather than upfront specs.
what_they_built: "Bubble Up," a HEY feature letting users schedule emails to resurface today, tomorrow, on the weekend, a specific date, or via a "surprise me" random option, with a "Pop" action to remove or reschedule bubbled messages.
evidence: Qualitative account of production outcomes: feature shipped within the six-week cycle plus cooldown, an internal staff-only rollout preceded the public launch to surface real-world behavior issues, the "Pop" animation was iterated multiple times based on feedback, and the mobile team was onboarded and shipped iOS/Android versions in weeks 5-6. No usage/adoption metrics are reported.
why_it_matters: Shows a PM/design leader how a Shape Up team deliberately keeps a pitch loosely scoped at kickoff and narrows decisions in-flight through developer collaboration rather than exhaustive upfront specification.

title: Design to Duty: The Accounting and Reporting Systems at Adyen
summary: Adyen's accounting and reporting systems faced insert volumes an order of magnitude higher than their transaction-processing traffic, threatening their PostgreSQL-based double-entry accounting pipeline. The team chose a simple round-robin sharding strategy over domain-aware routing to prioritize flexibility and simplicity, and built a custom PostgreSQL-based streaming solution instead of adopting Kafka, since exactly-once delivery was not yet supported by Kafka at the time. They set an internal "20x scaling factor" as a trigger for revisiting these choices. The homegrown design held up as traffic grew by an order of magnitude.
what_they_built: A sharded PostgreSQL-backed double-entry accounting and reporting pipeline with a custom Kafka-alternative streaming layer for propagating financial events.
evidence: The reporting framework "stood the test of time even though traffic increased by an order of magnitude"; accounting insert volume ran an order of magnitude higher than raw transaction throughput, and the team uses a 20x scaling-factor threshold to decide when to reconsider the architecture.
why_it_matters: Shows platform builders that a deliberately simple, boring architecture (round-robin sharding, homegrown streaming) can outlast years of hypergrowth if you set explicit thresholds for when to revisit the decision.

title: Design to Duty: How we make architecture decisions at Adyen
summary: Adyen explains how its Payments Acceptance Layer (PAL) — the edge service every payment passes through — was deliberately built stateless so capacity scales linearly with instance count, a design that has held since the company's founding. This statelessness created a problem: making the payments API idempotent (duplicate requests must return the same response without reprocessing) without reintroducing state into PAL. Rather than build a custom stateful solution, Adyen integrated CockroachDB, a distributed database, to handle idempotency while preserving PAL's scalability. The piece frames this as an example of Adyen's broader "pragmatic, KISS" approach to weighing homegrown builds against open-source/third-party tools.
what_they_built: A stateless edge payment-processing layer (PAL) that scales linearly by adding instances, paired with an external distributed database (CockroachDB) to add idempotency without breaking statelessness.
evidence: Qualitative but concrete: the stateless-scaling mechanism "has been basically unchanged since the start of the company," and any PAL instance can be taken down for maintenance without disrupting processing while new instances immediately add proportional capacity (X payments/sec per PAL, 2X for two PALs).
why_it_matters: Shows platform builders a concrete tradeoff pattern — preserving a stateless scaling primitive by pushing a new requirement (idempotency) into an external system rather than compromising the core architecture.

title: Making Airbnb's Android app more accessible
summary: Airbnb's Android engineering team describes how they built accessibility into their app development process rather than treating it as a one-off pass. They combined manual best practices (accurate content descriptions, logical grouping of related UI elements for screen readers, font-scale support) with automated enforcement: Espresso accessibility checks, Android Lint, custom Ktlint rules for localized screen-reader labels, and Happo screenshot tests at enlarged font sizes, all gating CI so failing pull requests cannot merge. Because their Design Language System centralizes UI into reusable components, an accessibility fix to one component (e.g., marking SectionHeader as an accessibility heading) automatically propagates to every screen using it. The post also covers how they handle content-description defaults in their Jetpack Compose migration to keep accessibility deliberate rather than accidental.
what_they_built: A CI-gated accessibility enforcement pipeline (Espresso accessibility checks, Android Lint, custom Ktlint rules, Happo screenshot tests) layered on top of Airbnb's shared Design Language System so component-level accessibility fixes propagate app-wide.
evidence: Process-level evidence rather than quantified metrics: automated checks run on every commit as part of CI and block merging non-compliant pull requests; component-level fixes (e.g., to SectionHeader) apply automatically across all screens using that component. No user-impact or adoption numbers are reported.
why_it_matters: Shows PMs/designers how pairing accessibility requirements with a shared component library and CI gates turns accessibility from a manual audit into a scalable, enforced default.

title: Working Type: How we introduced Airbnb Cereal to our UI
summary: Airbnb's design team found their existing typeface degraded legibility in small-size UI contexts and set out over 18 months, in partnership with type foundry Dalton Maag, to build a custom typeface (Airbnb Cereal) suited to product screens. They built a custom sentence-generator tool (using Unix regex/AWK) to produce realistic test copy from the limited character sets available mid-development, then iterated through roughly 30 style and weight variations across hundreds of UI screens over six months, followed by another six months refining with the foundry. Before shipping, they audited over 11,000 screenshots across four platforms and multiple device types to catch UI bugs, and ran an A/B business-metrics experiment on more than 2 million users to confirm the new font and associated code changes wouldn't hurt product performance. The typeface rolled out across brand and product in May 2018.
what_they_built: A custom product/brand typeface (Airbnb Cereal) co-developed with Dalton Maag, plus supporting tooling — a synthetic sentence generator for realistic UI copy testing and a large-scale screenshot audit pipeline — to validate legibility before system-wide rollout.
evidence: 18-month program: ~30 font style/weight iterations tested across hundreds of screens, an 11,000+ screenshot audit across four platforms/device types, and an A/B test on over 2 million users showing no negative business-metric impact, clearing the font for full rollout in May 2018.
why_it_matters: Shows a rigorous, evidence-gated process for shipping a system-wide visual/brand change (typeface) as a product decision, not just a design one — with a real business-metric guardrail before full rollout.

title: Designing Experimentation Guardrails
summary: Airbnb runs thousands of concurrent experiments weekly, and teams optimizing for their own metrics sometimes shipped treatments that harmed other teams' metrics undetected until rollback was needed. To catch this, Airbnb built a company-wide Experimentation Guardrails system (rolled out in 2019) with three independent checks: an Impact Guardrail that escalates when a treatment effect crosses a negative threshold, a Power Guardrail ensuring sample size is sufficient for the Impact Guardrail's statistical validity, and a Stat Sig Negative Guardrail flagging any statistically significant harm to top-tier metrics. The system also adjusts thresholds based on experiment traffic coverage and auto-approves positive-trending experiments that pass noninferiority tests.
what_they_built: A three-part statistical guardrail system that automatically screens experiments for harm to cross-team key business, user-experience, and strategic-priority metrics, escalating flagged cases for human review.
evidence: The system flags roughly 25 experiments per month for escalation; of those, 80% eventually launch after stakeholder discussion and additional analysis, and 20% (about 5 per month) are stopped before launch.
why_it_matters: Shows a PM how to operationalize cross-team metric protection in an experimentation platform so that one team's win doesn't become another team's silent regression.

title: Architecting a Machine Learning System for Risk
summary: Airbnb's trust and safety team needed a fraud-detection system that could act in real time on events like reservation creation while still letting data scientists iterate quickly on models. They built a two-part architecture: a Java Fraud Prediction Service that computes features via parallelized DB queries, and Openscoring, a customized JSON/REST wrapper around JPMML with added Kafka logging and StatsD monitoring, so new models could be deployed without redeploying the serving service. Load testing showed most Openscoring responses returned in under 10ms. The team's biggest operational lesson was that data quality of the ground-truth labels bounded model performance more than feature or algorithm choices did, pushing them toward heavier investment in labeling and logging.
what_they_built: A production real-time fraud-risk scoring system: a Fraud Prediction Service microservice paired with Openscoring, a JPMML-based model-serving layer with Kafka logging and StatsD monitoring, decoupling model iteration from serving infrastructure.
evidence: Load testing found most Openscoring responses took under 10ms; the team traces a precision/recall ceiling to ground-truth label quality rather than model or feature choices.
why_it_matters: Shows a PM/platform builder that in fraud and trust-and-safety systems, investing in ground-truth data quality and logging infrastructure pays off more than chasing model sophistication.

title: How Airbnb Measures Future Value to Standardize Tradeoffs
summary: Airbnb needed a way to estimate the long-term causal value of user and listing actions (like bookings or adding amenities) in cases where running an A/B test wasn't feasible for ethical, legal, or UX reasons. The data science team built Future Incremental Value (FIV), a self-serve platform that uses propensity score matching against roughly 1,000 control features to estimate one-year causal impact, letting any team submit a simple config file to define an action group without needing modeling expertise. The platform also applies "haircuts" to listing-side estimates to account for marketplace cannibalization effects. It became the common currency Airbnb uses to compare short- and long-term value across very different actions and to weight experiment evaluation criteria.
what_they_built: A self-serve internal platform (FIV) that computes one-year causal impact estimates of user/listing actions via tree-based propensity score matching, callable by any team through a simple query configuration rather than custom modeling.
evidence: The platform has been scaled to work with all teams across the company (demand-side, supply-side, platform teams like Payments and Customer Support, and Airbnb.org) and has computed over 150 FIV action-event estimates from the guest and listing perspective, leveraging roughly 1,000 control features.
why_it_matters: Shows how a PM-facing "common currency" metric platform can let non-modeling teams standardize tradeoff decisions (ROI, experiment prioritization) without each team building bespoke causal analysis.

title: Improving Performance with HTTP Streaming
summary: Airbnb's frontend rendered React pages as a single HTTP response, forcing the browser to wait for full server-side rendering (including data fetching) before it could start discovering critical resources like fonts, CSS, and JS. The team restructured page delivery into an HTTP streaming architecture with three chunks: an early-flushed head (fonts/CSS/JS), a late head plus body, and a deferred JSON data chunk, letting the browser start fetching resources while the server was still rendering and fetching data in parallel. In production they hit an unexpected ~200ms delay caused by an interaction between Nagle's algorithm and Delayed ACK on small early-flush payloads, which they fixed by disabling Nagle's algorithm on their HAProxy load balancer.
what_they_built: A three-chunk HTTP streaming response (Early Flush head, Late head + body, Deferred Data) that lets browsers begin fetching critical resources before the server finishes rendering and data-fetching.
evidence: Early Flush produced a flat ~100ms reduction in First Contentful Paint on every page tested, including the Airbnb homepage; a subsequent regression traced to Nagle's algorithm/Delayed ACK interaction added ~200ms of latency until disabled.
why_it_matters: Shows a PM/eng team how splitting a monolithic server response into streamed chunks trades implementation complexity for a measurable, site-wide perceived-performance win, plus the real infra gotcha (TCP-level tuning) that can silently erase such gains in production.

title: Scaling Airbnb's Identity Graph with a Unified Knowledge Graph Infrastructure
summary: Airbnb's identity graph — used across Trust and Safety to link accounts and flag suspicious activity — had grown to 7 billion nodes and 11 billion edges, adding roughly 5 million edges a day, and the third-party SaaS graph database it ran on since 2021 was hitting latency spikes, operational instability, and had no room for performance tuning. The team rebuilt the storage layer internally on JanusGraph with DynamoDB as the pluggable backend and OpenSearch for indexing, adding a custom transaction strategy using DynamoDB conditional writes, a parallel multi-slice fetch interface for high-fanout traversals, and internal distributed tracing forked into JanusGraph for observability. The new system beat the vendor's latency across all query patterns, scaled write QPS to 10x the prior solution under load testing, and eliminated the vendor's recurring requirement for manual instance reboots. The infrastructure now underpins fraud detection, account-linking, inventory knowledge graphs, and data lineage use cases beyond just Trust and Safety.
what_they_built: An internally-managed graph platform (JanusGraph + DynamoDB + OpenSearch, with custom transaction handling and parallel query fetching) replacing a third-party SaaS graph database to power Airbnb's identity graph.
evidence: Load testing showed write QPS scaled to 10x the prior vendor solution's write QPS; latency improved across all query patterns versus the vendor; the vendor's periodic manual instance reboots were eliminated, enabling faster incident response.
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete decision point and payoff for migrating a T&S-critical, high-scale internal platform off a managed vendor onto owned open-source infrastructure once vendor limits (latency, stability, tunability) start blocking product needs.

title: Variance Reduction Using In-Experiment Data: Efficient and Targeted Online Measurement for Sparse and Delayed Outcomes
publisher: KDD 2023 (ACM SIGKDD Conference on Knowledge Discovery and Data Mining), Applied Data Science track
summary: Airbnb's experimentation platform team faced high-variance, delayed, and sparse outcome metrics (like bookings) that made it hard to run fast, well-powered A/B tests, especially since standard pre-experiment covariate methods like CUPED had limited effectiveness on specific product surfaces. They built two in-experiment-data-only variance reduction methods: a model-based leading indicator metric (inspired by reinforcement learning) that continually tracks progress toward a delayed binary outcome and attributes it back to individual user-product interactions, and a counterfactual treatment exposure index that identifies which subjects were actually impacted by treatment to counter effect dilution. Applied to past experiments, both methods cut variance by 50% or more versus the raw delayed-outcome metric, and across a 141-experiment corpus the leading indicator metric correlated with the delayed outcome at 0.96 versus a previous best of 0.64.
what_they_built: Two variance-reduction techniques for online experiments that use only in-experiment (not pre-experiment) data: a model-based leading-indicator metric with continuous outcome attribution, and a counterfactual treatment-exposure index for targeting diluted treatment effects.
evidence: Both methods achieved variance reduction of 50% or more (the leading indicator alone ranged 50-85%) versus the bookings/delayed-outcome metric; across a 141-experiment corpus (32 with stat.-sig. delayed-outcome movement) the leading indicator metric correlated with the delayed outcome at 0.96, versus a previous best result of 0.64.
why_it_matters: Shows PMs and experimentation-platform builders a concrete alternative to CUPED for sparse/delayed conversion metrics, letting teams get statistically reliable read-outs faster without waiting on rare downstream events.

title: Harnessing the Power of Interleaving and Counterfactual Evaluation for Airbnb Search Ranking
publisher: KDD 2025 (ACM SIGKDD) Applied Data Science track / arXiv
summary: Airbnb's search ranking team faced a familiar experimentation bottleneck: traditional A/B tests are slow and low-sensitivity, so ranking model iterations queue up waiting for statistically significant reads. Airbnb staff (Qing Zhang, Alex Deng, Michelle Du, Huiji Gao, Liwei He, Sanjeev Katariya) built and deployed interleaving and counterfactual evaluation methods into their production experimentation workflow to get faster, higher-sensitivity reads on ranking changes before committing to full A/B tests. The paper documents the mechanism and reports practical insights from running these methods in production at Airbnb.
what_they_built: A production evaluation pipeline combining interleaving experiments and counterfactual (off-policy) evaluation techniques, used as a faster pre-A/B-test signal for search ranking model changes at Airbnb.
evidence: The paper reports the methods "increased the sensitivity of experiments by a factor of up to 100 (depending on the approach and metrics) compared to traditional A/B testing," plus practical insights gained from production usage.
why_it_matters: A PM running an experimentation program can borrow the pattern of layering cheaper, higher-sensitivity evaluation methods (interleaving, counterfactual/off-policy) ahead of full A/B tests to speed up iteration cycles without sacrificing rigor.

title: Building Airbnb's Internationalization Platform
summary: Airbnb needed to deliver translated, versioned content across its global product surface to hundreds of client apps with low latency and high reliability. The team built an internationalization platform spanning a content management system for phrase authoring, a translation vendor pipeline, a translation service that stores versioned translations, an on-device i18n agent for local caching, and a translator library handling fallback, pluralization, and interpolation. The design uses snapshot-based delivery and on-disk caching to keep lookups fast and resilient even when the network or central service is unavailable. It runs as shared internal infrastructure adopted across Airbnb's engineering org rather than a one-off integration.
what_they_built: An internal internationalization (i18n) platform with a phrase-based CMS, a vendor translation pipeline, a translation service, and a client-side i18n agent that caches translations locally for fast, resilient lookups.
evidence: The platform serves more than 1 million pieces of content in 62 languages, handling over 100 billion translate requests daily with microsecond-level latency (per the article's own reported production figures).
why_it_matters: Shows a PM building shared internal platform infrastructure how to trade central-service reliance for edge caching and versioned snapshots to hit both latency and availability targets at massive request volume.

title: Launching airbnb.jp in Record Time
summary: Facing a one-week deadline before a high-profile Tokyo visit by leadership, Airbnb needed a Japanese site but had 400,000 words of English content and no time to translate it all. The team built a prioritization system (a t() tracking function logging controller/action page requests via UDP to Redis) to rank which phrases and pages mattered most by traffic, paired with an in-context translator tool that showed headless-browser screenshots of how phrases actually rendered, and shipped translation updates live without redeploys. They also recruited hosts as community translators rather than relying solely on agencies. The launch drove strong Tokyo listing growth but also surfaced a discoverability/engagement problem versus their French site.
what_they_built: A translation-prioritization tracking system (t() function logging page/email view counts via UDP to Redis) combined with an in-context, screenshot-based translator editor and live phrase deployment, used to launch airbnb.jp in about a week.
evidence: Tokyo listings grew over 180% year-over-year following the launch; however, airbnb.jp's bounce rate was twice that of airbnb.fr, an explicitly acknowledged negative signal pointing to onboarding/navigation gaps for Japanese users.
why_it_matters: Shows a PM how to sequence a fast market-entry localization effort by instrumenting real usage to prioritize translation effort instead of translating everything, while transparently tracking a lagging engagement metric that reveals where the launch still under-served users.

title: Migrating Airbnb's JVM Monorepo to Bazel
summary: Airbnb's JVM monorepo (tens of millions of lines of Java, Kotlin, and Scala) had grown slow and unreliable to build under Gradle, with local builds often exceeding 20 minutes and CI p90 at 35 minutes. Over 4.5 years, the platform team migrated the entire monorepo to Bazel, starting with a proof-of-concept on the Viaduct service and scaling breadth-first while keeping Gradle builds co-existing during transition. Key innovations included an automated BUILD-file generator inspired by Gazelle and support for multiple third-party library versions. The team reports 3-5x faster local build/test times, 2-3x faster IntelliJ syncs and dev environment deploys, and a jump in build satisfaction (CSAT) from 38% to 68%, along with a candid lesson that migrating first and optimizing later would have sped up the rollout.
what_they_built: A company-wide migration of Airbnb's JVM monorepo build system from Gradle to Bazel, including a custom automated BUILD-file generator and tooling to support multiple third-party library versions during the transition.
evidence: Reported outcomes: Build CSAT improved from 38% to 68%; local build and test times 3-5x faster; IntelliJ syncs and dev environment deploys 2-3x faster; migration covered tens of millions of lines of code over 4.5 years.
why_it_matters: Shows a PM-relevant pattern for large internal-platform migrations: proof-of-concept first, breadth-first rollout with dual-running systems, and using a developer-satisfaction metric (CSAT) alongside hard performance numbers to justify and track a multi-year infrastructure investment.

title: Pay As a Local: Airbnb's Local Payment Methods Initiative
summary: Airbnb needed to support locally preferred payment methods (Pix, Naver Pay, UPI, M-Pesa, etc.) across its top 75 travel markets to reduce checkout friction and improve conversion, but each new method traditionally took months to integrate against hardcoded flows. The team built a config-driven platform: a central YAML-based Payment Method Config as the single source of truth for flows, eligibility, input fields and refund rules, with automated code generation, a connector/plugin-based processing architecture, a processor-agnostic Multi-Step Transactions framework, and a PSP Emulator for testing without unstable vendor sandboxes. Using this platform they shipped over 20 locally relevant payment methods across global markets in about 14 months, cutting integration time from months to weeks. They report meaningful booking and new-user-acquisition uplift in launched markets, alongside faster integrations, better observability, and standardized regression testing.
what_they_built: A config-driven local payment methods platform centered on a YAML-based Payment Method Config, three standardized payment flow archetypes (redirect, asynchronous, direct), a connector/plugin-based processor integration architecture, and a PSP Emulator for testing.
evidence: Rolled out 20+ local payment methods across markets in ~14 months, cutting integration time from months to weeks; reports "meaningful uplift" in bookings and new user acquisition in launched markets; cites market context that Pix reached over 76% of Brazil's population by late 2024 (BRL 26.4 trillion processed annually) and Naver Pay reached 30.6 million active users (~60% of South Korea's population).
why_it_matters: Shows how a PM/platform team can turn a repetitive, months-long market-entry integration problem into a declarative, config-driven platform capability that scales localization speed without new client releases.

title: How Airbnb Achieved Metric Consistency at Scale
summary: Different teams at Airbnb were reporting diverging numbers for the same business questions because metric logic was scattered and redefined ad hoc across dashboards and reports. The data team built Minerva, a centralized metrics platform that takes fact and dimension tables as inputs, performs denormalization, and serves a single validated, versioned set of metric definitions to every downstream consumer. The platform underpins tools like Dataportal, Metric Explorer, and the Experimentation Reporting Framework, so a metric is defined once and reused everywhere rather than recomputed differently by each team. By 2021 it had grown to over 12,000 metrics and 4,000 dimensions maintained by 200+ data producers across the company.</summary> <parameter name="what_they_built">A centralized metrics platform (Minerva) built on Airflow, Hive, Spark, Presto, and Druid that ingests fact/dimension tables, denormalizes them, and serves validated, reusable metric definitions to downstream analytics, reporting, and experimentation tools.
what_they_built: Airbnb built Minerva, a company-wide metric platform serving as the single source of truth for metrics, backed by Airflow, Hive/Spark, and Presto/Druid, with metric and dimension definitions stored in a GitHub repo under standardized code review and validation. It scaled to 12,000+ metrics and 4,000+ dimensions from 200+ data producers, and integrates with tools like Dataportal, Superset, ERF, and XRF to serve consistent metrics across analytics, reporting, and experimentation.
evidence: Adoption numbers given directly: 12,000+ metrics and 4,000+ dimensions defined, 200+ data producers using the platform, and a COVID-19 crisis-response dashboard built on Minerva that drew 11,000+ views and 1,500+ distinct viewers in 2020, with the dashboard itself assembled in "just under a few days."
why_it_matters: Shows a PM how to justify and scope a shared-platform investment (single source of truth for metrics) by pointing to the concrete cost it removes — teams disagreeing on numbers — and the adoption/velocity signals that prove it paid off.

title: Metric Infrastructure with Minerva @ Airbnb: How Airbnb Standardized Metric Computation at Scale (Part II)
summary: Airbnb had years of inconsistent, duplicated metric logic across teams, with no single source of truth for how a given metric was computed. The Minerva team built a unified metric platform with a four-stage computation pipeline (ingestion, data check, join, post-processing/serving) built on version-controlled, declarative metric definitions rather than table-level access control. Config-hash-based versioning triggers automatic backfills when definitions change, and a parallel staging environment plus a fast prototyping tool let analysts test new metrics/dimensions in minutes before pushing to production. A worked example shows an analyst (Alice) defining a new urban/rural listing dimension using NASA geospatial data and getting it adopted company-wide within days for COVID travel-pattern analysis.
what_they_built: A centralized, declarative metric computation platform (Minerva) with a standardized four-stage pipeline (ingest, data-check, join, post-process/serve), version-controlled metric/dimension definitions, automatic backfills on definition change, and a staging environment plus fast prototyping tool for safe iteration.
evidence: Reports scale/adoption numbers: the platform reuses materialized data across 5,000+ datasets serving 80+ teams, and its prototyping tool cut sampling time from days to minutes. The Alice/COVID dimension example shows the process working end-to-end with company-wide adoption within days.
why_it_matters: Shows a PM how to design internal data/metrics platforms around declarative definitions and staged rollout (staging + fast prototyping + auto-backfill) so cross-team consistency scales without becoming a bottleneck on every new metric request.

title: From vendors to vanguard: Airbnb's hard-won lessons in observability ownership
summary: Airbnb had relied on third-party vendors for metrics, logs, and traces, but ingestion-based pricing meant costs kept rising without corresponding gains in detection or repair speed, and being outside the feedback loop blocked optimization. The team built an in-house observability platform on open-source Prometheus, taking end-to-end ownership of collection, storage, and querying, and migrated 1,000 services, 300 million timeseries, 3,100 dashboards, and 300,000+ alerts off the vendor tools. Rather than porting legacy patterns as-is, they built a metadata engine to fix flawed queries, standardized on PromQL, and added AI tooling to help engineers write correct queries.
what_they_built: An in-house, Prometheus-based observability platform (metrics collection, storage, and querying) replacing third-party vendor tooling, including a metadata engine and AI-assisted query generation.
evidence: Migrated 1,000 services, 300 million timeseries, 3,100 dashboards, and 300,000+ alerts; internal customers can now diagnose incidents or build dashboards "in a matter of minutes," down from hours previously.
why_it_matters: Shows a PM-relevant pattern for evaluating build-vs-buy on internal platforms: treat an internal tool as a product, measure task time for internal customers, and use that as the migration success metric.

title: Scaling Airbnb's Payment Platform
summary: Airbnb started with manual, offline payments (PayPal plus mailed paper checks to hosts) and needed to internalize payments to build trust in a global marketplace. As it expanded internationally, regional payment methods like Brazilian Boletos exposed the limits of a US-centric system, so the team built "Payments V2": a Billing Interface API that treats transaction components as standardized line items, a Payments Gateway providing a unified API across processors via append-only data and Kafka event distribution, and a Spark/Scala financial data pipeline built on double-entry bookkeeping. The result is a platform now supporting 191 countries, 70+ currencies, and over two dozen payment routes.
what_they_built: A unified payments gateway and billing/financial-data platform (Billing Interface API, Payments Gateway, Kafka-based event pipeline, Spark-based double-entry financial reporting) that abstracts transaction and product details to support many local/regional payment methods.
evidence: Reports adoption/scale figures as the outcome: 191 countries, over 70 currencies, and a network of over two dozen payment routes; qualitatively describes enabling region-specific methods like Brazilian Boletos as proof the pattern generalizes.
why_it_matters: Shows how treating payments/billing as an internal platform product (standardized line-item abstraction plus an event-driven gateway) lets a marketplace scale into new countries without re-architecting for each local payment method.

title: Building a Faster Web Experience with the postTask Scheduler
summary: Airbnb's search results page had a Total Blocking Time of nearly 16 seconds on typical mobile hardware, meaning the main thread was too busy to respond to user input for long stretches. The team adopted the experimental browser postTask API to break up long-running JavaScript tasks into smaller, priority-tagged chunks scheduled around the event loop, and built a usePostTaskScheduler React hook to make this easy to apply across components. They also used postTask to drive image-carousel preloading — waiting for a listing to be 50% visible, then staggering next-image and detail-page preloads with cancellation if the carousel scrolled out of view. The result was close to a 10-second reduction in Total Blocking Time for a typical mobile web user.
what_they_built: A React hook (usePostTaskScheduler) wrapping the browser's prioritized postTask API to chunk long tasks and to schedule cancellable, staggered image/detail preloading in listing carousels.
evidence: Total Blocking Time on the search results page improved from nearly 16 seconds to roughly 6 seconds (about a 10-second reduction) for a typical mobile web user on average hardware; a specific 249ms long task was shown broken into sub-threshold chunks.
why_it_matters: Shows a PM/design-adjacent team using an emerging browser scheduling primitive to close a large, measured gap between technical load time and perceived responsiveness on a high-traffic page.

title: Scaling Airbnb's Experimentation Platform
summary: Airbnb's Experimentation Reporting Framework (ERF) went from handling dozens of concurrent experiments in 2014 to about 500 by 2017, with daily metric computation scaling roughly 100x. The original Ruby/Hive architecture ran monolithic queries with no checkpointing and blocked on all metrics finishing, making it slow and fragile at scale. The team rebuilt it as Python-based Airflow pipelines organized around "Event Sources" rather than individual experiments, added a tiered metric hierarchy (core, target, certified) and precomputed dimensional cuts, plus an interactive ERF Explorer UI backed by Presto for on-demand drill-down.
what_they_built: A re-architected experimentation reporting pipeline (Ruby-to-Airflow migration organized around shared "Event Source" query definitions) with a tiered metric hierarchy, precomputed dimensional cuts, and an interactive ERF Explorer UI for ad hoc drill-down.
evidence: Runtime dropped from "24+ hours to about 45 minutes"; the system now processes roughly 350 Event Sources daily computing about 50,000 distinct experiment/metric combinations, with 50 dimensions precomputed per metric to enable instant UI delivery.
why_it_matters: Shows how to scale an internal experimentation/metrics platform by restructuring the unit of computation (shared event sources, not per-experiment queries) rather than just adding hardware — a pattern applicable to any PM building internal analytics tooling under growing experiment volume.

title: Beyond A/B Test: Speeding up Airbnb Search Ranking Experimentation through Interleaving
summary: Airbnb's search ranking team needed a faster way to test ranking algorithm changes than standard A/B testing, which requires large traffic splits and long run times to detect subtle ranking quality differences. They built an interleaving framework using a "team drafting algorithm" that interleaves results from a control and treatment ranker within the same user session, matching listings at similar positions and randomizing presentation order to cancel out position bias, then measuring which ranker's listings users engage with more. In production use this let them reach the same conclusions as an A/B test with a fraction of the traffic and time, and they validated it against known A/B test outcomes to check reliability before relying on it for ranker assessment, hyperparameter tuning, and infrastructure changes.
what_they_built: An interleaving experimentation framework for Airbnb search ranking that blends control and treatment ranker results into a single ranked list shown to each user, using a team-drafting algorithm to pair comparable listings and randomize order, then attributes engagement to determine the better ranker.
evidence: The team reports achieving "a 50x speedup over an A/B test given the same amount" of traffic; in one concrete case a randomized ranking algorithm test reached the same conclusion using only 0.5% of the A/B test's duration and 4% of its traffic. They also report interleaving results agreed with traditional A/B test outcomes 82% of the time, used as a validation check on the method's reliability.
why_it_matters: A PM running any ranking, recommendation, or search product can borrow the pattern of interleaving-as-a-cheaper-proxy-metric to get faster read on which of two candidate models is better before committing to a full-scale, slower A/B test.

title: Building Services at Airbnb, Part 2
summary: As Airbnb moved to a service-oriented architecture, engineers building new microservices had to hand-roll context propagation, metrics, dashboards, and alerting for every service, which was slow and inconsistent. Airbnb's infrastructure team (authors Liang Guo and Frank Lin) built a Thrift-IDL-centered service platform that auto-generates standardized request/response context propagation, uniform "services_platform"-prefixed metrics and templated dashboards, and automatic latency/error/QPS alerts directly from each service's IDL definition. This let engineers focus on business logic instead of plumbing and monitoring boilerplate. The team's conservative estimate is that the platform saves 2-3 weeks of engineering time per new service versus the prior manual approach.
what_they_built: A Thrift IDL-driven internal service platform at Airbnb that auto-generates context propagation, standardized metrics/dashboards, and service alerts for every new microservice.
evidence: The authors give a conservative estimate that the platform saves 2-3 weeks of engineering time per new service compared to the prior manual setup process; no broader adoption or production-count metrics are given.
why_it_matters: Shows a PM how investing in generated, IDL-driven platform primitives (context, metrics, alerting) removes recurring per-team plumbing work and turns a one-time engineering investment into a per-project time savings that compounds across an organization.

title: Building Services at Airbnb, Part 1
summary: As Airbnb's engineering org grew, teams were building services with inconsistent, ad-hoc JSON-over-HTTP setups that duplicated boilerplate and lacked standard instrumentation. The Production Platform team built a service framework centered on Apache Thrift as an interface definition language, generating both service-side code and RPC clients with Airbnb's standard instrumentation baked in, and extended Dropwizard so services could handle both JSON and Thrift payloads. Teams could define a service's API in a .thrift file and get boilerplate, annotations, and clients generated automatically rather than hand-writing them per service. The platform was rolled out to multiple new Java services with a measured drop in time-to-production.
what_they_built: An internal service-development platform built on Apache Thrift IDL with an extended Thrift compiler and a Dropwizard extension, generating service scaffolding, RPC clients, and JSON/Thrift-compatible endpoints with standard instrumentation baked in.
evidence: "Several new Java services built with service IDL, from the service inception to taking production traffic, took only three weeks — we estimate it to have saved 2-3 weeks of engineering time per Java service over the previous development process."
why_it_matters: Shows how standardizing a golden-path IDL and codegen layer for internal services can be measured directly in engineering time saved, a template for justifying platform investment to stakeholders.

title: Fighting Financial Fraud with Targeted Friction
summary: Airbnb's trust team faced a binary choice problem in fraud detection: hard-blocking suspicious transactions stops fraudsters but also drives away legitimate guests who get flagged, directly hurting revenue and trust. Instead of a block/allow decision, they built an ML model that scores booking fraud probability and routes uncertain cases into "friction" steps — such as micro-authorization checks or requiring a billing-statement upload — that fraudsters are much more likely to abandon than genuine users. They formalize the tradeoff as a loss function balancing false positives, false negatives, and the good-user dropout rate caused by friction, and pick friction thresholds that minimize total expected loss rather than just fraud caught.
what_they_built: An ML-based fraud-scoring system paired with tunable "friction" interventions (micro-authorization, billing-statement verification) applied to medium-risk bookings instead of outright blocking, with thresholds set by an explicit loss-minimization formula.
evidence: Using a worked example with friction that is 95% effective against fraudsters (F=0.95) and only a 10% dropout rate among good users (G=0.1), the post shows total loss falling from roughly 6 (hard-blocking) to roughly 3 (targeted friction) — "cut total losses by nearly half" — versus a hard-blocking baseline.
why_it_matters: Shows PMs a reusable pattern for trust/safety and risk decisions generally: instead of binary allow/deny gates, insert calibrated friction whose cost to good users is measured and explicitly weighed against fraud reduction in a single loss function.

title: Rethinking Text Resizing on Web
summary: Airbnb's web experience failed WCAG 1.4.4 (Resize Text), which requires content to keep working when users scale text to 200% — a real problem given CDC data that over 3 in 5 Americans over 40 have vision issues. Fixed pixel units don't respond to font-scaling preferences, so the team migrated typography from px to rem across two CSS-in-JS systems, Linaria and React-with-Styles, building automated tooling (postcss-pxtorem with an allowlist and an escape-hatch casing convention, plus a custom withRemStyles HOC) rather than relying on manual conversion. They also handled Mobile Safari's nonstandard default font size and used a Figma accessibility-checker plugin to catch resize issues at design time. The rollout resolved more than 80% of existing text-resize issues and reduced the rate of new ones.
what_they_built: Automated px-to-rem migration tooling for two CSS-in-JS systems (a postcss-pxtorem-based pipeline for Linaria, a withRemStyles HOC for React-with-Styles), plus Mobile Safari-specific font-size normalization and a Figma-based design-time accessibility check.
evidence: "More than 80% of our existing Resize Text issues were resolved," with fewer new issues reported since the rollout.
why_it_matters: Shows how a PM can turn a compliance requirement (WCAG 1.4.4) into a scoped, tooling-driven migration with an escape hatch for edge cases, rather than a manual sweep, and how to close the loop by pushing the check upstream into the design tool.

title: Amazon.com 2002 Letter to Shareholders
summary: In its FY2002 shareholder letter, Amazon.com laid out its thesis that customer experience and low prices are not a tradeoff when customer-experience costs are largely fixed (like a publishing model) rather than variable (like traditional retail). It backed the thesis with concrete 2002 actions: eliminating shipping fees via year-round Free Super Saver Shipping on orders over $25, broad price cuts across categories, and a documented price comparison against a major bookstore chain showing Amazon 23% cheaper on the chain's own 2002 bestseller list. The letter reports the resulting financials and operational metrics for the year, framing the pricing/cost-structure choice as directly tied to both customer satisfaction and shareholder value.
what_they_built: A structural pricing decision — permanent free shipping on orders over $25 plus broad category-wide price reductions — justified by a cost-structure argument that customer-experience investment is largely fixed while fulfillment defects (the variable cost) are being driven down.
evidence: Net sales grew 26% to $3.9B with unit sales up 34%; free cash flow reached $135M, a $305M improvement over the prior year (2001 FCF was -$170M); fulfillment cycle time improved 17% and contacts-per-order improved 13% year over year; ACSI customer satisfaction score hit 88, the highest ever recorded in any service industry; a documented 100-book price comparison showed Amazon 23% cheaper than a major bookstore chain ($1,195 vs $1,561).
why_it_matters: Shows a PM-relevant case where a pricing/cost-structure choice (fixed vs. variable cost framing) is defended with matched operational and financial metrics rather than treated as a marketing announcement.

title: CEO Andy Jassy's 2023 Letter to Shareholders
summary: Amazon faced rising per-unit fulfillment costs and slower delivery speeds as its network scaled nationally. The company re-architected its US fulfillment network into eight interconnected regions so inventory is stored closer to customers, and built 58 dedicated same-day facilities in major metros that cut pick-to-ship time to as little as 11 minutes. The regionalization drove the first YoY reduction in global cost-to-serve since 2018, with US cost-to-serve down more than $0.45 per unit, while same-day/overnight delivered items rose nearly 70% YoY and total same/next-day items exceeded 7 billion globally.
what_they_built: A re-architected, regionalized US fulfillment network (eight interconnected regions) paired with 58 new same-day delivery facilities designed to store inventory closer to customers and shrink pick-to-ship cycle time.
evidence: "For the first time since 2018, we reduced our cost to serve on a per unit basis globally"; US cost to serve down more than $0.45/unit YoY; same-day/overnight delivered items up nearly 70% YoY; over 7 billion items delivered same or next day globally (4B+ in the US, 2B+ in Europe); same-day facilities cut pick-to-ship time to as little as 11 minutes.
why_it_matters: Shows how a large-scale operational/network redesign (not a software feature) can be measured and reported with the same rigor as a product launch — a template for PMs owning physical or logistics-heavy product surfaces.

title: Valuing an Engagement Surface using a Large Scale Dynamic Causal Model
summary: Amazon researchers faced the problem of proving what an "engagement surface" (the bundle of recommendations, order reminders, and delivery notifications shown to customers) actually contributes to business and customer value, since simple attribution or naive A/B comparisons overstate or misattribute impact at scale. They built a large-scale dynamic causal model run on roughly 100 million customers over three years at weekly granularity with over 2,000 features per time period, explicitly modeling same-period effects that simpler models miss. They used the model to decompose value by channel and feature, and validated it with bootstrap confidence intervals to check stability of the estimates.
what_they_built: A large-scale dynamic causal model that disentangles the value attributable to a retail engagement surface (recommendations, order reminders, delivery notifications) from other drivers of customer behavior, run on ~100M customers over 3 years at weekly grain with 2000+ features.
evidence: Quantitative results reported directly in the paper: one channel accounts for over 50% of total engagement-surface value; the top two features drive 73% of the surface's impact; over 85% of the value comes from low-average-selling-price, frequent-purchase product categories; excluding same-period effects underestimates the surface's value by 25% overall (and by over 25% for certain individual features); bootstrap confidence intervals on the estimates are narrow, typically within 5-7% of the point estimate even at the product-group level.
why_it_matters: Shows a PM-transferable pattern for justifying investment in a shared, cross-cutting surface (notifications, recommendations, reminders) when clean A/B isolation isn't available: build a causal model that decomposes value by channel and feature so you can defend ROI and prioritize which parts of the surface to keep funding.

title: A Blend of Fire and Ice: Accelerating Innovations Across Internet and Financial Services via Platform Engineering at Ant Group
publisher: KusionStack (Medium publication for Ant Group's open-sourced platform team)
summary: Ant Group's platform engineering team faced a mismatch between the risk-control needs of large-scale internet and financial services and the pace demanded by 10,000+ internal developers, with about 60% of production incidents traced to changes. They built Jiuzhou, a unified internal developer platform with the Kusion orchestrator (using the KCL configuration language) to separate app and infra configuration, layered with Alter Shield for policy-driven canary releases and automatic rollback, and a trust-native IAM system for identity and access control across containers and services. The platform now underpins services reaching over 1 billion users, 80 million merchants, and 2,000+ financial institutions, with a stated shift toward efficiency, risk control, and security as its core "Iron Triangle" metrics.
what_they_built: An internal developer platform (Jiuzhou/Kusion) with a declarative Kubernetes-based control plane, the Alter Shield canary-release and auto-rollback change-management system, and a trust-native IAM/identity-injection layer.
evidence: Reports adoption at 10,000+ internal developers, platform-supported reach of 1 billion+ users, 80 million merchants, and 2,000+ financial institutions, and the organizational-risk statistic that about 60% of Ant Group's incidents are caused by changes — motivating the canary/rollback design.
why_it_matters: Shows how a platform team quantifies change-related risk to justify investment in canary/rollback infrastructure and a unified control plane at large scale.

title: OpenDerisk: An Industrial Framework for AI-Driven SRE, with Design, Implementation, and Case Studies
summary: Ant Group's SRE teams faced root-cause analysis and diagnostic work that generic multi-agent frameworks (built for code generation, not investigation) couldn't handle well. They built OpenDerisk, an open-sourced multi-agent framework with a diagnostic-native collaboration model, pluggable reasoning engine, knowledge engine, and MCP-based protocol so internal developers could build their own specialist diagnostic agents. Over a three-month production rollout it was adopted by 13 new internal application scenarios with 50+ developer-built agents, and a one-month controlled trial of one new agent was validated by 1,743 developers across 6,000+ real cases with an aggregate root-cause success rate above 80%. The platform now runs at production scale serving 3,000+ daily active users and 60,000+ diagnostic runs per day.
what_they_built: An open-source, pluggable multi-agent SRE platform (OpenDerisk) that lets internal developers assemble specialist diagnostic agents (e.g., database expert, network analyst) via a standardized MCP protocol, deployed in production at Ant Group.
evidence: "Over a three-month production period: The framework has been applied to 13 new application scenarios. Internal developers have successfully created over 50 new specialized agents... The platform has achieved significant scale, serving over 3,000 daily active users and executing more than 60,000 diagnostic runs per day." Separately, a one-month production trial of a new RCA agent built on the framework was used by 1,743 developers across 6,000+ real cases with a success rate exceeding 80%.
why_it_matters: Shows how an internal developer platform for AI agents can be evaluated the same way as any product platform -- by developer adoption counts, agent-creation velocity, and usage-scale metrics, not just model benchmarks.

title: Engineering & Operational Excellence at "ASOS" scale — Part II
summary: ASOS's SRE and Operations teams faced a sprawling, poorly-modelled technical estate spanning 160+ teams, hundreds of services, and thousands of components, making ownership and operational understanding difficult at scale. They rolled out Spotify Backstage as an internal developer portal to catalog systems, services, and components, driving adoption through clear problem framing, senior leadership sponsorship, documentation, a "progress over perfection" approach, and a badge-based recognition system for participating teams. The effort produced a working software catalog with over 400 systems and 1,000 components modelled, feeding data into other internal systems. The team also outlines next steps: richer metadata, software templating, and custom plugins toward a unified engineering portal.
what_they_built: An internal developer portal and software catalog built on Spotify Backstage, cataloging ASOS's systems, services, and components with an accompanying team-adoption program (leadership sponsorship, documentation, and a badge/recognition mechanism).
evidence: Over 400 systems and 1,000 components modelled in the catalog, feeding data into other internal systems, across an organization of 160+ teams operating hundreds of services and thousands of components.
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete, evidenced playbook for driving voluntary team adoption of an internal developer platform (leadership backing, progress-over-perfection, gamified recognition) rather than just describing the tooling itself.

title: Turning Handoffs into Handshakes: Integrating Design Systems for AI Prototyping at Scale
summary: Atlassian's design org needed AI-generated prototypes to reliably match the Atlassian Design System instead of hallucinating components, so they built roughly 2,000 lines of custom instructions covering tokens, icons and buttons, plus two prototyping templates (Fast for speed, Full for complex interactions). To stop AI models from rewriting unintended code (e.g., during logo swaps), they moved from open-ended prompting to a structured JSON configuration layer that constrains changes to defined data rather than free-form code edits. They then scaled this through a champions program, workshops and self-serve courses reaching nearly 1,000 designers and PMs.
what_they_built: A design-system-constrained AI prototyping workflow: ~2,000 lines of custom instructions, Fast/Full prototype templates, and a JSON configuration layer replacing prompt-based edits to curb hallucination, rolled out org-wide via a training/champions program.
evidence: ~70% Atlassian Design System accuracy on first-pass AI generation; hallucinations reduced to "nearly zero" after switching to the JSON config layer; nearly 1,000 designers/PMs trained (6-10% as champions/experts); 85% of 1,000+ AI Builders Week participants reported increased confidence using AI tools.
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete, measured pattern for constraining generative AI outputs to an org's design system via structured configuration rather than prompting, plus how to scale adoption through a champions/training model.

title: Post-Incident Review: April 2022 Atlassian Cloud Outage
summary: A migration script meant to delete a standalone "Insight – Asset Management" app instead received full site identifiers due to a communication breakdown, and Atlassian's deletion API had no validation to distinguish app-level from site-level deletion. The script ran against 883 sites on April 5, 2022, wiping out 775 customers' entire cloud instances, and because the deleted sites also held customers' own admin contact data, many affected customers couldn't even reach support through the normal ticket form (which required a valid Cloud URL). Restoration took up to 14 days per site, with full recovery completed April 18. Atlassian committed to universal soft-delete safeguards, a faster/automated disaster-recovery process meeting a one-hour recovery point objective, large-scale-incident playbooks, and support/communications channels that don't depend on the product instance being reachable.
what_they_built: A public post-incident review documenting the root cause of a mass data-deletion outage and a set of committed engineering/process fixes: universal soft deletes with staged rollout safeguards, automated multi-site disaster recovery, large-scale incident playbooks, and backup customer-contact/escalation channels independent of the product itself.
evidence: Concrete figures given: 775 customers / 883 sites affected, outage lasting up to 14 days (April 5-18), over 99.6% of customers/users unaffected, one-hour RPO met with max 5 minutes of data loss (except 57 customers with a longer gap that Atlassian committed to recovering).
why_it_matters: Shows PMs/platform builders why critical support and admin-contact infrastructure must not be a hard dependency on the very product instances it may need to help recover.

title: How Content Assistant raised the design quality bar without slowing us down
summary: Atlassian's content design team faced growing demand for UX copy and voice consistency across products without enough content designers to keep pace. They built Content Assistant, an AI agent on their Rovo platform that drafts short-form UX copy, surfaces content standards, and routes review requests through a trust-tiered Content Review Desk, backed by machine-readable "context stores" of content guidelines and governance/feedback loops. Hundreds of Atlassians across design, engineering, PM, and marketing now use it monthly, with drafting time dropping from hours to about 15 minutes and review-desk throughput scaling to 1,500+ requests in under 9 months.
what_they_built: An AI-native content system comprising the Content Assistant agent (embedded in Slack, Confluence, and other workflows) for drafting UX copy against standards, plus a trust-tiered Content Review Desk and governance/feedback loops for quality control.
evidence: Reported outcomes: 1,500+ requests processed by the Content Review Desk in under 9 months, an 88% reduction in release notes production time, 700+ hours freed for content designers to focus on strategy and craft, and drafting time dropping from a couple of hours to about 15 minutes, with hundreds of employees using the tool monthly.
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete pattern for scaling a design-quality function (content design) via an AI agent plus a tiered review/governance model rather than headcount, which any PM building internal design-ops tooling can borrow.

title: From insights to impact: Content design research at Atlassian
summary: Atlassian's content design team describes how it operationalized research so language decisions aren't guesswork: every study starts with a one-page plan (problem, business justification, deliverables, method, timeline), and the team picks between moderated/qualitative methods (highlight tests, five-second tests, card sorting) to understand why users react a certain way, and unmoderated/quantitative methods (content variation tests, surveys) to validate what copy actually works at scale. As a worked example, the Growth team tested a blank CTA button in the upgrade modal, simply asking users what they thought the button should say, to calibrate the language a real person would expect before committing to copy. The team frames this as building toward reusable value-proposition libraries by combining moderated interview data with unmoderated survey results, rather than one-off research requests.
what_they_built: A repeatable content-design research operations process at Atlassian, pairing a standardized one-page research-plan template with a defined toolkit of moderated and unmoderated testing methods, exemplified by an open-ended CTA copy test run by the Growth team on the upgrade flow.
evidence: Qualitative, not quantitative: the team reports the open-ended CTA test "gave us a better idea of the kind of language a real person would expect," and states that combining moderated and unmoderated data lets them build reusable value-proposition libraries for future work. No adoption numbers, conversion lift, or before/after metrics are given.
why_it_matters: A PM can borrow the pattern of testing copy with open-ended "what should this say" prompts before locking language, and the discipline of a one-page research plan to keep ad hoc research requests structured and reusable.

title: Atlassian's DESIGN.md is here: what we learned testing portable design context in practice
summary: Atlassian's design system team built their own DESIGN.md file — an open Markdown format for giving AI tools portable design-system context — and ran it head-to-head against their existing ADS MCP server and an ADS Skill on a login-screen build task. DESIGN.md was useful for quickly making generic AI output look recognizably Atlassian, especially in unfamiliar environments, but in production-style testing it used far more tokens, had much higher run-to-run variance, and tended to make the AI recreate components from scratch rather than reuse the governed component library. They concluded DESIGN.md is a useful snapshot/prototyping aid, not a replacement for their governed MCP tooling.
what_they_built: A DESIGN.md file distilling Atlassian's design system into portable Markdown context, tested against their existing ADS MCP server and ADS Skill for AI-assisted UI generation.
evidence: On a login-screen task: no context reached ~5% design-system coverage at 4.20M tokens; ADS MCP reached ~80% coverage at 3.75M tokens; ADS Skill reached ~80% coverage at 4.43M tokens; DESIGN.md reached only ~30% coverage while using 7.21M tokens (~92% more than the MCP approach) with 2.7x higher variance across runs.
why_it_matters: Shows PMs/design-platform teams that a portable, human-readable context file is not a free substitute for governed, on-demand tooling (MCP servers) once you measure token cost, variance, and component reuse rather than just surface polish.

title: Behind the Screens: Accessible Design is Good Design
publisher: Atlassian (Work Life / How We Build blog)
summary: Two years ago Atlassian formed a dedicated Accessibility team inside its Design System org to move accessibility from a compliance checkbox into a structural design-quality bar. The team ran a system-wide component audit that surfaced contrast failures, inconsistent focus management, and inaccessible interaction patterns (e.g. a date-time picker requiring 52 keystrokes to switch years), then fixed color tokens, typography, focus order, and built Figma-based accessibility annotation tooling for designers. The date-time picker was rebuilt with semantic structure and proper labeling, cutting the keystrokes needed to change years from 52 to 12. Atlassian reports over 6,000 accessibility issues resolved across its products between June 2024 and June 2025 as a result of this foundation-level work.
what_they_built: A system-wide accessibility audit and remediation program inside Atlassian's Design System, including updated color/contrast tokens, focus-order fixes, Figma accessibility annotation tooling, and a redesigned date-time picker component.
evidence: Date-time picker keyboard input reduced from 52 to 12 keystrokes to switch years; over 6,000 accessibility issues resolved company-wide between June 2024 and June 2025 attributed to the design-system-level fixes.
why_it_matters: Shows how fixing accessibility at the design-system/component layer (rather than screen-by-screen) can compound into thousands of downstream fixes across product teams.

title: Atlassian Design System: Building the context engine for the AI era
summary: Atlassian's Design System (ADS) team faced growing pressure to make their design foundations legible not just to human designers/engineers but to AI agents generating and consuming design work at scale across 20+ apps. They restructured tokens, components and guidelines into a three-tier Core/Platform/App "constellation" model, published structured content files and an MCP server, and shipped an ADS-skills layer for AI agents (integrating with Rovo, Figma Make, Replit). Over a 12-month, 58-update cycle they ran evals comparing AI performance before and after these changes. The team also details specific foundation work — an accessibility-redesigned date-time picker and a 1.5px-stroke icon system used by 550+ designers.
what_they_built: A three-tier (Core/Platform/App) design-system composition model plus an AI-native infrastructure stack — an ADS MCP server, structured schema/markdown content files, and AI agent "skills" — layered on top of existing design tokens and components.
evidence: Reported eval results: 52% accuracy improvement in AI calls, 34% faster on average across ADS-specific tasks, 26% reduction in AI tooling calls, and 16% reduction in AI token usage, following 58 updates shipped over 12 months to thousands of product builders.
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete pattern for making an internal design/platform system legible to AI agents (structured schemas + MCP server + measured evals), not just humans — a template any PM running a design system or internal platform can borrow.

title: The power of a dollar: Quantifying UX challenges
summary: An Atlassian product designer found that "smart values," a feature in Automation, was the number-one qualitative pain point in user research, but qualitative findings alone weren't moving leadership priorities. Working with a Principal Product Engineer, she built a formula converting support ticket volume, average handling time, and support staff cost into a dollar estimate of the problem's business impact. That dollar framing helped win two quarters of funding to build an in-app smart values panel, which then measurably reduced support ticket volume. Other teams subsequently adopted the same costing metric to argue for their own priorities.
what_they_built: A support-ticket-cost quantification formula (tickets x average handling time x support staff hourly cost) used to translate a UX pain point into a dollar-value business case, plus the resulting in-app smart values panel it justified funding for.
evidence: Over 10% of Automation support tickets were tied to the smart values issue; the dollar-cost framing secured two quarters of funding; support volume dropped measurably after the fix shipped; and other team members independently adopted the same costing metric in their own presentations to leadership.
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete, reusable technique for PMs and designers to convert qualitative UX pain points into a leadership-legible dollar metric that competes credibly against other roadmap asks.

title: Life of a Growth Experiment
summary: Atlassian's Growth engineering team noticed 58% of users dropped off a generic Jira product-description screen reached via the product switcher, and hypothesized that replacing a static image with a carousel of use-case-specific visuals would reduce drop-off. They staged the change behind LaunchDarkly feature flags (5% -> 15% -> 25% -> 50% -> 100%) with a 50/50 experiment/control split by hashed user ID. For the Trello-to-Jira flow, click-through rose ~20% and expand rate rose nearly 30%, both statistically significant, with no harm to guardrail metrics. When the same design was rolled out for Bitbucket users, results inverted: expands fell 12% and retention guardrails fell 11%, both statistically significant, forcing a rollback.
what_they_built: A staged, feature-flag-gated (LaunchDarkly) A/B experiment replacing a static Jira value-prop image with a rotating, use-case-specific carousel on the product-switcher expand screen.
evidence: Trello-to-Jira: ~20% higher click-through, ~30% higher expand rate, statistically significant, no guardrail regressions. Bitbucket replication: 12% statistically significant decline in expands and -11% effect on retention guardrail metrics, leading to rollback.
why_it_matters: Shows PMs that a validated win in one user segment can invert in another, so experiments must be re-run (not just extrapolated) per segment before scaling a design change.

title: Behind the screens: Building Atlassian's new icon system
summary: After six years, Atlassian's icon set had grown to 350+ icons with inconsistent stroke widths, sizing, and fragmented custom icons across products. The design team redesigned the icons (thinner 1.5px strokes on a 16px canvas, rounder shapes to match the Atlassian Sans typeface) but the harder problem was migration: the new sizing and naming conventions touched more than 16,000 callsites across the codebase. To make that tractable they built a feature-flagged rollout with an "Icon Facade" for backwards-compatible runtime swapping, ESLint-based automated codemods that handled roughly 75% of migrations, a Custom Icon Extractor to audit legacy SVGs, and a centralized "Icon Lab" contribution platform with a Figma plugin. The system shipped across Jira, Confluence, Trello, and platform apps, and the team distilled explicit lessons about tooling being as important as visual design for large-scale design-system adoption.
what_they_built: A new 16px icon design language plus a migration toolchain (feature flags, an Icon Facade compatibility layer, ESLint-automated codemods, a Custom Icon Extractor, and an Icon Lab/Icon Explorer contribution platform) to roll the redesign out across a 16,000+ callsite codebase.
evidence: Quantified: more than 16,000 callsites updated, approximately 75% of icons migrated automatically via ESLint rules, successful deployment across Jira, Confluence, Trello, and platform apps. Also qualitative lessons: tooling proved as important as visual/experience design for adoption, and backwards compatibility with gradual migration paths let teams migrate at their own pace.
why_it_matters: Shows a PM how to de-risk a large-scale design-system migration by investing as much in compatibility/automation tooling (feature flags, codemods, facade layers) as in the redesign itself, so teams can adopt gradually without a coordinated big-bang cutover.

title: Writing High-Performing Copy
summary: An Atlassian designer describes the team's design-first approach to product copywriting: draft the narrative/journey before visuals, run early comprehension tests across copy variations, then A/B test messaging to quantify impact before shipping. Applied to the invite welcome email, the Growth team rewrote the messaging using psychological triggers and plain-language principles, then measured the result in a live experiment. The piece also lays out a practical checklist (voice guidelines, brevity, data support, peer review) for writing product copy.
what_they_built: A repeatable copy-testing workflow — early signal comprehension tests followed by A/B experiments — applied to rewrite the invite welcome email's messaging.
evidence: The reworked invite welcome email messaging produced a 3.27% increase in the log-in rate of invited users, which the team projected into meaningful monthly-active-user gains over 12 months.
why_it_matters: Shows a PM/designer how to treat copy as a testable product surface with its own experiment pipeline rather than a late-stage polish step.

title: Post-incident review for September 2025 change to status button location
summary: Atlassian moved the Status control on Jira's work item view to consolidate primary actions, based on limited experiments (Nov 2024-Mar 2025) that showed increases in new-user actions and retention. Status button location was never explicitly tested in the qualitative UX round that preceded the June 2025 GA decision, and existing-customer feedback during experimentation was understated internally. After September 2025 full rollout, high-volume and enterprise customers reported lost muscle memory and more scrolling, prompting an immediate revert and a paused UI roadmap for the work item view.
what_they_built: A relocated Status control on Jira's work item view, tested via limited team/company-managed project experiments and separate qualitative UX testing, then rolled out to general availability and subsequently reverted.
evidence: Qualitative: strong negative customer feedback, reported difficulty locating Status and more scrolling on longer issues; company explicitly reverted the change, paused all further work-item-view UI changes, and committed to larger release bundles, expanded enterprise review, longer opt-in/beta periods, and clearer "why this helps" communication.
why_it_matters: Shows how experiment design that measures new-user activation can miss the muscle-memory cost imposed on high-frequency existing users, and how skipping an explicit test of the specific change being shipped (button location) creates blind spots that surface only at full rollout.

title: Designed for delight, built for performance: The journey of pragmatic drag and drop
summary: Atlassian had accumulated multiple independent drag-and-drop implementations across its product suite, which bloated shipped code, slowed application performance, and produced inconsistent visual and accessibility experiences. A cross-functional team of engineers, designers, and accessibility specialists built Pragmatic Drag and Drop, a single shared library with a small core and many optional pieces so products only ship the code they need. The team built on native browser drag-and-drop primitives (adding abstractions to make them safe and ergonomic), rethought drag-preview visuals around constraints rather than fighting them, and gave assistive-technology users equivalent outcomes via keyboard-driven action menus rather than mimicking pointer drags. The library was then open-sourced with its behavior layer decoupled from Atlassian's specific design and accessibility choices.
what_they_built: A modular, open-sourced drag-and-drop library ("Pragmatic Drag and Drop") with a small core plus optional pieces, built on native browser primitives, that replaced multiple redundant legacy drag-and-drop implementations across Atlassian's products.
evidence: Qualitative: the team reports reduced shipped code by consolidating multiple drag-and-drop solutions into one modular library, established a consistent cross-product visual design language (lines/borders/background color instead of movement-based feedback), and delivered keyboard-accessible equivalents for assistive technology users; no quantitative before/after metrics are given in the retrieved text.
why_it_matters: Shows how a platform team can retire redundant internal implementations by shipping a small-core-plus-optional-modules architecture co-designed with design and accessibility from the start, rather than bolting those concerns on afterward.

title: 8 principles to guide your SaaS pricing strategy
summary: Atlassian's CRO Cameron Deatsch recounts two decades of the company's pricing decisions and the concrete outcomes each produced. From the 2002 $800 flat-fee Jira license (set with no market data), to a 2008-recession "starter plan" of $10 for 10 users with proceeds donated to charity, to eliminating that $10 fee entirely in March 2020 to make the first 10 users free, each change is tied to a measured signup or customer-acquisition effect.
