The piece also covers Atlassian's move to a consistent editions-based model (Free/Standard/Premium/Enterprise) across its product portfolio and its policy of transparent, non-negotiated public pricing.
what_they_built: A public, self-serve, non-negotiable pricing model built around low-friction entry points (free trials, $10-for-10-users starter plans, later a free first-10-users tier) and a simplified four-tier (Free/Standard/Premium/Enterprise) editions structure applied consistently across Atlassian's product line.
evidence: The 2008 $10-for-10-users starter plan drove "thousands of new customers" to adopt it and opened an entirely new customer cohort; eliminating the $10 fee in March 2020 caused signups to triple "nearly overnight," accelerating overall paid customer base growth; the company also reports new-customer numbers growing year-on-year every year since founding.
why_it_matters: Shows a PM how deliberately lowering (or removing) entry-price friction, rather than optimizing per-customer revenue, can be a primary lever for flywheel-style customer acquisition growth.

title: Using spec-first API development for speed and sanity
summary: Atlassian's Jira team had to build a feature-flag integration API on an eight-week timeline (six weeks of active development) with three external launch partners implementing simultaneously against different feature-flag vendor models. Their prior implementation-first process — design sketched in Confluence, real API design happening inside the code during implementation — produced slow feedback loops (days to weeks) and review comments that focused on code rather than API design. They switched to a spec-first process: write the Swagger spec first as the design artifact, review pull requests against it, encode example interactions as Pact files, expose the spec via swagger-ui for partners, and validate implementations against the spec and against the Pact contracts automatically.
what_they_built: A spec-first API development workflow for a Jira feature-flag integration API, combining a Swagger/OpenAPI specification as the primary design artifact with Pact-based consumer-driven contract testing, swagger-ui for partner-facing preview, and validator/codegen tooling (swagger-request-validator, swagger-mock-validator, swagger-codegen) to keep implementation and spec in sync.
evidence: Qualitative lessons rather than hard metrics: feedback loops shrank from days/weeks to hourly iteration cycles, review feedback shifted to focus on API design/consistency instead of implementation details, and contract tests caught breaking changes immediately — letting three external partners build against mocks in parallel with Atlassian's own six-week development window. The post also lists acknowledged gaps (no built-in breaking-change detection, limited tooling for documenting bad-request examples, difficulty merging multiple specs).
why_it_matters: A PM coordinating a platform API launch with multiple external partners can transfer the core move: ship the interface contract (and mocks) before the implementation, so partners build in parallel instead of waiting on a finished backend.

title: Teaching AI to speak our design language
summary: Atlassian's design system information was scattered across docs sites, Confluence, Figma, videos, and code packages, so AI coding agents generating Atlassian UI produced unpredictable results—outdated patterns, missed accessibility requirements, and invented components. The team built "structured content": consistent, machine-readable schemas for components, icons, tokens, lint rules, and guidelines authored as TypeScript files alongside code, feeding a Model Context Protocol (MCP) server that serves AI agents and other channels. They also built self-healing documentation that auto-updates the structured content when components change.
what_they_built: A schema-based "structured content" layer (components, icons, tokens, lint rules, guidelines) plus an MCP server that serves this machine-readable design-system content to AI coding agents, with self-healing docs that update automatically as components change.
evidence: Comparing agents without MCP, with MCP, and with MCP plus structured content: 52% accuracy improvement on specific queries, 34% faster task completion on average, 26% fewer tool calls, 16% reduced token usage, 4.9% more accurate code, and 11% fewer errors.
why_it_matters: Shows PMs/design leads that restructuring existing documentation into machine-readable schemas—rather than writing new content—can be the highest-leverage move for making internal AI agents reliably follow product/design standards.

title: Here's how one of Atlassian's critical services consistently gets above 99.9999% of availability
summary: Atlassian's Tenant Context Service (TCS) and its sidecar caching layer serve up to 32 billion requests a day across 10,800 compute nodes, but in 2019 a PaaS migration deployed several TCS instances with empty DynamoDB tables; systems treated the resulting 404s as valid and cached them, progressively taking cloud products offline across regions before the team recovered via bulk re-ingestion within hours. To prevent recurrence, the team built server-side protections (blocking empty-table deploys, monitoring 200-vs-404 response ratios, automatic canary rollback on anomalies) and sidecar-side protections (content-check keys, consensus comparison against peer response ratios, marking suspicious parents "untrusted" and dumping local caches). The piece credits this defensive redesign, alongside gRPC adoption and cache pre-warming, with sustaining above six-nines availability at scale.
what_they_built: A cache-poisoning defense system combining server-side response-ratio anomaly detection with automatic canary rollback, and sidecar-side consensus checks that mark upstream parents "untrusted" and flush local caches when their response patterns diverge from peers.
evidence: Reports concrete production metrics: 32 billion requests/day across 10,800 compute nodes, peak throughput of 586,308 requests/second, median response times of ~11 microseconds, and availability exceeding 99.9999% ("six nines"); also notes the 2019 incident was resolved within hours via bulk re-ingestion.
why_it_matters: Shows how a platform team turned a specific outage mode (silently poisoned distributed caches) into a generalizable trust/consensus mechanism rather than a one-off patch, which is a transferable pattern for any team running edge caches at scale.

title: Implementing typography at scale: the journey behind the screens
summary: Atlassian's design system team set out to modernize typography across 18+ production apps (Jira, Confluence, etc.), which suffered from vague guidance, no design/code parity, and visual inconsistency from relying on system fonts. They rolled out the rebuild in three phases: first tokenizing the existing system with no visible change to de-risk the framework, then introducing visual enhancements (a minor-third type scale, bolder headings, minimum text size raised from 11px to 12px, removal of ALL CAPS), and finally launching two custom typefaces, Atlassian Sans and Atlassian Mono, to eliminate cross-platform font rendering inconsistencies. A pilot group drawn from the Atlas, Confluence, and Jira teams ran workshops to validate the scale before wider rollout, and the team wrote up five lessons learned about co-design, simplification, breaking down silos, iteration time, and naming discipline.
what_they_built: A phased typography design-system rebuild — tokenization, a new type scale, and two custom typefaces (Atlassian Sans, Atlassian Mono) — validated with a cross-team pilot group and shipped across 18+ production apps.
evidence: Concrete before/after mechanism changes are reported: minimum heading/body size raised from 11px to 12px for accessibility, a new minor-third font/line-height scale, removal of ALL CAPS text, and two custom typefaces replacing system fonts (SF Pro, Segoe UI) to fix baseline inconsistencies; outcome described qualitatively as clearer visual hierarchy, enhanced design consistency, and improved accessibility, plus five explicit process lessons from running the rollout.
why_it_matters: Shows a PM/design-system lead how to de-risk a system-wide visual overhaul by decoupling tokenization from visual change and validating with a small cross-functional pilot before rolling out to dozens of teams.

title: We Raised Prices by 250%: The Good, The Bad, and What We Would Have Done Differently
summary: Baremetrics had not raised its MRR-tier-based pricing since launching in 2013 and believed it was underpriced relative to the value delivered. Over roughly a year, the team ran a three-round A/B/C pricing-page experiment (current vs +25%/+50%, then 50 vs 100 vs 150%, then 150 vs 250 vs 300%), with the CEO ultimately choosing a 250% increase implemented in April 2022 through a phased rollout of experimentation, customer notification, and negotiation conversations. The change lifted MRR 86% overnight while pushing churn to 4-6.9%, and the post closes with concrete process changes they'd make next time (shorter customer-focused emails, 90+ day notice windows, upfront pricing detail).
what_they_built: An 8-month, three-round A/B/C pricing-page experimentation process (escalating price-point variants) followed by a phased customer-facing rollout (notify, then negotiate, then implement) to execute a 250% price increase.
evidence: MRR increased 86% overnight, crossing into the $300k MRR range; post-increase churn fluctuated between 4-6.9%; the full process from testing through full implementation took over a year.
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete, evidenced playbook for de-risking a large pricing change via successive experiment rounds and a phased customer-communication rollout, plus an honest account of the churn tradeoff and communication mistakes to avoid.

title: We sold Intros for $100,000: Turning a product failure into a strategic win
summary: Baremetrics built Intros, a product connecting companies and investors using actual company data, and launched it in 2018. Within three months the product failed commercially because the target investor audience wasn't willing to invest in it, and founder Josh Pigford decided to shut it down rather than keep sinking resources in. Instead of quietly sunsetting it, Baremetrics sold the asset to Andrew Wilkinson at Tiny Capital for $100,000 after a couple weeks of negotiation, then spent a couple months decoupling the code for handoff. The cash injection was used deliberately to build up reserves beyond Baremetrics' existing breakeven-profitable state, giving the company more room to take risks.
what_they_built: Baremetrics Intros, a data-driven matchmaking product connecting companies with investors, which they shut down after three months and then sold as an asset to another company.
evidence: Product launched 2018, shut down after ~3 months due to investor-side commercial failure; sold to Tiny Capital for $100,000 following a couple weeks of negotiation and a couple months of code/handoff work, with proceeds explicitly earmarked to build cash reserves beyond the company's breakeven-profitable baseline.
why_it_matters: Shows a PM that a failed product isn't necessarily a write-off — a fast, honest post-mortem plus a willingness to sell the asset can convert sunk work into real strategic capital.

title: The Open Startups Initiative
summary: Baremetrics had already made its own revenue metrics public and saw a following build around it. In this post they formalize that into the Open Startups Initiative: an opt-in program where companies wire their billing data (via Baremetrics) into live public dashboards showing MRR, ARR, LTV, churn, and customer counts. Seven founding companies (Baremetrics, Buffer, ConvertKit, Ghost, Hubstaff, Promoter, StoreMapper) launched with it, and the post cites over 125,000 entrepreneurs following the participating companies' numbers. The stated rationale is that public metrics create accountability and cut through startup hype.
what_they_built: An opt-in mechanism (built on Baremetrics' own billing-data pipeline) that publishes a company's live MRR/ARR/LTV/churn/customer dashboard publicly, plus a coordinated multi-company initiative to recruit other startups into doing the same.
evidence: Seven founding companies live on the initiative at launch, and the post reports "over 125,000 entrepreneurs following along as our companies grow" — an adoption/audience figure rather than a controlled outcome metric.
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete, low-friction way a company can turn its own operating metrics into a public trust/community asset rather than treating them as competitively sensitive secrets.

title: Get One Piece Done (Shape Up, Chapter 11)
summary: Basecamp's Shape Up handbook describes a real internal case: while building "Clients in Projects" for Basecamp 3, a two-person team (one designer, one programmer) chose the visibility toggle as the first thing to fully integrate end-to-end, rather than designing all screens or building all backend logic first. The designer worked on rough affordances for the toggle while the programmer independently spiked the access model, then they wired the stubbed toggle to real state without full visual polish. Roughly three days into the project the working toggle was demoed to a manager, letting the team mark that piece done and build confidence going into the rest of the cycle.
what_they_built: A vertically-sliced, functionally-working (though not visually finished) visibility toggle for the "Clients in Projects" feature, built by parallelizing rough design affordances with an independent backend access-model spike.
evidence: Qualitative outcome: the toggle was demoed as a working piece to a manager about three days into the project cycle, and the team treated that as one integrated piece "designed, implemented, demoed, and settled" — no quantitative metrics given.
why_it_matters: Shows PMs and design/eng leads a concrete mechanism for de-risking a project early: pick the most central, hardest piece and get a rough end-to-end version working before polishing anything, rather than finishing all design or all backend first.

title: Building apps for everyone: discover why inclusivity and accessibility truly matter
summary: Two BBC Android engineers describe how the company operationalised accessibility across its app teams rather than treating it as a one-off compliance pass. Issues that can't be fixed immediately (e.g. screen-reader bugs, broken text-size scaling) are logged as "accessibility debt" so they stay visible and get revisited, and every product team has an embedded accessibility champion to advocate for and guide fixes. The piece frames the effort against UK disability statistics (16% of adults, rising above 30% counting temporary/situational impairment) and describes running research panels and lab sessions with disabled users to validate real-world usability, citing a screen-reader user's thank-you message after the BBC Sounds launch as evidence the work mattered.
what_they_built: An "accessibility debt" tracking practice for unresolved accessibility issues plus an accessibility-champions network embedded in every BBC product team, backed by recurring disabled-user research panels and lab sessions.
evidence: Qualitative outcomes: product teams shifted from questioning why the champions network existed to asking how to collaborate with it, and BBC Sounds received explicit user feedback thanking the team for screen-reader support post-launch; no hard adoption/defect-reduction metrics are given.
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete organisational pattern — a visible debt backlog plus a distributed champions network — for making accessibility a continuous team responsibility rather than a late-stage audit.

title: An Accessible Digital BBC — 2019 in review
summary: BBC's Senior Accessibility Specialist reviews a year of shipped accessibility work across BBC digital products. The team scaled an internal Accessibility Champions network past 200 people, shipped a reduce-motion preference in iPlayer and Sounds, introduced the Reith typeface for easier small-screen reading in News, built a keyboard-accessible scrolling story format for Visual Journalism, launched 7 new accessibility-pillared games for children's content with 8 more in development, and began opt-in testing of a redesigned 'accessible first' iPlayer Mobile player. It frames accessibility as embedded organisational practice (champions, guilds) rather than a single feature.
what_they_built: A set of shipped accessibility features across iPlayer, Sounds, News and children's products, plus an internal cross-team Accessibility Champions network and an opt-in redesigned iPlayer Mobile player interface built 'accessible first'.
evidence: 200+ Accessibility Champions in the network (plus 75+ alumni), 7 new accessibility-pillared games shipped with 8 more in development, reduce-motion and subtitle-size controls shipped to iPlayer/Sounds, and an opt-in accessible iPlayer Mobile redesign available via app settings.
why_it_matters: Shows how a large product org operationalizes accessibility as a measurable, cross-team practice (champions network, per-product pillars) rather than a one-off compliance feature.

title: Shifting left: how introducing accessibility earlier helps the BBC's design system
summary: A BBC design system team wanted to reuse an existing component for a new chronologically ordered content list because it looked visually similar. By requesting UX accessibility documentation during the design phase rather than later, the team discovered the component was built for user-driven discovery in high-density pages, not structured navigation, and would need ARIA workarounds and extra config to force the fit. That early catch led the team to adopt a new design principle — focus on what a component achieves, not just how it looks — before any development work began.
what_they_built: A process change requiring UX accessibility documentation (semantic HTML structure) earlier in the design phase, plus a new design-system principle to evaluate components by function rather than appearance.
evidence: Qualitative: the team caught the component-reuse mismatch before development started, avoiding what the author describes as a likely late-stage refactor at review; no quantitative metrics are given.
why_it_matters: Shows a PM/design-systems lead a concrete gate — accessibility documentation as an artifact required during design review, not just at QA — that catches misapplied component reuse before it becomes a costly rebuild.

title: Shifting Left: How Introducing Accessibility Earlier Helps the BBC's Design System
summary: The BBC's accessibility lead describes how design system components had grown overly flexible, so much so that a team assumed an existing link component could be reused for a new chronologically-ordered content list feature. The team began drafting semantic HTML structure and accessibility behavior (landmarks, headings, lists, focus management) collaboratively with UX during the design phase rather than leaving it to developers late in the build. Doing so surfaced, before any code was written, that the chosen component couldn't support the feature's required semantics without significant bloat. The BBC design system subsequently adopted a new principle: focus on what components achieve rather than how they look, to guide future reuse decisions.
what_they_built: A process change embedding accessibility/semantic-HTML documentation into the design phase of feature work (before development), alongside a new BBC design-system principle prioritizing component function over appearance.
evidence: Qualitative outcome: catching, during design documentation rather than at development/review stage, that a proposed component reuse would not support the feature's semantic requirements — described by the author as avoiding "a potentially time-consuming, stressful and messy refactor much closer to a deadline, possibly once the development work was at the review stage."
why_it_matters: Shows a PM/design-system owner a concrete, low-cost gate — documenting semantic structure and accessibility behavior during design review — that catches component-fit mistakes before they become expensive late-stage refactors.

title: Adding Voiceover To Carousel Components
summary: The BBC News app team was rolling out a new flexible story carousel component and needed it to work for VoiceOver users, who couldn't tell that carousel cards were tappable links or easily skip past the carousel. They evaluated and rejected three approaches informed by user testing: a custom rotor item (many VoiceOver users don't use the rotor day-to-day), a custom gesture (users rarely want to learn new app-specific gestures), and heading-based navigation (carousels don't always have a nearby heading). They shipped a custom UIAccessibilityElement subclass that overrides accessibilityValue and accessibilityTraits to expose the carousel as an "adjustable" element with a "button" suffix on labels, letting VoiceOver users swipe through or skip the carousel with standard gestures.
what_they_built: A custom iOS UIAccessibilityElement subclass for BBC News app carousels that overrides accessibilityValue/accessibilityTraits to present the carousel as an adjustable, skippable VoiceOver element with clearer link affordance.
evidence: Qualitative: user research directly ruled out the rotor-item and custom-gesture approaches ("many voiceover users do not use the rotor in their everyday life"; "voiceover users rarely want to learn new behaviour when using an app"), and the team explicitly notes Apple has no standard carousel accessibility pattern, so they plan further iteration based on ongoing user feedback rather than reporting hard adoption metrics.
why_it_matters: Shows how to convert accessibility user research directly into a build-vs-reject decision matrix rather than defaulting to the most "correct-looking" platform API.

title: The lessons learnt creating a design system for BBC Online
summary: BBC's design and engineering teams needed to move from building separate websites for News, Sport, CBBC and CBeebies to a single integrated BBC website (the WebCore project), which required a shared design system rather than per-team component libraries. They built a system with five layers - foundations, components, layout components, "levers" for theming, and containers - developed jointly by teams in a shared monorepo rather than owned centrally. The piece lays out organizational lessons drawn from running this in production: components are only built after being needed by at least two use-cases, ownership is shared rather than centralized, and strict component constraints paired with theming levers let very different BBC destinations retain distinct visual identities on the same underlying components.
what_they_built: A shared, multi-team design system (WebCore) with foundations/components/layout/levers/containers layers, developed collaboratively inside a single monorepo and documented across wikis, GitHub and Storybook.
evidence: Qualitative: the authors describe a shift from siloed, per-product component libraries to shared ownership across News, Sport, CBBC and CBeebies, with just-in-time component creation (built once two use-cases exist) and a theming-lever mechanism enabling consistent implementation with distinct branding; no numeric adoption metrics are given.
why_it_matters: Shows how a large multi-product org can scale a design system through shared ownership and need-based component creation rather than a centralized team dictating reuse.

title: How we managed to modernize the Booking.com app from the inside and out
summary: Booking.com set out to modernize its native app's codebase and design language across roughly 60 teams, initially estimating the effort at one year but revising to 2.5 years once real scope emerged. The team found individual code/UI changes rarely showed measurable impact on their own, so they built tracking systems to measure cumulative progress instead, eventually reaching 45% of the Accommodation Look-to-Book funnel modernized and 60% of the app overall. An internal engineer survey and design-language experiments provided the outcome evidence once enough of the app had shifted.
what_they_built: A multi-year, cross-team app modernization effort (codebase and design language) with automated progress-tracking systems to measure cumulative adoption across ~60 teams.
evidence: 45% of the Accommodation Look-to-Book funnel modernized after 6 months (against an 80% target); 60% of the app modernized by the time of an internal survey; 67% of surveyed engineers agreed development velocity improved and 70% agreed app stability improved; design-language experiments showed measurable conversion and engagement gains once enough of the app had converted.
why_it_matters: Shows PMs how to structure measurement for a long-horizon platform migration where no single change is individually measurable, by tracking cumulative adoption and pairing it with periodic qualitative/survey signals.

title: Broken Windows of Data: How Booking.com scales a shared DWH across multiple teams without turning governance into a delivery bottleneck
summary: Booking.com runs a hybrid centralized data warehouse (Snowflake, dbt, Data Vault, GitLab CI/CD, Airflow) shared across eight business units and 40+ use cases, and faced the classic "broken windows" risk: small unaddressed quality lapses eroding trust and slowing delivery as more teams contributed models. They built a six-stage DWH Models Quality Flow spanning pre-code business-model review, local pre-commit and dbt-score checks, CI/CD automation, AI-assisted merge-request review, human review for business logic and architecture, and post-deployment monitoring. The piece reports both quality and velocity outcomes rather than treating governance and speed as a tradeoff. It closes with the lesson that repeated exposure to quality checks trains contributors, not just controls them.
what_they_built: A six-stage governance pipeline for their shared dbt-based data warehouse — combining pre-commit dbt-score checks, CI/CD validation, AI-assisted merge-request review, and post-deploy monitoring — to keep model quality high as contribution volume from multiple teams scaled up.
evidence: "More than 95% of checks pass, the portfolio averages just above 9 out of 10, and around 85% of models achieve Silver or Gold status" across roughly 1,000 dbt models; "merged MR volume in the warehouse more than tripled" over seven months while review responsiveness improved; median time to DWH approval fell below two hours by late 2025 and to under one hour by April 2026.
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete pattern for PMs/platform owners running a shared internal data or code platform: layered automated + human + AI review gates can raise both quality metrics and delivery velocity simultaneously rather than trading one for the other.

title: Meta-Experiments: Improving Experimentation Through Experimentation
summary: Booking.com's centralized experimentation platform team noticed many teams ran underpowered A/B tests, so they built a "low-power alert" feature that warns experimenters when their experiment lacks sufficient statistical power and offers a one-click fix. Rather than relying on qualitative feedback or noisy time-series correlation, the team ran a randomized controlled trial on their own platform (a "meta-experiment"), randomizing at the experiment level rather than experimenter level, running it for months instead of the usual weeks to get sufficient power on only ~1000 concurrent internal experiments. Both the primary metric (share of sufficiently powered experiments) and a supporting behavioral metric (whether power-related settings were changed) increased significantly, with no significant change in shipped-experiment rate, and the team acknowledged likely upward bias from variant spillover among experimenters running multiple experiments. The feature was then rolled out platform-wide to all users.
what_they_built: A "low-power alert" feature in Booking.com's in-house experimentation platform that flags underpowered experiments to experimenters and links to a one-click fix, validated by running a proper RCT ("meta-experiment") on the platform itself with experiments as the randomization unit.
evidence: Both the primary metric (proportion of sufficiently powered experiments) and the supporting metric (rate of power-setting changes) "increased significantly" in the treatment group; no significant effect on whether experiments shipped; the team judged the effect real (if possibly overestimated due to spillover) and rolled the alert out to all low-powered experiments platform-wide.
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete pattern for internal platform/process teams — treat your own interventions as products and validate them with the same rigor (RCTs) you demand of customer-facing teams, rather than relying on anecdote or noisy trend-watching.

title: 8 steps to a UX copy strategy that rocks
publisher: Booking.com — UX Writing (Booking.com's official Medium publication)
summary: Booking.com's UX writing team found customers were confused about when and how much they'd be charged during booking, driving avoidable customer-service contacts. They designed a new copy block on the book-process page stating exact payment dates and amounts in plain, jargon-free, personalized language (avoiding terms like "preauthorize"), developed through user testing and competitor analysis. An A/B test showed customers who saw the block contacted customer service less, and a follow-up survey found they could state payment timing and amount more accurately than the control group. The approach was later reused for mobile and for cancellation-policy copy.
what_they_built: A redesigned copy block on the booking-process page giving customers exact payment dates and amounts in plain, personalized language, replacing vague/jargon-heavy prior copy.
evidence: Qualitative/directional: an A/B test found customers who saw the new block contacted customer service less than those who didn't, and a comprehension survey found they could state payment timing and amount more accurately than the control group; no exact percentages disclosed.
why_it_matters: Shows how UX writing changes can be treated as testable product interventions with measurable service-load and comprehension outcomes, not just stylistic polish.

title: UX Writing meets Machine Learning
summary: A Booking.com UX writer describes partnering with a senior data scientist on the User Generated Content team to turn thousands of raw guest reviews into upfront, skimmable summaries. The data science side built a multi-class text classifier to bucket review sentences into topics (comfort, breakfast, location, etc.) and a sentiment model to mark each topic positive, negative, or neutral; the UX writer then hand-authored the natural-language copy that presents each topic/sentiment combination to guests before they read full reviews. The piece walks through the collaboration process between writer and data scientist rather than model internals. After launch the team observed guests booking higher-rated properties, which they read as a sign of more informed decision-making.
what_they_built: An automated, topic-and-sentiment-tagged review summary feature on property pages, combining a data-scientist-built text classifier/sentiment model with hand-written UX copy for each topic-sentiment combination.
evidence: Qualitative outcome only, stated directly by the team: "we noticed people are now booking higher-rated properties. This means they are now making more informed decisions." No numerical conversion/engagement figures are given in the article.
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete pattern for PMs/designers pairing with data science teams — using a classifier's structured output as scaffolding for human-authored copy rather than fully automating the language.

title: How to track Design System Adoption
summary: After more than 25 internal projects had adopted Brevo's Indigo design system within a year, the team had no systematic way to measure how deeply it was being used or to justify its ROI to stakeholders. They built an automated tracking pipeline: a monthly GitHub Actions cron job clones every frontend repo in the org, parses each codebase with react-scanner to detect component usage, and pushes the parsed data into Metabase via Prisma. The resulting dashboards let them see total Indigo component instances org-wide, per-project component counts, which components are popular versus underused, package version drift across projects, and the ratio of Indigo to legacy components.
what_they_built: An automated, monthly GitHub Actions job that clones all org frontend repos, parses them with react-scanner, and loads component-usage data into Metabase via Prisma for org-wide design-system adoption dashboards.
evidence: Reports concrete tracked outputs used to inform decisions: total Indigo component instances across the org over time, distinct component counts per project, most-used vs. underutilized components, package version distribution (to catch outdated versions), and the ratio of Indigo to legacy frontend-library components across 25+ adopting projects.
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete, low-cost pattern any platform/design-systems team can copy for turning "is anyone using this?" into a measurable, automated adoption metric rather than anecdote.

title: 18 Small Changes That Made Buffer a Little Better in 2025
summary: Buffer's team faced the perennial tension between shipping new features and fixing accumulating bugs and technical debt. They built a formal triage system with SLA tiers (P1 bugs resolved within 14 days, P2 within 30, P3 within 60), reserved roughly 10% of engineering capacity specifically for bug fixing and technical housekeeping, and instituted bi-weekly refinement sessions plus quality reports tracking volume, severity, and resolution time. The post lists the 18 concrete, mostly small fixes and improvements that shipped across authentication, integrations, publishing reliability, and platform-specific behavior in 2025 as a production output of that process.
what_they_built: A structured bug-triage and maintenance-capacity process — tiered SLAs, dedicated engineering time, bi-weekly refinement, and quality reporting — that produced a shipped list of 18 specific product fixes in 2025.
evidence: Concrete SLA tiers (P1: 14 days, P2: 30 days, P3: 60 days), ~10% of engineering capacity reserved for bug/housekeeping work, and an itemized list of 18 shipped changes (e.g., Zapier support for LinkedIn first comments, fixed logout bug after password changes, Pinterest bulk upload with Board Name column, calendar fix for 53-week years) as the tangible output.
why_it_matters: Shows a PM how to formalize and defend investment in bug-fixing/tech-debt work against feature pressure by giving it explicit SLAs, dedicated capacity, and visible reporting rather than leaving it to ad hoc prioritization.

title: Our 7th Profit Share: Behind the $377,005 We Distributed to the Buffer Team
summary: Buffer publishes its 7th annual profit-share distribution, walking through the exact mechanism it uses to split 15 percent of annual net profit among the team: 40 percent split equally, 40 percent weighted by salary, and 20 percent weighted by tenure. For 2025 this produced a $377,005 pool from $2,513,367 in net profit, averaging $5,095 across 75 team members. The post also situates this year against prior years, including two consecutive years (2022-2023) of net losses when the profit-share pool was zero, and cites a director's reflection on the recovery.
what_they_built: A formalized, formula-driven annual profit-sharing mechanism (15% of net profit split 40% equal / 40% salary-weighted / 20% tenure-weighted) that Buffer runs and discloses every year.
evidence: 2025: net profit $2,513,367, profit-share pool $377,005 (15%), 75 team members, average payout $5,095 — contrasted against 2022 and 2023 net losses of -$1,109,745 and -$743,936 when the pool was zero, and prior distributions of $394,997 (2018) and $368,051 (2019).
why_it_matters: Gives a PM or ops leader a concrete, working template for designing and transparently communicating a formulaic profit-sharing scheme tied to measurable financial performance rather than discretionary bonuses.

title: Reflecting on a Decade of Transparent Salaries at Buffer
summary: After a decade of publicly transparent salaries, Buffer found its old single-formula approach was producing inconsistent, hard-to-explain outcomes as the team grew from 17 to 75 people and revenue scaled from $2.2M to $18M. They rebuilt compensation around three independent, decoupled components: external market data (from Carta Compensation), an internal benchmark grid per role/level, and individual salary calculation with cost-of-living and legacy adjustments. The redesign is documented with the actual before/after financial impact and named remaining trade-offs still under consideration.
what_they_built: A three-part compensation architecture — market data ingestion, a role/level benchmark grid derived from level-5 market data with consistent percent increases across levels, and individual salary calculation layering cost-of-living (100% high-cost, 90% global) and legacy-choice adjustments — replacing a single opaque formula.
evidence: Rolling out the new system produced a salary increase for 56 teammates (71% of the team), with total annual budget impact of $236,644; the post also quantifies unfunded future options such as fully location-independent salaries ($1,030,772 impact) and aligned percentiles ($314,467-$802,551).
why_it_matters: Shows a PM/org-design pattern for decomposing a single opaque policy formula into independently adjustable, auditable components, and for pricing the financial cost of each transparency/equity trade-off before committing to it.

title: We Updated Our Dependent Stipend For The First Time In 8 Years, Here's What Changed
summary: Buffer's dependent stipend, launched in 2016 and unchanged for eight years, had been applied inconsistently -- covering spouses, parents, siblings and even a grandparent alongside children, at $3,000 per dependent. In 2025 Buffer narrowed eligibility to dependent children under 18 who are primarily cared for by the teammate, up to four per person, to focus the benefit on childcare needs and apply it consistently across the company. The company published the exact before/after breakdown of who was covered and what it cost.
what_they_built: A revised eligibility policy for Buffer's Dependent Stipend, restricting the $3,000/year-per-dependent benefit to dependent children (under 18, living with and primarily cared for by the teammate) rather than any family dependent.
evidence: Under the prior policy (2024), Buffer supported 99 dependents (59 children, 22 spouses/partners, 15 parents, 2 siblings, 1 grandparent) at a cost of $297,000/year; under the new policy, 59 dependents across 31 teammates are covered with a projected budget of $117,000/year.
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete, numbers-first playbook for narrowing an inconsistently-applied benefit's scope while being transparent with employees about the before/after cost and eligibility rationale.

title: We Made a Decision That Lowers Our MRR and ARR, Here's The Story
summary: Buffer previously counted a cancelling customer's revenue as active until the end of their paid period, which overstated MRR/ARR relative to real-time churn. After cancelling 1,361 inactive annual legacy subscriptions surfaced the gap, Buffer's Director of Business Operations describes rebuilding the calculation to recognize cancellations immediately. The change dropped reported July figures from $1.93M MRR ($23.1M ARR) to $1.84M MRR ($22M ARR), with September then reading $1.87M MRR ($22.4M ARR) under the new methodology.
what_they_built: A revised MRR/ARR calculation methodology that recognizes subscription cancellations in real time instead of at the end of the paid billing period, with historical data restated to match.
evidence: Before/after figures: July closing numbers moved from $1.93M MRR ($23.1M ARR) to $1.84M MRR ($22M ARR) under the new methodology; September reads $1.87M MRR ($22.4M ARR). Triggered by cancellation of 1,361 inactive annual legacy subscriptions.
why_it_matters: Shows a PM/finance-adjacent decision to deliberately shrink a headline growth metric for measurement accuracy, and how to communicate that change transparently to stakeholders.

title: Effect of Pro Price Change on Mobile Revenue
summary: Buffer's data team retrospectively measured the effect of a March 2018 pricing change (renaming the Awesome Plan to Pro and raising it from $10/mo to $15/mo) on mobile app revenue, which they'd previously overlooked in favor of Stripe (web) revenue. Using the CausalImpact R package, they built a counterfactual forecast of mobile MRR growth from pre-change data and compared it to observed post-change MRR. They found the price change coincided with a sharp decline in mobile MRR growth, estimating a cumulative loss of roughly $25K in MRR with over 99% posterior probability of a true causal effect, and were candid that the effect was worse than expected and different in character from web revenue.
what_they_built: A causal-impact analysis pipeline (dbt-sourced daily MRR data + R's CausalImpact package) to isolate and quantify the effect of a specific pricing change on mobile subscription revenue, distinct from an unrelated promotional offer that overlapped in time.
evidence: Cumulative effect estimated at about $25K (model output: -$30,913, 95% CI [-47643,-13833]) loss in MRR; average daily MRR growth was $44/day before Feb 26 vs -$60/day after March 26, a relative effect of about -235% to -238%, with posterior probability of a true causal effect over 99.95%.
why_it_matters: Shows a PM how to isolate a pricing decision's true causal effect on a revenue channel using pre/post counterfactual modeling, even when a confounding promotion overlaps the change window, and to report a negative result candidly rather than only the intended outcome.

title: Buffer's Transparent Pricing 2016
summary: Buffer published a full breakdown of how it spends every dollar of its $10 Awesome Plan subscription, as part of its long-running "Open" transparency practice. The post itemizes team salaries, server costs, payment processing fees, rent/coworking, tools, travel, and taxes as shares of the subscription price, and reports total monthly operational costs of $584,969. It ties this to a strategic choice not to prioritize near-term profit and to reinvest revenue into the company, alongside the context that headcount grew 156% (25 to 64 employees) over the prior year and the company closed its physical office in favor of remote coworking stipends.
what_they_built: A public, itemized cost-allocation breakdown of Buffer's core subscription price, published as a recurring transparency artifact on its Open blog.
evidence: Reports concrete figures: monthly operational costs of $584,969; per-dollar breakdown of the $10 plan (salaries $6.89/68.89%, servers $0.82/8.18%, Stripe fees $0.40/3.99%, rent $0.16, tools $0.29, travel $0.33, taxes $0.48); and 156% year-over-year team growth (25 to 64 employees).
why_it_matters: Shows how a company can use radical pricing transparency (publishing real unit-cost breakdowns) as a trust-building and positioning lever rather than treating cost structure as proprietary.

title: How a Single Search Experiment Generates Million-Dollar Impact for Indonesia
summary: Bukalapak's Core Search team faced a promoted-push search auction where sellers' bids weren't translating efficiently into sales conversion. They redesigned the auction's relevance weighting using ML-informed analysis of seller and buyer profiles, then validated the new strategy with a staged A/B rollout (a 50:50 split followed by a 20:80 split) over roughly two weeks. The new strategy outperformed the incumbent, and the team rolled it out fully. The piece walks through their four-step approach: profiling, evaluating auction-effectiveness factors, aligning stakeholders, and implementing with a controlled experiment.
what_they_built: A revised promoted-push search auction ranking/weighting strategy for Bukalapak's marketplace, validated through a two-phase traffic-split A/B experiment before full rollout.
evidence: Reports "an additional US$1 million increase in annual revenue," "tens of millions of USD in additional sales" to small sellers, and a "double-digit percentage increase on overall conversion rate," with the new strategy outperforming the incumbent across both the 50:50 and 20:80 traffic-split test iterations.
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete pattern for staging a high-stakes marketplace-ranking change safely — profile-driven hypothesis, ML-informed weighting, then a graduated traffic-split experiment — before committing to full rollout.

title: Canva incident report: API Gateway outage
summary: On November 12, 2024, Canva suffered a roughly 52-minute outage when a stale Cloudflare traffic-management rule mis-routed IPv6 traffic over lossy public transit, causing about 66% packet loss and stalling a JavaScript asset load. Cloudflare's cache-stream mechanism queued over 270,000 user requests behind that single stalled asset; when it finally loaded 20 minutes later, all requests fired at once, producing a 1.5 million requests/second thundering herd (3x normal peak) at the API Gateway. Combined with an unrelated telemetry library bug causing lock contention on the gateway's event loop, this exhausted memory and triggered the Linux OOM killer to terminate all gateway containers within two minutes, cascading faster than autoscaling could respond. Canva restored service by blocking traffic at the CDN, ramping back up region by region under rate limits, patching the telemetry bug, and increasing gateway capacity and load-shedding rules.
what_they_built: A public incident post-mortem documenting the outage's three converging root causes and the remediation/hardening steps Canva applied to its CDN and API Gateway infrastructure.
evidence: Quantified: 1.5 million requests/second at peak (3x normal), ~66% packet loss at peak, over 270,000 queued user requests behind one asset, all API Gateway containers OOM-killed within two minutes, ~52-minute outage window (9:08-10:00 AM UTC).
why_it_matters: Shows how an unrelated CDN routing bug and a dormant telemetry-library defect can compound into a full-platform outage, underscoring why platform teams need load-shedding and canary detection even when each individual fault looks minor.

title: How to design in every language at once
summary: Canva's engineers ship new features daily and wanted them translated and live for international users without slowing down releases or breaking UI layouts across 104 languages. The Internationalization Engineering team built a pipeline combining pseudo-localization (a special en-psaccent locale that accents, brackets, and expands text to expose truncation before translation), ICU-formatted source messages with translator-facing JSDoc/JavaDoc comments, an automated GitHub bot that uploads strings to Smartling and blocks PR merges until "blocking locales" are translated, and visual regression screenshots (including Burmese, German, and RTL) to catch layout regressions. The system lets engineers see space requirements for translated text without manually checking every locale, while translation quality is checked by experts without blocking releases.
what_they_built: A continuous localization pipeline integrating pseudo-localization testing, ICU-format message files, an automated GitHub-to-Smartling translation bot with blocking-locale enforcement, and visual regression testing across expansion-heavy and RTL languages.
evidence: Translations for any language representing more than 0.03% of active users are completed by human professionals within two days; new features roll out once translation covers 99.8% of the active user base (the defined "blocking locales" threshold as of October 2021); the full upload-to-merge translation cycle usually takes around 48 hours for new message strings.
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete pattern for decoupling fast, continuous feature shipping from translation completeness by defining a coverage threshold (blocking locales) rather than requiring 100% locale translation before merge.

title: How we build experiments in-house
summary: Canva's experimentation platform started as a data-scientist-only Python CLI package in 2019 for standardized metric calculations, then grew into a Django web app with scheduled updates and a shareable UI. As Canva scaled past 100M MAU, duplicated deployments for canva.com and canva.cn, warehouse-credential bottlenecks, and a months-long onboarding curve for new data scientists eroded trust in the platform and locked out non-technical stakeholders. The team rebuilt it as a production microservice integrated with their Feature Control platform, adding a point-and-click UI that abstracts SQL and running statistical models on AWS Lambda, alongside an education program to drive adoption.
what_they_built: An internal experiment-analysis microservice (evolved from a Python CLI tool and Django app) integrated into Canva's Feature Control platform, with a point-and-click self-service UI and AWS Lambda-based statistical computation.
evidence: Reports that an education program on experimentation "resulted in a drop of over 50% in help requests in our team Slack channel," alongside the organizational failure modes that motivated the rebuild: data scientist bottlenecks on warehouse credentials, non-technical stakeholders locked out, and months-long onboarding for new data scientists.
why_it_matters: Shows a PM-relevant pattern for internal platforms: when a specialist-only tool becomes an organizational bottleneck, self-service UI plus deliberate education/enablement can measurably cut support load and widen adoption.

title: Measuring Commercial Impact at Scale at Canva
summary: Canva teams previously took hours to hand-calculate the commercial impact (MAU, ARR) of an experiment, making impact estimation slow and inconsistent across teams. The engineering team built IMPACT, a self-serve internal app combining a Streamlit interface, Snowpark for pre-calculating metrics like growth factors and retention, and Cortex for natural-language summarization of results, so any team can run standardized pre- and post-experiment commercial impact analysis. The tool carefully scales observed uplifts only to the user segments actually exposed to an experiment rather than the whole user base. It is now used daily across the company with measured time savings.
what_they_built: IMPACT, an internal Streamlit/Snowpark/Cortex-based self-serve tool that standardizes and automates commercial-impact (MAU/ARR) calculations for experiments at Canva.
evidence: Reduced analysis time from hours to under 10 minutes per experiment (time-to-insight cut from hours to minutes); supports roughly 100 unique users daily.
why_it_matters: Shows how a PM/data team can turn a slow, bespoke, spreadsheet-driven metric-estimation process into a standardized self-serve internal product that scales trustworthy experiment impact numbers across an entire company.

title: The science of routing print orders
summary: Canva's print fulfillment team needed to route millions of print orders to the optimal production site across a global network of print partners, balancing cost, delivery time, and emissions. They built a modular system split into three subsystems—Build and Retrieve (async graph construction from a relational production-site model), Decision (rule-based path ranking rather than numerical cost scoring), and Traversal (graph search with "regret" handling to backtrack from suboptimal early routing choices). The system uses Redis/ElastiCache with sharding by destination for regional performance, and reports production latency and availability numbers.
what_they_built: A modular, rule-based routing/decision engine (Build-and-Retrieve, Decision, Traversal subsystems) that computes optimal delivery paths across Canva's global print-partner network using a reactively-synced graph structure.
evidence: Routing latency 5-50 milliseconds (50th-99th percentile); graph retrieval 6-20 milliseconds; 99.999% uptime for routing data access.
why_it_matters: Shows how to decompose a fulfillment-routing decision engine into independently-evolvable subsystems (data build, rule-based decisioning, traversal) so business rules can change without touching core routing infrastructure—a pattern applicable to any marketplace/logistics-routing platform.

title: Subscription analytics at scale
summary: With over 3 million Canva Pro subscribers, Canva's third-party analytics tool (Profitwell) could no longer support the segmentation the business needed — no per-country, per-plan-type breakdowns — and analysts spent half a day per query. The Data team rebuilt subscription data infrastructure around four requirements (scalability, flexibility, trustworthiness, accessibility), migrating subscription tables from Presto/Hive to Snowflake with dbt for templating and testing. On top of the new foundation they shipped a trial-to-paid conversion predictor (operational since September the prior year, favoring explainable logistic regression over XGBoost) and adopted the Fader-Hardie churn model, which forecasts churn adequately from just two months of retention data.
what_they_built: A rebuilt subscription data platform (Presto/Hive to Snowflake + dbt) feeding unified cross-team metrics, plus a trial-to-paid conversion predictor and a Fader-Hardie churn forecasting model built on top of it.
evidence: Concrete before/after: querying subscriber insights went from "half a day to query" under the old system to unified, accessible dashboards; trial-to-paid predictor has been operational since September of the prior year; churn model works with just two months of retention data; infrastructure serves Product, Growth, Finance, Design, and Engineering teams.
why_it_matters: Shows a PM-relevant sequencing lesson — durable metric infrastructure and data quality must precede ML-driven product decisions, not follow them.

title: How to Improve Search Without Looking at Queries or Results
summary: Canva's search team needed to evaluate changes to their private design-search pipeline but, unlike public search, could not label or even look at real user queries or designs due to Canva's privacy commitments. Their prior process relied on a small set of known-bad queries plus slow, risky online A/B experiments, which limited how many ideas they could test concurrently. They built a pipeline that uses GPT-4o to generate synthetic but realistic designs and queries (seeded from aggregate distribution statistics), runs the full search stack locally via Testcontainers against ElasticSearch replicas and production-configured ML models, and visualizes results in a custom Streamlit tool for debugging by query type and difficulty. The offline synthetic evaluations tracked closely with online experiment outcomes while running far faster and more cheaply.
what_they_built: A synthetic-data generation and local offline evaluation pipeline for search quality — GPT-4o-generated fictional designs/queries, a Testcontainers-based local replica of the full search stack, and a Streamlit dashboard for metric visualization and query-level debugging.
evidence: Runs 1000+ test cases in under 10 minutes; enabled 300+ offline evaluations in the time a single online experiment would take; offline results showed "robust alignment" with online A/B experiment outcomes.
why_it_matters: Shows PMs and platform teams a concrete pattern for building fast, privacy-safe offline evaluation loops that substitute for slow, risky online experimentation when real user data can't be inspected.

title: How server-driven UI is revolutionizing mobile app experiences
summary: Before AppEngine, Careem's Marketing, Product, Product Ops, and Engineering teams needed weeks of planning and code-level changes to redesign app screens for seasonal campaigns like Ramadan. Careem built AppEngine, a server-driven UI system with three parts: a lightweight mobile renderer with no business logic, a backend that dynamically generates UI components based on personalization and business rules, and a self-serve authoring tool for non-engineers. With it, Product Ops can launch Ramadan-specific discovery screens and sub-pages, run A/B tests, and configure promotions without deploying code, cutting the cycle from weeks to hours.
what_they_built: AppEngine: a server-driven UI platform for Careem's mobile app consisting of a lightweight renderer app, a backend UI generation service, and a self-serve authoring tool used by Product Ops/Marketing to launch and A/B test screens without app releases.
evidence: Reported outcome is a before/after time-to-ship claim: campaigns and screens that previously required "weeks of planning across Marketing, Product, Product Ops, and Engineering" now "go live in hours, not weeks," with A/B testing and promotions described as now possible "without deploying code changes." No hard adoption or performance metrics are given beyond this qualitative before/after claim.
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete pattern for how a platform team can shift UI/campaign authoring from engineering release cycles into a self-serve tool for product ops, cutting iteration time for time-boxed marketing moments.

title: Facial recognition to detect duplicate Captain accounts
summary: Careem's driver ("Captain") marketplace faced a recurring problem: some Captains created duplicate accounts to grab extra orders and bonuses, unbalancing supply and hurting service for everyone else, and document checks alone couldn't catch it because photos and papers could be manipulated. The Integrity team built an offline facial-recognition pipeline that extracts embeddings from Captain photos stored in S3, uses FAISS-GPU to run similarity search across a 2M+ image dataset in under an hour, clusters likely-duplicate photos with a connected-components algorithm, and routes flagged pairs to a dedicated fraud-review team for manual verification before any account is blocked -- explicitly to avoid falsely blocking legitimate cases like a Captain switching limo companies. Along the way they worked through real data-quality problems (RGBA vs RGB images, poor lighting/resolution, a wall portrait misidentified as a face inflating false clusters). The system detected and blocked over 2,000 fraudulent accounts after manual verification, and Careem also tightened its registration process afterward to reduce new duplicates at the source.
what_they_built: An offline facial-recognition fraud pipeline (face_recognition embeddings + FAISS-GPU similarity search + connected-components clustering) that flags likely-duplicate Captain accounts for manual review before blocking.
evidence: Detected and blocked over 2,000 fraudulent accounts following manual/background verification; indexing and top-5 similarity retrieval across 2M+ images ran in under an hour; the team also reports the registration process was subsequently made more stringent, "significantly reducing the creation of duplicate accounts" (qualitative, no number given for that specific claim).
why_it_matters: Shows how to pair an ML detection signal with a human-in-the-loop review step and explicit business-rule exceptions so a trust and safety system catches fraud without punishing legitimate edge cases.

title: Transforming the way customer disputes are resolved at Careem
summary: Careem's Care Engineering team faced care agents manually recalculating fares and resolving disputes with calculators and tribal knowledge, causing long handling times and onboarding bottlenecks. They first shipped a lightweight fix — Google Forms embedded in an iframe inside the web app — to enforce consistent policy adherence across 20+ resolution categories within three months. As Google Forms hit limits on data access, automation, and policy-change flexibility, they migrated to Flowable, an open-source BPM engine, letting non-technical staff edit resolution flows without engineering involvement, and worked through production performance/database tuning with the Flowable community. The system now runs 20+ resolution flows handling 50k+ transactions daily across 100+ contact reasons.
what_they_built: A two-stage internal dispute-resolution platform: an initial Google Forms-based guided-workflow prototype, then a production migration to the open-source Flowable BPM engine so non-engineers could author and modify resolution flows.
evidence: Reports 50k+ transactions processed on Flowable every day, 20+ resolution flows in production, and coverage of 100+ different contact reasons.
why_it_matters: Shows a low-cost prototype (Google Forms) can validate a workflow-automation bet before committing engineering effort to a full BPM platform migration.

title: Temporary holds: Leveraging machine learning models to reduce fraud while improving customer experience
summary: Careem's rapid ride volume growth (over 1 billion rides) attracted card fraud, so it used rule-based pre-authorization holds to catch bad cards before payment failure. But blanket holds hurt customer trust and rules were brittle — hard to maintain and reverse-engineerable once fraudsters learned to stay just under thresholds. The team replaced the rules with a CatBoost model trained on targeted fraud-behavior features and only real-time-available data, validated with rolling time windows, and required it hit at least 0.9 recall while maximizing precision. It shipped through shadow-mode testing before a gradual live rollout on their internal YODA ML platform.
what_they_built: Replaced a rule-based system for triggering credit-card pre-authorization holds with a CatBoost-based ML model, deployed in shadow mode then rolled out live on Careem's internal YODA platform.
evidence: Reported a 60% increase in precision while keeping recall above the 0.9 floor, and a reduction in customer complaints tied to unnecessary holds.
why_it_matters: Shows a PM-relevant tradeoff pattern for trust-and-safety systems: pairing a hard recall floor with a precision target lets a team cut false-positive customer friction without giving up fraud coverage.

title: Temporary holds: Leveraging machine learning models to reduce fraud while improving customer experience
summary: Careem's Integrity team faced a tradeoff between fraud losses and customer friction from pre-authorization holds on rides: rule-based thresholds for placing temporary card holds were easy for fraudsters to reverse-engineer and adapt around, while also inconveniencing legitimate customers when holds took days to lift. The team replaced the rule-based approach with a CatBoost machine learning model that required a minimum 0.9 recall while maximizing precision, validated with time-based rolling windows, and deployed through Careem's internal YODA ML platform with a shadow-mode phase (predictions logged but not acted on) before full production rollout. The result was a 60% increase in precision and a reduction in customer complaints.
what_they_built: An ML-based (CatBoost) fraud pre-authorization scoring model deployed via Careem's internal YODA platform, replacing a rule-based hold system, with shadow-mode testing before production rollout.
evidence: Reported a 60% increase in precision and a reduction in customer complaints after switching from rules to the ML model, while maintaining a minimum 0.9 recall requirement.
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete pattern for shipping a trust-and-safety model change safely (shadow mode before rollout) while explicitly tracking customer-experience cost, not just fraud-catch metrics, as the success criteria.

title: Optimus: Dynamic Dependency Management
summary: Careem's Data & AI team had outgrown their original ETL orchestration system, Optimus, which only managed task dependencies within a single batch config on one cluster. As Careem scaled to 10+ clusters, teams needed dependencies between tasks running on different clusters, with no manual tracking. They built a two-level state model: an in-memory graph for internal dependency state, and Zookeeper ephemeral nodes to expose cross-cluster task status asynchronously, letting Airflow-generated DAGs coordinate parallel execution across clusters with idempotent, freshness-aware task submission.
what_they_built: A cross-cluster dependency management layer for their in-house Optimus ETL orchestrator, using Zookeeper ephemeral nodes for external task-status coordination and a `dependenciesWithMeta` config property for per-dependency freshness windows.
evidence: Qualitative operational outcomes: idempotent task submission (safe to resubmit, only relaunches if the prior run failed), elimination of manual cross-cluster execution-graph tracking by support teams, and cost savings from ephemeral create-and-destroy EMR clusters paying "only for utilized compute resources." The team also names a concrete production limitation: dependency coordination is tightly coupled to Zookeeper, making it a single point of failure if the Zookeeper cluster goes down.
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete pattern (ephemeral coordination nodes plus a two-level state model) for extending single-cluster orchestration into multi-cluster dependency management without a rewrite, alongside an honest tradeoff a platform PM should probe before scaling it further.

title: Optimising Core Web Vitals at Carousell
summary: Carousell's engineering team found homepage LCP at a slow 6.8 seconds and set out to systematically fix Core Web Vitals on its highest-traffic homepage and product pages, using Google Search Console real-user data plus a Lighthouse CLI automation script for repeatable local measurement. They implemented selective server-side rendering (fetching only above-the-fold data while lazy-loading the rest, with user-agent detection to keep full HTML for crawlers), trimmed the JS bundle using Chrome's coverage tool, audited and removed unused GTM third-party scripts, and added layout-shift placeholders plus fixed image aspect ratios. The interventions cut LCP by 65%, improved FID by roughly 300ms, and raised the share of pages with 'Good' CLS from 41% to 74%.
what_they_built: Selective SSR for above-the-fold homepage content with crawler-aware fallback rendering, plus a JS-bundle trimming and GTM script audit process and CLS-preventing layout placeholders/image-ratio constraints.
evidence: Homepage LCP dropped 65% (from a 6.8s baseline), FID improved by about 300ms, JS bundle shrank over 8% and ~25kB of unused GTM scripts were removed, and pages with 'Good' CLS in Search Console rose from 41% to 74%.
why_it_matters: Shows a PM/platform team how to sequence Core Web Vitals work by highest-traffic surface first and pair each technique (SSR, bundle trimming, script governance, layout placeholders) with a measured before/after rather than applying best practices blindly.

title: How We Built the Category's Fastest Usage Rating Engine
summary: Chargebee's engineering team needed a usage-metering pipeline that could ingest and rate massive volumes of usage events without sacrificing accuracy or predictable latency, since in billing "every event is money" and stale or duplicated data directly causes bill shock or disputed charges. They built a system with three ingestion pathways (real-time API streaming, batch file push, S3 pull), an asynchronous queue that decouples ingestion from database writes, minute-level event bucketing for aggregation, and composite-key (subscription ID, timestamp, dedup ID) deduplication performed during bulk writes rather than as a post-processing step. The result, demonstrated during a Gartner evaluation, was processing 1 million usage events in 25 seconds, and the production system sustains 200K+ events per second with zero duplicate events. The engine now powers usage billing for 500+ businesses, including companies like DeepL and Lambda, at volumes ranging from 10,000 events/day to billions/day.
what_they_built: A usage-event ingestion and rating engine with multiple ingestion pathways, async queue-decoupled batched writes, minute-level bucketing, and composite-key deduplication for real-time usage-based billing at scale.
evidence: Processed 1 million usage events in 25 seconds in a Gartner demonstration; production system handles 200K+ events per second with zero duplicate events; powers 500+ businesses at volumes from 10,000 events/day to billions/day.
why_it_matters: Shows PMs building metering/billing or high-throughput event pipelines how prioritizing predictable latency and write-time deduplication over average-case speed avoids the accuracy failures that directly erode customer trust.

title: Chewy S-1 Registration Statement — Autoship Subscription Program
summary: Chewy's S-1 registration statement (filed April 29, 2019, ahead of its IPO) describes Autoship, its recurring auto-replenishment subscription program for pet food and supplies, and discloses its financial contribution across fiscal 2016-2018. Autoship customer sales grew from $565.2M (62.8% of net sales) in fiscal 2016 to $2.32B (65.7% of net sales) in fiscal 2018, and the company reports that existing customers generated 120% of the prior year's sales in fiscal 2018 (net revenue expansion). The filing frames Autoship as central to Chewy's retention economics and predictable revenue.
what_they_built: Autoship, a recurring auto-ship/subscription ordering program for pet food, treats and supplies that customers opt into for repeat, scheduled deliveries.
evidence: Autoship customer sales: $565.2M (62.8% of net sales, FY2016) to $1,294.9M (61.5%, FY2017) to $2,322.5M (65.7% of net sales, FY2018); "in fiscal year 2018 our business achieved 120% of sales from our existing customer base from the prior fiscal year."
why_it_matters: Shows a PM-legible link between a specific subscription/auto-replenishment mechanic and measurable net-revenue retention above 100%, illustrating how a recurring-purchase feature can be quantified as a core business-model lever in a public filing.

title: Code Orange: Fail Small — our resilience plan following recent incidents
summary: After two major 2025 outages (Nov 18, ~2h10m global traffic failure; Dec 5, 28% of applications down for ~25min), both caused by configuration changes propagating instantly network-wide via Cloudflare's Quicksilver system, Cloudflare declared a company-wide "Code Orange" priority program called Fail Small. The plan extends the controlled, staged-rollout deployment gating already used for software binaries (their Health Mediated Deployment/HMD system, with automatic per-metric rollback) to configuration changes, which previously deployed instantly with no gates. It also covers a review of failure-mode assumptions at every internal service interface (defaulting to fail-open rather than fail-closed) and fixing break-glass access and circular dependencies that slowed incident response.
what_they_built: A named, prioritized resilience program (Code Orange: Fail Small) with three workstreams: extending existing controlled/staged software-release rollout gating (HMD) to all network configuration changes propagated via Quicksilver; auditing and redesigning failure-mode handling at critical service interfaces to fail open rather than cascade; and fixing break-glass/circular-dependency issues that blocked incident responders.
evidence: Concrete incident metrics: Nov 18, 2025 outage lasted ~2 hours 10 minutes network-wide; Dec 5, 2025 outage affected 28% of applications behind Cloudflare for ~25 minutes. The piece is largely forward-looking on remediation (work "underway," rollouts to complete by Q1 2026 per the harvest agent's framing) rather than reporting post-fix outcomes, so the evidence is the diagnosed root cause and incident scale, not yet a measured after-state.
why_it_matters: Shows that safe-deployment discipline (staged rollout, automatic rollback, defined success/failure metrics) must be applied uniformly to every mechanism that can change production behavior instantly — including config and data changes, not just binary releases — a lesson transferable to any platform PM owning an internal deployment or feature-flag system.

title: Post mortem on the Cloudflare Control Plane and Analytics Outage
summary: A power failure cascade at a third-party Oregon data center (Flexential PDX-04) knocked out Cloudflare's control plane, dashboard/API configuration, log push, and Stream uploads for roughly 36 hours in November 2023. The postmortem found that the company's intended three-data-center redundancy design was undermined in practice: log-processing systems (Kafka, ClickHouse) lived only in the failed facility, several newer GA products had never been integrated into the high-availability cluster, and full facility failover had never actually been tested. In response, Cloudflare launched an internal "Code Orange" initiative that redirects engineering resources to eliminate core-data-center dependencies for the control plane, mandates HA-cluster integration and tested disaster recovery for all GA products, and requires chaos testing including full facility removal.
what_they_built: A public root-cause postmortem plus a named internal remediation program (Code Orange) mandating high-availability-cluster integration and tested disaster recovery as a gating requirement for any product reaching general availability.
evidence: Concrete failure modes reported: log push data unrecoverable for most of the ~36-hour incident, Stream uploads down and requiring manual reimplementation, some analytics datasets permanently missing data for regions without EU replication, and an explicit admission that full-facility failover had never been tested despite the design intent.
why_it_matters: Shows a PM-relevant governance lesson: redundancy designed on paper isn't redundancy until every GA product is actually enrolled in it and the failover path has been tested end-to-end, not just per-datacenter.

title: A deep dive into Cloudflare's September 12, 2025 dashboard and API outage
summary: Cloudflare's dashboard and API became unusable for about an hour on September 12, 2025 after a React useEffect dependency-array bug caused the dashboard to fire excessive repeated API calls. That retry storm overwhelmed the internal Tenant Service, which sits in the API request authorization path, so once it failed, authorization could not be evaluated and requests returned 5xx across the board. The team walks through the cascading failure mechanism and the concrete fixes it drove: prioritizing migration to Argo Rollouts for automatic rollback, adding randomized retry backoff to break the thundering-herd pattern, and provisioning substantially more capacity plus better monitoring thresholds for Tenant Service.
what_they_built: A public root-cause analysis plus a set of process/architecture fixes: an Argo Rollouts-based auto-rollback deployment pipeline, randomized retry backoff on the dashboard frontend, and expanded capacity/observability for the internal Tenant Service.
evidence: Outage lasted roughly an hour (17:57-19:12 UTC) with dashboard severely impacted throughout and API availability severely impacted during two distinct windows when Tenant Service failed; the postmortem names specific engineering changes (Argo Rollouts migration, randomized retry delays, added capacity) adopted as a direct result.
why_it_matters: Shows a PM/platform builder how a small frontend bug (a dependency-array mistake) can cascade into a full outage through a shared internal auth service, and what durable fixes (auto-rollback tooling, backoff, capacity headroom) actually address the underlying fragility rather than just the symptom.

title: Backwards-compatibility in Cloudflare Workers
summary: Cloudflare Workers runs all customer Workers on one shared, constantly-updated runtime, unlike traditional servers where developers control their own upgrade timing. This meant Cloudflare could not fix even clear bugs (like an incorrect fetch() Response body-stream behavior) without risking breaking someone's production Worker. Kenton Varda describes the compatibility-date system they built: new Workers default to today's date and get new behavior, existing Workers keep running whatever behavior matched their stored compatibility date, and developers opt into fixes by bumping the date in wrangler.toml. The post walks through a concrete example of a breaking fetch() behavior change shipped this way, with old compatibility dates supported indefinitely.
what_they_built: A "compatibility date" mechanism in the Cloudflare Workers runtime: each Worker declares a date (in wrangler.toml), and the runtime implements both old and new behavior for any breaking change, selecting which to run based on that date, so upgrading the shared runtime never breaks a live Worker.
evidence: Qualitative rather than metrics-driven: the post states the resulting policy ("old compatibility dates are intended to be supported forever") and walks through a real shipped example (a `compatibility_date` flag date, e.g. "2021-09-20", gating a fetch()-behavior fix) as proof the mechanism is in production use, rather than reporting adoption numbers.
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete pattern for shipping breaking platform changes to a multi-tenant runtime without a deprecation window or customer coordination burden — useful for any PM/platform builder managing shared infrastructure with many independent consumers.

title: So long, and thanks for all the deployments: deprecating Wrangler v1
summary: Cloudflare's Wrangler CLI had grown difficult to maintain as its original codebase accumulated complexity, so the team rewrote it as Wrangler v2 with simplified workflows and expanded library capabilities. To move developers off the legacy v1 tool, they shipped a migration guide, added detailed in-tool deprecation messages, documented breaking changes, and actively triaged issue reports with fixes. They tracked npm download volume for both versions as the adoption signal, and set August 2023 as the point when v1 stops receiving updates and its GitHub repo is archived.
what_they_built: A structured deprecation program for Wrangler v1 (the Cloudflare Workers CLI) — migration guide, in-CLI deprecation messaging, and monitored issue response — to move users onto the rewritten Wrangler v2.
evidence: npm download counts for Wrangler v2 surpassed those for v1, which the team treated as the signal that migration had succeeded; v1 updates were set to cease entirely by August 2023 with the repo archived.
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete, low-drama pattern for sunsetting a widely-used developer tool: pair a hard migration deadline with in-product nudges and a quantifiable adoption metric (download crossover) rather than just announcing an end-of-life date.

title: Calculating A/B test metrics
summary: Coupang's data engineering team built an in-house experiment platform starting mid-2014, and metric calculation for A/B tests became a bottleneck as scale grew — early on it took close to three hours to compute metrics for just two dozen tests.
