The team rebuilt the metric calculation framework through three phases: from Hive with incremental calculations, to a Spark-based lambda architecture (batch layer as source of truth plus a speed layer delivering partial results every 15 minutes), to a later cloud migration. The Akka actor framework was added to parallelize test processing and isolate failures across simultaneous experiments.
what_they_built: An internal A/B test metric-calculation framework using a Spark-based lambda architecture (batch + speed + serving layers), later migrated to run on cloud infrastructure.
evidence: Batch layer processing time dropped from 4 hours to 1.5 hours (a 60%+ reduction) after the cloud migration; the speed layer delivers a subset of results every 15 minutes; the system supports metric calculations for more than 200 simultaneous A/B tests, versus roughly three hours to compute metrics for just two dozen tests in the earliest version.
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete, staged path (batch-only to lambda architecture to cloud) for scaling an internal experimentation platform's metrics pipeline as test volume grows, which is a common bottleneck any PM running many concurrent experiments will hit.

title: Minimizing Technical Incidents During A/B Testing at Scale
summary: Coupang's mobile engineering team found that 80% of their technical incidents traced back to A/B testing, and that the standard mitigation of reverting B logic often failed because A and B logic weren't cleanly separated at the code level — turning off B could still corrupt A. To catch this before it shipped, they built an automated code-review tool that runs at pull-request time, diffing changes via Abstract Syntax Trees and flagging operational changes to A-logic-adjacent code against predefined rules. After rollout, per-thousand-line code violations dropped from 22.6 to 16.5 (about 27%) and unintended changes to A logic fell by roughly 30%.
what_they_built: An automated, PR-triggered code review system that uses Git-level change detection plus AST diffing to flag code modifications that risk contaminating A (control) logic during A/B testing.
evidence: Code violations decreased from 22.6 to 16.5 per thousand lines (~27% reduction), and a 30% decrease in changed A logic after the tool was introduced; the team also states 80% of mobile technical incidents were attributable to A/B testing before this fix.
why_it_matters: Shows PMs running experimentation platforms a concrete, measurable engineering control (AST-based diff review) for a common but under-discussed experimentation failure mode: control-group logic contamination.

title: Coupang's Data Platform 2022: Global Expansion in Petabytes
summary: Coupang's data organization needed to scale ingestion, ML training, and experimentation across roughly 50 internal teams while keeping bad experiments from silently damaging the business. They built a Universal Data Ingestion (UDI) framework with a plug-in model to onboard new sources with minimal engineering effort, an ML platform with pre-packaged training containers and GPU/hyperparameter tooling, and an experimentation platform with a circuit breaker that continuously monitors live A/B tests and auto-halts ones showing statistically significant harm. The post walks through the architecture (S3, Hive, Spark, Presto, ZooKeeper) behind each layer and reports the resulting scale and impact.
what_they_built: A Universal Data Ingestion framework plus an ML training/serving platform and an experimentation platform with an automated circuit breaker that halts harmful live experiments.
evidence: "Processes over five thousand jobs and extracts over 2 TB of data from approximately 70 different sources every day," serves ~50 teams; models trained month-over-month more than tripled in six months post-launch; the circuit breaker evaluates experiment metrics every 2 minutes and detects/stops bad experiments in about 10 minutes across 3 billion events and roughly a thousand live experiments per day.
why_it_matters: Shows how a platform team can pair self-serve data/ML infrastructure with an automated experiment-guardrail system to protect the business from harmful live tests at scale.

title: Coupang, Inc. Form S-1 (2021 IPO Registration Statement) — Rocket WOW and Active Customer metrics
publisher: Coupang Inc (SEC filing / S-1 business & MD&A sections)
summary: Coupang's S-1 registration statement (filed ahead of its 2021 NYSE IPO) describes the 2019 launch of Rocket WOW, a flat monthly-fee membership program bundling unlimited free shipping, Dawn/Same-Day Delivery, free returns, and Rocket Fresh groceries, built atop Coupang's owned-inventory and end-to-end logistics infrastructure. The filing quantifies the program's effect on customer growth and monetization: Active Customers rose from ~11.8 million (Q4 2019) to ~14.8 million (Q4 2020), while Net Revenues per Active Customer rose from ~$161 to ~$256 over the same period, with Rocket WOW members comprising 32% of Active Customers by Q4 2020. Coupang attributes the acceleration explicitly to owned-inventory expansion, Rocket Delivery, and Rocket WOW membership benefits.
what_they_built: A flat-fee membership program (Rocket WOW) layered on Coupang's owned-inventory and logistics network, bundling free/expedited shipping, free returns, grocery delivery (Rocket Fresh), and eventually food delivery (Coupang Eats) as retention and monetization levers.
evidence: Active Customers grew from ~11.8M (Q4 2019) to ~14.8M (Q4 2020); Net Revenues per Active Customer grew from ~$161 to ~$256 over the same period; Rocket WOW members were 32% of Active Customers in Q4 2020; total net revenue on a constant-currency basis grew 93.1% year over year per the filing's non-GAAP reconciliation table.
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete, audited example of how bundling a paid membership with logistics investment can simultaneously lift both customer base size and revenue-per-customer, giving PMs a real reference point for justifying membership/loyalty program investment with hard before/after numbers.

title: CRED Goes Copper
summary: CRED needed its visual system to scale as it expanded from credit-card payments into a broader set of lifestyle and money-management offerings, without redesigning from scratch for each new product. The team built Copper, an extensible design/engineering framework that lets any visual "skin" sit over a shared template engine, and used it to ship the company's first neumorphic-styled mobile experience. They also made a deliberate scope cut, shipping dark-mode only, grounded in the observation that nine in ten members already used dark mode exclusively.
what_they_built: Copper, an extensible mobile design system and template engine that decouples visual "skin" from underlying app structure, first applied via a neumorphic UI redesign of the CRED app.
evidence: Qualitative/usage-data evidence rather than performance metrics: the dark-mode-only decision is explicitly justified by the stat that "nine in ten members use only dark mode"; the post also notes the system was built to support "multiple experiments," the first of which shipped with this update.
why_it_matters: Shows a PM/design-org tradeoff worth copying elsewhere: use actual usage-data thresholds (90% dark-mode usage) to justify cutting a variant rather than shipping both, and invest in an extensible skinning layer before scope expands to many product lines.

title: How CRED made disaster recovery drills push-button simple
summary: CRED's disaster recovery drills used to be a manual, tribal-knowledge-dependent ordeal: infrastructure in the secondary region was stood up by hand, database replication configured step by step, and a single full-business-line drill occupied the SRE team for weeks with high risk of drift and manual-step errors. The team rebuilt DR on three pillars: Acropolis, a Pulumi-backed IaC platform letting engineers declare microservices and all their cloud dependencies (queues, IAM, buckets) as code; "implicit environments" that compute DR configuration at deploy time as prod config plus a small platform-owned regional overlay, eliminating config drift by construction; and Charon, a lightweight, purpose-built workflow orchestration engine that runs DR drills from committed YAML with state-aware rollback, interactive checkpoints before high-risk steps, and version-controlled runbooks. The result: drill prep time and headcount both collapsed once the one-time IaC migration cost was absorbed.
what_they_built: A three-pillar internal DR platform: Acropolis (Pulumi-backed infrastructure-as-code for services and their cloud dependencies), implicit environments (computed-at-deploy-time DR configuration instead of duplicated explicit environments), and Charon (a custom lightweight workflow orchestration engine with rollback closures and interactive checkpoints for running failover drills from committed YAML).
evidence: "The first drill took the better part of a quarter... a later drill takes a matter of days. An exercise that once tied up five SREs now takes one or two. The most recent drill took just two days of prep and a single SRE to run." The most recent drill also moved 35+ services across an entire line of business through a full failover and back, with the switchover itself taking about 20 minutes.
why_it_matters: Shows a PM/platform-team pattern for turning a dreaded, high-coordination-cost operational event into a routine capability by investing upfront in declarative infrastructure and purpose-built (not off-the-shelf) orchestration tooling scoped tightly to the team's actual operating model.

title: Case Study on Large Scale Re-architecture: Evolution of Order Management System at CRED — Part 1
summary: CRED's Order Management System started as a monolith built only for credit card bill payments, then splintered as the company added lines of business (P2P investing, credit line, e-commerce, travel, vehicle management) — each LOB ended up building its own duplicate order-management logic on top of a shared payments suite, causing data duplication, inconsistent order state, and slow go-to-market for new LOBs. The engineering team set out to build a single central "Platform OMS" that any LOB could plug into, and this post documents the lessons from that redesign process: the need for a crisp definition of what counts as an "order," clearly separating OMS responsibilities from pre-order and post-order systems, supporting LOB-specific custom order attributes with queryable/indexable fields, and handling wildly different order lifecycles across business lines. It is framed as Part 1 of a series, focused on the problem discovery and design principles rather than final implementation.
what_they_built: A central, domain-agnostic "Platform OMS" intended to replace CRED's legacy payments-only Order Management System and the per-line-of-business OMS forks that had proliferated around it.
evidence: Scale context given as production evidence: 1/3 of credit card bill payments (by value) in India were processed on CRED in FY22-23, with total payment value (TPV) through the platform of ₹4.4 Lakh Crore in the same period; the post also enumerates concrete pre-redesign failure modes (duplicate order entities, inconsistent order state across LOB-specific OMS instances, inability to support multi-LOB orders, higher costs from data duplication, and longer time-to-live for new LOBs).
why_it_matters: Shows a PM/platform-team pattern worth generalizing: when a domain entity (here, "order") lacks a crisp definition and clear system boundaries, each new business line reinvents it, and the fix is not just consolidation but first re-deriving the entity's definition and the platform's responsibility boundaries before building.

title: A/B Test Decisions: Reducing Type 1 Errors and Using Elasticity
summary: Criteo runs A/B tests across tens of thousands of advertiser campaigns simultaneously, and testing each campaign as a separate measurement made Type 1 (false-positive) error rates approach 100% in aggregate. To fix this, they built a single unified metric, "Advertiser Value" (actions times value per action), that consolidates results across all campaigns into one measurement per test, cutting the Type 1 error exposure down to a single significance level. They layered an iso-elastic economic model on top to predict advertiser value under different budget-response scenarios (Iso-Cost vs Iso-ROI) as advertisers react to engine changes, accounting for diminishing returns.
what_they_built: A unified "Advertiser Value" metric plus an iso-elastic economic model that Criteo applies to any A/B test to produce one statistically valid measurement per experiment instead of thousands of per-campaign measurements.
evidence: The approach reduces Type 1 error exposure from a near-100% aggregate risk (when testing tens of thousands of campaigns individually) down to the significance level of a single measurement, and lets Criteo converge to statistically significant test decisions in about two weeks using data from tens of thousands of campaigns.
why_it_matters: Shows PMs running experimentation platforms at scale how to design a single composite metric and account for market-equilibrium effects, rather than naively multiple-testing across many sub-populations.

title: Building Criteo API, What We've Built
summary: Criteo's decade-old public API had grown unwieldy, so the team rebuilt it around a new gateway, an OAuth-based authentication module, and a set of modular internal services (EXAMs) split by domain (Reporting, Campaigns, Audiences), fronted by developer, partner, consent, and status portals. They also began dynamically generating external SDKs in Java, PHP, and Python from an internal SDK library to keep client implementations consistent. About a year after launch they fully migrated clients off and decommissioned the legacy Marketing API.
what_they_built: A new API gateway plus modular internal service layer (EXAMs) with OAuth authentication, generated multi-language client SDKs, and developer/partner/consent/status portals replacing the old Marketing API.
evidence: By Q1 2023: 223 million requests sent to the API, an average of 3 million calls per day, 2,100 active API applications, and 30 EXAMs exposed publicly; the legacy Marketing API was fully decommissioned one year after the new platform's release once all clients migrated.
why_it_matters: Shows how a platform team sequences an internal-developer-platform rebuild (gateway, auth, generated SDKs, portals) around a hard migration deadline and measures adoption with concrete usage metrics rather than just launch announcements.

title: Big Data Quality at Criteo
summary: Criteo runs a reporting data lake on Hadoop with 450+ PB of storage and 300,000+ daily MapReduce/Spark jobs producing ~90 TB of new data per day, making manual data-quality assurance impossible at that scale. The team translated data-quality expectations into SLAs and built 7000+ automated checks covering completeness, consistency, and accuracy, running hourly as partitions are created, using Hive default statistics plus custom post-query aggregations and outlier detection to flag anomalies. Results feed dashboards and alerts for an operational team, with flexibility to add or tune checks while minimizing false positives. The piece also argues that catching problems downstream isn't enough and pushes for better producer-consumer communication and data lineage tooling to fix issues at the source.
what_they_built: An automated, SLA-driven data-quality-check framework (7000+ checks) running hourly across Criteo's Hadoop reporting data lake, combined with outlier detection, dashboards, and alerting for an operational monitoring team.
evidence: 450+ PB of storage, ~90 TB of new data daily, 300,000+ daily jobs, and 7000+ automated data-quality checks on completeness, consistency, and accuracy, run hourly.
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete pattern for scaling data-quality governance via SLA-defined automated checks plus process fixes (producer-consumer communication, lineage) rather than relying on manual review or downstream anomaly catching alone.

title: Building Criteo API, What We've Learned
summary: This is the final post in a three-part series on Criteo's three-year effort to move from legacy integrations to a modern public API. The team recounts five concrete operational lessons: multiple teams built inconsistent data models for the same resources despite a manual "API Guild" review process, prompting work on an automated Canonical Model; they shipped the simpler Client Credentials OAuth2.0 flow first and added Authorization Code flow months later to get early client feedback; generating SDKs (Java, PHP, Python) via OpenAPI Generator surfaced mismatches between spec files and real API behavior, requiring endpoint-by-endpoint audits; relying on individual client email contacts for critical deprecation notices proved fragile when staff changed roles; and giving clients too much flexibility in choosing auth flows led some to misuse Client Credentials for multi-client scenarios, losing per-user rate limiting.
what_they_built: Criteo's public developer API and accompanying SDKs (Java, PHP, Python), including OAuth2.0 Client Credentials and Authorization Code flows and an in-progress "Canonical Model" tool to enforce data-model consistency.
evidence: Qualitative operational lessons rather than metrics: recurring data-model inconsistencies caught only via manual API Guild review, recurring OpenAPI spec/behavior mismatches found only through endpoint-by-endpoint audits, and partner misuse of Client Credentials flow that lost user-level rate limiting.
why_it_matters: Shows a PM/platform team how sequencing simpler-first API capabilities, then course-correcting on governance and communication channels, plays out over years of real external-developer adoption.

title: ABEMAにおけるA/Bテスト運用への取り組み
summary: ABEMA's data team faced scattered A/B test results, analyst-dependent inconsistent methodology, and a per-test workload that didn't scale as more teams wanted to run experiments. They built a company-wide framework: a Tableau dashboard (fed by a Google Forms intake sheet) that renders all past, present, and future tests as a clickable Gantt chart linking straight to result reports; a switch from frequentist to Bayesian A/B testing so results could be shown mid-flight without pre-computing sample size; and a Cloud Composer-orchestrated 5-task DAG that ingests form submissions into BigQuery, extracts test parameters, and posts daily report updates to Slack automatically. The stated outcome is qualitative: broader non-analyst participation in running tests, reduced analyst workload, and standardized reporting quality across ABEMA's product teams.
what_they_built: A company-wide A/B testing operations framework combining a Tableau Gantt-chart dashboard for test visibility, a move to Bayesian statistical methodology, and a Cloud Composer 5-task DAG that automates intake-to-Slack-report generation.
evidence: Qualitative only: the post states the automation "reduced analyst workload" (分析者の作業工数を削減することができました) and enabled the ideal state of teams executing tests alongside analysts rather than being fully dependent on them; no quantitative before/after metrics are given.
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete pattern for scaling an internal experimentation practice beyond a data team's headcount: standardize the stats framework, centralize visibility, and automate the reporting pipeline rather than adding analysts.

title: なぜあなたのA/Bテストはうまくいくのか？A/Bテストの分析で注意すること
summary: CyberAgent's Dynalyst DSP team noticed that A/A tests (comparing identical treatments) were flagging statistically significant differences even though nothing differed, and traced the cause to a mismatch between the randomization unit (user) and the analysis unit (impression), which biases variance estimates because heavy users contribute many correlated impressions. They ran 500 simulated A/A test replications comparing naive impression-level CTR against three fixes: user-level CTR averaging, the delta method, and cluster-robust standard errors, checking whether resulting p-values were uniformly distributed as they should be under a true null. They found impression-level CTR p-values clustered incorrectly near zero (false positives), while the three corrected methods produced properly uniform p-value distributions confirmed via Kolmogorov-Smirnov tests. In production Dynalyst adopted a hybrid: simple impression-level metrics for quick exploratory looks, but delta-method-corrected variance for final go/no-go decisions.
what_they_built: A validated experimentation-analysis methodology (and adopted production practice) for computing statistically correct significance when the randomization unit (user) differs from the analysis unit (ad impression) in an ads DSP.
evidence: 500-replication A/A test simulation: impression-level CTR p-values were skewed near zero (false-positive-prone), while user-level CTR, delta-method, and cluster-robust standard-error approaches produced approximately uniform p-value distributions, confirmed by Kolmogorov-Smirnov tests.
why_it_matters: Any PM running A/B tests where the randomized unit (user) differs from the metric's natural grain (impressions, sessions, events) risks false-positive experiment reads unless variance is corrected for that mismatch.

title: 3DS Rule Experimentation and Optimization
summary: Deliveroo's Trust team found that its blanket 3D Secure (3DS) authentication rules at checkout, while reducing fraud, were adding friction that hurt conversion and customer retention. In Q4 2022 they ran A/B tests on their highest-volume 3DS rules, exempting a treatment group from specific checks and using Ravelin's platform to run the tests without heavy engineering investment. They evaluated each rule against a financial framework combining operational profit, 3DS fees saved, and additional chargeback/compensation costs, then rolled back or narrowed the rules that scored poorly. The result was a nearly 40% reduction in 3DS rate without a corresponding increase in fraud exposure.
what_they_built: An A/B-testing and cost-benefit evaluation process for individual 3DS fraud-prevention rules, run on Ravelin's platform, that measures each rule's net financial impact (profit and fees saved minus chargeback and compensation costs) rather than fraud rate alone.
evidence: Nearly 40% reduction in 3DS rate for genuine customers without exposing Deliveroo, customers, or partners to additional fraud risk, reported as improving checkout conversion and partner/rider order volume.
why_it_matters: Shows PMs how to treat a fraud/trust control as a tunable product surface with its own A/B-tested cost-benefit model rather than a fixed compliance checkbox.

title: Deliveroo IPO Prospectus — Editions Delivery-Only Kitchens
publisher: Deliveroo Holdings plc (LSE IPO Prospectus, March 2021)
summary: Restaurant partners on Deliveroo's marketplace were often growing their delivery volumes faster than their dine-in kitchens could handle, and many local markets lacked enough restaurant content to satisfy consumer demand. Deliveroo responded by building Editions: it leases premises, handles planning permission and kitchen fit-out, and licenses turnkey delivery-only kitchen space to partners (who bring their own chefs and menus), while Deliveroo manages utilities, site operations, and logistics software. The prospectus reports the model scaled to close to 250 Editions kitchens across eight markets by the 2021 filing, with delivery-only sites cutting rider wait times enough that Q4 2020 Editions orders arrived on average four minutes faster than non-Editions orders, alongside overall GTV growth of 58.5% (2019) and 64.3% (2020) attributed partly to growth businesses like Editions.
what_they_built: Editions — a network of delivery-only kitchens that Deliveroo leases, fits out, and licenses to restaurant partners, letting partners expand into new locations without taking on their own real estate risk.
evidence: Close to 250 Editions kitchens live across eight markets at filing time; Q4 2020 Editions orders delivered ~4 minutes faster on average than non-Editions orders; company GTV grew 58.5% in 2019 (£1.57bn to £2.48bn) and 64.3% in 2020 (£2.48bn to £4.08bn), with growth businesses (Editions, Plus, Signature) cited as contributors to that growth mix.
why_it_matters: Shows how a marketplace can turn a partner operational bottleneck (dine-in kitchens choking on delivery volume) into a owned real-estate layer that both fixes partner economics and deepens the platform's own supply-side moat.

title: How to Experiment Rapidly Without Losing Rigour
summary: Deliveroo's data science team wanted to run experiments faster without waiting for a full fixed-sample size to be reached before making a call. They built a sequential testing framework using power-law error-spending functions (p=2 for the success boundary, p=3 for the futility boundary) with interim checks at 10%, 50%, and 100% of the maximum sample, letting experiments stop early in either direction without inflating false-positive risk. They quantify the trade-off precisely: the sequential design needs a maximum sample only 6.4% larger than a traditional fixed design, in exchange for the ability to conclude much earlier when true effects are larger than the minimum detectable effect assumed upfront.
what_they_built: A sequential hypothesis-testing framework with power-law error-spending boundaries and three interim checkpoints (10%/50%/100% of max sample) enabling early stopping for both success and futility.
evidence: Reports the exact statistical cost of the design: "N_Sequential = 1.064 · N_Fixed" — a 6.4% larger maximum sample size than a fixed design — in exchange for the ability to stop experiments early.
why_it_matters: Gives a PM running an internal experimentation platform a concrete, named methodology and quantified trade-off for adding early-stopping to A/B tests without compromising statistical rigor.

title: Experimentation at Delivery Hero is Fun with Flags
summary: Delivery Hero's engineering team originally built "Fun With Flags" (FwF) in 2018 as a simple feature-flagging tool for hotfixes and toggles, but it grew so fast that teams began misusing it for ad hoc, statistically invalid experiments. To fix this, the team layered rigorous experimentation practices onto the flagging infrastructure — hypothesis-driven setups, runtime health checks like sample ratio mismatch (SRM) detection, proper statistical tests, group sequential testing to shorten experiment duration, and holdout groups for concurrent experiment tracking. The platform now serves as Delivery Hero's unified system for both feature flagging and democratized, self-serve experimentation across the company.
what_they_built: A company-wide feature-flagging and experimentation platform (Fun With Flags) that evolved to include hypothesis-driven experiment setup, SRM-based runtime health checks, group sequential testing for faster experiment conclusions, customizable metrics, and concurrent holdout groups.
evidence: FwF scaled from a niche flagging tool to handling roughly 100 million flag requests per week within a year, with 90% of all Delivery Hero platforms adopting it.
why_it_matters: Shows PMs and platform teams how a lightweight flagging tool can be evolved into a rigorous, self-serve experimentation platform that democratizes data-driven decision-making across an org while avoiding the statistical pitfalls of naive flag-based testing.

title: 上流からの品質管理で障害が80%も減。DeNAが目指す、当たり前品質を超えた「デライト品質」とは？
publisher: フルスイング by DeNA (DeNA's owned media)
summary: DeNA's quality management department, under new leadership, moved from a reactive, testing-only function into an upstream partner embedded with business teams. They reorganized QA into groups aligned to business domains (gaming, services, etc.), used historical defect data to identify and eliminate redundant processes, and started reviewing specifications alongside business teams before development began. The changes were paired with structured milestone-based improvement systems and a clearer articulation of what each group owned. The team frames the next goal as "delight quality" — going beyond baseline defect prevention to continuously track evolving customer expectations post-launch.
what_they_built: A restructured quality-management organization split into business-domain-aligned groups that engage upstream in specification review (rather than only testing pre-release), backed by data-driven analysis of historical defects to cut redundant QA steps.
evidence: Reported outcomes: overall failure/defect incidents fell by roughly 80% compared to before, and coding errors fell by 73%, attributed to the upstream spec-review involvement and process overhaul.
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete, measured example of how moving quality/QA involvement earlier into planning and spec review — not just adding more testing — can materially reduce production defects, a pattern any PM can apply when deciding where to insert quality gates.

title: 滴滴数据驱动利器：AB实验之分组提效 (Adaptive Grouping for Didi's Apollo A/B Experimentation Platform)
publisher: 滴滴技术 (Didi Tech, official engineering blog)
summary: Didi's Engineering Efficiency team found that simple random assignment in their Apollo A/B testing platform (running 6000+ parallel experiments a week) often produced badly imbalanced control/treatment groups, undermining experiment validity. They built and tested three grouping approaches — complete randomization (CR), re-randomization (RR), and an adaptive/covariate-adaptive randomization method (CAR) that dynamically assigns units while scoring balance via ANOVA — on a 10,000-driver sample over 400 iterations. The adaptive method got a single pass to an acceptable balance over 95% of the time, without RR's extra computation cost.
what_they_built: An adaptive (covariate-adaptive) randomization algorithm added to Didi's Apollo experimentation platform that dynamically balances group assignment using balance coefficients and ANOVA-based metric scoring, replacing plain complete-randomization or repeated re-randomization.
evidence: On a 10,000-driver test over 400 iterations: complete randomization (CR) had max group difference exceeding 14%; re-randomization (RR) reduced max difference to under 2.7% but cost extra compute; the adaptive method (CAR) controlled the difference below 0.8% in 95% of cases in a single grouping pass.
why_it_matters: Shows PMs running high-volume internal experimentation platforms a concrete, measurable way to fix group-imbalance bias without paying re-randomization's compute cost, directly improving trust in every downstream A/B result.

title: 滴滴数据仓库指标体系建设实践 (Didi's Metric System and Data Warehouse Governance Practice)
publisher: Didi Technology Blog (滴滴技术), mirrored on cnblogs.com
summary: Didi's Data Warehouse Team (Foundation Platform Division) faced sprawling, inconsistently defined business metrics across ride-hailing, accessibility, and vehicle-service lines, making cross-team analysis unreliable. They built a company-wide metric-definition governance system combining an OSM model (Objective-Strategy-Measurement) for identifying core indicators, AARRR for user-lifecycle metrics, and a T1/T2/T3 tiered classification scheme, organized around a "people-product-scene" framework. The approach deliberately favored "light standards, heavy scenarios," shifting metric ownership from top-down control toward a service model for business teams. It was rolled out across the company's core business divisions with measurable adoption.
what_they_built: A hierarchical, company-wide metric-definition and data-warehouse governance system (OSM + AARRR + T1/T2/T3 tiering) for standardizing business indicators across product lines.
evidence: 5,000+ standardized indicators entered the metric system, covering the company's core business segments across 88 data domains, 385 business processes, and 52 business scenarios.
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete pattern for scaling metric governance across many product lines without freezing teams into rigid top-down definitions — useful for any PM standing up analytics/data-platform standards across a multi-business-line org.

title: How We're Fighting Spammers on Discord
summary: Discord's Safety team faced three distinct categories of platform spam: mass-generated accounts, compromised legitimate accounts, and manually-operated human spam campaigns. They built a layered defense: a prominent Report Spam button in DMs to surface community signal, automatic warning/hiding of messages from flagged accounts, an experimental "Server Safe Mode" that triggers captchas when a server sees suspicious surges in new members, and a suspicious-link warning system similar to browser phishing protections. Community reporting increased their ability to identify bad actors by 1000%, feeding both faster manual takedowns and improved automated detection models. The team frames this as an ongoing "cat and mouse" problem requiring continuous iteration rather than a solved state.
what_they_built: A multi-layered anti-spam system combining a user-facing Report Spam button, automated message flagging/hiding, an experimental Server Safe Mode that auto-triggers captchas on inauthentic member surges, and a suspicious-link warning feature.
evidence: Community reporting increased Discord's ability to identify bad actors by 1000%, which the team credits with both faster spammer removal and improved automated detection models; they also note catching many spammers after only a few messages sent.
why_it_matters: Shows how pairing a lightweight user-reporting surface with automated triage can compound into a large, measurable lift in abuse-detection coverage without solely relying on model improvements.

title: Maintaining Trust and Safety at Discord With Over 200 Million People
summary: Discord's Trust and Safety team describes how it triages and reviews user reports at scale — over 800 reports a day, more than 6,000 a week — and the four factors (scale, privacy, nuance, freedom) it weighs when designing enforcement policy. The post walks through three ambiguous real-world moderation scenarios (mutual harassment, disputed ban appeals, impersonation with protective intent) to show how those factors trade off in practice. It also commits Discord to publishing its first quarterly transparency report by the end of April 2019.
what_they_built: A report-review workflow and policy-design framework for handling Terms of Service and Community Guidelines violations across a 200M+ user platform, paired with a recurring public transparency report.
evidence: Concrete operational volume: 800+ reports reviewed daily, 6,000+ per week; the team frames even a 99.9% decision-confidence rate as producing roughly 6 wrong calls out of 6,000 weekly reports, and commits to quarterly transparency reports starting end of April 2019.
why_it_matters: Shows a PM building trust-and-safety or moderation systems how to reason explicitly about the scale/privacy/nuance/freedom trade-off and how to communicate an inherent error rate to users rather than claiming perfection.

title: Universal Holdout Groups at Disney Streaming
summary: Disney Streaming's experimentation team found that standard 1-3 week A/B tests couldn't detect long-term effects like novelty wear-off, lacked sample size for precise impact estimates, and couldn't reveal how many separately-winning features actually combined to move the business. They built a universal holdout group, sized to detect a 1% change in hours watched per subscriber, that runs alongside normal experimentation and evaluates the shipped changes' aggregate effect over months rather than weeks. Running it surfaced that cumulative feature impact was much smaller than the sum of individual experiment wins, exposing cannibalization between features and cases where features that won in A/B tests decayed or reversed over time. The team also reports that maintaining dual code paths (holdout vs. shipped) across the stack was costly and error-prone, requiring extra QA oversight.
what_they_built: A universal holdout mechanism that withholds a small, randomly-sampled percentage of users from all shipped product changes for a 4-month cycle (3-month enrollment plus 1-month evaluation) to measure the cumulative, long-run impact of features rather than relying only on individual short-window A/B tests.
evidence: Cumulative feature impact measured via the holdout was "much smaller than expected" versus the sum of individual A/B wins, revealing cannibalization between features; several features with strong initial A/B results showed decreasing or inconsistent impact over time (attributed to seasonal/novelty effects); the holdout was sized specifically to detect a 1% change in hours watched per subscriber as the threshold of financial meaningfulness. Operational cost is also reported: maintaining dual code paths across the tech stack was costly and introduced errors needing QA oversight.
why_it_matters: Shows a PM-relevant pattern for any metrics-heavy org: short-window A/B wins can overstate true cumulative impact, so a standing holdout is a governance mechanism for catching feature cannibalization and novelty decay that individual experiments miss.

title: DoorDash, Inc. Form 10-K FY2024 — MD&A: Net Revenue Margin expansion driven by advertising revenue
summary: DoorDash's FY2024 10-K reports that its Net Revenue Margin (revenue as a percentage of Marketplace GOV) rose to 13.4% in 2024 from 12.9% in 2023, and that total revenue grew 24% ($2.1B) versus 20% growth in Marketplace GOV — a gap the company attributes explicitly to a rising contribution from advertising revenue layered onto its core delivery marketplace. The filing also shows Contribution Profit rising to $3.5B from $2.5B. This is the audited MD&A disclosure of a company monetizing a second, higher-margin revenue line (ads) on top of a lower-margin logistics marketplace, with three years of comparative figures.
what_they_built: An advertising revenue line (sponsored placements/promotions for merchants) sold alongside DoorDash's core delivery marketplace, disclosed and quantified as a driver of take-rate expansion in the company's annual SEC filing.
evidence: Net Revenue Margin: 13.4% (2024) vs 12.9% (2023). Revenue: $10,722M (2024) vs $8,635M (2023), up $2,087M / 24%. Marketplace GOV: $80.2B, up 20%. Contribution Profit: $3.5B (2024) vs $2.5B (2023). The filing states revenue outpaced GOV growth "primarily due to an increased contribution from advertising revenue."
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete, audited example of how a marketplace can expand blended take-rate by adding a second monetization layer (ads) without raising core transaction fees — a pattern PMs building marketplace or platform businesses can point to when making the business case for an ads/monetization product.

title: Meet Dash-AB: The Statistics Engine of Experimentation at DoorDash
summary: DoorDash ran thousands of experiments monthly with no standardized analysis, leading to error-prone results (e.g. mishandled clustering in diff-in-diff/switchback tests), duplicated data-scientist effort, and siloed learning across teams. They built Dash-AB, a centralized statistics library plugged into their experimentation platform Curie, that takes a simple JSON config and runs validation, variance calculation (regression, delta-method, or bootstrapping), variance reduction (CUPED/CUPAC), and hypothesis testing (fixed-horizon or sequential/mSPRT). The library standardized methodology across all product teams and let non-statisticians run trustworthy, scalable experiment analysis.
what_they_built: A centralized statistics library (Dash-AB) integrated with DoorDash's Curie experimentation platform: a JSON-config interface plus a pipeline of data validation (imbalance/flicker tests), variance calculation (regression-based, delta-method, bootstrap), variance reduction (CUPED, CUPAC), and hypothesis testing (fixed-horizon t-test or sequential mSPRT testing), plus a sample-size calculator.
evidence: Adoption of CUPED and CUPAC variance reduction methods reduced the sample size needed for statistical significance by 10-20%, letting experiments conclude faster.
why_it_matters: Gives PMs and platform teams a template for how to scale trustworthy self-serve experimentation across an org — standardizing statistical methodology behind a simple config interface so product teams don't need in-house statisticians to run and trust A/B tests, while directly cutting experiment duration via variance reduction.

title: DoorDash, Inc. Form S-1/A (Amendment) — DashPass subscription program
summary: In its pre-IPO S-1/A registration statement, DoorDash describes DashPass, a $9.99/month subscription that waives delivery fees and reduces service fees on eligible orders, framed as the mechanism to increase consumer engagement and lower the effective cost of delivery. The filing reports that DashPass subscribers show higher order frequency than non-subscribers (based on data since the 2018 launch, with the caveat that early adopters may not represent future subscribers), that average consumer fee per order fell 20% in Q3 2020 versus Q3 2017 partly via DashPass, and that DashPass had crossed 5 million subscribers by September 30, 2020.
what_they_built: DashPass, a paid membership/subscription product ($9.99/month) offering $0 delivery fees and reduced service fees on eligible orders, positioned to reward and retain the platform's most engaged consumers and merchants.
evidence: Over 5 million consumers on DashPass as of September 30, 2020; DashPass subscribers show higher order frequency than non-subscribers since the 2018 launch; average consumer fee per order down 20% in Q3 2020 vs Q3 2017.
why_it_matters: Shows how a marketplace can use a paid membership tier to simultaneously lift engagement/frequency and lower the perceived per-order cost to consumers, with the filing itself modeling how to report such a program's traction with real metrics rather than aspiration.

title: Improving Online Experiment Capacity by 4X with Parallelization and Increased Sensitivity
summary: DoorDash's legacy experimentation system could only run one experiment at a time per surface and required large sample sizes to detect the increasingly small effect sizes typical of mature products, which throttled how many product changes could be tested per quarter. To fix this, the team built "Dynamic Values," a layered experiment framework that splits users into disjoint groups so multiple mutually-exclusive experiments run concurrently, and implemented CUPED to shrink metric variance using pre-experiment data so smaller samples could still detect small effects. Together these changes quadrupled DoorDash's overall experiment capacity, letting teams like store-ranking and carousel-ranking run competing algorithm tests in parallel instead of sequentially over multiple quarters. The post also flags trade-offs (thinner traffic per variant, no built-in interaction-effect measurement) and next steps like multi-layer designs and multi-armed bandits.
what_they_built: DoorDash's Experiment Platform team built "Dynamic Values," a layered experimentation infrastructure that lets multiple mutually-exclusive experiments run simultaneously on disjoint user segments (single-layer and multi-layer designs), plus adopted CUPED (Controlled-experiment Using Pre-Experiment Data) to reduce metric variance and increase statistical sensitivity without needing larger sample sizes.
evidence: Reported a 4x increase in overall experiment capacity (title/thesis of the post); illustrative example shows two large-effect experiments completing in 7 days run in parallel vs. 14 days sequentially, and a simulated MDE analysis shows detecting a 0.1% effect requires 25x the sample size of detecting a 0.5% effect on a key DoorDash business metric.
why_it_matters: Gives product and platform PMs a concrete pattern for scaling experiment throughput (parallel layers, traffic allocation trade-offs, generalizability vs. speed) and for detecting shrinking effect sizes via variance reduction instead of just growing sample size — directly relevant to how PMs plan and prioritize experiment roadmaps.

title: Business Policy Experiments using Fractional Factorial Designs: Consumer Retention on DoorDash
summary: DoorDash needed to evaluate many possible consumer retention/promotion policies but full randomized experiments across every combination of policy factors were too slow and too expensive to run at scale. The team applied fractional factorial experimental design, a technique from classical design of experiments, combined with modern heterogeneous treatment effect estimation, to test combinations of policy "factors" more efficiently than a full factorial or one-at-a-time test plan. They ran this on real consumer promotion policies at DoorDash and used it to identify a policy configuration that outperformed the status quo. The paper lays out both the statistical method and its assumptions, then reports the business result from applying it in production.
what_they_built: A fractional-factorial experimentation framework that factorizes a business policy (e.g., a promotion/retention policy) into component decision factors, tests a fraction of the full combinatorial design space, and layers on heterogeneous treatment effect estimation to recover policy-level effects at lower experimental cost.
evidence: The method discovered a consumer promotion/retention policy that delivered 5% incremental profit at 67% lower implementation (experimentation) cost compared to the alternative approach, per the paper's own abstract.
why_it_matters: Shows a PM-transferable pattern for testing multi-factor policies (pricing, promotions, retention levers) faster and cheaper than exhaustive A/B testing, by borrowing fractional factorial design from classical DOE instead of testing every combination separately.

title: The 4 Principles DoorDash Used to Increase Its Logistics Experiment Capacity by 1000%
summary: DoorDash's Assignment team faced volatile, network-effect-heavy logistics dynamics that made offline sizing unreliable, plus failure-averse culture, ad hoc SQL/notebook analysis, and unmanageable review meetings as experiment volume grew. They built a culture of fail-fast learning, standardized goal/guardrail/diagnostic metrics, a two-zone parallelization design for switchback experiments to manage cross-experiment interactions, a fixed weekly experiment cadence, and a centralized automated analysis platform. As a result, experiment volume grew from about 10/month in 2018 to over 100/month by mid-2021, a roughly 1000% increase, sustaining around 30 concurrent switchback experiments processing up to 200,000 QPS of bucket evaluations.
what_they_built: A four-pillar experimentation system: a fail-fast/learn-fast experimentation culture; a scalable process with standardized goal/guardrail/diagnostic metrics and a weekly experiment cadence; a two-zone (zone 1 preliminary filter, zone 2 validation) layered design for running many concurrent switchback experiments while managing interaction effects; and a centralized, automated experimentation analysis platform replacing ad hoc SQL/Python/R notebooks.
evidence: Experiment capacity increased over 1000% in three years — from around 10 experiments/month in 2018 to over 100/month in June 2021; DoorDash now runs about 1,000 experiments per year, including 30 concurrently running switchback experiments handling up to 200,000 QPS of bucket evaluations; roughly 25+ experiments reviewed weekly.
why_it_matters: Shows platform PMs a concrete, non-ML pattern for scaling experimentation velocity in operationally networked (marketplace/logistics) environments where standard A/B testing breaks down — combining process design (cadence, metric standardization), statistical design (two-zone filtering for switchback experiments), and tooling investment to 10x experiment throughput.

title: Next-Generation Optimization for Dasher Dispatch at DoorDash
summary: DoorDash's dasher-dispatch system originally framed dasher-to-order matching as a bipartite matching problem solved with the Hungarian algorithm, which was too slow for large real-time instances and couldn't support multi-delivery routes. The team reformulated it as a mixed-integer program solved with the commercial Gurobi solver, which was faster and flexible enough to model vehicle-routing-style multi-stop deliveries. Because dispatch is a tier-zero system, they couldn't risk a standard live experiment, so they built a combined optimizer that ran the new MIP logic alongside the legacy algorithm in production, always served the legacy output, and logged divergences to validate the new approach before cutting over.
what_they_built: DoorDash replaced the Hungarian-algorithm bipartite-matching core of its real-time dasher dispatch system with a mixed-integer program (MIP) solved via a commercial solver (Gurobi), enabling multi-delivery routes and faster solve times. To ship it safely, instead of running a live experiment on this tier-zero system, they built a third "combined" optimizer that ran both the old and new logic in parallel behind an extensible interface, always returned the legacy result to users, and logged where the two diverged.
evidence: Gurobi solved the matching problem up to 10x faster than the Hungarian algorithm and benchmarked 34x faster on average than the open-source CBC solver; the new MIP optimizer's output matched the legacy optimizer's output more than 99% of the time in shadow-mode logging (mismatches traced to multiple equally-good solutions), giving the team confidence to cut over without a live experiment.
why_it_matters: Shows platform/PM teams a pattern for swapping a business-critical real-time decision engine with zero downtime by shadow-running the new logic against production traffic and using agreement rate as the go/no-go signal, rather than a traditional randomized experiment.

title: Iterating Real-time Assignment Algorithms Through Experimentation
summary: DoorDash's operations research scientists found that the classic offline "prepare-prototype-productionize" approach used in railway/airline optimization didn't work for its real-time Dasher assignment system, because building an accurate simulator was as hard as the underlying logistics problem and pre/post analysis was too noisy given volatile daily metrics. They replaced it with a preparation-experiment-production framework where ORS dig into the live assignment codebase and implement their own algorithm changes as switchback experiments, randomizing control vs. treatment across short time windows (as short as a few hours) and geographic regions. Engineers step in only for complex changes, with code review as the safety net. This let DoorDash measure real production impact rigorously and roll out validated changes quickly instead of relying on unreliable pre/post observational studies.
what_they_built: A three-stage (preparation, experiment, production) algorithm-iteration framework replacing offline prototyping: ORS gain deep knowledge of the live assignment codebase, propose changes to input data, MIP model/objective/constraints, or output execution, validate ideas via historical data or a preliminary simulator, then implement and run switchback experiments directly in the production system — randomizing control vs. treatment algorithm by time window and geographic region, with engineers reviewing code rather than building the experiments themselves.
evidence: Switchback experiments typically deliver enough data within about two weeks, and because ORS implement their own experiments directly as production code, validated changes roll out to full production almost immediately afterward, eliminating the discrepancy between the validated algorithm and the productionized one and removing the engineer-ORS coordination overhead that previously slowed iteration.
why_it_matters: Shows platform PMs a concrete pattern for experimentation infrastructure in real-time, highly volatile operational systems where classic offline simulation and pre/post analysis both fail — enabling domain experts (not just engineers) to own experiment design and implementation end-to-end, shortening the path from insight to validated production rollout.

title: DRS: Making high-impact & informed decisions with Dream11's In-house Experimentation Platform
summary: Dream11, serving over 130 million users with sharp traffic spikes during events like IPL, found that off-the-shelf experimentation tools couldn't handle its custom user-sampling, assignment, and backend experimentation needs. So they built DRS (Data-Driven Review System), an in-house platform supporting frontend and backend experiments, multiple cohorting and sampling strategies (cluster-based, manual mapping, stratified), and randomized or round-robin assignment. The system layers Cassandra as source of truth, Aerospike for caching, and Redshift for analytics, with an SDK at the API gateway to minimize unnecessary service calls, and rolls out experiments gradually using a canary-inspired slow-release mechanism. In production it has sustained peak loads described in the post.
what_they_built: An in-house A/B experimentation platform (DRS) with configurable cohorting, sampling, and assignment strategies, isolation controls, and gradual canary-style traffic rollout.
evidence: The post reports the experiment service serving a peak load of 16M requests with API latency of p95 < 10ms, and the broader system handling 120 million requests per minute (RPM) during peak load such as IPL matches.
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete pattern for building a custom experimentation platform when commercial A/B tools can't handle domain-specific cohorting and extreme traffic-spike scale.

title: Lessons learned from running GraphQL at scale
summary: Dream11's GraphQL layer faced extreme, minutes-long traffic bursts during live cricket matches (e.g. 14 million to 28 million requests in 2 minutes) ahead of IPL 2021, requiring roughly 7,500 instances to serve 1 million requests per second. The engineering team implemented four concrete optimizations over several months: increasing Node.js's max-semi-space-size GC flag (12% CPU reduction), migrating from Express to Fastify (10% CPU reduction), caching GraphQL query parsing/validation for repeated queries (26% CPU time reduction), and tuning load-balancer DNS TTL for better traffic distribution. Comparing IPL 2021 to IPL 2020, they report P95 latency down 50%, GraphQL execution time down 70%, instance count down 80%, and daily infra cost down 60%, projecting over $1 million in annual savings.
what_they_built: A set of GraphQL server and infrastructure optimizations (GC tuning, Express-to-Fastify migration, query parsing/validation caching, and load-balancer DNS TTL tuning) to handle massive, sudden traffic bursts during live cricket match events.
evidence: IPL 2021 vs IPL 2020: 50% P95 latency reduction, 70% GraphQL execution time reduction, 80% fewer provisioned instances, 60% lower daily average infra cost, and a projected >$1M annual savings; individual optimizations independently measured at 12%, 10%, and 26% CPU reductions.
why_it_matters: Shows a PM/platform team how to translate a predictable, extreme-concurrency product event (live sports) into a concrete, measured infra cost-and-latency reduction program rather than just over-provisioning.

title: Experimentation at Dream11: Chapter I - Intelligent Traffic Routing (ITR)
summary: Dream11's existing rule-based payment gateway routing struggled to sustain payment success rates under match-day concurrency of over 11 million users and 10,000+ transactions per second. The engineering team built an Intelligent Traffic Routing (ITR) system that evaluated multiple multi-armed bandit algorithms (epsilon-greedy, UCB, Thompson sampling, Boltzmann Gumbel) to dynamically route payment traffic across gateways, choosing an agent-based architecture over HTTP services or clipper-based alternatives for scalability and latency. A sliding-window UCB variant (window length 200, discount factor 0.6) was selected after evaluation and run as a live experiment against the rule-based baseline across roughly 240 million transactions over 60 days. The bandit approach produced a statistically significant cumulative uplift of 0.92% over one month versus the incumbent system.
what_they_built: A production multi-armed-bandit-based payment gateway routing system (Intelligent Traffic Routing) replacing a rule-based router, using an agent-based architecture to handle high-throughput, high-concurrency traffic.
evidence: Statistically significant cumulative uplift of 0.92% over one month versus the rule-based baseline, evaluated across approximately 240 million transactions over 60 days, on a system handling over 10,000 transactions per second and 11 million concurrent app users.
why_it_matters: Shows how to graduate an online experimentation methodology (bandits) from a research technique to a production decision system with real business-metric evidence, a template for PMs owning infra-adjacent optimization decisions at scale.

title: Power of Machine Learning ahead of big matches
summary: Dream11's platform faced massive, unpredictable traffic spikes during major cricket events, and manual auto-scaling-group configuration ahead of these matches was slow and error-prone. The engineering team built an ML model that forecasts required capacity from historical and real-time signals (participating teams, tournament stage, virality, new vs. repeat user patterns), letting infrastructure scale up or down automatically without manual intervention. Separately, they adopted AWS CloudWatch Metric Streams to fix an operational blind spot where metrics were lagging 20-30 minutes behind real time. The post reports the resulting scale handled and the latency improvement from the monitoring fix.
what_they_built: A machine-learning-based predictive capacity-scaling system for pre-match infrastructure provisioning, paired with a switch to AWS CloudWatch Metric Streams for near-real-time metrics delivery.
evidence: Reported outcomes: 6.21 million concurrent users handled at the edge layer during the T20 ICC World Cup; processing of 25+ TB of data and 24+ billion events on peak match days (2.5x normal-day volume); CloudWatch metrics delay cut from an approximate 20-30 minutes down to a negligible one to two seconds after adopting Metric Streams.
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete pattern for turning a recurring, high-stakes manual ops process (pre-event capacity planning) into an automated, model-driven one with measurable reliability and latency gains.

title: Observability at scale: How we built a cutting-edge Dream11 monitoring ecosystem
summary: Dream11's SRE team faced monitoring gaps at IPL-scale traffic: their CloudWatch-based metrics pipeline (API scraping) had a 20-30 minute delivery delay and struggled with the volume of edge-service traffic (100 million requests/minute) and 65+ billion daily tracing spans. They built a unified monitoring ecosystem centered on CloudWatch Metric Streams plus distributed tracing to replace slow polling, and instrumented alerting on API latency (>200ms) and error-rate thresholds (>0.01%), along with cross-AZ TCP retransmit monitoring to catch infrastructure issues early. The rebuilt pipeline cut metrics latency from 20-30 minutes down to 1-2 seconds and cut cost roughly 4x versus the prior scraping approach.
what_they_built: A unified observability platform combining CloudWatch Metric Streams (replacing slow API-polling) with distributed tracing and automated cross-AZ network health checks, run internally by Dream11's SRE team.
evidence: Metrics delivery latency dropped from 20-30 minutes to 1-2 seconds; CloudWatch Metric Streams cost roughly 4x (about 75%) less than the prior scraping approach; system ingests 65+ billion spans/day and 700+ million CloudWatch metrics/day during major events, handling 100 million requests/minute at peak.
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete internal-platform pattern (switch from polling to streaming telemetry) that materially improves incident-detection speed and cost at large scale, a tradeoff any PM running high-traffic infrastructure should recognize.

title: Observability at Scale: How we built a cutting-edge Dream11 monitoring ecosystem
summary: Dream11's engineering team faced extreme match-day observability demands: 100 million requests per minute at edge services, 50+ TB of data and 65+ billion ingested spans daily, plus a CloudWatch metrics pipeline that lagged 20-30 minutes and hit API rate limits. They built a unified "top dashboard" for actionable event monitoring, added network-level tracking (TCP retransmits, AZ-level filtering) to catch faulty availability zones, and adopted an APM tool for unsampled end-to-end distributed tracing across 100+ microservices. Switching to AWS CloudWatch Metric Streams with OpenTelemetry collectors cut metric latency from 20-30 minutes to 1-2 seconds at roughly a quarter of the prior cost.
what_they_built: An internal, org-wide observability/monitoring platform combining a unified top-level dashboard, network and AZ-level monitoring, unsampled distributed tracing via an APM tool, and a CloudWatch Metric Streams + OpenTelemetry collector pipeline replacing slower metric-scraping.
evidence: Metrics latency dropped from 20-30 minutes to 1-2 seconds and became "four times cheaper" than the prior AWS CloudWatch GWD-Metrics approach; system handles 100M RPM at edge, 50+TB/day, 65+ billion spans/day, and 700 million CloudWatch metrics/day during major events like IPL, across 100+ microservices.
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete, measurable pattern for how a platform team justifies and executes an observability-pipeline migration under extreme, spiky product-usage load (live sports events).

title: To Scale In Or Scale Out? Here's How We Scale at Dream11
summary: Dream11's DevOps/SRE and data science teams faced extreme, spiky traffic swings around IPL and other cricket matches, where reactive autoscaling, spot-instance limits, and load-balancer shard constraints couldn't provision fast enough. They built Scaler, a proactive scaling platform driven by an XGBoost-based model (200+ variables) that forecasts hourly user concurrency per match, feeding infrastructure provisioning across 100+ microservices in pre-defined concurrency slabs before, during, and after matches. They paired this with a homegrown performance-testing framework (Torque) and provisioning framework (Playground), validated through regular 'Game Days,' plus deployment optimizations like pre-baked AMIs and weighted DNS across load-balancer shards. The result: operations that used to take hours now take minutes, lower infrastructure cost, and the ability to sustain 100M+ requests per minute at the edge and 5.5M concurrent users during peak IPL traffic.
what_they_built: A proactive infrastructure-scaling platform called Scaler, driven by an XGBoost concurrency-prediction model and validated via a custom performance-testing (Torque) and provisioning (Playground) toolchain with recurring Game Day load tests.
evidence: Reported outcomes: over 100 million requests per minute at edge services, 5.5 million concurrent users supported, 30,000+ compute resources during peak IPL traffic, operational scaling processes cut from hours to minutes, and lower monthly infrastructure cost from schedule-based tiered provisioning.
why_it_matters: Shows how a PM/platform team facing extreme predictable-but-spiky demand (event-driven traffic) can trade reactive autoscaling for a forecasting-driven provisioning system, and the operational/cost payoff of doing so.

title: Creating a Culture of Accessibility
summary: Dropbox's design and engineering org found that most employees, including engineers, had little exposure to accessibility because it's rarely covered in CS curricula, so awareness rather than technical difficulty was the real blocker. To fix that they stood up a cross-functional accessibility working group and a rotating set of concrete programs: an "Assistive Technology Lab" where employees hands-on test screen readers, speech recognition, head mice and switches; an accessibility debug tool built into the site footer that started with a small set of custom checks and was expanded to 51 checks (many pulled from Deque's open-source aXe framework); regular bug bashes that specifically exercise keyboard navigation and screen-reader flows; an internal recognition badge for employees who drive accessibility fixes; and direct user research with screen-reader and low-vision users. The piece reports that tasks which stumped employees at the first Assistive Technology Lab session were no longer difficult by later sessions, attributed to the resulting UI improvements.</summary> <parameter name="what_they_built">A recurring internal accessibility program at Dropbox — an Assistive Technology Lab, an in-house automated accessibility debug tool (custom checks plus Deque's aXe framework), and structured bug bashes and recognition mechanisms — run by a cross-functional working group.
what_they_built: Dropbox built a cross-functional accessibility working group (engineering, design, research, communications, legal) that ran pop-up assistive-technology labs, an internal accessibility debug tool embedded in the site footer that grew from checking 5 to 51 automated accessibility issues (incorporating Deque's open-source aXe framework), an "Accessibility Advocate" employee badge/nomination system, accessibility testing folded into existing bug bashes, and a quarterly internal tech talk series on accessibility topics.
evidence: The debug tool grew from a small initial set of custom checks to 51 automated accessibility checks; qualitatively, Dropbox reports that tasks which were challenging in the first Assistive Technology Lab session were no longer challenging in later sessions due to the resulting design/engineering fixes. This is mostly a qualitative before/after claim rather than hard adoption metrics.
why_it_matters: Shows a PM how to operationalize accessibility as a recurring cross-functional program (hands-on labs, automated checks, bug bashes, recognition) rather than a one-off compliance pass, and how to use qualitative task-difficulty signals as evidence of progress when hard metrics aren't yet available.

title: Accelerating our A/B experiments with machine learning
summary: Dropbox's experimentation team faced a tradeoff in evaluating A/B tests on the subscription model: fast proxy metrics like file uploads didn't correlate with real user value, while waiting for actual conversion and revenue data took 30-90 days, limiting how many experiments they could run per year. They built Expected Revenue (XR), a machine-learning-based metric combining a predicted conversion probability with predicted two-year lifetime value, generated within days of a trial starting rather than months later. The models (gradient boosted trees in TensorFlow) run through a daily Airflow-orchestrated pipeline on AWS Hadoop, with Hive storage, S3-persisted models, and Spark-based evaluation, segmented and calibrated by trial type and geography. This let the team draw statistically sound revenue-neutrality conclusions on experiments in days instead of months.
what_they_built: A machine-learning metric (Expected Revenue) and supporting daily pipeline that predicts two-year subscription revenue per user shortly after a trial begins, used as a fast proxy for A/B experiment decisions.
evidence: The post reports XR predictions are "typically within 5% of actual two-year revenue on a weekly basis," with systematic uncertainty reduced to about 3% via a lift-ratio methodology, enabling revenue-neutral experiment calls in days instead of the 30-90 days previously required.
why_it_matters: Shows a PM-relevant pattern for experimentation platforms: replacing a slow ground-truth metric with a calibrated predictive proxy metric to cut experiment decision latency without sacrificing statistical rigor.

title: Making Dropbox for iOS More Accessible
summary: Dropbox's team went through the iOS app screen by screen to fix VoiceOver gaps for blind and low-vision users, working with the AppleVis accessibility community during the process. They rewrote VoiceOver announcements in the welcome/sign-in tutorial so the sign-in button and resulting screen are properly narrated, made photo thumbnails announce descriptive titles instead of generic labels, simplified the passcode lock digit-selection flow, and added a close button to the More Actions menu for VoiceOver users to exit cleanly. The payoff was an explicit third-party validation: AppleVis rated the app "fully accessible with VoiceOver" and "easy to navigate and use." Dropbox closed by inviting continued community feedback and offering pre-release builds to accessibility testers.
what_they_built: A set of targeted VoiceOver accessibility fixes across the Dropbox iOS app's onboarding, photo browsing, passcode lock, and file-actions menu.
evidence: Third-party outcome signal: AppleVis, an independent accessibility review community, rated the updated app "fully accessible with VoiceOver" and "easy to navigate and use" — not a self-reported metric, but an external community's assessment after the changes shipped.
why_it_matters: Shows a PM how to close the loop on accessibility work by using an independent, credible third-party community (rather than internal QA alone) as the validation bar before calling a redesign done.

title: Building Dropbox's New User Experience for Mobile, Part 1
summary: Dropbox's growth/design team rebuilt the first-run mobile experience to drive activation, running an A/B test on whether the intro tour should appear before or after login. They found the tour-before-login variant produced higher login/signup rates and engagement despite adding steps, and paired it with a two-way sync checklist (upload from phone, view desktop files on mobile) and a QR-code-based desktop connect flow that avoided manual credential entry via a "meta-installer." The intro and desktop connect flows shipped on both Android and iOS, while a separate "Get Started" flow was still in experimentation on Android only, and the team noted users remained reluctant to complete the final step of adding files.
what_they_built: A redesigned, A/B-tested mobile onboarding funnel: an ordered intro tour, a two-way file-sync checklist, and a QR-code desktop-connect flow with an autonomous "meta-installer."
evidence: A/B test showed higher login/account-creation rate and higher subsequent engagement for users shown the tour before login versus after, despite the added step; intro and desktop-connect flows shipped on Android and iOS, while a file-upload completion gap was identified as unresolved.
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete case where adding friction (an extra pre-login step) improved downstream activation and engagement, illustrating that onboarding funnel order should be settled by experiment, not intuition.

title: Bird's Eye: A powerful tool for exploring app screenshots
summary: Duolingo runs hundreds of simultaneous A/B tests across 23 languages and 11 devices/browsers, generating over 5,000 screenshots per weekly app release, and had no efficient way to visually compare app variations across all that surface area. The team built Bird's Eye, an internal web app that lets staff filter and browse this screenshot corpus (60,000+ images) via dropdown-driven queries backed by a Python metadata pipeline and the Tamper/PourOver libraries for compression and fast client-side filtering. The tool is now used by roughly 40 people per release across localization, QA, product/design, and monetization teams, each using it for a different visual-verification task.
what_they_built: An internal screenshot-browsing and filtering tool ("Bird's Eye") that indexes automated UI test screenshots by language, device, and app version, using Tamper to compress/serialize the data and PourOver for fast in-browser filtering.
evidence: Averages 40 users per weekly app release across departments; searches over 60,000+ screenshots per release; localization team reports a 3.5x productivity increase from using the tool.
why_it_matters: Shows how a lightweight internal data-browsing tool, not a full ML system, can unlock measurable cross-team productivity gains when a company's release complexity (multi-language, multi-device QA) outgrows manual review.

title: Elevating craft: How we refreshed our core tabs
summary: Duolingo's core app tabs (lessons, quests, leaderboards, video calls, profile, social feeds) had each been designed adequately on their own but ended up with inconsistent header sizes, varying typography hierarchy, and uneven spacing, making the app feel disjointed as a whole. The design team explored four distinct visual directions (Punchy, Soft, Modular, Flat) and had to resolve two tensions: consistency versus purposefulness of each tab, and simplicity versus clarity. They landed on a framework using tiered header sizes, an evolved typography system, and intentional whitespace rather than forcing every tab into identical containers.
what_they_built: A unified header, typography, and spacing system applied across Duolingo's core app tabs, shipped on iOS with Android to follow.
evidence: Qualitative outcome only, no numbers disclosed: the team reports higher engagement across tabs since launch while core learning metrics stayed steady.
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete framework (tiered hierarchy + purposeful variation, not rigid uniformity) for reconciling design-system consistency with each surface's distinct purpose across a multi-tab product.

title: How dogfooding helps us build a better Duolingo
summary: Duolingo has over 70% of its employees (including the CEO) using internal, most-recent app builds daily to surface bugs and UX issues before public release. To scale the signal from this practice, the Test and Release Infrastructure team built three tools: a Release Dashboard that surfaces crash/ANR/frame-rate telemetry within hours of a new build, an AI-powered tool called Jeeves that clusters dogfooding and external bug reports into trending "spikes," and Shake-to-Report, which lets any employee file a bug with an automatic screenshot, Fullstory recording, device/experiment metadata, and log file. A Monday QA gate blocks public rollout unless there are no blocking or significantly impactful bugs.
what_they_built: A company-wide dogfooding program backed by three internal tools — Release Dashboard (build telemetry), Jeeves (AI-clustered bug/feedback aggregation), and Shake-to-Report (one-tap bug filing with screenshots, session replay, and logs) — plus a Monday pre-release QA gate.
evidence: Ahead of the iOS Math and Music course launch, persistent dogfooding bug reports and elevated Crashlytics crash rates led engineers to add detailed logging; analysis of those logs identified the root cause as an edge case from pausing songs in a specific pattern, and the team shipped a fix a few weeks before public launch. Qualitative process claim: over 70% of the company dogfoods, and no public rollout proceeds unless there are no blocking or significantly impactful bugs.
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete, low-cost pattern for catching production-quality regressions before they hit users: pair a broad internal-usage culture with lightweight, purpose-built bug-capture tooling and a hard go/no-go QA gate before every release.

title: Improving Duolingo, one experiment at a time
summary: Duolingo built an internal experiments service, used to run over 2,000 A/B tests over three years, that splits users into control and treatment groups and runs nightly statistical analysis against templated, curated metrics. The post walks through two case studies: a Leaderboards experiment on iOS that increased both lesson starts and completions and was subsequently launched broadly, and a Duolingo Plus offline-lesson promotion that increased subscription revenue but was killed after the team found it hurt daily active users and retention. The piece is notable for owning the negative result rather than only showcasing wins.</summary> <parameter name="what_they_built">An internal A/B testing/experimentation platform with metric templates and nightly automated statistical comparison of control vs. experiment groups, used to gate feature launches like Leaderboards.
what_they_built: Duolingo built an internal, company-wide A/B testing service that lets teams define experiment objectives, arms, audience eligibility, and hypotheses, then gradually roll out and monitor experiments, pausing on bugs or metric drops, with the service auto-generating nightly statistical reports from curated per-experiment-type metric templates plus universal engagement/learning metrics. Over three years it powered more than 2,000 experiments and thousands of shipped feature changes.
evidence: Leaderboards experiment increased both lesson starts and completions, leading to full launch. The Duolingo Plus offline promotion produced "a slight increase in revenue" but "a decrease in user retention," and was shut down for hurting daily active users and learning habits despite the revenue gain.
why_it_matters: Shows a PM how to structure an experimentation program with pre-approved success/guardrail metrics so that a revenue-positive feature can still be correctly killed when it damages retention.

title: How we make sure Duo motivates learners across the globe
summary: Duolingo found that directly translating English UI and notification copy into other languages didn't always land with the same motivational effect. To fix this, they built Expurrimenter, an A/B testing tool embedded in their internal Computer-Assisted Translation (CopyCAT) system, letting localization teams run copy experiments per-language instead of relying on literal translation. Results varied by market: a more encouraging Spanish quit-session prompt cut abandonment, reframing German notification copy around learning benefits lifted opt-in 8%, and expanded Chinese feature descriptions for Duolingo Plus increased free-trial sign-ups. The post also notes that winning copy in one language did not automatically transfer to another, reinforcing the need for per-language testing.
what_they_built: Expurrimenter, an in-house A/B testing tool integrated into Duolingo's CopyCAT translation system that lets teams test and localize motivational in-app copy and notifications across 22+ languages.
evidence: German notification opt-in copy change increased opt-in rate by 8%; Spanish quit-session copy change reduced session abandonment and increased learning time; Chinese feature-description changes increased Duolingo Plus free-trial sign-ups; cumulative volume of copy tests more than doubled within three months of launching the tool.
why_it_matters: Shows PMs building for global audiences that literal translation of tested copy can silently underperform, and that localized experimentation infrastructure (not just localized text) is needed to capture per-market wins.

title: Meaningful metrics: How data sharpened the focus of product teams
summary: Duolingo's product teams were tracking many engagement metrics without a clear sense of which ones actually moved the needle on DAU.
