The data science team built a Growth Model, a Markov model that segments learners into seven mutually exclusive activity states (new, current, reactivated, resurrected, two at-risk states, dormant) and tracks transition probabilities between them. Running simulations across possible metric improvements showed that increasing Current User Retention Rate (CURR) by 2% month-over-month had the largest impact on DAU, so teams were refocused around CURR as the north-star lever. Duolingo reports DAU grew 4x since 2019 as the framework guided prioritization, and the team is now exploring unsupervised segmentation to keep finding growth levers as CURR approaches diminishing returns.
what_they_built: A Markov-model-based "Growth Model" that decomposes the user base into seven activity states and runs simulations to identify which metric shift has the greatest leverage on DAU.
evidence: DAU grew 4x since 2019; simulations identified that a 2% month-over-month increase in CURR had the largest impact on DAU, which became the org's guiding metric for team prioritization.
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete method (state-transition simulation) for cutting through a sprawling metrics dashboard to find the single lever worth aligning product teams around.

title: Why Duolingo Switched From Hearts to a Battery
summary: Duolingo's old Hearts system deducted one life per mistake for free users, and beginners — who make more mistakes while still learning — were twice as likely to run out of hearts mid-lesson and get blocked. The team replaced it with Energy, a daily-refilling battery that depletes per lesson (not per mistake), regenerates on answer streaks, and can be topped up via rewarded ads or gem purchases. The post frames this as a deliberate rebalancing of the free-tier limiter to reduce beginner drop-off while still sustaining the freemium/ad model. It is presented as an early, limited rollout still being iterated on, with only qualitative outcome data disclosed.
what_they_built: Replaced the mistake-penalized "Hearts" life system with "Energy," a lesson-based (not mistake-based) resource that depletes per lesson, regenerates via correct-answer streaks and daily reset, and can be refilled with rewarded ads or gem purchases.
evidence: Company states beginners were "2X more likely to run out of hearts mid-lesson" under the old system, and that "our data shows [Energy] helps more learners complete lessons" — no specific completion-rate percentage or sample size is disclosed; evidence is directional/qualitative.
why_it_matters: Shows how a PM can reframe a punitive free-tier limiter around the behavior that actually predicts drop-off (lesson attempts, not individual mistakes) without abandoning the underlying monetization lever.

title: Language learning for the next billion: Duolingo in India
summary: Duolingo's India team found that newly-online users unfamiliar with recurring billing treated Duolingo Plus promotional screens as a hard paywall and churned. They first removed the initial Duolingo Plus promotion shown early in a new learner's lifecycle, seeing a 1% DAU increase, then went further and removed Duolingo Plus entirely in India and a few other Asian countries. The full removal produced a 6% increase in DAUs, alongside other India-specific product changes around English-course positioning and colloquial-Hindi onboarding copy.
what_they_built: A market-specific monetization rollback: staged removal of Duolingo Plus subscription promotion, then full removal of the paid subscription tier, for users in India and select Asian markets.
evidence: Removing the initial Plus promotion yielded a 1% DAU increase; fully removing Duolingo Plus in India and similar markets yielded a 6% DAU increase (compared against separate 2% DAU gain from English-course repositioning and 1% onboarding-completion gain from colloquial Hindi copy).
why_it_matters: Shows a PM that a monetization surface can actively suppress core engagement in markets unfamiliar with subscription billing, and that pulling the paywall entirely can be a measured, defensible growth lever rather than pure revenue sacrifice.

title: How Our Learners (That's You!) Help Improve Duolingo
summary: Duolingo describes how it blends qualitative UX research (surveys of hundreds/thousands of learners plus one-on-one interviews) with large-scale A/B testing to shape the product. One-on-one interviews surfaced learner frustration about repeatedly making the same grammar mistakes, which directly informed the design of the Practice Hub, a feature letting learners revisit past mistakes via targeted practice. The post also reports a small controlled experiment comparing a grammar-focused lesson format against the regular lesson format for teaching Spanish preterite tense, showing a much larger accuracy gain for the grammar-focused group.
what_they_built: The Practice Hub, a subscription feature giving learners targeted practice on past mistakes and specialized speaking/listening lessons, informed by interview-surfaced learner pain points.
evidence: In a controlled experiment on Spanish preterite tense, the grammar-focused lesson group improved accuracy from 36% to 86% (50-point gain) versus the regular-lesson control group's improvement from 40% to 54% (14-point gain).
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete pipeline from qualitative interview insight to a shipped feature, paired with a controlled experiment validating the underlying pedagogical mechanism before scaling it.

title: Lessons from Asia: Turning local research into global experiments
summary: Duolingo's dedicated Asia teams found that local user research kept surfacing problems that turned out to be global issues in disguise. In Japan, interviews showed learners avoided the word "test" out of anxiety, so the team renamed the placement test to "check your English level," which doubled test completion and improved retention when rolled out worldwide. In Vietnam, a 15% lower registration rate traced back to a global under-13 registration flow that converted at only 62% versus 90% for other users, a defect Vietnam's demographics made visible first. Rather than forking the app for regional customization, the team's operating principle was to convert localized insights into global experiments.
what_they_built: A localization-to-globalization product process where region-specific user research (interviews, funnel analysis in Japan and Vietnam) is used to design global A/B experiments — including rewording the placement-test entry point, repositioning/recontextualizing notification opt-in prompts, and fixing an under-13 registration flow.
evidence: Placement-test copy change ("take a placement test" to "check your English level") doubled placement tests taken and increased retention; Japanese learners were 50% less likely to accept notification prompts before the opt-in redesign; under-13 users had a 62% registration rate versus 90% for other users, a gap fixed globally after being spotted via Vietnam's user mix.
why_it_matters: Shows PMs a repeatable pattern for using underserved-market research as an early-warning signal to find global product defects, then validating fixes with experiments before shipping worldwide.

title: How machine learning supercharged our revenue by millions of dollars
summary: Duolingo's logic for choosing whether to show a learner a subscription-promotion ad ("in-house ad") or an external network ad had accumulated into a tangled, hard-to-optimize web of A/B-tested heuristic rules scattered across the codebase. The team replaced this with a centralized ML model built on existing dbt and BigQuery ML infrastructure, which decides ad type per learner and was later reframed as a contextual-bandit problem (identifying learners for whom in-house ads most increase purchase probability) rather than simple subscription-likelihood prediction. They also moved from dedicated holdout groups to random sampling for retraining. The system shipped fast by reusing existing data pipeline infrastructure and was approved by leadership after early results.
what_they_built: A centralized ML-based decision system (using dbt pipelines and BigQuery ML, later modeled as a contextual bandit) that determines per-learner whether to show an external ad network ad or an in-house subscription-promotion ad, replacing scattered rule-based heuristics.
evidence: Staged revenue outcomes reported: "millions of dollars in incremental annual revenue" in the first few months, growing to "tens of millions per year" after refinement, with the ads decision system now driving "roughly a quarter" of Duolingo's year-over-year revenue growth.
why_it_matters: Shows how reframing a monetization decision as a contextual-bandit ML problem, built on existing data infrastructure rather than new systems, can retire fragile rule-based heuristics and unlock outsized, compounding revenue with fast time-to-value.

title: "Hi, it's Duo": Meet the AI behind the meme
summary: Duolingo's practice-reminder notifications were a fixed set of messages sent on a schedule, with no way to tell which ones actually drove learners back into a lesson. The team built a multi-armed bandit system that treats each notification variant like a slot machine arm, exploring options and reinforcing the ones that lead to a completed lesson, personalized per learner and per language (e.g. "Time for [language]" worked well for Chinese learners but not English learners). They also added spacing based on the same forgetting-curve model used for word learning, since repeating the same notification too often lost effectiveness. The pipeline ran on AWS Kinesis Firehose and Apache Spark to process roughly 200 million practice reminders over 34 days, at tens of millions of records per week.</summary> <parameter name="what_they_built">A production multi-armed bandit system for selecting and timing lifecycle/practice-reminder notifications per learner, backed by a Kinesis Firehose + Spark data pipeline, with forgetting-curve-based repetition spacing to avoid notification fatigue.
what_they_built: Duolingo's Machine Learning Engineers built a bandit-algorithm system that personalizes daily push notifications/practice reminders for each learner, continuously learning from ~200 million reminders sent over 34 days which message drives a given user to complete a lesson, while using fairness comparisons across similar user segments and forgetting-curve-based spacing to avoid repeating recently-seen notifications; it runs on AWS Kinesis Firehose and Apache Spark processing tens of millions of records per week.
evidence: Analyzed ~200 million practice reminders sent over 34 days; within a few weeks of deploying the bandit algorithm "more learners were completing lessons more frequently," and it was "especially successful at helping tens of thousands of new learners return to their lessons."
why_it_matters: Shows how to turn a static lifecycle-messaging program into a self-optimizing, per-segment experiment system by defining the right "payout" metric (lesson completion, not just open/click) and building in fatigue controls.

title: Duolingo Path Meets Expectations for Proficiency Outcomes
summary: In 2022 Duolingo replaced its home-screen "tree" navigation (learners pick what to do next) with a linear "path" (learners follow a fixed sequence, with mandatory stories, spaced-repetition review, and interleaved difficulty levels). This 2024 research report re-ran the company's standing efficacy methodology to check whether that IA change actually improved learning outcomes, testing Spanish and French learners at the end of A2-level content with the STAMP 4S standardized proficiency test (ACTFL scale) and comparing time-to-completion against the earlier tree-design cohorts. Learners in the path reached Intermediate Mid (Spanish) and Intermediate Low (French) overall proficiency, and self-efficacy plus session accuracy predicted better outcomes. The path took significantly longer to complete than the tree (Spanish +58.79 hours, French +62.22 hours on average) but delivered better proficiency, which the authors treat as the intended trade-off given the added mandatory content.
what_they_built: Duolingo's 2022 home-screen redesign from a branching "tree" (free navigation) to a linear "path" (fixed sequence with obligatory stories, review lessons, and interleaved difficulty tiers), evaluated here via standardized proficiency testing.
evidence: Spanish path learners reached Intermediate Mid overall (reading Intermediate High, listening Intermediate Mid, writing Intermediate Low, speaking near Intermediate Low); French learners reached Intermediate Low overall. Regression analysis versus the prior tree-design efficacy studies (Jiang et al., 2020) showed path learners spent significantly more time to reach the same A2 content point (Spanish +58.79 hours, French +62.22 hours on average, ~200 hours total), and that extra time was associated with better proficiency outcomes.
why_it_matters: Shows a rare, disciplined pattern for PMs: re-running the same standardized outcome measurement before and after a major IA redesign, so a UX change can be judged on the actual behavioral/learning outcome it was meant to improve, not just engagement or completion-rate proxies.

title: Duolingo Q4 & FY 2023 Shareholder Letter
summary: Duolingo's Q4/FY2023 shareholder letter (filed as an SEC 8-K exhibit) lays out the company's subscription-tier strategy across its Free, Super, and Max plans. Management describes an ongoing plan to actively test tier names, pricing, features, and framing to optimize monetization, alongside a deliberate push to grow the family plan, which still represented only 18% of paid subscribers despite bookings growth. The letter reports the results of the quarter these tiers ran in: paid subscribers reached 6.6 million (+57% YoY), subscription bookings hit $157.8 million (+57% YoY), and family plan bookings grew over 100% YoY.
what_they_built: A three-tier subscription structure (Free, Super, Max) with an explicit, ongoing experimentation program on tier pricing, naming, and feature bundling, plus a targeted push to grow family-plan adoption.
evidence: Q4 2023: paid subscribers 6.6M (+57% YoY), subscription bookings $157.8M (+57% YoY), total bookings $191.0M (+51% YoY), revenue $151.0M (+45% YoY); FY2023 family plan bookings grew over 100% YoY but family plan subscribers remained only 18% of paid subscribers.
why_it_matters: Shows how a subscription product can treat tier design (naming, pricing, feature gating) as a continuously tested lever rather than a fixed decision, while tracking segment-level adoption gaps (family plan) as a distinct growth vector.

title: We hear you! How learner feedback helps improve course content
summary: Duolingo receives roughly 200,000 user reports a day on course content, 72% of them claiming "my answer should have been accepted." Because a single sentence can have thousands of valid translations, manually triaging this volume was infeasible, so the team built a Report Quality Estimation Tool that scores reports using signals like the reporting user, how many others flagged the same answer, and how different the answer is from known acceptable translations. The tool surfaces the highest-priority reports for course creators to review first, and the team found only about 15% of reviewed "should have been accepted" reports turn out to be valid additions.
what_they_built: An ML-based Report Quality Estimation Tool that scores and ranks the ~150,000 daily "my answer should have been accepted" user reports so course creators review the highest-likelihood-valid ones first.
evidence: Processes ~200,000 user reports daily (72% in the "answer should have been accepted" category); only ~15% of reviewed reports in that category are validated as correct and added as accepted translations, illustrating the scale of noise the scoring tool filters.
why_it_matters: Shows how a PM facing an unmanageable volume of low-signal user feedback can build a lightweight scoring model to prioritize triage rather than trying to review everything manually.

title: Improving the streak: Forming habits one lesson at a time
summary: Duolingo found that nearly 40% of active users with "intense" daily goals had no streak, because the streak mechanic required completing the full daily goal to extend it — punishing exactly the users trying hardest. The team decoupled the two mechanics so a streak now extends after completing just a single lesson, while daily-goal progress is tracked separately. An A/B test over 20 days showed the change increased habit formation without cannibalizing daily-goal completion, and a one-year follow-up showed the share of daily learners with 7+ day streaks roughly grew from a third to over half.
what_they_built: Decoupled the streak-extension requirement from the daily-goal-completion requirement, so a single completed lesson extends a user's streak independent of whether they hit their full daily goal.
evidence: A/B test over 20 days reported a 3.3% increase in Day 14 retention, 1% increase in DAU, 10.5% increase in streak adoption among daily learners (19% among new learners); one year later, the share of daily learners with 7+ day streaks rose from roughly a third to just over half.
why_it_matters: Shows how unbundling two conflated success metrics/mechanics in a product can remove an unintended barrier to habit formation, illustrated with a rigorous before/after experiment and durable long-term follow-up.

title: The Duolingo Streak Uses Habit Research to Keep You Motivated
summary: Duolingo's product team examined why the streak feature was such a strong retention driver, noting learners who hit a 7-day streak are 3.6x more likely to complete their course and over 6 million users maintain 7+ day streaks. They shipped two specific changes grounded in habit and goal-pursuit research: new milestone animations shown after extending a streak, and doubling the Streak Freeze allowance so learners can equip up to two freezes at once. Both changes were tested and measured against control, with the animations lifting new-learner 7-day retention and the added Freeze slack lifting daily active learner engagement rather than undermining habit formation as one might expect.
what_they_built: Streak milestone completion animations and a doubled Streak Freeze allowance (up to two freezes equippable at once) inside the Duolingo streak feature.
evidence: Milestone animations increased the likelihood a brand-new learner was still active 7 days later by +1.7%; doubling the Streak Freeze allowance increased daily active learner engagement by +0.38%; learners reaching a 7-day streak are 3.6x more likely to complete their course.
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete, measured example of how adding controlled slack (streak freezes) to a loss-aversion mechanic can increase rather than decrease engagement, a transferable lesson for any PM designing habit/streak features.

title: How Streaks keep Duolingo learners committed to their language goals
summary: A Duolingo Growth PM describes two A/B-tested features aimed at protecting learners' streaks, the app's core retention mechanic. Streak Wager let learners bet in-game currency that they'd keep their streak alive for 7 more days, doubling their stake on success, and drove statistically significant Day-1, Day-7, and Day-14 retention gains. Weekend Amulet let users equip an item to preserve their streak over a weekend they skipped, addressing a 5-10% weekend engagement drop, and counterintuitively increased both return rate and streak survival. The piece frames both as evidence that flexible, forgiving mechanics beat rigid all-or-nothing streak rules.
what_they_built: Two shippable, A/B-tested retention features tied to Duolingo's streak system: Streak Wager (bet in-game currency to protect a streak for 7 days, double reward on success) and Weekend Amulet (an equippable item that preserves a streak through an inactive weekend).
evidence: Streak Wager produced statistically significant increases in Day-1, Day-7, and Day-14 retention, with Day-7 showing the largest lift at +14%. Weekend Amulet users were 4% more likely to return a week later and 5% less likely to lose their streak.
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete, measured example of using loss-aversion and flexible commitment devices in a habit-forming product to lift retention without punishing lapses.

title: How we developed our addictive and delightful widget
summary: Duolingo's home-screen widget started as a hackathon project and took over a year to ship because the team had to solve two problems: what utility to show (they tested and landed on just streak length + whether the daily lesson was done, deliberately keeping it minimal) and how to drive installs (in-app promotion with an animated explainer made installs "skyrocket"). Once adoption grew, the team partnered with an in-house Design Studio to reskin the widget with mood-based Duo illustrations that grow more anxious as midnight approaches, to differentiate it from a plain notification. The team reports the widget correlates with materially better long-term retention and streak length.
what_they_built: A home-screen (and later lock-screen) widget showing streak length and daily-lesson completion status, iteratively redesigned with mood-based illustrations and backed by an in-app install-promotion workstream.
evidence: "Half of the learners with the widget installed have a streak of at least 6 months," and widget users showed "far better retention on Duolingo, even when controlling for the fact that learners who add the widget tend to be more committed to begin with"; in-app promotion testing caused install rates to "skyrocket" (no exact percentage given).
why_it_matters: Shows how deliberately limiting a surface's information (only streak + completion, nothing else) plus dedicated discovery/promotion workstreams can matter as much as the feature itself for driving adoption and retention.

title: How eBay Modernized the Most Important Page on Our Platform
summary: eBay's View Item page (250M daily views) hadn't been intentionally rewritten in a decade, forcing engineers to duplicate work across four legacy platform-specific services (desktop, mobile web, iOS, Android) and slowing feature delivery. The team built a new unified service inserted between the data layer and legacy system, then migrated modules one at a time so velocity gains accrued from day one rather than only at project completion. Two years in, with about 90% of modules migrated, they report a 200% velocity increase and 50% reduction in effort (days) for new product improvements, measured against the Accelerate/DORA framework. They also describe a concrete misstep: an early holistic module migration that bundled UX changes was A/B tested and showed a negative customer impact, costing extra time to pivot, which led them to separate back-end modernization from user-facing changes going forward.
what_they_built: A new unified data/service layer inserted between eBay's data layer and its legacy View Item page services, migrated module-by-module so all four platforms (desktop, mobile web, iOS, Android) consume the same modernized data instead of duplicating logic three times.
evidence: Reported 200% velocity increase on related new features from early in the project, a 50% reduction in effort (days) for new product improvements near project completion, elimination of 1/3 duplicated work, and improvement across all four Accelerate/DORA metrics to "elite" status; also reports a production A/B test that showed negative customer impact from a bundled UX change, requiring rework.
why_it_matters: Shows a PM/platform leader how to structure a multi-year re-architecture to deliver continuous, measurable velocity gains instead of a big-bang rewrite, and how separating backend modernization from user-facing A/B-tested changes reduces rollback risk.

title: Etsy, Inc. Form 10-K FY2019 — 2018 seller transaction fee increase (3.5% to 5%) results
summary: Etsy's FY2019 10-K MD&A discloses that on July 16, 2018 the company raised its seller transaction fee from 3.5% to 5% of each completed transaction and extended it to cover shipping costs, not just item cost. In the FY2019 filing (reporting on both 2019 and 2018 results), Etsy quantifies the effect: transaction revenue grew 48.9% year-over-year in 2019, with roughly 30 percentage points of that increase attributed to the 2018 pricing updates, and 59.6% growth in 2018 with about 41 points attributed to the same change. This gives a rare audited, multi-year view of how much of a marketplace's revenue growth a take-rate increase actually drove, net of any seller/buyer behavior response.
what_they_built: A seller transaction-fee increase from 3.5% to 5% of completed transaction value, broadened to apply to shipping costs as well as item costs, implemented July 16, 2018.
evidence: Transaction revenue increased 48.9% YoY in 2019 (with 2018 pricing updates driving ~30 points of that) and 59.6% YoY in 2018 (with pricing updates driving ~41 points of that), per Etsy's own MD&A disclosure.
why_it_matters: Shows a PM/marketplace operator how a take-rate change's revenue contribution can be isolated and tracked across multiple fiscal years in audited disclosures, useful as a benchmark for modeling pricing-change impact and persistence over time.

title: Imbalance detection for healthier experimentation
summary: Etsy runs many concurrent A/B tests, and misconfigured experiments can produce "imbalance" — treatment and control groups with dissimilar user populations — which makes it impossible to reliably attribute metric changes to the tested feature. Manually checking every experiment for this became unsustainable as experimentation volume grew, so Etsy built an automated detection system based on a permutation test for independence between user segment and assigned variant, using contingency tables and a discrepancy statistic (U) from prior statistical work. The system was engineered to scale to run automatically across hundreds of experiments daily and surface imbalance warnings early, before experimenters could draw invalid conclusions from tainted data.
what_they_built: An automated statistical guardrail system that detects "imbalance" in A/B tests — cases where treatment and control groups end up with dissimilar user populations on pre-treatment attributes (e.g. region), which would invalidate causal attribution of metric changes to the treatment. The system builds contingency tables of segment-by-variant counts for each experiment and runs a permutation-based independence test (using a discrepancy statistic U from published statistical literature) to flag statistically significant imbalance early in an experiment's life, before teams draw conclusions from tainted data.
evidence: System was built and scaled to run automated imbalance checks across "hundreds of experiments every day" at Etsy, functioning as an automated guardrail layer on top of their experimentation platform.
why_it_matters: As Etsy's experimentation volume grew, manual review of every experiment's setup became unsustainable, so teams needed automated protection against silently invalid A/B test results (e.g. from asymmetric triggering/logging between variants) that could otherwise lead product decisions astray.

title: Etsy, Inc. Form 10-K for Fiscal Year 2022
summary: Etsy's FY2022 10-K MD&A discloses that effective April 11, 2022 it raised its seller transaction fee from 5% to 6.5%, framing the increase against a stated "fair exchange of value" principle and stating most incremental revenue was reinvested into marketing, seller tools, and customer experience. The filing quantifies the outcome: marketplace revenue grew from $1.7B to $1.9B (9.5%) largely due to the fee increase, but this was partially offset by a $173.4 million decline in marketplace GMS volume year over year. Total GMS across marketplaces fell 1.3% to $13.3B for 2022. This is an audited, after-the-fact account of a take-rate change and its measured trade-off between price increase and volume erosion.
what_they_built: Etsy raised its core seller transaction (take-rate) fee from 5% to 6.5% of transaction value, effective April 11, 2022, and committed a stated portion of the incremental revenue to marketing, seller tools, and customer experience investment.
evidence: Marketplace revenue rose 9.5% year over year to $1.9 billion, driven primarily by the fee increase, but was partially offset by a $173.4 million decrease in marketplace GMS; total company GMS declined 1.3% to $13.3 billion for the year.
why_it_matters: Gives PMs a real, audited example of the revenue-vs-volume trade-off from a marketplace take-rate increase, with the company's own quantification of how much elasticity/volume erosion offset the price gain.

title: How Etsy Handles Peeking in A/B Testing
summary: Etsy's product and engineering teams wanted to peek at running A/B tests to speed up decisions and catch bugs early, but naive peeking against a fixed p-value threshold inflated false-positive rates (over 50% after 500 observations in simulation, versus a 10% target). Etsy built a sequential-testing stopping rule into its experimentation platform that computes a dynamic p-value threshold curve, allowing statistically safe early stopping, and added a haircut formula to correct effect-size overestimation plus UI changes exposing power and confidence intervals. Simulations showed the approach did not materially raise false-positive rates and cut typical 3-week experiment runtimes by at least a week when there was a real effect, particularly benefiting low-baseline, low-traffic experiments.
what_they_built: Etsy replaced fixed-horizon significance testing in its internal A/B testing tool with a sequential-testing stopping rule (based on Evan Miller's difference-in-converted-visits method) that produces a p-value threshold curve, letting teams stop experiments early once the threshold is crossed without inflating false positives. They paired this with a haircut formula to correct overestimated effect sizes from early stopping, a 7-day minimum runtime to smooth weekday/weekend effects, and UI changes surfacing power and confidence intervals to stakeholders to discourage naive peeking.
evidence: Simulations: without correction, naive peeking pushed false-positive rate to over 50% after 500 observations at a 0.1 significance level (vs. 10% with no peeking); the new sequential threshold, tuned for a 5% false-positive rate, did not materially increase false positives; for experiments that would take 3 weeks under standard power analysis, the method saved at least a week in most cases with a true effect.
why_it_matters: Gives PMs and platform teams a concrete pattern for balancing experimentation velocity against statistical rigor -- a common tension in any team running many concurrent product experiments, especially on low-traffic surfaces.

title: Improving performance by prefetching product pages from Etsy Search
summary: Etsy wanted to speed up navigation from search results to product listing pages without the risk of full prerendering. They implemented the Speculation Rules API to prefetch listing pages on a 200ms hover over search cards, ran it as an A/B experiment, and layered on custom handling for the browser's two-page memory cache limit, non-cacheable HTML, cookie-setting side effects, and analytics double-counting via the Sec-Purpose header. The rollout produced substantial page-performance gains and surfaced numerous edge cases (video hover targets, redirects, mutating hrefs) documented as lessons for other teams adopting SRA.
what_they_built: Etsy's search and web performance teams implemented the browser's Speculation Rules API (SRA) to prefetch listing pages when users hover over organic search result cards on desktop, using a CSS-selector-based JSON ruleset with moderate eagerness (200ms hover trigger) and a data attribute to opt in specific links, tested via an A/B experiment.
evidence: A/B experiment on desktop Chromium search results showed some performance metrics improved 20-24%, with some metrics dropping all the way to 0ms values.
why_it_matters: Demonstrates a practical, low-risk browser platform capability (prefetch vs. full prerender) that meaningfully speeds up page loads without large engineering investment, plus a rich set of production gotchas (caching limits, cookies, redirects, analytics double-counting) that any team adopting SRA-based prefetching needs to plan around.

title: A/B Testing and the Cloak of Invisibility: A Better Way to Scope Variant Code in Your React App
summary: An Expedia engineer describes how her team's A/B test variant code kept sprawling across selectors, components, and event handlers, making tests hard to build and clean up. The team built an AbTestContainer wrapper component that centralizes test-bucket branching in a parent component and automatically logs exposure on mount, replacing scattered conditional logic. After adopting the pattern, the author reports faster and easier test builds, less stray code, and higher team satisfaction. The post also sketches a possible future extension for multi-test support with inline prop overrides, noting the tradeoff in added complexity.
what_they_built: AbTestContainer, a reusable React wrapper component that conditionally renders variant children based on a user's test bucket and auto-logs test exposure on mount.
evidence: Qualitative team-reported outcome: "the speed and ease of building new tests has improved and overall satisfaction with it is high," with cited benefits of quicker two-step test implementation, less stray code, automatic exposure logging, and easier cleanup — no numeric metrics given.
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete internal-platform pattern for containing experiment code sprawl that any team running client-side A/B tests in a component framework can adapt.

title: Expedia Group shares Backstage proof of value metrics 2.0
publisher: Backstage blog (authored by Expedia Group engineer Guillermo Manzo)
summary: Expedia Group adopted Backstage in August 2020 to cut developer friction across a platform serving 168 million loyalty members and thousands of travel partners. This post, an update to their 2021 proof-of-value writeup, reports second-generation adoption metrics: an engineer onboarding bootcamp ("Ship on Day One") with 850+ graduates and ~95% first-attempt success, thousands of components onboarded, and daily active usage and documentation-reach figures for the platform two-plus years in. It frames these numbers explicitly as evidence the internal developer platform investment paid off, not just as launch-announcement claims.
what_they_built: An internal developer platform on Backstage, including a "Ship on Day One" onboarding bootcamp, a component catalog for service/app creation, and TechDocs-based documentation for services.
evidence: 850+ engineers completed the onboarding bootcamp (~95% first-attempt success); 7,000+ components created or onboarded with a 4-minute average creation/onboarding time; 4,000+ unique users spending ~20 minutes/day in Backstage; 500+ services tied to TechDocs generating 50,000+ average monthly doc views; 15%+ of internal developer tools integrated into Backstage; 10+ open-source contributions back to Backstage.
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete, multi-year metrics framework (onboarding completion, component throughput, daily active usage, doc reach) a platform PM can reuse to prove an internal developer platform's ongoing value beyond its initial launch.

title: How Expedia Group Platform Engineering Revamped Their Compute Platform
summary: Expedia Group's brands had grown up on fragmented infrastructure — some on Amazon ECS, some on Kubernetes, some with no containerization at all — creating inconsistent developer experience and duplicated operational overhead. Platform Engineering built a unified, self-service "paved road" compute platform on Kubernetes, identifying 10k+ applications to migrate onto it (with 3,000+ needing decommissioning, refactoring, or containerization first) across 500+ clusters and 40,000+ nodes spanning four regions. The build addressed seven core components — zero-trust auth via Istio mTLS, full-stack observability, layered security, chaos-tested resilience with Karpenter autoscaling, multi-tenancy isolation, GitOps-based lifecycle management via Flux and Kubefed V2, and FinOps cost controls. The team reports the platform fostered innovation, cut time to production, standardized workflows, and reduced cloud costs.
what_they_built: A centralized, self-service Kubernetes-based compute platform (500+ clusters, 40k+ nodes across 4 regions) replacing per-brand fragmented infrastructure, built around a "paved road" managed-platform model plus a shift-left CI/CD testing approach.
evidence: Quantified scale and adoption: 10,000+ applications identified for migration, 3,000+ requiring rework, 500+ Kubernetes clusters, 40,000+ nodes, across 4 regions and 35+ functional domains; qualitative outcomes reported as fostered innovation, reduced time to production, standardized workflow, and reduced cloud costs.
why_it_matters: Shows how a large multi-brand org sequences an internal platform migration (paved-road self-service plus shift-left testing) to hit adoption at 10k+ app scale without a big-bang cutover.

title: Interleaving for Accelerated Testing
summary: Expedia's ranking teams found that detecting a small (0.3% relative) conversion-rate uplift via standard A/B testing required roughly 92 million users across variants, spanning several months, which made iterating on ranking-system changes far too slow. They adopted interleaving — blending recommendations from different ranking variants into a single set of results shown to users and comparing which variant's items users prefer — as a fast, within-subject accelerated-testing stage that sits between offline backtesting and full A/B tests. The team built a three-stage experimentation funnel (backtest, then interleave, then A/B test top performers) tracking clicks (property detail page views) and bookings to compute a relative preference "lift metric." In a concrete comparison, interleaving flagged a degrading ranking treatment within days while a parallel A/B test failed to detect the same degradation even at full sample size.
what_they_built: A three-stage ranking-experimentation funnel at Expedia Group — offline backtesting, then interleaving tests that blend ranking variants into shared result sets, then A/B tests for the surviving top performers.
evidence: Reported sensitivity increases of 10x to 100x versus traditional A/B testing; a power analysis showing ~92 million users needed to detect a 0.3% relative conversion lift via A/B testing; and a head-to-head example where interleaving detected a degrading ranking treatment within days while A/B testing missed the same degradation even at full sample size.
why_it_matters: Shows PMs facing slow, underpowered A/B tests on ranking/recommendation surfaces a concrete alternative experimentation stage that can catch regressions in days instead of months.

title: How to Size For Online Experiments With Ratio Metrics
summary: Expedia's experimentation team faced the problem that standard sample-size formulas break down for ratio metrics (e.g. clicks per visit) where the randomization unit and the analysis unit are misaligned, violating the independence assumption behind conventional power calculations. They built a Python sample-size calculator that uses the multivariate delta method (per Deng, Knoblich and Lu 2018) to approximate the sampling distribution and correctly derive variance for such ratio metrics. They validated the approach with a 1,000-iteration simulation at a nominal 80% power and 5% significance level, and compared p-value distributions with and without synthetic treatment effects added.
what_they_built: A Python-based sample-size calculator for online experiments that applies the delta method to correctly size tests on ratio metrics where analysis-unit and randomization-unit differ.
evidence: Simulation testing across 1,000 iterations against an 80% nominal power target found the test results were significant 83.4% of the time, indicating the delta-method-based sizing approach closely tracked its nominal statistical guarantees.
why_it_matters: Shows PMs and data scientists running ratio-metric experiments (common in engagement/conversion metrics) why naive sample-size formulas can mis-power a test, and gives a concrete, validated fix.

title: Preventing Revenue Loss With Real-Time A/B Test Monitoring
summary: Expedia's experimentation platform previously relied on batch analysis, leaving a blind spot in the first 24 hours after a test launched where a broken variant could run unchecked and destroy revenue. The team built EGTnL Circuit Breaker, a real-time monitoring pipeline on Apache Flink that ingests exposure, clickstream, and bot-classification Kafka streams, tracks per-user state via a custom KeyedProcessFunction, and aggregates metrics across a two-window (partial then repartitioned) scheme to flag or halt bad tests within minutes. Over its first six months in production it monitored 95% of A/B tests and 21% of release changes, auto-detected 36% of experiment issues within 24 hours, and once halted a test showing a -39% conversion impact within minutes. The post also candidly lists limitations: a 5-minute result-delay from fixed-interval recomputation, and scaling ceilings during aggregation-window bursts.
what_they_built: A real-time Flink-based streaming pipeline ("EGTnL Circuit Breaker") that monitors live A/B tests against exposure and clickstream data and can automatically halt underperforming experiments within minutes of launch.
evidence: In its first six months: monitored 95% of all A/B tests and 21% of release changes, auto-detected 36% of experiment-related issues within 24 hours, and halted one test showing a -39% conversion impact within minutes; team also reports it "saved thousands of testing days" by accelerating misconfigured-experiment detection.
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete pattern for closing the pre-statistical-significance blind spot in experimentation platforms — real-time guardrails that catch catastrophic test regressions long before a batch readout would, which any PM running an experimentation program should consider building or demanding.

title: How Figma's data science and user research teams weave together insights that count
summary: Figma's activity team didn't know where in the notification funnel to focus improvement efforts, so a data scientist (Caitlin Hudon) and a researcher (Jennifer Sanders) ran parallel quantitative and qualitative investigations, syncing via a shared Slack channel, 30-minute tactical work sessions, and 60-minute synthesis meetings. Cross-referencing their findings revealed the real problem was upstream of what anyone assumed: most personal-account users weren't receiving notifications at all, while designers on team accounts were opening 80% of comment emails, meaning the two user segments needed fundamentally different treatment. The synthesis also explained why the notification bell was ignored (users lived in email and rarely visited the file browser), leading to the bell's relocation and a shipped feature extending comment notifications to file editors, not just file owners.
what_they_built: A structured cross-functional research process pairing one data scientist and one researcher with dedicated sync rituals (shared Slack channel, 30-min tactical sessions, 60-min synthesis sessions) to jointly investigate and improve Figma's notification funnel.
evidence: Quantified findings: most personal-account users received zero notifications; team-account designers opened 80% of comment notification emails; a companion figure cited elsewhere in the piece states 71% of users don't receive any notifications. Concrete shipped output: an experiment sending comment notifications to file editors (not just owners) was run and released, and the notification bell was moved from the top-right to a more prominent upper-left position.
why_it_matters: Shows a PM-transferable pattern for research operating models: pairing quant and qual investigators with tight, scheduled synthesis rituals surfaces root causes (a segmentation problem, not an engagement problem) that neither method alone would catch.

title: Figma S-1: What Drove Net Dollar Retention — Seat Expansion, Plan Upgrades, and New Product Launches (FigJam, Enterprise Plan, Dev Mode)
summary: Figma's S-1 registration statement discloses the specific product and pricing mechanisms behind changes in its Net Dollar Retention Rate (NDR) from 2022 through Q1 2025. The company attributes NDR movement to three combined levers — seat expansion, plan upgrades, and new product adoption — and walks through how the 2022 launch of FigJam plus a new Enterprise plan lifted NDR, how NDR cooled in 2023 as those launches lapped their one-year mark amid macro-driven seat contraction, and how the 2024 launch of Dev Mode drove a subsequent expansion. It reports exact NDR figures at three period-ends (122% end of 2023, 134% end of 2024, 132% as of March 31, 2025) tied to a defined methodology (Current Period ARR / Previous Period ARR, restricted to Paid Customers with over $10,000 in ARR).
what_they_built: A seat-based SaaS pricing and packaging model (per-seat pricing, an Enterprise plan tier, and sequential product launches — FigJam, then Dev Mode) used deliberately as retention/expansion levers, formally tracked via a defined Net Dollar Retention Rate metric.
evidence: NDR was 122% (Dec 2023), 134% (Dec 2024), and 132% (Mar 2025); the filing explicitly ties the 2022 NDR benefit to FigJam and the Enterprise plan launch, the 2023 dip to lapping those launches plus macro-driven seat contraction, and the 2024 expansion to the Dev Mode launch.
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete, quantified example of how a PM/monetization team can decompose retention-metric movement into seat, pricing-tier, and new-product drivers rather than treating NDR as a single opaque number.

title: Behind Our Redesign: The Design Process for Figma's New UI (UI3)
summary: Figma's product design team set out to redesign its core UI (UI3) to solve four problems: decluttering the canvas so work took visual priority, balancing newcomer accessibility with power-user efficiency, adapting to how design practice had evolved (components, AI), and building a foundation for future products. They explored hundreds of approaches — including radically hiding UI until hovered — but found some, like disappearing sidebars, made the workspace feel too unstable and were dropped. On the Auto Layout panel specifically, they tried condensing the alignment grid for simplicity, but it moved around too much in testing and broke power users' muscle memory, so they reverted to the original grid layout while just updating its visual style. A similar muscle-memory failure showed up when they tried inverting the traditional x/y/width/height field order, forcing them to keep familiar positioning while unifying the controls differently.
what_they_built: A ground-up redesign of Figma's core product UI (UI3), including a new resizable-panel canvas layout, a redesigned Auto Layout panel, and roughly 200 custom hand-drawn icons with optional text labels for newcomers.
evidence: Qualitative failure modes surfaced through iteration/testing rather than metrics: disappearing sidebars made the workspace feel unstable and were scrapped; a condensed alignment grid in the Auto Layout panel "moved around too much" and broke power-user muscle memory, forcing a revert to the original grid with only visual restyling; inverting the x/y/width/height field hierarchy similarly "disrupted muscle memory too much" and had to be walked back to familiar positioning.
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete, named case where a design team tested a simplification, measured it against power-user muscle memory rather than novice-only usability, and reverted based on that specific failure mode — a reusable pattern for PMs deciding when to preserve familiar interaction patterns versus pursue a cleaner redesign.

title: Financial Times Data Platform: From zero to hero
summary: The Financial Times traces five generations of its internal Data Platform from 2008 to 2020, built to give BI and data science teams self-service access to reader interaction data instead of relying on the platform team for every change. Early batch-based systems gave way to an event-driven pipeline (tracking library plus SNS/SQS/Kinesis) that cut data-lake latency to 1-5 minutes, though a later deduplication step added back roughly 4 hours of latency to the warehouse. By 2019-2020 the platform moved to a PaaS model emphasizing "configuration over implementation" so stakeholder teams could build features independently, and adopted Kafka, Spark streaming, Delta Lake, and Presto as a virtualization layer.
what_they_built: A multi-generation internal data platform at FT evolving from batch ETL jobs to an event-driven ingestion pipeline (tracking library, API service, SNS/SQS/Kinesis) and eventually a Kafka/Spark/Delta Lake/Presto-based self-service PaaS for BI and data science teams.
evidence: Concrete before/after latency figures: switching to the event-driven pipeline reduced data-lake latency to 1-5 minutes; adding a deduplication step in a separate Redshift cluster later increased data-warehouse latency to approximately 4 hours, an explicit trade-off the team documented.
why_it_matters: Shows a PM/platform team how an internal data platform's architecture decisions trade off directly against latency and team autonomy, and how "configuration over implementation" can be used to scale self-service without growing the platform team.

title: Enhancing Flipkart's Beauty Category — UX Research Case Study
summary: Flipkart's design team wanted to understand why the beauty category's online experience wasn't matching how people actually shop for beauty products offline. A cross-functional team (researcher, designer, PM) first ran mall intercept observations in Bangalore, then conducted in-depth interviews with 16 participants across four cities (Bangalore, Kolkata, Pune, Jaipur), including shop-along tasks tracing full purchase journeys. The research surfaced non-linear buying journeys, heavy reliance on YouTube/Instagram for reviews, strong brand loyalty, and offers/ratings as key decision drivers, which informed page redesign changes. Post-launch, the team measured increased engagement with the redesigned widgets.
what_they_built: A redesigned Flipkart beauty category product page (details widget and recommendation widget) informed by a two-stage qualitative field research study.
evidence: Details widget usage increased by 20%; recommendation widget conversion (units/clicks/visits) increased by over 3%; overall page interaction with different widgets increased.
why_it_matters: Shows how pairing offline retail-behavior observation with in-depth interviews can directly redirect an e-commerce category redesign and produce measurable engagement lift, not just qualitative insight.

title: Beyond the Data Horizon: Flipkart's Race to Cloud at Unmatched Scale
summary: Flipkart needed to migrate its internal Big Data Platform (FDP), handling 10,000+ pipelines and up to 1PB/day of ingestion during Big Billion Days sales, from its private/legacy stack to GCP in just six months with zero downtime — far faster than the multi-year phased migrations done by Netflix, Spotify, or Twitter. The team built shadow pipelines and a validation framework to certify parity before cutover, organized execution into accountable PODs (schema, processing, reporting, governance, billing), and tracked hidden metrics like data hygiene scores and shadow-pipeline parity percentages. The article recounts a production incident days before Big Billion Days where a 0.5% mismatch from a decimal-rounding bug was caught, hotfixed, and backfilled (30TB in 8 hours) without rollback, avoiding blind pricing dashboards during the sale.
what_they_built: An internal migration program for Flipkart's Big Data Platform (FDP) from on-prem/private cloud to GCP, using shadow-mode pipeline duplication, automated validation frameworks, and cross-functional POD-based execution across 100+ teams.
evidence: Completed migration of 10,000+ pipelines in 6 months with zero downtime and >99.5% post-migration data validation accuracy across tables, pipelines, and reports; caught a 0.5% pipeline mismatch pre-Big Billion Days, hotfixed and backfilled 30TB of data in 8 hours; identified a 7x BigQuery slot cost spike during dry runs and redesigned partition strategy before production.
why_it_matters: Shows how shadow-mode validation, hidden metrics (parity %, hygiene scores, simulated billing), and accountable POD structures let a platform team compress a typically multi-year cloud migration into six months without service disruption.

title: Unifying the Gates: Building Flipkart's Centralized API Gateway for 10x Scale
summary: Flipkart's engineering teams had historically managed API gateway functions (auth, rate-limiting, session management) independently per team, causing duplicated work, inconsistent developer experience, and poor observability that raised mean time to diagnose (MTTD) incidents. The team built Nexus, a centralized API gateway on top of open-source APISIX (chosen over Nginx and Kong after evaluation), structured as a three-plane architecture (management, control, data) with a self-serve developer portal and API catalog. As a concrete adoption case, the MART org (Marketplace and Retail Technology) consolidated its Seller Dashboard and Mobile App Interface channels onto Nexus, letting the team begin deprecating legacy systems like Harmony and various Node servers.
what_they_built: Nexus, a centralized, self-serve API gateway platform built on open-source APISIX with a management/control/data three-plane architecture, deployed across multiple data centers with cross-DC replication and active-active/active-passive failover.
evidence: Qualitative: enabled deprecation of legacy systems (Harmony, various Node servers) and reduced infrastructure/management overhead for the MART org after consolidating Seller Dashboard and Mobile App Interface gateway logic onto Nexus. Design targets stated (not reported as achieved results): 99.99% availability, p99 response-time overhead under 10ms, and millisecond-level dynamic config propagation via etcd.
why_it_matters: Shows how a platform team builds internal buy-in for a centralized gateway by proving value through one org's migration before a phased, non-critical-first rollout — a pattern any PM driving internal platform adoption can reuse.

title: Transforming Data Analytics at Flipkart: Self Serve Insights on Petabytes scale data
summary: Flipkart ingests close to a petabyte of data daily across tens of petabytes processed and tens of thousands of tables, which was slowing decisions, fragmenting metric ownership (a metric like GMV could live in hundreds of tables with inconsistent logic), and making onboarding new businesses an engineering-heavy effort. The team built Plato, an internal analytics platform with a semantic modeling layer that groups thousands of tables into a handful of owned domains (Sales, Fulfillment, Traffic, etc.) each exposing standardized, clearly-defined metrics and dimensions. Behind the scenes, Plato transparently redirects user queries to auto-generated pre-computed cubes chosen by an automatic cubing engine, and routes processing across real-time, mini-batch, or batch pipelines depending on SLA and cost. The platform also adds guardrails (access control, query limits, sample-data guidance, cost controls) and is now being extended with a GenAI copilot (Data Copilot) for natural-language querying on top of the standardized semantic layer.
what_they_built: Plato, an internal self-serve BI platform built around a semantic layer with domain-level metric ownership, a query-redirection engine that routes queries to auto-generated pre-computed cubes, and SLA-aware processing/storage tiering (Flink/Spark, Pulsar/Iceberg).
evidence: Quantifies the scale problem being solved (~1PB ingested daily, tens of PB processed daily, tens of thousands of tables, hundreds of data engineers/analysts and thousands of business users) and describes qualitative before/after: metric fragmentation (GMV existing in hundreds of tables with different caveats) replaced by domain-owned standardized metrics, and manual query optimization replaced by an automatic cubing/query-redirection engine. No adoption or performance numbers post-launch are given in the retrieved text.
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete pattern — a semantic layer plus domain ownership plus an automated query-redirection/cubing engine — for solving metric fragmentation and self-serve BI agility at large scale, transferable to any org wrestling with proliferating tables and inconsistent metric definitions.

title: Redesign of our Referral Experience (Shopsy)
summary: Shopsy's referral program was contributing nearly 10% of overall negative app sentiment, driven by unclear program mechanics, delayed payouts, and no way to track referral status. Flipkart Design mined support tickets and Play Store reviews to diagnose the problems, then redesigned the flow: simplified top-banner messaging, a consolidated reward widget, a three-step "How it Works" explainer, an accordion FAQ, WhatsApp-first sharing (since 75% of shares already happened there), referral status tracking, and an outstanding-balance nudge to collect bank details. The redesign shipped and was measured against pre-launch baselines.
what_they_built: A redesigned Shopsy referral flow with WhatsApp-first sharing, a status-tracking feature, simplified copy/imagery, and a bank-detail collection nudge for pending payouts.
evidence: Negative sentiment attributable to referrals dropped from 9% to 2.4%; acquisition numbers roughly doubled (2X) within one month of launch; referrer-to-referee ratio increased 2X.
why_it_matters: Shows how mining support tickets and app-store reviews to diagnose a growth-loop's UX friction, then redesigning around the channel users already use (WhatsApp), can move both acquisition and sentiment metrics together.

title: Flipkart Goes Multilingual for Bharat: How we are breaking language barriers to expand access to e-commerce
summary: Flipkart found that new 'Bharat' users from Tier II+ cities struggled with an English-only interface, undermining their confidence to shop independently. Ethnographic research across Coimbatore, Mysore, Visakhapatnam, and Salem revealed sharply different regional preferences: North Indian users wanted partial Hindi-English mixing while South Indian users wanted much higher translation coverage, and even the three southern languages disagreed on whether to translate or transliterate terms like color names. In response, over 20 teams built a scalable Vernacular Platform covering language detection (including code-mixed Hinglish text), a centralized localization string system, an in-house deep-learning transliteration model, and an in-house neural machine translation model, rolling out Hindi first and then Tamil, Telugu, and Kannada.
what_they_built: An in-house Vernacular Platform with language-detection ML (including code-mixing detection), a centralized localization service, and deep-learning transliteration and neural machine translation models to support multiple Indic languages across the Flipkart app.
evidence: Qualitative outcome: launched Hindi first with strong user response, then expanded to Tamil, Telugu, and Kannada; cites a KPMG projection that 536 million (~73%) of India's 735 million projected 2021 internet users will be vernacular users, framing the scale of the addressable problem rather than reporting post-launch metrics.
why_it_matters: Shows that localization at scale is a UX-research problem as much as an engineering one — regional and even within-language-family preferences on translation vs transliteration can't be solved with a single one-size-fits-all model.

title: Redesigning the Virtual Cards Experience
summary: Barter (Flutterwave's fintech app) let users create virtual dollar cards for online spending, but usability testing surfaced four friction points: new users didn't understand how to create dollar cards, card creation was confusing, cards were hard to locate, and card details/fund/withdraw actions were hidden behind a card-flip interaction. The design team shipped a first iteration with a swipeable carousel exposing withdraw, fund, and view-details actions at the bottom of each card. After launch, feedback revealed a power-user segment (social media managers and marketers managing many client cards) who struggled scrolling through a long card list, prompting a second iteration to a grid layout that made card creation and differentiation easier without heavy scrolling.
what_they_built: A redesigned virtual-card management flow for Barter: a swipeable carousel card UI with exposed fund/withdraw/details actions, later replaced by a grid layout to serve power users managing many cards.
evidence: Qualitative production outcomes reported by the team: the rate of card deletion "reduced massively" since users no longer needed to delete cards to withdraw funds, and the value of dollars funded into cards increased because the funding action was no longer hard to access. No specific percentage figures given.
why_it_matters: Shows how iterating past the first shipped fix based on real usage data (discovering a hidden power-user segment) can matter as much as the initial usability-testing-driven redesign.

title: The Async Shift: Scaling Our Data Service Without Scaling Our Costs
summary: Freshworks' customer-facing analytics data service was hitting hard limits under synchronous query processing: warehouse credit costs scaled linearly with load and latency spiked under peak demand. The team re-architected the service to process queries asynchronously via a queue instead of executing them synchronously against the warehouse, smoothing load distribution rather than scaling infrastructure proportionally. The change cut average query execution time from 23-30 seconds down to 2-4 seconds and roughly halved warehouse credit consumption per query, while the service now handles over 44 million queries weekly (about 4,300/minute).
what_they_built: An asynchronous query processing and queuing layer for Freshworks' internal analytics/data warehouse service, replacing synchronous query execution.
evidence: Query execution time dropped from 23-30 seconds (sync) to 2-4 seconds (async); async queries consume less than half the warehouse credits of synchronous ones; the service processes over 44 million queries weekly (~4,300/minute, ~73/second) without proportional infrastructure scaling; sequential/sync processing showed load-time spikes exceeding 25 seconds versus consistently lower queue/load/running times under async.
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete pattern for decoupling cost/latency from user demand growth in a data-heavy product surface by changing execution model rather than just adding infrastructure.

title: A faster FT.com
summary: The FT wanted to understand whether page load speed actually caused changes in reader engagement, rather than just correlating with it, so its product/engineering team ran a controlled experiment injecting artificial delays of 1, 2, 3, and 5 seconds into page loads for randomized subscriber segments via blocking CSS. They measured articles read per user over 7 and 28 days across device types and engagement levels, and translated the engagement drop into an estimated revenue impact from subscription renewals and ad exposure. The results showed measurable, compounding harm from even small delays, with sharper effects on tablet users and infrequent visitors.
what_they_built: A controlled A/B experiment on FT.com that artificially injected 1s, 2s, 3s, and 5s page-load delays (via blocking CSS) into randomized user segments to causally measure the effect of speed on reading behavior.
evidence: Over the first 7 days, a 1-second delay produced a 4.9% drop in articles read and a 3-second delay produced a 7.2% drop; by 28 days these grew to 5% and 7.9% respectively. Tablet users saw an 18.8% drop in articles read at just a 1-second delay. The team estimated the resulting revenue impact at "hundreds of thousands of pounds" short-term and "millions" long-term across subscription renewals and advertising.
why_it_matters: Shows PMs a concrete, replicable method for proving a causal (not just correlational) link between a UX/performance metric and business outcomes by deliberately degrading the experience rather than only measuring existing variance.

title: The Case for Accessibility
summary: FT wanted to bake automated accessibility checks into its build pipeline without stalling development across many teams' apps. They integrated Pa11y (via pa11y-ci) into CI, using a `.pa11yci.js` config per project to test URLs against WCAG2AA on every build and branch. Rollout was phased: first "silently," reporting errors without blocking builds, then removing the threshold flag so new violations broke the build and blocked merges to master. By the time of writing, about 60% of FT's apps had moved to build-breaking enforcement, with the rest scheduled to follow by October 31st.
what_they_built: A CI-integrated automated accessibility testing pipeline using Pa11y/pa11y-ci, run against WCAG2AA on every build across all branches, with a phased silent-then-enforcing rollout per app.
evidence: Concrete adoption metric: roughly 60% of FT's apps no longer used the silent/non-blocking (-T) option and instead enforced build-breaking accessibility checks, with the remaining apps scheduled to switch over before October 31st; the team also notes the tool actively blocked a real error in CI.
why_it_matters: Shows a practical playbook for rolling out a new quality gate (accessibility, but generalizable to any CI check) across many teams without a big-bang cutover: monitor silently first, then flip to enforcement team-by-team with a tracked adoption percentage.

title: How the FT Puts 1 Million Readers at the Heart of Product Development and the Business
summary: Having crossed 1 million paying subscribers (about 75% digital-only), the FT needed a systematic way to keep product decisions grounded in reader experience rather than advertiser-era audience metrics. The team built a Quality Experience (QX) score that pulls feedback from eight touchpoints across the customer journey (trial end, site/app visits, customer service queries, cancellation, and annual influencer/corporate-license surveys) and weights themes by recency, volume, and source context into a 0-100 index tracked over time. They paired this with a process mandate requiring FT.com and app product managers to hold at least two direct reader conversations per quarter, and extended research participation to 60+ senior leaders including the editor and board members.
what_they_built: A proprietary Quality Experience (QX) scoring system that aggregates and weights feedback from eight customer touchpoints into a tracked 0-100 index, combined with a quarterly reader-conversation quota for product managers.
evidence: 90% of FT.com and app product managers met the quarterly quota of at least two direct reader conversations; the QX score surfaced and drove fixes to podcast player functionality, data privacy messaging, and login experience; over 60 senior leaders including the editor and board members took part in customer research interviews.
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete pattern for turning scattered qualitative feedback into a single weighted, trackable metric that can be tied to team-level accountability (quotas) rather than left as ad hoc research.

title: How Sequential Testing Accelerates Our Experimentation Velocity
summary: GetYourGuide's experimentation program suffered from long-running fixed-sample A/B tests, most of which fail, wasting runtime and delaying product decisions. Senior Data Analyst Konrad Richter built BaseMATH, a sequential testing method modeling experiment results as a Brownian-motion random walk with a single futility boundary, simpler to implement than traditional Group Sequential Tests. They rolled it out in four stages: a pilot with a small stakeholder group on Databricks, a rebuild of the experimentation analysis platform in Python, a company-wide presentation of the methodology, and ongoing support for experimenters. The result was a 36% average reduction in experiment runtime at the cost of up to a 6% larger maximum sample size, with a calculated 21% experiment success rate needed to break even on the tradeoff.
what_they_built: BaseMATH, an in-house sequential-testing methodology and rebuilt Python-based experimentation analysis platform that lets GetYourGuide stop unpromising A/B tests early using a single futility boundary.
evidence: 36% average reduction in experiment runtime versus fixed-sample testing, at a roughly 6% maximum sample-size cost, with a 21% break-even experiment success rate calculated for the tradeoff.
why_it_matters: Shows a PM-relevant way to cut experimentation cycle time by killing bad tests early rather than only optimizing for stopping winners, directly increasing how many ideas a team can test per quarter.

title: Ghost's Pricing Transformation: $0 to $350,000/year (2014 Report)
summary: Ghost launched its hosted platform in 2013 with a base plan of $5/month scaling to $80/month, and found it was too low to build a viable business — competitors at similar $2.99-$5.99 price points had entered and exited the market. In their 2014 annual report, Ghost describes restructuring pricing to a $10/month base plan up to $250/month for business users, but first validated the change with a 33-day A/B test before rolling it out broadly. The test showed the higher prices actually increased conversion rate, not just revenue per customer. By year-end they reported $29,177 MRR, $350,124 ARR, 3,675 paying subscribers, and had reached profitability with a six-person team.
what_they_built: A restructured hosted-Ghost pricing ladder ($10-$250/month replacing $5-$80/month), rolled out only after a 33-day A/B test validated the change against conversion and revenue metrics.
evidence: 33-day A/B test showed increased conversion rate at the higher prices; resulting outcomes were roughly 2x revenue and more paying customers than before, with year-end 2014 figures of $29,177 MRR, $350,124 ARR, 3,675 subscribers, 4.87% churn, and profitability reached in December 2014.
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete, tested pattern for de-risking a price increase — validate the hypothesis that "underpricing" is actually suppressing revenue via a controlled A/B test before committing to a full rollout.

title: It's our birthday! Three years down, $600,000 annual revenue, and what's coming next
summary: Three years in, Ghost's founder publishes the company's open financial dashboard and explains a strategic repositioning. The team observed that the "personal blogging" market they originally targeted had largely dried up, while demand from professional publishers and content-driven companies (like Buffer and Intercom) grew alongside infrastructure load rising 10x in a year. In response, Ghost raised prices to reflect a professional-publisher positioning and began building subscriber management, audience-growth, and content-analytics features, while grandfathering existing users on old pricing.
what_they_built: A repositioning of Ghost(Pro) from a personal-blogging product to a platform for professional publishers, paired with a price increase (existing subscribers grandfathered) and a roadmap of subscriber/audience/analytics features.
evidence: ARR $600,456, MRR $50,038, ARPU $10.59, churn 4.9%, 4,705 Ghost(Pro) subscribers, and infrastructure requests growing from 10M/month to 100M/month year-over-year, cited as the basis for the pricing and positioning change.
why_it_matters: Shows a PM-relevant pattern of using open revenue/usage metrics to justify a market repositioning and price increase while managing existing-customer trust via grandfathering.

title: Building GitHub's next chapter in accessibility
summary: GitHub describes how it evolved its accessibility program from an internal-only focus to a community-facing effort. The team shipped concrete product changes — a redesigned pull request files-changed page with keyboard navigation, adjustable contrast controls across themes, screen-reader support in the GitHub CLI, and an open-sourced Figma accessibility annotation toolkit — while also changing how they triage and analyze issues, using AI-powered feedback analysis to speed up resolution. They report a 62% cut in resolution time and that 89% of accessibility issues now close within 90 days, alongside community-facing initiatives like an Accessibility Summit and an Enterprise Accessibility Advisory Panel.
what_they_built: Shipped accessibility features across GitHub's core product and CLI (keyboard-nav PR diff view, contrast controls, CLI screen-reader support), open-sourced a Figma annotation toolkit, and introduced AI-powered analysis to speed up accessibility issue triage and resolution.
evidence: 62% reduction in issue resolution time via AI-powered feedback analysis; 89% of issues now close within 90 days; 48% of accessibility issues could have been prevented during the design phase; Accessibility Summit drew 300+ registrants and 500+ waitlist entries.
why_it_matters: Shows how pairing product-level accessibility fixes with process changes (AI-assisted triage, design-phase prevention tracking) can materially cut resolution time, a pattern any PM running a quality or compliance backlog can borrow.

title: Making GitHub's CI Workflow 3x Faster
summary: GitHub's engineering fundamentals team tracked Lead Time for Changes (one of DORA's Four Key Metrics) and found developers waited an average of 45 minutes for CI to finish before they could merge a change. To fix this without weakening quality gates, they built a "deferred compliance" mechanism that lets long-running, non-blocking checks run after merge instead of before it: if a deferred CI job fails, a GitHub issue labeled deferred compliance is filed and the team gets a 72-hour window to fix it before deployments are halted. The change cut CI lead time from 45 minutes to 15 minutes, a 3x improvement.
what_they_built: A "deferred compliance" CI mechanism that removes certain long-running/non-critical checks from the pre-merge blocking path, instead filing a labeled GitHub issue with a 72-hour remediation window on failure, backed by measurement against the DORA Lead Time for Changes metric.
evidence: CI lead time dropped from an average of 45 minutes to 15 minutes (3x faster), measured via the Lead Time for Changes DORA metric.
why_it_matters: Shows how reframing "what must block a merge" against a real DORA metric — rather than just adding more automated gates — can be the highest-leverage lever for developer-platform teams trying to cut engineering cycle time.

title: Design system annotations, part 2: Advanced methods of annotating components
summary: GitHub's Primer design system had grown too large to hand-annotate every component for accessibility, so the accessibility design team built "Preset annotations" — pre-configured accessibility detail sets for the highest-impact components. They used an internal tool, Primer Query, to prioritize which of Primer's components to cover first based on organizational priority, accessibility audit frequency, React availability, and usage prevalence, landing on ten components. Rolling this out surfaced concrete friction: Figma's properties panel truncates long property names, Preset annotations sometimes overlapped with other annotation types causing confusion, and keeping annotations synced with a maturing, changing design system created ongoing maintenance burden. In response GitHub also adopted Figma's Code Connect to embed accessibility attributes (like aria-label) directly into exported code rather than relying purely on annotations, and open-sourced their GitHub Annotation Toolkit.
what_they_built: Preset accessibility annotations for ten prioritized Primer components, selected using an internal usage-tracking tool called Primer Query, plus an open-sourced GitHub Annotation Toolkit for viewing Primer A11y Preset implementations and visual regression testing.
evidence: Qualitative: prioritized and shipped Preset annotations for 10 Primer components using audit-frequency, org-priority, React-availability, and usage-prevalence criteria; documented real friction (Figma property-name truncation, annotation-type overlap causing designer confusion, ongoing sync burden on a maturing system) and a follow-up mitigation (Figma Code Connect embedding aria-label directly in exported code) plus release of an open-source toolkit.
why_it_matters: Shows PMs/designers running a design system a concrete, evidence-based way to prioritize which components get deep accessibility treatment first, and the operational costs (truncation, overlap, sync burden) that come with scaling annotation-based systems.

title: The GitHub GraphQL API
summary: GitHub's REST API had grown to account for over 60% of requests to its database tier, and hypermedia-style responses forced clients into multiple round trips while still returning too much or too little data. The API platform team built a GraphQL API instead, starting with a narrow pilot (emoji reactions on comments) to model a full workflow before expanding the schema, and validated new GraphQL-backed code paths against existing systems using GitHub's Scientist library. Frontend engineers dogfooded the same public GraphQL platform internally via Relay to power UI features like Projects and star/watch counts, meaning application engineers and third-party integrators consumed identical API surface.
what_they_built: A public GraphQL API (replacing/supplementing REST) built incrementally on a Ruby schema (graphql-ruby, graphql-batch) and consumed internally via Relay for site UI.
evidence: Reports that REST was responsible for over 60% of requests made to GitHub's database tier, motivating the scalability rationale; describes using Scientist to validate GraphQL resolvers against production REST behavior before cutover, and cites the Projects feature as a shipped example where the same GraphQL schema powers both the site UI and public API access.
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete org pattern — unifying internal and external API consumption on one platform — that forces API design quality and lets a platform team dogfood its own product before external release.

title: GitHub Mobile and GraphQL
summary: GitHub's mobile team was maintaining REST-based network models by hand, which meant manual encoding/decoding, over-fetching, and fragile API versioning that risked breaking older clients on backend changes. They migrated GitHub Mobile (iOS and Android) to GraphQL with the Apollo client, using Apollo's codegen to statically type network responses instead of hand-writing models. GraphQL's field-level deprecation let the backend evolve without breaking existing mobile clients, and heavy use of reusable fragments (e.g. actorFields) reduced model duplication and kept view models composable across screens.
