what_they_built: A GraphQL API layer for GitHub Mobile built on Apollo client libraries for iOS and Android, with generated statically-typed network models and a fragment-based composition pattern for view models.
evidence: Outcome is qualitative rather than metric-based: the team reports they can "be certain any updates to our backend models don't break our mobile consumers" thanks to field-level deprecation, and that the approach let them "move faster and get more done with less hassle and no over-fetching." No adoption numbers or before/after metrics are given.
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete alternative to whole-API versioning for platform teams serving multiple client types: field-level deprecation plus generated types can decouple backend evolution from client breakage.

title: GitHub's JavaScript SDK: From 48k Lines to 10
summary: Octokit.js maintainer Gregor Martynus recounts a multi-year rework of GitHub's official JavaScript REST SDK. The team faced a manually maintained ~8,000-line routes.json describing API endpoints plus a monolithic core library that had ballooned to tens of thousands of lines. They replaced manual route maintenance with a nightly scraper that turns GitHub's REST API docs into a machine-readable JSON spec and automates PRs on changes, and they decomposed the monolithic core into small composable packages (@octokit/endpoint, @octokit/request, @octokit/core, @octokit/auth, plugins) assembled by a top-level entrypoint that shrank to just 10 lines of code, while converting usage examples into integration tests and enforcing 100% coverage.
what_they_built: A plugin-based architecture for the Octokit.js SDK (separate endpoint/request/core/auth packages plus composable plugins) paired with an automated nightly scraper (octokit/routes) that regenerates the API route specification from GitHub's REST documentation instead of manual edits.
evidence: Core library entrypoint reduced from roughly 16,000-48,000+ lines of legacy code down to 10 lines by composing plugins; routes.json automation replaced ~8,000 lines of manual maintenance and grew to 10,275 LOC of automatically generated, more complete route coverage; 100% test coverage enforced as a minimum threshold via converted integration tests.
why_it_matters: Shows how treating an SDK's internal spec (routes.json) as a generated artifact rather than hand-maintained data, combined with plugin decomposition, can turn a maintenance-heavy internal platform into a low-overhead, high-coverage one.

title: Design system annotations, part 1: How accessibility gets left out of components
summary: GitHub's Accessibility Design team noticed that even though its Primer design system components were built accessible, designs assembled from them still shipped with access barriers -- broken heading hierarchies, missing alt text, unclear focus order -- because component accessibility alone doesn't capture layout- and context-specific intent. Their fix was to embed "Preset" annotations (a Stamp pointing to the component plus a Details annotation with pre-set prompts) directly into ten frequently used Primer Figma components, each linked to component documentation and Storybook demos. The goal was to cut annotation effort for designers, raise adoption of accessibility annotation practice, and reach teams without dedicated accessibility support. Part 2 (referenced but not covered here) extends the same preset-annotation approach to other design systems like Material, Polaris, and Carbon.
what_they_built: Primer A11y Preset annotations: pre-configured Stamp + Details annotation pairs embedded into ten commonly used Primer Figma components, each pre-filled with prompts for the accessibility details developers need (assistive-tech navigation order, alt text, viewport/zoom behavior, virtual keyboard type, focus management) and linked to component docs and Storybook demos.
evidence: Qualitative: the team reports the presets reduce time spent annotating and are intended to increase design-system-wide accessibility-annotation adoption and reach teams lacking dedicated accessibility support; no quantitative adoption or defect-reduction metrics are given in this (part 1) post.
why_it_matters: Shows a PM/design-system team pattern for closing the gap between "our components are accessible" and "our shipped designs are accessible" by baking reusable, context-specific guidance directly into the design tool rather than relying on ad hoc annotation each time.

title: Unlocking inclusive design: how Primer's color system is making GitHub.com more inclusive
summary: GitHub's design team found that Primer's color system had hundreds of WCAG contrast failures across light and dark themes, spanning over 1,000 use cases. They rebuilt the system around base, functional, and component color layers, audited more than 100 color pairs against 4.5:1 (text) and 3:1 (UI element) contrast requirements, and wrote a script that included a blend algorithm to correctly evaluate semi-transparent colors against their backgrounds. The contrast checker was wired into a CLI and GitHub Actions so regressions in new color pairs would be caught automatically in pull requests, and the new tokens were rolled out behind feature flags with monitoring before full release.
what_they_built: A three-layer (base/functional/component) color token system for Primer with an automated contrast-checking script (CLI plus GitHub Actions integration) that handles opacity via a blend algorithm.
evidence: Audited 100+ color pairs across all themes/variations and resolved hundreds of accessibility contrast issues spanning over 1,000 use cases, bringing color pairs into WCAG compliance (4.5:1 text, 3:1 UI elements).
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete pattern for scaling accessibility compliance in a design system: automate contrast checking as a CI gate rather than relying on manual review.

title: To infinity and beyond: enabling the future of GitHub's REST API with API versioning
summary: After running a single unversioned REST API (V3) for over a decade, GitHub faced the problem of needing to ship breaking changes without forcing millions of integrators to upgrade on GitHub's timeline. They built a calendar-based versioning scheme where developers opt into a version via the X-GitHub-Api-Version request header, with non-breaking changes applying across all versions and breaking changes gated behind a new dated version. GitHub committed to supporting each prior version for at least 24 months after a newer one ships, expecting to often support them longer in practice.
what_they_built: A calendar-dated REST API versioning system (e.g. version "2022-11-28") selected per-request via the X-GitHub-Api-Version header, decoupling non-breaking changes (always available) from breaking changes (isolated to new dated versions).
evidence: No adoption or usage metrics are given; the evidence is an explicit policy commitment — minimum 24-month support for prior versions — and the stated production constraint that a decade of unversioned operation left no mechanism for safe breaking changes.
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete pattern for retrofitting version governance onto a long-running, widely-integrated public API without breaking existing consumers.

title: Postmortem of database outage of January 31
summary: On January 31, 2017, an engineer resolving PostgreSQL replication lag accidentally deleted the production database directory on GitLab.com, wiping roughly 300GB of data and causing an 18-hour outage. Recovery was slow because all three backup mechanisms (pg_dump, disk snapshots, and email failure alerts) had been silently broken for months. GitLab published a 5 Whys root-cause analysis and committed to over 15 tracked remediation issues covering hourly LVM/disk snapshots, automated backup-restore testing, Prometheus backup monitoring, and assigning an explicit owner for data durability.
what_they_built: A public root-cause postmortem (5 Whys) plus a tracked remediation program: hourly LVM and Azure disk snapshots, Prometheus-based backup monitoring, automated PostgreSQL backup-restore testing, and pgbarman/WAL-E continuous archiving, with an assigned data-durability owner.
evidence: Roughly 300GB of production data deleted; ~5,000 projects, ~5,000 comments, and ~700 new user accounts permanently lost from a ~6.5-hour window; outage lasted approximately 18 hours; three independent backup mechanisms were all found to have been silently failing.
why_it_matters: Shows PMs/platform owners that untested backups are equivalent to no backups, and that surviving a severe outage well means publishing an unflinching RCA and converting it into owned, tracked remediation work rather than a one-off apology.

title: GitLab Enterprise Edition Price Change
summary: GitLab had three Enterprise Edition tiers (Basic, Standard, Plus) with seat minimums that forced small teams to overbuy and made premium support bundled and inflexible. They collapsed the three tiers into a single per-seat plan at $39/seat/year with no minimum purchase, and unbundled premium support into a separate add-on priced at $9,900/year or $99/seat above 100 seats (capped at $50,000). The post walks through six real customer scenarios showing who pays less (e.g., a 3-user Basic customer drops from $390 to $117/year) and who pays more (e.g., a 100-user Standard customer with Premium Support rises from $4,900 to $8,850 even after a first-year discount).
what_they_built: A restructured Enterprise Edition pricing model: one simplified per-seat plan replacing three tiered plans, with premium support and add-ons (like GitLab Geo) unbundled and separately priced.
evidence: Worked before/after cost comparisons across six customer segments: 3-user Basic decreases from $390 to $117/year; 100-user Standard with Premium Support increases from $4,900 to $8,850/year (post-discount). Existing customers were given until April 30, 2016 to renew at old rates, and Plus subscribers got complimentary access to future Enterprise Edition products as a transition concession.
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete pattern for unbundling tiered SaaS pricing into per-seat base plus opt-in add-ons while being transparent about which customer segments win and lose, with grace-period mechanics to manage the transition.

title: Small experiments, significant results and learnings
summary: GitLab's Growth team found through multi-user journey mapping that inviting teammates was slow and interrupted with waiting days. They ran a minimal-viable-change experiment adding an "Invite members" link to the assignee dropdown for admin users, then a follow-up experiment showing non-admin users a modal explaining the feature and directing them to contact an admin. Both experiments, despite low click-through, correlated with meaningful growth in namespaces with two or more users, validating demand for a lower-friction, in-workflow invite experience.
what_they_built: Two lightweight MVC experiments: an admin-only "Invite members" link in the assignee dropdown, and a non-admin-facing modal explaining the unavailable feature and prompting them to contact an admin, which together informed building an inline invite-members modal.
evidence: Admin-only invite link produced a 0.16% click-through rate but correlated with a 2% increase in namespaces with two or more users; the non-admin modal experiment showed preliminary data suggesting up to a 20% increase in multi-user namespaces.
why_it_matters: Shows how a growth team can validate demand and prioritize a UX investment using cheap, low-fidelity MVC experiments before building the full feature.

title: How we use the Jobs-To-Be-Done Framework to rethink user workflow
summary: GitLab's Pipeline Execution design and research team wanted to reassess their CI/CD vision without anchoring interviews to the existing UI, since prior research had skewed toward feature-level feedback. They redesigned their JTBD interviews around a deliberately UI-blank canvas listing six generic workflow stages (Define, Locate, Execute, Monitor, Modify, Secure/Deploy/Debrief) that participants co-annotated live. This forced the conversation to workflow-level jobs rather than existing feature likes/dislikes. The exercise validated their existing understanding of user jobs while surfacing three previously under-recognized opportunity areas.
what_they_built: A collaborative, UI-blank interview canvas mapped across six generic CI/CD workflow stages, used live with participants to co-plot their process instead of walking them through GitLab's actual interface.
evidence: Qualitative: the method validated the team's existing JTBD assumptions and additionally surfaced three new opportunity areas — "learnability of the tool, post code-integration monitoring capabilities and discoverability of the code-verification practices" — that hadn't previously been prioritized.
why_it_matters: Shows PMs/researchers a concrete technique for de-biasing discovery interviews when a team is at risk of only hearing feedback on the UI it already built, rather than the underlying job the user is trying to do.

title: Measuring the value of our design system
summary: GitLab's design team wanted to know whether its Pajamas design system was actually paying off, so they ran the same usefulness/time-allocation survey of designers in April 2018 and again roughly 2.5 years later. The 2018 baseline showed designers rarely referenced the system and found it not very useful; the follow-up survey, sent to designers and cross-functional partners like engineers and PMs, found over 75% of designers now rated it very useful. Designers also reported spending 50-75% of their time solving real user problems versus a 25% average in 2018, with fewer basic component-clarification questions and more direct system referencing.
what_they_built: A recurring internal survey instrument to measure designer sentiment and time allocation around GitLab's Pajamas design system, repeated longitudinally to track system maturity.
evidence: Usefulness perception rose from "rarely referenced... not very useful" in 2018 to over 75% of designers finding it very useful in the follow-up; time spent on real user problems rose from ~25% average (2018) to 50-75% (follow-up), with fewer clarification questions and stable documentation time despite expanded content.
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete, repeatable method for proving an internal platform's ROI to stakeholders using the same instrument run longitudinally rather than one-off satisfaction snapshots.

title: How we measure engineering productivity at GitLab
summary: As GitLab's engineering org grew from 100 to 280 engineers in 1.5 years, leadership needed a shared way to talk about productivity and settled on Merge Request (MR) Rate — total MRs divided by team members in a month, with a target of 10. The label-based version of the metric degraded as the org scaled: a labeling bot occasionally mislabeled MRs, producing bad data, so the team split the metric into a label-based rate and an authorship-based "Narrow MR Rate" to restore trust in the numbers. They paired this with a Definition of Done to stop teams gaming volume at the expense of quality, and later added a Say-Do Ratio to capture commitment-keeping alongside raw throughput. The piece is explicit that no single metric tells the full productivity story and warns against over-indexing on one number.
what_they_built: A composite engineering-productivity measurement system at GitLab centered on Merge Request Rate (with label-based and authorship-based variants), backed by a Definition of Done quality gate and a Say-Do Ratio commitment metric.
evidence: Reports organizational scale of growth from 100 to 280 engineers in 1.5 years, a target MR Rate of 10, and a concrete data-quality failure mode (bot-driven mislabeling of MRs) that prompted splitting the metric into label-based and authorship-based ("Narrow") variants.
why_it_matters: Shows a PM/eng-leadership team how a single throughput metric degrades as an org scales and how to pair it with quality and commitment metrics to avoid gaming and restore trust in the data.

title: How to turn screw-ups to your advantage
summary: GitLab recounts two 2014 incidents: an 8-hour GitLab.com outage affecting roughly 20,000 monthly active users, and a missed customer installation call. In both cases GitLab responded with public transparency — the CEO published an open postmortem for the outage and personally apologized for the missed call, followed by an internal root-cause review and documented process changes shared with the affected customer. The post frames this as a repeatable organizational pattern: acknowledge openly, act swiftly, find root cause, improve process, and communicate the fix back to those affected.
what_they_built: An open incident-response and postmortem process — public postmortem documents for outages, plus internal review and documented process revisions communicated directly to affected customers.
evidence: Qualitative outcomes: the public postmortem discussion reached the top of Hacker News, and the customer whose installation call was missed chose to renew and upgrade their subscription (from basic to standard) after seeing GitLab's detailed process-improvement response, rather than churning.
why_it_matters: Shows a PM/support-org pattern where transparent, process-focused incident follow-up with customers can convert a service failure into retained or expanded revenue rather than churn.

title: 5 things we learned from you in recent UX research
summary: GitLab's UX research team shares five findings from recent studies. The headline case is the Secure team's redesign of the security checks enablement flow: prior research found only 1 of 5 participants could complete the task with the old design, so they rebuilt it to let users configure security checks directly in the UI based on discovered user mental models, and a follow-up usability test found all participants completed the task with the new design. Other findings cover snippets as an informal knowledge base, a naming survey where ~200 developers preferred keeping the term "Auto DevOps," gaps in billing flows that favor large orgs over small businesses, and unmet needs around searching/surfacing insights from logs.
what_they_built: A redesigned security checks enablement interface letting users configure checks directly from the UI, validated with a moderated usability test before and after the change.
evidence: Task completion rose from 1 of 5 participants (20%) with the old design to all participants (100%) with the redesign; separately, a naming survey of nearly 200 developers favored retaining "Auto DevOps" over alternatives.
why_it_matters: Shows how a small, targeted usability test with as few as 5 participants can produce a stark before/after signal that justifies a UI redesign decision.

title: Why we built a UX Research Insights repository
summary: GitLab's first UX researcher found that static Google Docs research reports were unsearchable, created research silos, weren't actionable, and quickly went stale as the team and product grew. After running 1:1 interviews with every product manager at GitLab and confirming they wanted findings surfaced as actionable issues rather than buried in reports, she replaced reports with a UXR Insights repository built entirely on GitLab's own issue tracker: individual issues per insight, labels for search/filtering, epics to group issues from the same study, and templates so any UX researcher or designer could contribute consistently. The team explicitly dogfooded their own product rather than adopting external research-repository tooling.
what_they_built: A research-ops "insights repository" built inside GitLab itself, using one issue per insight, labels for tagging/search, epics to group issues from a single study, and contribution templates, replacing prior Google Docs-based research reports.
evidence: Qualitative: PM interviews explicitly surfaced that reports weren't actionable and PMs wanted findings delivered as issues; after launch the team reports "early signs indicate that (unsurprisingly) no UX researchers are missing writing reports!" No hard adoption metrics are given, and the team planned a 90-day review to assess how the repository was working.
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete pattern for turning research-ops infrastructure into an internal product built on tools teams already use, rather than adopting new external tooling.

title: How UX research impacts product decisions
summary: GitLab's UX research team ran a study (part of their "Concurrent DevOps" research line) into how operations professionals monitor system health, finding users track 1-100 performance/system health metrics spread across 1-5 separate dashboards, with CPU, memory, availability, request rates, and response time as the most common metrics. Rather than requiring users to juggle multiple dashboards, the findings directly informed the design of a consolidated, flexible Operations dashboard. The post also describes GitLab's broader UX research intake process: contributors submit proposals, the UX team prioritizes and runs studies, and findings are documented to feed product decisions. It also surfaces related pain points, like 65% of respondents reporting insufficient staffing for operations work.
what_they_built: A consolidated, flexible Operations dashboard designed off direct UX research findings about how many metrics/dashboards operations teams juggle day to day.
evidence: Qualitative and quantitative research findings: users monitor 1-100 metrics across 1-5 dashboards, common tracked metrics are CPU, memory, system availability, request rates, and response time/status; 65% of respondents reported insufficient operations staffing.
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete, low-tech pattern for turning a structured UX research intake process into a specific, defensible product design decision (consolidate rather than fragment) rather than acting on assumptions.

title: Cluster-Randomized Experiments at Glovo
summary: Glovo's data science team faced experiments where treatment can only be randomized at an aggregate level (e.g. city or customer) while outcomes are measured per-order, a setup ripe for underestimated variance if analyzed naively. They implemented cluster-randomized experiment design with clustered standard errors to correctly account for within-cluster correlation, applying it to cases like city-level rollouts and customer-level fraud-mitigation treatments. A 10,000-replication simulation on real order data from 100 cities compared naive versus clustered standard errors under the null hypothesis. The naive approach badly inflated the false-positive (Type I error) rate, while the clustered approach matched the nominal 5% level, validating the method for production use.
what_they_built: A cluster-randomized experimentation methodology with clustered standard error estimation, used at Glovo for experiments where treatment assignment happens at the city or customer level (including fraud mitigation treatments) rather than the individual order level.
evidence: 10,000-iteration simulation using real order data from 100 cities: with naive standard errors the Type I error rate was "completely off" (far above the nominal level), while with clustered standard errors it matched the nominal 5% level.
why_it_matters: PMs and analysts running experiments where treatment is assigned above the individual-user level (regions, cohorts, stores) need clustered inference or they will systematically over-trust noisy wins.

title: Variance reduction in experiments using covariate adjustment techniques
summary: Glovo's data science team needed to speed up A/B test read times without sacrificing statistical rigor, since many product tests were underpowered or slow to reach significance. They compared four covariate-adjustment techniques — multivariate regression, CUPED, CUPAC, and doubly robust estimation — using 1,000-run simulations across linear, non-linear, and irrelevant-covariate scenarios. They found doubly robust and CUPAC won when outcome relationships were non-linear, but all methods performed equally poorly with unrelated covariates, showing covariate quality matters more than method choice. They then applied these techniques in production to recommendation-system tests (using historical conversion rates as covariates) and order-assignment tests (using historical delivery metrics), reporting real variance reduction.
what_they_built: A comparative evaluation and production rollout of covariate-adjustment variance-reduction methods (multivariate regression, CUPED, CUPAC, doubly robust estimation) for Glovo's internal experimentation platform.
evidence: Simulation-based comparison across three scenarios (linear, non-linear, wrong-covariate) showed doubly robust estimation "obtained the best results among all the estimators in all the scenarios," with CUPAC close behind on non-linear data, while all methods degraded equally with poorly chosen covariates; the techniques were then applied to real recommendation-system and order-assignment experiments at Glovo using historical conversion rates and delivery metrics as covariates.
why_it_matters: Shows a PM-adjacent lesson for any team running an internal experimentation platform: investing in covariate quality (not just fancier estimators) is what actually shrinks required sample sizes and speeds up test velocity.

title: Of Dreams, Deadlines, and a Design System
summary: Gojek's design org faced 17 products built from 300 disconnected Sketch files and 1,200 artboards, with no shared visual or component language across teams. Rather than mandate a top-down system, they piloted a shared component library on GO-RIDE (their highest-scale, highest-risk product at 1.3M orders/day) as a deliberate "development mule," then let adoption spread team-by-team as the data made the case for itself. Over 14 months this produced the Asphalt design system: 200 reusable components used across 700+ screens, with each subsequent product redesign getting dramatically faster as it reused components from the last.
what_they_built: Asphalt, a shared cross-product design system and component library for Gojek's Android/iOS apps, rolled out incrementally starting with the GO-RIDE app.
evidence: Redesign timelines fell sharply as component reuse compounded: GO-RIDE (the pilot) took 8 months, GO-FOOD took 6 months, GO-MART took only 4 weeks reusing GO-FOOD's components, and GO-SEND took just 2 weeks reusing GO-RIDE's components; the team also reports a halving of app crash rates tied to adopting the shared components, plus measurable order-conversion improvements post-rollout.
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete playbook for driving cross-team platform/design-system adoption bottom-up via a single high-visibility pilot and hard rollout-speed data, rather than top-down mandate.

title: Detecting Fraudsters In Near Real-Time With ClickHouse
summary: Gojek's existing fraud detection ran as periodic batch jobs, letting fraudulent orders complete before rules caught them. The team built a near-real-time layer on a 5-node ClickHouse cluster (dedicated insertion, production-query, and playground nodes) fed by a custom Golang Kafka ingestor, so rules could evaluate account and booking events before an order finishes. They tuned partitioning, encoding, and node roles to hit sub-second query latency at production write volumes.
what_they_built: A 5-node ClickHouse cluster with read/write-separated insertion, production, and playground nodes, plus a custom Golang data ingestor, to run near-real-time fraud rules against streaming account and booking events before order completion.
evidence: Query duration for a test scenario (50,000 account events, 2,000,000 booking events, queried every 5 seconds) was reduced from about 1.5 seconds to under 800ms against a target of completing within 1 second; insertion nodes handle tens of thousands of writes per second; the system had run in production for about a year with tens of near-real-time rules active.
why_it_matters: Shows how to architect a shared analytical data store (node role separation, ingestion pipeline, schema tuning) to move a trust-and-safety capability from batch to near-real-time without over-provisioning for worst-case load.

title: Protecting Indonesia's Largest Digital Payments App
summary: GoPay handles over half of Gojek's transaction volume, making fraud mitigation a critical shared concern across the ecosystem. Gojek's engineering team built the Fraud Rules Service (FRS), a rule-based engine (Clojure, gRPC) that evaluates velocity rules, simple threshold clauses, and watchlist allow/deny-lists against transactions. They chose Redis ZSETs for velocity checks to get idempotency, time-window evaluation, and fast lookups without storing full history, and moved from hard-coded field extraction to a JQ-parser-based approach for flexibility, plus separated Redis read/write paths to cut processing time and duplication.
what_they_built: A rule-based Fraud Rules Service (FRS) using Redis ZSETs for velocity rules, JQ-based dynamic field extraction, watchlist rules, and a custom query DSL for multi-data-point fraud checks protecting GoPay transactions.
evidence: Qualitative: read/write segregation to Redis "reduced processing time and eliminated data duplication through catalog ID reuse"; the architecture maintains SLA performance for fast data access. No specific before/after metrics or fraud-reduction percentages are given in the retrieved text.
why_it_matters: Shows how a payments platform team can design a flexible, low-latency rules engine for trust and safety without needing a full ML pipeline, trading some sophistication for speed, auditability, and extensibility.

title: A Tale of Two Designs — a UX case-study on redesigning the GO-FOOD Checkout experience (Part 3 of 3)
summary: Gojek's design team rolled out a redesigned two-step GO-FOOD checkout on August 12, 2018 that instead caused a 10% drop in orders, with 8% of users abandoning the flow at the first step. The team ran qualitative research to diagnose the failure, then tested multiple design variants via A/B/C experimentation, ultimately shipping a consolidated single-page checkout on September 5, 2018. That fix recovered bookings, improving the booking rate by roughly 5% and significantly cutting booking time, while a related fix also reduced cancellations from wrong-address selection by about 2%.
what_they_built: A redesigned, consolidated single-page GO-FOOD checkout flow (replacing an earlier two-step redesign that had hurt conversion), validated through A/B/C testing.
evidence: Initial two-step redesign caused a 10% drop in orders and 8% step-1 abandonment; the subsequent single-page redesign produced ~5% increase in booking rate, a significant drop in booking time, and about a 2% reduction in cancellations from wrong-address errors.
why_it_matters: Shows a PM/design team publicly owning a metric-driven design failure and using qualitative research plus controlled experimentation to recover and beat the original baseline, rather than reverting blind.

title: How we use Machine Learning to match Drivers & Riders
summary: Gojek needed to allocate drivers to riders across millions of daily requests while balancing competing goals like acceptance rate, completion rate, driver ratings, and fairness, rather than optimizing a single naive metric. They built Jaeger, a modular system with four components: Lasso orchestrates ranking requests to microservices via YAML-configured workflows, Feast serves ML features at low latency and high throughput, Meister aggregates outputs from multiple specialized ranking models using goal programming, and Babel provides a Bayesian A/B testing library to measure impact. The team stresses that correctly framing what to predict mattered more than raw model accuracy, and that this reframing eliminated feedback loops that had previously biased allocations toward certain metrics at the expense of balance and fairness. After deployment, the system produced over 1 million additional completed trips within weeks, plus measurable gains in dispatch time, cancellation rate, driver utilization, and driver income.
what_they_built: A modular, multi-objective driver-rider allocation system (Jaeger) composed of an orchestration service (Lasso), a low-latency feature store (Feast), a goal-programming model aggregator (Meister), and a Bayesian A/B testing library (Babel), replacing single-metric-driven allocation logic.
evidence: Quantified: "over 1 million additional completed trips within weeks" of launch, plus reported improvements in dispatch time, cancellation rates, driver utilization, and driver income metrics.
why_it_matters: Shows how reframing the prediction target (not just improving model accuracy) and decomposing a multi-objective marketplace problem into specialized services with a formal aggregation layer (goal programming) can remove hidden feedback loops that bias a platform's optimization away from balance and fairness.

title: How the Marketplace Team Makes Sure Everyone Gets a Slice of the GOJEK Pie
summary: Gojek's Marketplace team needed to match millions of weekly ride/food/delivery orders to drivers while balancing competing goals like proximity, customer wait tolerance (which varies by product and time of day), pricing, incentives, and driver utilization and income. Their original approach, a single standalone ML ranking model, couldn't balance these multiple business objectives as the platform scaled. They replaced it with Jaeger, a modular multi-objective allocation system combining ML models with real-time features and manual configuration flexibility, built on four components (Feast, Lasso, Meister, Babel) from the Data Science Platform team. After launch, Gojek reported measurable gains in trips completed, dispatch time, cancellation rates, driver utilization, and driver income.
what_they_built: Jaeger, a modular multi-objective driver-allocation system (four components: Feast, Lasso, Meister, Babel) replacing a single standalone ML ranking model for matching drivers to customers across Gojek's ride, food, and delivery products.
evidence: "The smarter allocation decisions enabled 1 million additional completed trips within weeks of the system's launch, with significant improvements seen in dispatch time, cancellation rates, driver utilization, and income" (specific size of improvements not quantified beyond the trips figure).
why_it_matters: Shows how a marketplace/platform team moves from a single-objective ML model to an explicitly multi-objective, modular allocation system once real-world constraints (time-of-day, product type, driver economics) outgrow what one model can balance.

title: Behind the Screens: The Evolution of Gojek's User Communications
summary: By 2020, Gojek's 20+ services were bombarding users with over 10 push notifications a day with no centralized system tracking who was sending what to whom, driving complaints (especially during COVID lockdowns) and a CTR of just 0.81% against an 8% industry benchmark — one user got 922 notifications in three months. The product/tech team used design-thinking methods (journey mapping, storyboarding, 5 Whys) to build a centralized communications strategy with targeting rules and user-level preference controls, plus new lower-friction channels (ephemeral "Snippets," chatbots for critical info, product subscription channels). Within six months, 32% of users saw 10% fewer monthly notifications, 0.5% of users hit 100% reduction in promotional messages, 3 million users gained preference controls, SMS costs dropped significantly, and Customer Effort Score improved.
what_they_built: A centralized, cross-channel communications governance system for Gojek's app — targeting rules, user-level notification preference controls, and new lower-noise channels (Snippets, chatbots, subscription channels) replacing uncoordinated per-team messaging.
evidence: In the first six months: 32% of users received 10% fewer push notifications monthly, 0.5% of users saw a 100% reduction in promotional messages, 3 million users gained communication preference controls, SMS costs fell by millions of dollars year-over-year, and Customer Effort Score improved; baseline CTR had been 0.81% versus an 8% industry standard, with one user previously receiving 922 notifications in three months.
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete playbook for a PM tackling notification fatigue across a multi-team super-app: centralize visibility into who sends what to whom, add targeting and user-level controls, and measure success via reduced volume plus CTR/cost/CES rather than send-volume alone.

title: Google Clips and Human-Centered AI
summary: Google's Clips team faced the classic problem of designing an AI product without a clear way to validate what "good" output looked like. They hired a documentary filmmaker, photojournalist, and fine-arts photographer to define what makes a memorable moment, using their judgment as ground truth for the model, and moved usability testing from simulated content to users' own real photos/video once they realized fake content produced misleading results. That research reshaped the product: they dropped a Minority-Report-style sci-fi interface for a plain viewfinder and hardware capture button, committed to on-device-only processing given the camera's personal nature, and deliberately surfaced more candidate clips than the algorithm's "best" picks so users retained final curation control.
what_they_built: The Google Clips AI camera and its supporting design/research process: expert-informed ground-truth definitions, real-content prototyping, and a simplified UI built around a viewfinder, hardware button, and user-editable clip selection.
evidence: Qualitative account rather than metrics: the team describes concretely changing course after finding usability tests on simulated/fake content misled them, after recognizing internal AI-expert bias distorted their sense of general users, and after deciding privacy required on-device-only processing — each described as a direct product decision, not just a stated intention.
why_it_matters: Shows a transferable pattern for any PM building an AI feature with no ground truth: borrow domain-expert judgment to define quality, prototype on real user content instead of synthetic data, and default to less automation/more user control when the model's confidence is uncertain.

title: Expressive Design: Google's UX Research Behind Material 3 Expressive
summary: Google's Material Design team faced a tension between visual expressiveness and usability as they built the next generation of their design system, Material 3 Expressive. Rather than shipping on intuition, they ran a multi-year research program: 46 separate studies with more than 18,000 participants worldwide, combining eye-tracking, surveys, focus groups, and controlled experiments to test whether bolder, more expressive UI actually helped or hurt users. The research found expressive design was strongly preferred across age groups (especially 18-24 year-olds, up to 87%), boosted desirability metrics (subculture perception, modernity, rebelliousness), and let users spot key UI elements up to four times faster than standard Material 3 while narrowing age-related performance gaps in finding elements.
what_they_built: A large-scale, multi-method UX research program (eye-tracking labs, surveys, focus groups, and A/B-style usability experiments across 46 studies) to validate and refine Material 3 Expressive, Google's shipped design system update.
evidence: Reports concrete before/after metrics: up to 4x faster identification of key UI elements versus standard Material 3, up to 87% preference for expressive design among 18-24 year-olds, and desirability lifts of 32% (subculture perception), 34% (modernity), and 30% (rebelliousness), plus reduced age-related gaps in element-finding speed.
why_it_matters: Shows a PM how to pair a subjective design direction (more expressive, bolder visuals) with a rigorous, quantified research program before shipping, rather than relying on aesthetic judgment alone.

title: Connectivity, Culture, and Credit
summary: A field researcher on Google's Next Billion Users team synthesizes nine design principles for emerging-market products, drawn from research trips across India, Indonesia, Kenya, and the Philippines. The most concrete case is Google Station, Google's free public WiFi service in Indian train stations (5 million daily users across 100 stations): user research found women were reluctant to trust the sign-on portal because Indian law requires a phone number, and interviewees linked this fear to a real incident where a woman's photo was misused and she later took her life. In response, the team added explicit trust messaging ("Your number is safe with us") before the phone-number field and linked to an FAQ addressing the concerns raised in research. The piece also covers payment norms (cash-on-delivery given ~2% credit card penetration in India), data constraints, device constraints, and multilingual UI needs.
what_they_built: Redesigned the Google Station WiFi sign-on portal's trust messaging and phone-number-collection flow (plus multilingual UI) in direct response to field research on user trust and safety concerns in India.
evidence: Qualitative: field interviews surfaced a specific real-world trust incident driving women's reluctance to share phone numbers; the team responded by adding explicit reassurance copy ("Your number is safe with us") and an FAQ link before the phone-number field. Scale context given: Google Station serves 5 million users a day across 100 train stations in India; only 17% of India's population had internet access and under 2% held credit cards at time of writing.
why_it_matters: Shows how grounding a trust/safety UX decision in specific field-research evidence (not just general privacy best practice) can directly reshape a high-stakes onboarding flow in an emerging market.

title: Overlapping Experiment Infrastructure: More, Better, Faster Experimentation
summary: Google's experimentation program had outgrown a single-layer A/B testing infrastructure where each query could only be in one experiment at a time, creating traffic starvation and bias problems as more teams wanted to run more experiments across search, ads, and ranking. The team built a layered "overlapping experiment infrastructure" that partitions system parameters into independent layers so a single query can be routed into one experiment per layer simultaneously, plus supporting tools (canonical metrics/analysis tooling) and an "experiment council" review process for new experimenters. Deployed since March 2007, the system let Google run far more experiments concurrently while trying to keep experiment quality high and iteration fast. The paper reports adoption trends and qualitative quality improvements rather than a single business metric.
what_they_built: A layered overlapping A/B-testing infrastructure (queries diverted per-layer rather than per-system) plus a canonical experiment-analysis tool and an "experiment council" checklist/review process for launching experiments.
evidence: Trend charts (axes elided for confidentiality) show "we have enabled nearly an order of magnitude more experiments, more launches, and more people running experiments" since the March 2007 rollout; qualitative gains reported include fewer misconfigured/forgotten experiments and fewer disputes over metric definitions thanks to a canonical analysis tool.
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete architectural pattern (parameter-layering plus a lightweight review checklist) for scaling an internal experimentation platform without sacrificing experiment quality as more teams and non-engineers start running tests.

title: The Making of YouTube Go
summary: YouTube's research team found that users in India, Nigeria, Brazil, and Indonesia were frustrated by unpredictable data costs, with some avoiding the app entirely because "data just flies away." The team's first instinct—lower-quality video with keyframes and audio to save data—was rejected by users who insisted "video is video," so the team pivoted to a different approach. Over a two-year effort they built YouTube Go, a separate app that shows exact data cost per video, supports offline download and nearby peer-to-peer sharing, and works reliably in spotty-connectivity conditions.
what_they_built: YouTube Go, a standalone video app for emerging markets showing upfront per-video data costs, offline download/management, and device-to-device "nearby sharing" of downloaded videos.
evidence: Qualitative: in-field interviews across India, Indonesia, Brazil, and Nigeria drove a documented pivot away from a lower-quality-video concept after user rejection, plus a cited stat that 96% of Indian users rely on prepaid data plans, which shaped the data-transparency design.
why_it_matters: Shows how rejecting a plausible cost-saving design (lower quality) based on direct field feedback led to a better-fit solution (transparency + offline tools) for a specific user context.

title: The journey of building a comprehensive attribution platform
summary: Grab's ads and analytics teams needed to measure which ad and promo touchpoints drove orders across the superapp, but early attribution was slow and siloed. The team built an attribution platform in stages: a Kappa-architecture real-time engine, then a unified engine merging ads and promo attribution to stop double-counting orders, then a Lambda architecture adding a batch layer for historical modeling and fault tolerance. The post walks through the tech stack (Kafka, DynamoDB, S3, Redis, ScyllaDB, RDS) and tradeoffs at each phase, plus a proposed future phase separating real-time and batch pipelines by use case.
what_they_built: An internal, multi-phase attribution platform that unifies ads and promotion attribution for merchants, moving from a pure real-time (Kappa) engine to a hybrid real-time+batch (Lambda) architecture.
evidence: Reduced attribution latency from 2-3 days to a few minutes, achieved data discrepancies of less than 1%, and cut real-time data processing costs by roughly 25%, while the added offline/batch layer improved fault tolerance.
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete pattern for evolving an internal measurement platform from real-time-only to a hybrid batch/real-time design as accuracy, cost, and reliability requirements grow.

title: Automated Experiment Analysis - Making experimental analysis scalable
summary: Before this system, each experiment at Grab underwent manual, ad-hoc statistical analysis, which created inefficiency from analysts repeatedly rebuilding pipelines, inconsistent quality/standardization across teams, and poor scalability since results depended on individual analyst skill and non-transferable methods. Grab built Automated Experiment Analysis, extending its GrabX experimentation platform with a bronze/silver/gold data pipeline (Azure Data Factory, Databricks, CosmoDB, Azure Data Lake) and a "Decision Engine" Python package that runs standardized statistical analysis automatically, surfacing results via embedded Power BI dashboards inside GrabX. The goal was to standardize basic analysis, ensure reproducible results under company-wide standards, and democratize experimentation knowledge across functions beyond just data scientists. Impact is described as speeding up decision-making and freeing analyst/data-scientist bandwidth for deeper analysis, with future plans to add experiment guardrails and sequential testing/causal inference methods.
what_they_built: An automated experiment-analysis pipeline (bronze/silver/gold datasets plus a "Decision Engine" Python package) embedded in Grab's GrabX experimentation platform, surfacing standardized statistical results through Power BI dashboards instead of manual per-experiment analysis.
evidence: Qualitative outcome only: the post states the system "speeds up the decision-making process and frees up the bandwidths of analysts and data scientists to conduct deeper analyses," with no specific quantitative metrics given.
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete pattern for turning ad-hoc, analyst-dependent experiment readouts into a standardized internal product, which any PM running a large experimentation program can use to cut analyst bottlenecks and inconsistency.

title: Orchestrating Chaos Using Grab's Experimentation Platform
summary: Grab's engineers needed a way to verify that non-critical service failures (e.g. bookmarks, driver location service) wouldn't take down critical flows like taxi booking in their hundreds-of-services microservice architecture. Rather than adopting a standalone chaos-engineering tool, they built a chaos SDK on top of their existing Experimentation Platform (ExP) SDK, integrating it into Grab-Kit middleware and DB layers, plus a dedicated web UI so non-specialist engineers could configure experiments without touching the underlying implementation. The platform supports eight failure primitives (error, CPU load, memory leak, latency, disk space, goroutine leak, panic, throttle) injected at the gRPC/HTTP middleware layer, run ad-hoc, programmatically, or scheduled, with control/treatment experiment groups and statistical-significance analysis of impact. Because it rides on ExP's existing async tracking, the team can potentially attribute business metrics (e.g. affected rides and revenue) directly to a given chaos failure.
what_they_built: A chaos-engineering SDK and web UI layered on Grab's existing Experimentation Platform (ExP) SDK and Grab-Kit middleware, letting engineers inject failure modes (latency, memory leaks, panics, etc.) into non-critical services and measure downstream impact via control/treatment experiments.
evidence: Qualitative: the piece reports capability rather than a specific incident's numbers — it states chaos experiments run against staging/production either confirm resilience or surface a fixable problem before it causes an outage, and that ExP's tracking lets them "determine how many rides were negatively affected and how much money was lost" from a given chaos failure; no concrete before/after metrics or adoption figures are given in this article.
why_it_matters: Shows how to get chaos engineering adoption across a large eng org cheaply by piggybacking on an existing experimentation platform's SDK and UI rather than standing up a separate tool, and by tying failure injection directly to business-metric attribution rather than just infra health.

title: Ensuring data reliability and observability in risk systems
summary: Grab's risk platform GrabDefence ingests large volumes of upstream JSON data to power real-time fraud detection rules and models, and data quality discrepancies in that pipeline could silently degrade fraud prevention. The team built a data observability layer using Flink SQL (with custom table functions like JSONEXPLOAD to flatten nested JSON and 5-minute tumbling window aggregations) feeding into Datadog for anomaly detection and Slack-integrated alerting, organized by source stream. This cut detection latency from days-to-weeks down to same-day or same-hour, and reduced monitoring overhead by structuring alerts around individual data streams. The team plans to extend coverage to more GrabDefence checkpoints.
what_they_built: A near-real-time data observability pipeline (Apache Flink SQL streaming aggregation plus Datadog monitors/alerts) that detects data-quality anomalies feeding Grab's fraud-detection platform, GrabDefence.
evidence: Detection latency dropped from "days to weeks" to "the same day (or hour)"; alerts organized by source stream also reduced monitoring load, per the team's own account.
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete, low-tech pattern (streaming aggregation + off-the-shelf monitoring tool + stream-scoped alert routing) for building data-quality observability into a trust-and-safety pipeline without building a bespoke platform.

title: Driving smarter with the new Driver AI Assistant
summary: Grab built the Driver AI Assistant, a voice-and-text always-on tool for driver-partners across Indonesia, Malaysia, Philippines, Singapore, Thailand, and Vietnam, aimed at giving drivers instant on-road answers without pulling over. The team expected it to be used mostly for troubleshooting (e.g. lost items, passenger issues) but found drivers overwhelmingly used it as a self-improvement and performance-coaching tool — asking about earnings strategies, past performance, and new features rather than filing complaints. The assistant is context-aware, personalizing advice based on each driver's history and current location. Within two months of rollout it logged over 1.24 million driver messages.
what_they_built: A context-aware, voice-and-text AI assistant embedded in the driver-partner app that gives personalized, real-time advice based on a driver's history and location, deployed across six Southeast Asian markets.
evidence: Over 1.24 million messages from driver-partners to the Assistant within two months of rollout; qualitative finding that "the vast majority of drivers use the assistant as a performance coach" with most messages focused on work optimisation rather than support complaints.
why_it_matters: A reminder that internal AI tools built for one job (support/troubleshooting) can get adopted for an entirely different job (coaching/self-improvement) — worth instrumenting usage intent early rather than assuming the stated use case.

title: We've cut the number of steps it takes to join Grab as a driver
summary: Grab found that prospective drivers previously had to hop across a sign-up website, a separate training portal, and the driver app to onboard, and that in 2022 about 20% of new drivers and 70% of reboarding drivers were rejected due to incomplete or non-compliant document submissions. The team built an in-app driver sign-up and onboarding tool, piloted in Singapore and Malaysia in 2022 and expanded across Southeast Asia by April 2023, adding checklists and photo-preview comparisons against official guidelines to reduce documentation errors. The consolidated flow cut the number of steps needed to join as a driver.
what_they_built: An in-app driver sign-up and onboarding feature within the Grab driver app that consolidates registration, document submission with guided checklists/photo previews, and training into a single flow, replacing a fragmented multi-platform process.
evidence: More than half of new driver-partner sign-ups now come through the new onboarding tool, with the majority completing the entire process unassisted; this addressed a prior state where ~20% of new drivers and ~70% of reboarding drivers were rejected over documentation issues.
why_it_matters: Shows how consolidating a fragmented multi-platform onboarding flow into a single guided in-product experience, with explicit checklists against the top rejection causes, can materially shift adoption and completion rates in a two-sided marketplace supply funnel.

title: A Lean and Scalable Data Pipeline to Capture Large Scale Events and Support Experimentation Platform
summary: Grab needed to capture, ingest, and process rapidly growing event volumes to feed its internal experimentation platform's result computation. They built a batch pipeline (the Mobile event Collector and Dispenser, or "McD") using Apache Spark for compute, Hive as metastore, and Airflow as workflow engine, running on AWS EMR with data stored in Apache ORC format partitioned by event type and ingestion time. The system scaled to handle roughly 400,000 events per second, and over a 3.5-month window processed 2.5 petabytes of data while only consuming 500TB on S3 thanks to compression and partitioning, with ORC delivering 12.5-80% performance gains over Parquet across use cases.
what_they_built: A batch event-capture and ingestion pipeline (Spark/Hive/Airflow on AWS EMR, ORC-formatted storage, multi-AZ scheduling) that feeds Grab's internal A/B experimentation platform with the raw event data needed to compute experiment results.
evidence: Processed 2.5 petabytes of data over 3.5 months while consuming only 500TB on S3; scaled to ~400,000 events/sec; ORC with Snappy compression showed 12.5-80% performance gains over equivalent Parquet storage across different use cases.
why_it_matters: Shows a PM/platform team how to size and justify the unglamorous data-infrastructure investment (format choice, partitioning, orchestration) that experimentation and analytics products silently depend on.

title: Building Grab's Experimentation Platform
summary: Grab needed a way to validate features across its multi-sided marketplace (drivers, passengers, merchants) before full rollout, without wasting engineering effort on ineffective ideas or letting concurrent experiments interfere with each other via marketplace network effects. They built ExP, an internal experimentation platform supporting randomized and time-sliced variable assignment, metric tracking, and geo-temporal segmentation modeled loosely on Google's Domains & Layers framework to keep concurrent experiments from contaminating each other. Adoption scaled from a handful of experiments to about 25 running concurrently, with roughly 2,500 distinct metrics computed daily across ~50,000 experiment/metric combinations.
what_they_built: An internal unified experimentation platform (ExP) with variable/metric abstractions, randomized and time-sliced assignment strategies, and geo-temporal segmentation to prevent cross-experiment interference in a multi-sided marketplace.
evidence: Concurrent experiments grew from a handful to about 25; ~2,500 distinct metrics computed per day; roughly 50,000 distinct experiment/metric combinations.
why_it_matters: Shows how to design experimentation infrastructure for a multi-sided marketplace where naive A/B testing risks cross-side demand-supply interference, a pattern relevant to any PM running experiments on two-sided platforms.

title: How Grab Experimented with Chat to Drive Down Booking Cancellations
summary: Grab's Service Design and Data teams noticed anecdotally that driver-partners who messaged passengers en route had lower booking cancellation rates, and confirmed the correlation in their data. They built and iteratively A/B-tested automated GrabChat messages, first optimizing delay time before sending, then testing message tone, phrasing, and localization across cities like Kuala Lumpur and Bangkok. The rollout cut post-allocation booking cancellations by up to 2 percentage points, and revealed that message quality (a clear, directed question) mattered more than message volume in driving the effect.
what_they_built: An automated, delay-tuned, city-localized GrabChat message sent to passengers and driver-partners immediately after a ride match, designed to prompt a quick human exchange before pickup.
evidence: Reduction of booking cancellations by up to 2 percentage points across tested cities/verticals; Control groups had more messages per booking than Treatment groups yet higher cancellations, indicating engagement quality (not message quantity) drove the effect.
why_it_matters: Shows how a low-cost, well-timed nudge (message delay + localized phrasing) validated through iterative experimentation can move a core operational metric more than raw engagement volume.

title: From failure to success: The birth of GrabGPT, Grab's internal ChatGPT
summary: Grab's ML Platform team was overwhelmed by repetitive support questions on Slack, cutting into engineers' time for real work. An engineer first tried building a platform-specific chatbot on GPT-3.5-turbo, but the 8,000-token limit forced compressing 20,000+ words of docs into under 800 words, and the approach proved unscalable and was abandoned. He pivoted over a weekend to build an enterprise-wide, model-agnostic ChatGPT alternative on Grab's internal model-serving infrastructure (catwalk), with Google login, private routing, and audit trails, which was then adopted broadly across the company.
what_they_built: An internal, model-agnostic ChatGPT-style tool ("GrabGPT") built on Grab's catwalk model-serving infrastructure, supporting OpenAI, Claude, and Gemini with Google SSO, private data routing, and audit trails for governance.
evidence: Adoption numbers: Day 1: 300 users registered; Day 2: 600 new users; Week 1: 900 new users; Month 3: 3,000+ users with 600 daily actives; currently used by nearly all Grab employees.
why_it_matters: Shows how an internal platform team can turn an unscalable point solution into a broadly-adopted internal developer platform by generalizing scope and reusing existing serving infrastructure.

title: Grab Experiment Decision Engine - a Unified Toolkit for Experimentation
summary: Grab's experimentation platform (GrabX) was running thousands of experiment variants a week, but analysis was done manually and inconsistently team-by-team, with no shared standards for sample sizing, statistical tests, or variance reduction. Grab's data science org built the GrabX Decision Engine, an internal Python package that codifies a "trusted advisor" for pre-experiment design (sample sizing, power checks), a standardized post-experiment analysis toolbox, and advanced tools like variance reduction and multiple-comparison correction. To sustain adoption they also stood up an Experiment Council to govern and support the package and a GrabCausal Methodology Bank as a shared repository of causal-methodology code and case studies. This targets core-pm-craft: institutionalizing experimentation rigor and metric-analysis consistency across a large organization rather than any single model or feature.
what_they_built: An internal Python package (GrabX Decision Engine) standardizing pre- and post-experiment statistical analysis across Grab, paired with an Experiment Council governance body and a GrabCausal methodology-sharing repository.
evidence: Qualitative: the post reports the platform runs "thousands of experimental variants each week," names the three problems solved (lack of contextual toolkit, lack of standards, lack of scalability/efficiency), and describes the resulting governance structures (Experiment Council, GrabCausal Methodology Bank) that now maintain company-wide standards — but does not give specific adoption-rate or before/after metrics.
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete pattern for scaling experimentation rigor org-wide: a shared toolkit plus a standing governance council, not just a one-off statistics library.

title: Griffin, an Anti-fraud Risk Rule Engine Making Billions of Predictions Daily
summary: Grab's Trust/Identity/Safety engineering team faced an unsustainable manual, code-based fraud-rule process: rules had grown roughly 10x more complex than simple examples, took developers long hours to understand, and required weekly code deployments to change. They built Griffin, a rule engine that separates data orchestration from rule evaluation and exposes a web portal so non-engineers can author, test (via shadow mode and percentage rollouts), and deploy fraud rules without code changes. The platform now runs at massive scale and has been adopted beyond fraud into safety, identity, and appeals automation.
what_they_built: Griffin, an internal rule-engine platform with a web portal that lets non-engineers author, test, and deploy fraud/safety/identity rules without code deployments, backed by an architecture that decouples data orchestration from rule evaluation.
evidence: Reported outcomes: predicts billions of events daily at 100K+ QPS on just 6 EC2 instances with sub-6ms prediction latency; rule deployment time cut from about one week to about one minute; adoption expanded from fraud into safety, identity, and appeals use cases, reducing manual review effort by 50%.
why_it_matters: Shows how decoupling a domain-expert-facing authoring/deployment surface from the underlying evaluation engine can turn a bottlenecked, engineer-only process into a self-serve internal platform other teams adopt.

title: Why Anti-Fraud Technology Is The Secret Sauce to Winning Ride-hailing Platforms
summary: Grab faced systemic fraud in its Southeast Asia ride-hailing marketplace — driver-side scams like fake-GPS spoofing and multiple-account schemes designed to game incentive bonuses, which the piece says could represent up to 20% of rides without controls. In response Grab built a layered anti-fraud stack: proprietary machine learning models that score rides as high-risk in real time, tooling to detect fake-GPS apps and other spoofing techniques, and an industry-first whistleblower programme (rolled out late 2017) that lets insiders report fraud. The centerpiece described is 'Lawan Opik' ('Fight Fraud'), a dedicated anti-fraud programme launched in Indonesia in 2018 combining these mechanisms with driver-protection features. Grab reports its internal research metrics showed an 80% decrease in the estimated number of fraud cases on the platform over the trailing 12 months.
what_they_built: A machine-learning-based fraud risk-scoring system paired with fake-GPS detection tooling and an internal whistleblower reporting programme, packaged in Indonesia as the 'Lawan Opik' anti-fraud initiative.
evidence: Grab states: "Internal research metrics point to a dramatic decrease of 80% in the estimated number of fraud cases on the Grab platform in the last 12 months." (Note: this is Grab's own internally-reported estimate, not independently audited.)
why_it_matters: Shows how a marketplace PM can pair ML-based risk scoring with a low-tech but high-leverage lever (a whistleblower channel) to attack platform-integrity problems that pure algorithmic detection misses.

title: Grab Merchant App: The Digital Toolkit for MSME Growth
summary: Grab's merchant-facing GrabMerchant app targets low-digital-literacy MSME owners across Southeast Asia who previously needed a Grab manager to get onboarded. The team automated much of the signup flow so merchants can download the app and start selling without manual intermediation, and layered in progress indicators and data nudges (e.g., prompting merchants to add photos to their menu) to drive profile completeness. The app has since expanded into a broader toolkit covering ads, loans, POS, QR payments, and loyalty tools. Grab reports the automated onboarding and product nudges contributed to a 2.5x increase in merchants signed onto the platform during COVID-19 and up to a 35% higher average order value when 75% of a merchant's menu includes photos.
what_they_built: An automated, self-serve merchant signup flow inside the GrabMerchant app, paired with progress-bar/nudge UX that pushes merchants to complete profile and menu-photo tasks, plus a broader toolkit (ads, loans, POS, payments, loyalty) layered onto the base ordering app.
evidence: Grab reports merchant count on the platform grew "about 2.5 times" during COVID-19, and that average order value increases "up to 35 per cent" when 75% of a merchant's menu includes photos; the merchant advertising tool reaches over 36 million monthly users.
why_it_matters: Shows how removing a human-mediated onboarding bottleneck (manager sign-up) and replacing it with automated self-serve flows plus lightweight completion nudges can be a major growth lever for a two-sided marketplace serving low-digital-literacy SMB supply.

title: Grab You Some Post-Mortem Reports
summary: Grab's engineering org found that post-mortem reports written after production incidents were too thin to help other teams learn from them—missing timelines, root-cause detail, and concrete next steps—which was especially costly in a service-oriented architecture where failures cross team boundaries. The team standardized post-mortems around four required elements: Chronology (a timeline of detection, response, and downtime to expose bottlenecks in alerting), Context (specific technical detail on the service and bug mechanics instead of vague statements), Empowerment (blameless, tutorial-style writing so readers gain the diagnostic skill, not just the fact of the fix), and Solutions (categorized fixes across People, Product, and Process). The post walks through a weak example report and shows what was missing versus what a report meeting the standard looks like.
what_they_built: A standardized internal post-mortem report format requiring four sections—Chronology, Context, Empowerment, Solutions—for every production incident writeup at Grab.
evidence: Qualitative: the post contrasts a real deficient report (no chronology, no root-cause detail, no downtime numbers) against the new standard, and argues the standard is needed because most engineers "know how to grep logs" but only the responder has the tacit knowledge of exactly what to grep—knowledge the old reports failed to transfer.
why_it_matters: Shows a PM-relevant pattern: process standards for internal documentation (like post-mortem templates) are a lightweight, high-leverage lever for cross-team knowledge transfer in a microservices org.

title: Grab Holdings F-4 Registration Statement — Quick Cash for MSMEs in Thailand
publisher: SEC EDGAR (Grab Holdings Ltd. F-4 Registration Statement)
summary: In its F-4 registration statement for the SPAC merger that took it public, Grab describes how it used data from its ride-hailing and delivery platform — including how much its driver- and merchant-partners earn — to build credit profiles and extend responsible lending to underserved merchants. It launched Quick Cash for MSMEs in Thailand in 2020, described as one of the first fully digital, instant cash loan products for merchants in the country, aimed at helping small businesses affected by COVID-19 lockdowns bridge cash-flow gaps. The filing reports that in the six months ended June 30, 2021, the number of active Quick Cash loans in Thailand grew 13x, which Grab attributes to strong merchant demand for quick financing during the pandemic.
what_they_built: Quick Cash for MSMEs — a 100% digital, instant cash-loan product for merchant-partners in Thailand, underwritten using platform-derived income and transaction data to build credit profiles for otherwise underserved small businesses.
evidence: Active Quick Cash loans in Thailand grew 13x in the six months ended June 30, 2021, cited by Grab as evidence of strong merchant demand for digital instant cash loans during COVID-19 lockdowns.
why_it_matters: Shows how a platform can convert operational data exhaust (partner earnings/transaction history) into a defensible underwriting advantage for a financial-services cross-sell product launched directly into an existing partner base.

title: Reshaping Chat Support for Our Users
summary: Grab's support team found users preferred text-based chat over voice-only hotlines for its multitasking and documentation benefits, so they built an in-house chat support platform. They validated the approach with Google Design Sprint techniques (Crazy 8s, solution sketching) before development, then applied a Little's-law-based dynamic queue limit, an ML-powered autocomplete for agents, and a visual timer/nudge system to manage chat duration. Each intervention was measured independently against clear success metrics defined up front.
what_they_built: An in-house chat support platform with a Little's-law-driven dynamic queue limiter, ML-based reply autocomplete for agents, and a visual timer-and-nudge UI showing chat duration and queue pressure.
evidence: Reported outcomes: ~30% reduction in chat waiting time and ~7% reduction in unresponsive users from the queue-limit change; 12% reduction in average chat time from the autocomplete feature (despite 85% of agents still free-typing); 22% reduction in overall chat duration from the timer and nudge system.
why_it_matters: Shows how pairing a queueing-theory model with UX interventions (autocomplete, ambient nudges) and design-sprint validation can move support-ops metrics without simply adding headcount.

title: Streamlining RiskOps with the SOP agent framework
summary: Grab's RiskOps team manually investigated account-takeover (ATO) fraud tickets, a slow, inconsistent process. They built the SOP agent framework, splitting the work into an SOP Planner that generates the required investigation steps and API calls using a DFS-inspired traversal of a decision tree, and a Worker Agent that executes those steps (calling APIs/SQL, storing results) and feeds outcomes back into the plan. The framework produces a synthesized investigation report at the end of each case. After launch, it cut per-ticket handling time from 22 to 3 minutes, automated 87% of ATO cases, and reported a zero-error rate.
what_they_built: An internal SOP planner + worker agent framework that automates account-takeover fraud investigations by traversing a structured decision tree of investigative steps and executing the required API/SQL calls.
evidence: Reduction in handling time from 22 to 3 minutes per ticket; automation of 87% of ATO cases since launch; a zero-error rate reported post-launch.
why_it_matters: Shows how splitting an agentic system into a deterministic planner (structured SOP traversal) and a separate execution worker can make an internal trust-and-safety workflow both faster and safer to operationalize.

title: Data mesh at Grab part I: Building trust through certification
summary: As Grab's ride-hailing, food delivery, and financial services businesses scaled, its centralized data warehouse model became unwieldy, making it hard for consumers to trust which datasets were reliable. In 2024 Grab began building "Signals Marketplace," a data mesh that decentralizes ownership to domain teams and introduces a certification system so producers can guarantee schema, quality, and freshness. The system layers in Business and Technical Data Owners, formal data contracts, and automated Data Production Incidents that fire when quality tests fail and auto-assign tickets. The post reports early adoption and cleanup metrics from the certification push.
what_they_built: A data mesh governance layer (Signals Marketplace) with domain-owned data products, a dataset certification engine, formal data contracts, and automated incident tickets for data quality failures.
evidence: 75% of Grab queries now hit certified assets; deprecated tables increased 400% year-over-year; the number of P80 datasets (top 80% most-used data) fell by over 58% since the campaign started.
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete pattern for turning data quality/trust into a measurable, owned internal product rather than a vague governance initiative.

title: Understanding Supply & Demand in Ride-hailing Through the Lens of Data
summary: Grab's analytics team needed a way to quantify supply-demand imbalance across a two-sided ride-hailing marketplace in order to steer both drivers and passengers toward better matching. They built two geohash-based metrics — a Supply Demand Ratio and a Supply Demand Difference — treating idle online drivers as supply and fare-checking passengers as demand, with distance-weighted aggregation per location and time interval. These metrics were then productized into two shipped features: an updated driver-app heatmap that nudges drivers away from post-peak oversupplied zones (e.g. CBD after morning rush) toward undersupplied ones, and a passenger-facing Travel Trends widget showing predicted hourly demand patterns to shift non-urgent trips. The piece also discusses the computational cost of scaling the underlying algorithm as supply/demand units grow.
what_they_built: A geohash-based Supply Demand Ratio/Difference analytics system, surfaced as a driver heatmap (supply-side rebalancing) and a passenger Travel Trends widget (demand-side shaping).
evidence: The Travel Trends widget is reported as ranked #1 among all of Grab's widgets, used by "hundreds of thousands of people" daily; qualitative description of the heatmap's role in reducing CBD oversupply after morning peak.
why_it_matters: Shows how a PM can turn a raw two-sided marketplace metric (supply-demand ratio) into two distinct, targeted product interventions — one for each side of the marketplace — rather than a single blunt instrument.

title: Tourists on GrabChat!
summary: Grab's data team analyzed a sample of GrabChat usage by tourists across Singapore, Malaysia, and Indonesia from December 2018 to March 2019 to understand how travelers used the in-app auto-translation, message-template, and image-sharing features to communicate with drivers. They found over 3.7 million tourist-sent messages in the sample period, with usage patterns varying sharply by nationality and market (e.g., East Timor, Nigerian, and Ukrainian travelers sent the most messages per booking; Indonesian tourists translated most and used templates most). The team tied template usage directly to a business outcome: bookings where templates were used had a 10% higher completion rate. Image sharing clustered around high-traffic tourist hubs like Bali's Kuta beach and Kuala Lumpur's City Centre.
what_they_built: A data analysis of real-world usage of GrabChat's auto-translation, message-template, and photo-sharing features by tourists across three Southeast Asian markets, breaking down adoption by nationality, market, and use case.
evidence: 3.7+ million individual GrabChat messages sent by tourists in the sample period; template usage was 60% among Indonesian tourists vs 53% in Malaysia and 33% in Singapore; bookings using templates saw a 10% higher completion rate; East Timor, Nigerian, and Ukrainian travelers averaged 6.0, 5.6, and 5.1 messages per booking respectively.
why_it_matters: Shows how a PM can mine in-product communication data to validate which localization/UX features (translation, templates, images) actually drive a hard business metric like booking completion, and where usage clusters geographically.

title: Using Grab's Trust Counter Service to Detect Fraud Successfully
summary: Grab's fraud team needed a way to compute real-time behavioral counters (e.g. how many rides a driver-passenger pair has shared, how often a payment method is reused) across combinatorial fraud signals, without engineers hand-building a new pipeline for every rule data scientists wanted to test. They built the Trust Counter Service on top of Grab-Stats, a storage layer running above ScyllaDB, using multi-bucket time aggregation (15-minute, 1-hour, 1-day windows) and a UI-based rule editor so data scientists could self-serve counter definitions and expressions evaluated by a Counter Processor. The system was built to handle up to 100,000 QPS, saw peak-hour stream loads of about 5,000 QPS and ~4,000 counter writes, and hit a p99 read latency under 150ms, meeting their SLA, while costing roughly 10x less than an AWS ElastiCache-based alternative.
what_they_built: A self-service, ScyllaDB-backed counter/aggregation service (Trust Counter Service atop Grab-Stats) that lets data scientists define and run real-time fraud-detection counters via a UI rule editor and expression evaluator, without engineer involvement per rule.
evidence: Concrete production metrics: peak-hour stream throughput of ~5,000 QPS and ~4,000 counter writes/hour, designed for up to 100,000 QPS, p99 read latency from ScyllaDB under 150ms ("satisfies our SLA"), and a stated ~10x cost reduction versus AWS ElastiCache.
