why_it_matters: Shows a concrete pattern for trading engineer-per-rule bottlenecks for a self-serve rules/counter platform in a trust-and-safety domain, with the specific latency/cost tradeoffs (ScyllaDB vs. ElastiCache) a PM can cite when justifying platform investment for a similar self-serve detection use case.

title: Measuring Marketing Effectiveness in a Cookie-Less World
summary: Grammarly's Data Science team faced the classic top-of-funnel attribution problem: traditional first/last/multi-touch models undervalue video and brand advertising and can't separate correlation from causation. They built a geo-experimentation framework that splits the 210 US Designated Market Areas into control and "go-dark" test groups, using Google's open-source TBR package (Bayesian structural time series) to build a synthetic counterfactual baseline and measure incremental lift, applying it first to YouTube marketing spend. Because Grammarly's freemium model creates a long lag between signup and Premium revenue, they deliberately chose "new active users" rather than revenue as the primary KPI so lift could be measured within the test window. YouTube experiments showed substantial incremental lift outside peak season that moderated during high-demand periods, with minimal lag effects.
what_they_built: A geo-experimentation (go-dark) testing framework using DMA-level control/test splits and a Bayesian structural time-series causal-inference model (Google's TBR package) to measure the true incremental impact of ad channel spend, starting with YouTube.
evidence: Qualitative/directional findings reported: YouTube incremental lift was "substantial" outside peak seasons and moderated during high-demand periods (attributed to organic demand crowding out ad impact), and lag effects between ad exposure and conversion were minimal, suggesting the channel operates more downstream than assumed.
why_it_matters: Shows PMs a concrete, transferable pattern for choosing a KPI proxy (new active users over revenue) when the "true" business metric is too many funnel-steps removed from the marketing lever being tested.

title: Taming the Storm: Building Groww's Internal Chaos Engineering Platform
summary: Groww's engineering team found that synthetic load tests and isolated staging environments missed subtle failure modes — small Redis or SQL slowdowns cascading into user-visible latency spikes under real traffic patterns. To close that gap they built an internal Chaos Testing Platform on a dedicated load-test Kubernetes cluster, using a Traffic-Replayer to mirror real production traffic, Argo Workflows as the orchestration control plane, and Chaos Mesh (chosen over Chaos Monkey and LitmusChaos for its Kubernetes-native CRDs and workflow chaining) as the fault-injection engine, with failures correlated against production dashboards via their internal observability tool Olly. Ahead of Groww's IPO, the platform was used to run pod-kill and simulated SQL-downtime tests against IPO-facing services to validate autoscaling and failover behavior before real traffic hit.
what_they_built: A multi-cluster internal chaos-engineering platform combining a production-traffic replayer, Argo Workflows for experiment orchestration, and Chaos Mesh for Kubernetes-native fault injection, integrated with their Olly observability stack.
evidence: Qualitative outcome: run against IPO-facing services before Groww's listing, the pod-kill and simulated-SQL-downtime tests "did not discover major resiliency gaps" but "provided significant confidence by validating autoscaling behaviour under load and graceful degradation paths." No quantitative metrics are given.
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete pattern — mirrored production traffic plus declarative, chained fault injection isolated from prod — for building organizational confidence in reliability ahead of a high-stakes event, transferable to any PM shipping into a traffic spike or launch window.

title: Rewriting Legacy Systems: The Safe and Calm Way
summary: Groww's engineering team faced a critical, high-traffic delivery order validation system (handling a large share of Indian trades) that had become risky and slow to change due to years of accumulated legacy complexity. Rather than a rewrite from scratch, they hotwired new logic into existing services, verified correctness via shadow-mode traffic forking and reconciliation dashboards comparing old vs. new outputs, then rolled out gradually starting at roughly 5% of traffic, followed by an internal "war-room" feedback session, all backed by a fast feature-flag-based rollback plan. The rewrite achieved zero downtime, a 37% (26ms) latency reduction, a 10% error-rate reduction, and surfaced four previously hidden legacy bugs.
what_they_built: A shadow-mode traffic-forking and reconciliation system plus a staged, feature-flag-gated rollout process used to safely replace a critical trading order-validation system with zero downtime.
evidence: Reported outcomes: 37% reduction in latency (26ms improvement), 10% reduction in error rates, zero downtime during the transition, and discovery of four previously hidden legacy bugs.
why_it_matters: Gives PMs and platform leads a concrete, staged de-risking playbook (shadow mode, reconciliation dashboards, percentage rollouts, war-room feedback, tested rollback) for migrating high-stakes production systems without downtime.

title: Before the Bell Rings: How Groww Prepares for Market Open
summary: Groww runs a fintech app across equity, F&O, commodities, mutual funds, SIP, MTF, IPO and UPI, each on a different exchange schedule and failure surface, and needed a repeatable way to be ready before markets open. They built a four-checkpoint daily cadence (5am beginning-of-day check, 8:30-9:15am prod-readiness call with named owners per domain, 3:30pm post-market check, 11:30pm end-of-day reconciliation) backed by three layers of detection (rule-based SLO alerting, AI anomaly detection, human monitoring by Product Support/Solution Engineers) and a custom LGTM-stack dashboard with six views (SLO rules, API gateway, on-call alerts, per-route app health, capacity/memory, consumer lag). A concrete decision trace is included: they rejected a proposed 5-minute delay before paging on transient alert spikes because it would let a real incident go undetected for nearly 7 minutes, opting instead to fix noise at the source by deleting ownerless alert rules.
what_they_built: A daily four-checkpoint operational readiness process plus a custom observability dashboard (on a Loki/Grafana/Tempo/Mimir stack) combining rule-based SLO alerting, AI anomaly detection, and human monitoring across all of Groww's trading and payment product surfaces.
evidence: Over 10 days the system generated 13,000+ alerts, most auto-resolving within minutes; the team calculated that a proposed 5-minute pre-page delay plus their 45-second metric-collection latency would leave a real incident undetected for nearly 7 minutes, so they rejected the delay and instead deleted alert rules lacking an owner and remediation path.
why_it_matters: Shows a PM-relevant tradeoff pattern: when tuning alert/notification thresholds, model the actual detection-latency math (not just "reduce noise") before trading signal speed for quieter pagers.

title: Engineering for Instantaneity: How a Team's Persistence Transformed Our Trading Platform
summary: Groww's engineering team faced distributed, multi-layer latency in its trading order pipeline that was hurting slippage and user outcomes, especially during market-open bursts. They built deep observability (distributed tracing with Honeycomb, custom metrics, latency-bisection dashboards) to find the sources, then ran a six-phase program of architectural simplification (consolidating position services, cutting order-flow hops from 7+ to ~5), workload isolation via controlled experimentation (separating AMO/fresh orders, priority queues, earlier validation), and infrastructure migrations (database, message queue, caching, network). The result was large measured latency and cost improvements across the pipeline.
what_they_built: A six-phase latency-reduction program spanning distributed observability tooling, order-flow architectural simplification, peak-time workload isolation/experimentation, and infrastructure migrations (database, message queue, network SDKs, Redis caching) for Groww's trading order pipeline.
evidence: Cash order 99th-percentile latency improved ~90%, FNO ~80%, at market open; position update web-flow latency down ~50%, non-web ~60%; message queue latency 350ms to 50ms; order-processing consumer latency improved 66%, producer peak latency reduced 97%; DB commit latency dropped from ~11-12ms to ~1-2ms; infrastructure costs reduced ~35%; log volume dropped from 108GB to 30GB/day.
why_it_matters: Shows a PM-relevant pattern for any latency-sensitive product surface: instrument first, then simplify architecture before chasing infra upgrades, and tie every latency metric back to a concrete user/business outcome (here, slippage).

title: Incident Review: Working as Designed, But Still Failing
summary: Honeycomb's trigger performance degraded for days and was initially blamed on Lambda capacity exhaustion caused by triggers accessing cold storage due to a customer's future-timestamped telemetry. The incident commander's early framing of the problem dominated the team's investigation for roughly 9 hours, leading to mitigations that only addressed symptoms. A fresh, uninvolved engineer eventually traced the real cause to Basset, the SLO evaluation service, which was continuously restarting a 60-day cold-data backfill every minute for a single customer SLO that never returned valid results, consuming the Lambda capacity the team had misattributed to triggers.
what_they_built: A post-incident writeup documenting the failure mode and the fixes: ingest-time constraints limiting the trigger/Lambda dependency and tiered alerting to prevent degradation from being normalized.
evidence: Qualitative: the team identified and fixed the root cause (Basset's repeated 60-day backfill loop), implemented ingest-time controls to limit the problematic usage pattern, added tiered alerting, and reevaluated what counts as standard workload as the company scales. No numeric before/after metrics were given in the retrieved text.
why_it_matters: Shows PMs and platform builders how early framing by whoever is "in the room" during an incident can trap an investigation, and why bringing in fresh, uninvolved eyes and hardening resource-sharing boundaries are concrete process fixes.

title: Incident Review: What Comes Up Must First Go Down
summary: On July 25, 2023, Honeycomb suffered a full-platform outage lasting 68 minutes, taking down all user-facing components — its most severe incident since acquiring paying customers. A cluster switch left a feature-flag-controlled cache-write path silently disabled, causing database read load to spike and triggering a rare MySQL internal deadlock that exhausted connections and took down the primary database. Recovery required circuit-breaking ingest traffic, failing the database over to a replica, and manually resetting schema timestamps to rebuild the cache.
what_they_built: A detailed public incident review tracing a feature-flag bug through cache failure to a MySQL deadlock, paired with concrete remediation: removing the feature-flag code path that could disable cache writes and introducing new database failover procedures.
evidence: 68-minute total outage across all user-facing components, described as the company's most severe incident since it began serving paying customers; recovery required database failover and manual schema-timestamp resets to restore service.
why_it_matters: Shows how PM-trusted reliability practices (feature flags, frequent deploys, deploy freezes) can themselves compound into a severe outage, underscoring the need to test flag-toggle reversibility and cache-dependency failure modes before trusting them operationally.

title: Honeycomb Incident Report: Kafka Maintenance in May 2026
summary: Honeycomb replaced a decade-old, self-managed Kafka cluster with a modernized EKS-based setup using KRaft and open-source tiered storage, because the old infrastructure had node recovery times of 8-12 hours (up to 48-72 hours) and AZ-wide failures that could take weeks to fix. The maintenance window caused about 25 minutes of unqueryable data in the EU instance and roughly 1.5 hours in the US instance, with no data loss but visible dashboard/trigger/SLO anomalies. The real failure was organisational: advance notice wasn't broad enough, impact language was ambiguous ("gap in visible data" was read as hours of missing indexing rather than a few hours of lag), and customer-facing teams weren't looped into planning until close to the migration date. Honeycomb's fix was process, not infra: new communication channels with named owners and stakeholders for future maintenance events.
what_they_built: A migration of Honeycomb's core Kafka ingestion cluster from legacy self-managed nodes to an EKS-hosted, KRaft-based cluster with tiered storage, paired with a new maintenance-communication protocol with named owners and channels.
evidence: Concrete before/after infra metrics (node recovery time went from 8-12 hours historically up to 48-72 hours on the old cluster, vs. AZ failures that could take weeks) plus measured customer impact (~25 min unqueryable data in EU, ~1.5 hours in US, zero data loss); the post-mortem explicitly names the root cause as a missing process for looping in customer-facing teams and commits to specific new communication ownership as remediation.
why_it_matters: Shows a PM/eng org that a technically clean, zero-data-loss infra migration can still register as a customer-trust failure if the comms plan and impact language aren't treated with the same rigor as the technical plan.

title: Incident Report: Running Dry on Memory Without Noticing
summary: A slow memory leak across Honeycomb's ingest backends caused coordinated crashes in four 20-minute windows, rejecting 1-3% of customer telemetry data at ingest — enough to threaten breaching their quarterly <0.1% error budget. On-call engineers initially fixated on a wrong hypothesis (AWS ALB failure) and didn't search for alternative explanations, believing they could only wait on a third party. The postmortem documents the root cause (a memory leak affecting all ingest backends equally) and the resulting process change: promoting SLO burn-rate alerts to page on-call the same way blackbox probes do, to catch this failure mode faster and reduce diagnostic tunnel vision next time.
what_they_built: A self-published incident postmortem plus a concrete alerting change: SLO error-budget burn alerts were promoted to paging severity, on par with end-to-end blackbox probe alerts.
evidence: Reports concrete outcome numbers: 1-3% of customer telemetry rejected at ingest across four 20-minute incidents, against a quarterly error budget of <0.1% total errors; explicit lesson that confirmation bias delayed correct diagnosis, leading to the SLO-alert-to-paging process change.
why_it_matters: Shows a PM/platform team how to turn a data-quality/reliability incident into a concrete, measurable process change (promoting SLO burn alerts to paging) rather than a vague "be more careful" lesson.

title: Incident Report: The Missing Trigger Notification Emails
summary: A November 2022 deployment meant to enrich business-intelligence telemetry silently broke Honeycomb's trigger-notification emails: roughly 94.1% of alert emails failed to send for four days, and the outage was only caught because a customer reported it. The root cause traced to an engineer adding request metadata without realizing the third-party email SDK's error-handling contract, combined with uninstrumented API calls, mocked-not-real integration tests, and an SLO that never actually detected the hidden failures. Honeycomb's fix added instrumentation around the SDK boundary, moved to integration testing against the real partner API instead of mocks, and validated that the SLO now correctly surfaces this failure class.
what_they_built: A public incident post-mortem plus concrete engineering fixes: added tracing/instrumentation at the third-party email SDK boundary, replaced mocked API tests with integration tests against the real partner API, and corrected an SLO that had been blind to this failure mode.
evidence: 94.1% of trigger notification emails failed to send over a 4-day window (Nov 18-22, 2022); the failure was undetected by monitoring/SLOs and only surfaced when a customer reported it.
why_it_matters: Shows a PM/platform team why "we have an SLO" isn't enough — the observability and test coverage around third-party dependency boundaries has to actually exercise real failure modes, not mocks.

title: Carbon: Designing inside Big Blue
summary: IBM had over 2,000 products company-wide and needed a way to keep Bluemix, which grew from basic functionality at its 2014 launch to over 130 services, visually and functionally consistent. The Carbon design system team built for two goals: serving both designers and engineers, and being easy for service teams to adopt. They semantically versioned the system so engineers could trust it wouldn't break their code without warning, distributed it through standard channels (npm and bower) to fit developers' existing workflows, and built every component to meet WCAG AA accessibility standards including color-contrast and keyboard-navigation checks.
what_they_built: A component-based design system (Carbon) with semantic versioning, npm/bower package distribution, and WCAG AA-compliant components, plus documentation aimed at non-designers.
evidence: Bluemix scaled from basic functionality at its June 2014 launch to over 130 different services (plus infrastructure offerings) by the time of writing, attributed in part to the trust and adoption mechanisms described; qualitative evidence of engineer trust from semver discipline rather than a hard adoption-rate metric.
why_it_matters: Shows a PM/design-systems owner how packaging and versioning discipline (treating a design system "like a product") is what actually drives internal adoption, not just component quality.

title: The power to serve: lessons on design system team operation and workflows
summary: IBM.com's Carbon design system team faced a huge, globally distributed base of adopter teams under deadline pressure who were tempted to bypass the system with custom hacks, while the small system team risked becoming either a bottleneck or overwhelmed by requests. They restructured around a service-design model: self-serve public docs/GitHub, a rotating "fire line" support team, twice-weekly cross-functional triage, weekly office hours, embedded "Away Missions," and a disciplined two-week release cadence with published freeze dates. They tracked adoption via percentage of IBM.com pageviews served by Carbon-built pages rather than by legacy design systems.
what_they_built: The Carbon for IBM.com design system leadership team restructured how the team operates as a service rather than a one-off project: a rotating "fire line" support roster (designer + developers) triaging Slack/GitHub questions, a twice-weekly "four-in-the-box" (design lead, dev lead, product owner, PM) triage of GitHub issues, weekly open office hours, "Away Missions" where team members embed with adopter teams, and a disciplined two-week release cadence with published design-freeze, PR-submission, and code-freeze dates so adopters could plan around a transparent, predictable process.
evidence: Pages built with Carbon for IBM.com grew from 6.2% of all IBM.com pageviews at the start of the year to 44.8% by the end of November (seven-fold growth in about ten months), while pageviews on the deprecated Northstar system fell from 54% to 21%, indicating owners were actively migrating existing pages rather than adoption coming only from new pages.
why_it_matters: Shows platform/design-system PMs a concrete operating model — support rotations, triage rituals, and a published release calendar — for scaling adoption of a shared design system across many autonomous internal teams without becoming a bottleneck, with adoption tracked via a single pageview-share KPI.

title: Consistency in the Cloud
summary: IBM Cloud's Platform Integration Experiences (Cloud PAL) team faced a fragmented UI across 155+ services and needed to move the whole portfolio from Carbon 9 to Carbon 10 without breaking teams mid-flight. They built a Cloud-specific Pattern and Asset Library with 22 patterns covering 90% of Cloud UIs (including "connected components" with APIs built in), ran the migration through governance reviews with priority-level requirements, and staged it as a four-phase rollout: ship Carbon 10 components under the old theme, toggle the theme, adopt Cloud PAL patterns, then move to continuous delivery. A visual design guild iterated on the patterns via weekly feedback sessions with the core Carbon team. By January 2021, 116 of 155 services had adopted Carbon 10.</summary> <parameter name="what_they_built">A Cloud-specific pattern library (Cloud PAL) with 22 reusable, API-connected UI patterns, plus a staged four-phase governance process for migrating all IBM Cloud services from Carbon 9 to Carbon 10.
what_they_built: The IBM Cloud team migrated hundreds of Cloud services from Carbon 9 to Carbon 10, building the Cloud Pattern and Asset Library (PAL)—22 coded templates and patterns covering 90% of Cloud interfaces—and rolled it out in four sequential phases with governance reviews at each stage.
evidence: Quantified outcomes reported on the page: NPS improved 57% in the first three months post-transition, seven steps were cut from user journeys with a 4% improvement in average service-provisioning time, 88% of users preferred the new screens, support tickets dropped 18%, 116 of 155 services had adopted Carbon 10 as of January 2021, and design/development teams saved roughly 2,000 hours per pattern reused.
why_it_matters: Shows a PM-transferable playbook for large-scale design-system migrations: stage the rollout in reversible phases (theme-only, then pattern adoption, then continuous delivery), gate it with governance/priority tiers rather than a big-bang cutover, and instrument it with both efficiency metrics (support tickets, provisioning time) and perception metrics (NPS, preference) to prove the case.

title: What to do when your design system isn't enough
summary: After IBM merged infrastructure and platform services into IBM Cloud, the shared Carbon design system (34 components, 12 patterns) still wasn't enough to unify the experience across 200+ products because teams implemented Carbon differently within their own product areas, producing visual and interaction inconsistencies. IBM Design built Cloud PAL (Pattern and Asset Library), a curated layer on top of Carbon offering four component types — compound, connected, hosted, and layout — plus governance practices like shared ownership and recurring cross-team critique to keep the library from becoming one team's side project. The post reports the initiative's impact on user perception of IBM Cloud as a unified product.
what_they_built: Cloud PAL, a product-specific pattern and component library layered on top of IBM's Carbon design system, with defined component types and cross-team governance to keep implementations consistent across IBM Cloud's many product teams.
evidence: Reports NPS increased 57% and positive brand association rose 88%, with users associating IBM Cloud more with power, innovation, and security after the changes.
why_it_matters: Shows a PM/design leader that a shared component library alone doesn't guarantee product cohesion — you also need a governance layer (shared ownership, cross-team critique) enforcing consistent implementation.

title: IBM Commerce Platform
summary: The IBM.com Commerce Platform team needed to modernize an aging checkout flow without disrupting an active revenue channel. Over roughly three months (Jan-Apr 2020), they trained the team on Carbon Design System tokens and terminology, then adopted Carbon incrementally rather than in one big rewrite: starting with a self-contained new feature (a country-switching modal), mixing new Carbon components into the existing interface, replacing individual components (inputs, buttons) before restructuring whole pages, and building a global address form with real-time validation. The rollout avoided large simultaneous changes to reduce risk to a live commerce flow.</summary> <parameter name="what_they_built">A redesigned IBM.com checkout flow migrated onto the Carbon Design System, including a new country-switching modal and a global address form with real-time validation, rolled out incrementally alongside the legacy interface.
what_they_built: The IBM.com Commerce Platform team redesigned their e-commerce checkout flow by migrating from custom UI components to the Carbon Design System, iteratively replacing a modal, then individual inputs and buttons, then full page layouts—and adding a global address form that validated addresses as users entered them.
evidence: Conversion rate increased by 5%, with over 50% of customers converting to purchase (bringing IBM.com to industry-benchmark conversion levels); the team also reports increased orders/revenue, faster development cycles, and reduced QA burden after full adoption.
why_it_matters: Shows PMs a low-risk pattern for migrating a critical revenue flow onto a new design system incrementally (single new feature first, then component-by-component swap) rather than a risky big-bang rewrite, while tying the effort to a measurable conversion outcome.

title: Auto Approval: como o iFood acelerou a revisão de código em 33% com avaliação automática de risco
summary: iFood's engineering org faced slow merge-request review times as MR volume grew, with reviewers spending time on low-risk changes that didn't need deep scrutiny. They built Auto Approval, a system that filters eligible MRs (excluding sensitive repos and diffs over 30 lines), applies static heuristic checks, then uses Gemini 2.5 Flash to score diff risk (0.1-1.0) with contextual repo information, auto-approving scores at or below 0.4 and routing the rest to human review. The system runs on iFood's internal GenPlat generative-AI platform, which supplies rate limiting, privacy controls, and multi-provider fallback. Rolled out at scale, it cut global MR approval time from 65 to 43 hours (about 33%) and cut approval time for small MRs (under 30 lines) from 12 to 5 hours (58%), while analyzing roughly 16,000 MRs/month and auto-approving about 7,000 of them at under $90/month in inference cost.
what_they_built: An AI-assisted auto-approval system for low-risk merge requests that combines eligibility filtering, static heuristics, and an LLM (Gemini 2.5 Flash) risk-scoring step to automatically approve safe code changes and route riskier ones to human reviewers.
evidence: Global MR approval time dropped from 65 to 43 hours (~33% reduction); approval time for MRs under 30 lines dropped from 12 to 5 hours (~58% reduction); as of August 2025, ~16,000 MRs/month were evaluated with ~7,000 auto-approved; average analysis latency was 13.07 seconds per MR; operating cost was under $90/month.
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete pattern for PMs building internal developer platforms: use a cheap, fast LLM risk-scoring gate with a conservative threshold and clear scope limits (line-count cap, sensitive-repo exclusion) to safely automate a slow human process while measuring both throughput and cost.

title: Canary 2.0: how iFood turned production deploy into a safe and automated process
summary: iFood's original canary-deploy tooling (Canary 1.0) had grown organizationally unmaintainable and technically weak: it was tightly coupled to manual GitLab pipeline steps, 21% of deploys skipped canary entirely, 48% used aggressive progression, and canary/stable pods ran on separate auto-scalers so promotion risked overload. The platform team rebuilt it as Canary 2.0 on Kubernetes and Argo Rollouts, adding a deploy API with unified config, a Tompero CLI for full rollout visibility and control from the terminal, structured progression stages with business-hour "freezing windows," a Canary-on-Branch mode to validate pre-merge, and an Auto Abort feature that ties Datadog monitors and regression tests directly into automatic rollback. The rebuild changed both behavior (near-universal canary adoption, aggressive progressions largely eliminated) and outcomes (fewer incidents), and was rolled out to hundreds of services with no incidents during migration.
what_they_built: Canary 2.0: a Kubernetes/Argo Rollouts-based progressive-deploy platform with a deploy API, the Tompero CLI (get/promote/abort/retry/restart), structured 0-100% rollout stages with peak-hour freezing windows, branch-level canary validation, and an Auto Abort mechanism wired to Datadog and regression tests.
evidence: "37% reduction in total deployment-related incidents between semester cycles"; mitigable incidents dropped 47%; retrospective analysis found "43% of deployment-related incidents from the previous cycle would have been avoided if Auto Abort had been active"; canary-flow adoption rose from 79% to near-universal and aggressive-progression deploys fell from 48% to minimal; 881 non-critical services migrated across 3 waves over ~3 weeks with zero incidents.
why_it_matters: Shows how tightening the default path (structured progression, safe/instant abort, and org-wide auto-rollback) rather than just adding tooling optionality is what actually moves incident-rate metrics at platform scale.

title: 30+ clusters, zero planilhas: nossa jornada para scaling inteligente com o Foodscale
summary: iFood's infrastructure teams relied on manual Slack coordination, spreadsheets, and unapproved Python scripts to scale Kubernetes capacity ahead of high-demand events like Black Friday, a process that took 4-5 days and left no audit trail. A near-miss AWS EC2 provisioning incident, where emergency scaling would have required hours of manual coordination, pushed the team to build Foodscale, an internal orchestration platform. The platform evolved through three stages -- a Go CLI querying Datadog for historical peaks, a Temporal.io-based orchestrator with approval gates, and finally a pluggable architecture decoupled from Kubernetes HPAs -- and now runs formally-approved, auditable, auto-rollback-capable scaling across 30+ production clusters in minutes.
what_they_built: Foodscale, a distributed Go-based orchestration platform (three microservices: foodscale-api, foodscale-workflow, foodscale-sync) built on Temporal.io that automates and audits infrastructure capacity scaling across Kubernetes clusters.
evidence: Preparation time for scaling high-demand events dropped from 4-5 days of manual coordination to minutes; the platform now orchestrates scaling with full data-driven timing, traceability, and approval gates across 30+ production clusters, and executed infrastructure-wide pre-scaling during a live AWS incident in minutes versus the hours manual coordination previously required.
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete pattern for PMs building internal platforms: replace an ad hoc, tribal-knowledge operational process with a staged build (CLI -> workflow engine -> pluggable platform) that pairs automation with governance (approval gates, audit trail, rollback) rather than removing human control entirely.

title: The Next Era of Data at Instacart
summary: Instacart's data ecosystem had become fragmented — multiple event-tracking frameworks, a dozen-plus Airflow instances, and inconsistent governance — creating a real tax on using data. A cross-org engineering initiative called "Ground Truth" consolidated event instrumentation into a single system (Snowgoose), unified pipeline orchestration on dbt + Airflow, built a company-wide declarative data-quality-check platform (DDC), rolled out fine-grained access control via Immuta, and replaced their BI tool with Mode. The post reports concrete adoption and reliability outcomes and lays out a follow-on strategy to decentralize data-building work now that the platform foundation is in place.
what_they_built: A consolidated internal data platform ("Ground Truth"): a single event-instrumentation framework (Snowgoose) replacing three prior systems, a company-wide declarative data-quality-check platform (DDC), CDC-based warehouse ingestion, a unified dbt+Airflow pipeline stack, Immuta-based access policies, and a Mode Analytics BI rollout.
evidence: Snowgoose handles billions of events (hundreds of terabytes/day) across thousands of event types; DDC has hundreds of critical data validation checks across teams; dbt has thousands of data models from hundreds of authors; tens of thousands of columns annotated for governance; Mode reached 8,000+ dashboards in 6 months while cutting failed dashboard runs 7x and Snowflake costs 3x versus the prior BI tool.
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete, metric-backed playbook for consolidating fragmented internal data tooling into a governed self-serve platform before trying to decentralize data ownership across product teams.

title: Optimizing Search Relevance at Instacart Using Hybrid Retrieval
summary: Instacart's search team needed to retrieve relevant items from a 1.4 billion-document catalog spanning 1500+ retailers, where query distributions and specificity vary wildly by retailer. They built a hybrid retrieval architecture that runs PostgreSQL-based text search (GIN indexes) alongside FAISS-based semantic search over embedding vectors, then merges the two candidate sets using Reciprocal Rank Fusion and convex combinations of lexical and semantic scores. Instead of pulling a fixed number of candidates from each retrieval path, they introduced an adaptive recall mechanism keyed on query entropy (a measure of query specificity) to dynamically size the recall set per query and retailer, reducing wasteful over-fetching. Production testing showed measurable gains in ranking quality and efficiency.
what_they_built: A hybrid search retrieval system combining PostgreSQL text search and FAISS semantic (embedding-based) search, merged via Reciprocal Rank Fusion/convex combination, with an adaptive query-entropy-driven mechanism to dynamically size how many candidates are pulled from each retrieval path.
evidence: Reported production improvements: mean converting position improved by 1.7% and latency reduced by 1.5%.
why_it_matters: Shows PMs how to think about retrieval-quality tradeoffs (recall breadth vs. latency) as a tunable, query-adaptive system parameter rather than a fixed engineering constant.

title: Supercharging Discovery in Search with LLMs
summary: Instacart's search results balanced relevance with user demand for inspirational, discovery-driven content, but pre-LLM approaches to the "Related Items" section were limited — returning loosely related products (e.g. cookies for a "croissant" search) or missing complementary follow-up intents. The team built two LLM-based content generation techniques (a basic query-to-LLM approach and an advanced approach fed with query-understanding signals, historical conversions, and search logs) to generate complementary and substitute product carousels with LLM-written titles. They further extended this by feeding the LLM sequences of "next converted search terms" (what users buy after an initial purchase) to reduce bias from single-query context.
what_they_built: LLM-powered content generation pipeline integrated into Instacart Search that produces discovery-oriented product carousels (complementary and substitute items) with LLM-generated titles, using both direct query prompting and Instacart-specific signals (query understanding, conversion history, search logs, and cross-query purchase sequences).
evidence: Online experiments showed the next-converted-search-terms methodology led to an 18% improvement in engagement rate with the inspirational content; the team also reports "notable boosts in user engagement and revenue" from the broader approach, though only the 18% figure is quantified.
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete pattern for using LLMs to augment (not replace) an existing relevance-ranked search stack with a secondary discovery objective, validated through online A/B experimentation rather than offline model metrics alone.

title: Building The Intent Engine: How Instacart is Revamping Query Understanding with LLMs
summary: Instacart's legacy query-understanding stack used separate bespoke models per task (FastText classification, session-mined rewrites), which struggled with broad/ambiguous queries, noisy pseudo-labels, and long-tail/rare searches, and only covered 50% of traffic for rewrites. The team consolidated these into an LLM-based pipeline layered as prompting, RAG context-engineering, and fine-tuning, applied across three components: category classification (retrieve-then-rerank with post-processing guardrails), query rewrites (specialized prompts for substitutes/broader/synonyms), and a hybrid offline "teacher" (RAG) / real-time fine-tuned "student" (Llama-3-8B, LoRA) system for semantic role labeling on long-tail queries. They report specific production results and describe the latency-reduction engineering (adapter merging, H100 upgrade, quantization trade-offs, GPU autoscaling) needed to hit a ~300ms serving target.
what_they_built: A unified LLM-based query-understanding system for search — LLM-powered category classification and query rewrites, plus a hybrid offline-RAG-teacher / real-time-fine-tuned-student (Llama-3-8B + LoRA) architecture for semantic role labeling that serves head queries from a cache and tail queries via a low-latency fine-tuned model.
evidence: Query rewrite coverage rose to over 95% with 90%+ precision across all three rewrite types (vs. 50% traffic coverage previously); the fine-tuned 8B SRL model matched the larger teacher model's F1 (95.7% vs 95.8%) with higher precision (96.4% vs 95.4%); A/B testing showed average scroll depth for tail queries dropped 6% and user complaints about poor search results for tail queries fell 50%; latency was cut from ~700ms to a 300ms target via adapter merging and H100 GPUs, with only 2% of queries needing real-time inference due to caching.
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete pattern — offline LLM pipeline to bootstrap cache + training data, then a small fine-tuned model for real-time long-tail serving — for shipping LLM-driven product features under strict latency and cost constraints, with A/B-verified UX impact rather than just offline model metrics.

title: How Instacart Uses Machine Learning-Driven Autocomplete to Help People Fill Their Carts
summary: Instacart's search team needed autocomplete suggestions that were both accurate (correct matches, typo-tolerant, deduplicated) and commercially useful (driving add-to-cart, not just clicks). They built a vocabulary of ~785k autocomplete terms derived from 11.3M products, added fuzzy matching for misspellings, used a search-embeddings model for semantic deduplication, and used the standardized cross-retailer catalog to solve cold start for new retailers and tail queries. Ranking evolved from simple popularity, to a binary engagement classifier, to a multi-objective model that combines an autocomplete-engagement model with an add-to-cart model via P(add to cart | prefix) = P(AC conversion | prefix) x P(add to cart | term). Each stage was validated with A/B tests reporting engagement, conversion, ad revenue, and GTV impact.
what_they_built: A machine-learning-driven autocomplete pipeline (vocabulary construction, fuzzy matching, embeddings-based semantic dedup, cross-retailer cold-start augmentation) paired with a ranking model that evolved from popularity-based to a multi-objective model balancing autocomplete engagement against add-to-cart probability.
evidence: Reported stagewise A/B results: fuzzy matching +1% autocomplete engagement and +0.5% query conversion; engagement model +2% items added per search and +1.6% ad revenue; cold-start solution +0.8% new-user autocomplete rate and +0.7% basket size; multi-objective ranking model +2.7% autocomplete engagement, +0.3% search conversion, and an increase in GTV per user.
why_it_matters: Shows a PM-relevant pattern for search/recommendation surfaces: moving a ranking objective from single-signal engagement to a multi-objective formula tied to downstream business value (add-to-cart), validated stage by stage with production A/B metrics rather than offline model scores.

title: Building for Balance
summary: Instacart's Marketplace team needed a system to keep shopper supply and customer demand in balance across geographies, coordinating three levers: shopper onboarding, shopper incentives, and customer incentives. They built SAGE, an internal optimization engine that forecasts supply-demand imbalance and allocates lever spend within ROI thresholds, launching v1 in 2020 using linear programming pooled across all markets. A year later they rebuilt it as v2, moving to multi-week mixed-integer programming with region-level parallel solving (AWS/Celery) to handle nonlinear efficiency curves and reduce spillover in A/B tests. The migration to v2 reduced customer lost demand, increased shopper utilization in healthy markets, and lowered aggregate spend while improving solve times.
what_they_built: SAGE, a marketplace supply-demand balancing engine that forecasts imbalance and optimizes allocation across shopper onboarding, shopper incentives, and customer incentive levers; v2 rearchitected it from single global linear programming to multi-week, region-parallelized mixed-integer programming (Python-MIP).
evidence: Reported outcomes from the v1-to-v2 migration: reduced customer lost demand (delivery slots unavailable due to supply shortfall), increased shopper access to work/utilization in healthy markets after scaling back interventions there, a "notable reduction in aggregate spend levels," and shorter solve times (v1 solves previously exceeded one hour) plus reduced A/B test spillover from region-level parallelization.
why_it_matters: Shows how a two-sided marketplace can formalize competing supply/demand levers into one constrained-optimization system and rearchitect it as scale and lever complexity grow, rather than relying on ad hoc ops-team heuristics.

title: Real-time Fraud Detection with Yoda and ClickHouse
summary: Instacart's fraud and risk team needed a way to catch fraudulent shopper and customer behavior in real time without engineers hand-coding every heuristic. They built Yoda, a self-serve fraud decision platform where analysts author rules against pre-defined features in a UI, backed by ClickHouse as the real-time datastore: PostgreSQL changes flow through Debezium into Kafka, an in-house ingester and config-driven Flink jobs batch-insert events into ClickHouse, and dynamic SQL queries retrieve features fast enough to support sub-second decisioning (warnings, suspensions, selfie ID checks). The shift moved rule creation from engineers to ops/analysts directly. Results cited: multi-million dollar annual savings from real-time catches, and engineer time spent on heuristic development dropped from roughly 80% to 20%.
what_they_built: Yoda, a real-time fraud decisioning platform combining a self-serve rules-authoring UI with a ClickHouse-backed feature store fed by a Debezium/Kafka/Flink CDC pipeline for sub-second fraud decisions.
evidence: The article states real-time detection "has already resulted in millions of dollars a year in savings," and that moving rule creation to a self-serve model dropped the share of engineer time spent on heuristic development from about 80% to 20%.
why_it_matters: Shows a PM-relevant pattern for trust-and-safety platforms: investing in a self-serve rules/feature layer so domain experts (not engineers) iterate on fraud logic can simultaneously cut losses and free up engineering capacity.

title: Maximizing user research across a product's lifecycle
summary: Intercom embeds researchers directly inside product teams rather than dispatching them ad hoc, and the researcher describes running two different research models depending on product maturity. For the brand-new Smart Campaigns product, the team was in a constant state of hypothesis creation and testing: multiple rounds of customer interviews, concept tests, a stream of research messages, and three separate betas covering six product variations before launch. For the more mature Messenger, the team instead ran eight rounds of in-person usability testing in Dublin plus remote studies with international participants to iteratively refine an existing design. The piece argues that being embedded and adjusting research cadence/method to the product's lifecycle stage is what let research keep pace with fast iteration.</summary> <parameter name="what_they_built">An embedded-researcher operating model with two distinct research playbooks — "lean research" (rapid interview/concept-test/beta cycles) for new 0-to-1 products and "iterative research" (repeated usability testing rounds) for mature products being redesigned.
what_they_built: Intercom built a maturity-based user research operating model that matches research approach to product stage: a "lean research" mode where researchers embed full-time with new-product teams to run rapid small-scale hypothesis testing (e.g. testing six variations of Smart Campaigns), and an "iterative research" mode where researchers stay closely connected to established-product teams to run recurring usability testing cycles (e.g. eight rounds of testing for the Messenger redesign, roughly every three weeks).
evidence: Concrete process counts rather than business metrics: Smart Campaigns went through multiple interview rounds, concept tests, and three betas testing six product variations before launch; the Messenger redesign underwent eight rounds of usability testing in Dublin plus additional remote sessions. The evidence is qualitative/process-based — the lesson being that matching research intensity and format to product maturity (via an embedded, not parachuted-in, researcher) is what made research keep up with iteration speed.
why_it_matters: A PM can transfer the idea of deliberately choosing a different research cadence and method (lean/hypothesis-driven vs iterative/usability-driven) based on whether a product is pre-launch or mature, rather than applying one research process uniformly.

title: Exploratory research: how to use it to drive product development
summary: Intercom's product team describes how it runs exploratory research (interviews, focus groups, case studies) before starting design work, using the build of its conversation ratings feature as the example. Initial signals suggested customers just wanted a scoring metric, but exploratory interviews surfaced that support managers actually used ratings data to motivate their teams, not just to gauge customer sentiment. That insight reshaped the feature toward emoji-based ratings with real-time positive-feedback notifications for support staff. The team reports that 2-3 weeks of upfront research narrowed the candidate solution set from 4-5 options down to 1-2, cutting downstream design and build iteration.
what_they_built: A conversation ratings feature using emoji-based scores with real-time positive-feedback notifications to support staff, shaped by upfront exploratory user research rather than the initially assumed scoring-only approach.
evidence: Intercom states that 2-3 weeks of exploratory research upfront saved weeks of downstream product development by reducing the number of candidate design solutions from 4-5 to 1-2, avoiding wasted design/build iterations.
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete case where qualitative upfront research uncovered a hidden internal-stakeholder use case (managers motivating teams) that a metrics-only reading of customer requests would have missed, directly narrowing solution scope before build.

title: From resolutions to outcomes: Evolving how Fin delivers value
summary: Intercom originally priced its Fin AI support agent purely on "resolutions" — cases fully closed by the AI with no human involved — because it was simple, fair, and tied revenue to outcomes. As Fin got more capable, customers increasingly deployed it into complex, multi-step workflows (billing disputes, subscription changes) where Fin gathers context and takes action but intentionally hands off to a human for compliance or oversight reasons, work the old metric didn't count as value. Intercom is therefore redefining its billing unit from "resolutions" to "outcomes," a broader category that includes both full AI resolutions and these human-handoff "procedures," so pricing keeps reflecting actual value delivered as Fin takes on harder work.
what_they_built: A redefined product/billing metric ("outcomes") for Fin that counts both full autonomous resolutions and human-handoff "procedures" as billable value, replacing a resolution-only pricing metric.
evidence: More than 7,000 teams use Fin; average resolution rate across customers has risen every month to 76%, even as Fin handles increasingly complex, multi-step queries requiring human handoff.
why_it_matters: Shows how a PM should evolve a core value/billing metric as product capability and usage patterns shift, without abandoning the original fairness principle behind it.

title: Navigating product-market fit: An inside look at Intercom's new PMF Panel
summary: Intercom wanted customers using its live chat but still relying on external ticketing tools (Zendesk, Freshdesk, ServiceNow) to fully switch, and needed a faster way to know which missing features actually blocked that switch versus which were just nice-to-have. They built a "PMF Panel": a small, carefully selected cohort of process-oriented target customers tracked in a simple table (current tool, decision timeline tied to contract end dates, real-time status), with five to six dedicated teams working feedback requests down to a one-day turnaround. The panel let them classify features like custom ticket states as nice-to-have versus back-office/tracker ticket handling as true must-haves, gating roadmap prioritization by real switching behavior rather than guesswork.
what_they_built: A tracked panel of target-persona prospective-switcher customers, monitored via a status table and served by dedicated cross-functional teams, used to validate must-have vs nice-to-have features before committing roadmap resources.
evidence: Panel members gradually made the switch to Intercom, and a similar switching trend was observed among prospects who were not part of the panel at all; feedback turnaround was accelerated to within one day of requests.
why_it_matters: Shows PMs a concrete alternative to broad survey-based PMF measurement: a small, tightly tracked cohort of real switching-decision customers can cheaply and quickly separate must-have from nice-to-have roadmap bets.

title: How to Build a Content Design Agent
summary: Intercom's content design team faced a bottleneck: content designers were manually fielding repetitive requests for copy drafts and policing style/tone/naming rules across product teams. They built VERBI, a RAG-based AI content design agent grounded in curated sources of truth — the Intercom style guide, tone-of-voice guidelines, product and monetization glossaries, and Storybook-hosted design-system component content indexed via the Glean Indexing API. Since launch a few months prior, VERBI had been triggered over 700 times, reducing manual rule-policing and letting designers move from blank page to first draft faster while the content lead reclaimed time for higher-impact work.
what_they_built: VERBI, an internal RAG agent that drafts and checks first-pass content against Intercom's design system and style/tone/glossary sources, using the Glean Indexing API to pull live content out of Storybook.
evidence: VERBI had been triggered 700+ times since launching a few months earlier; reported qualitative effects included reduced manual policing of content rules and faster designer iteration from blank page to first draft.
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete pattern for grounding an internal AI agent in a design system's existing artifacts (style guide, glossary, Storybook) rather than building a bespoke corpus, which any PM standing up a similar internal tool could reuse.

title: The Software Architectural Evolution of Quickteller (Part 1)
summary: Interswitch's engineering team recounts how Quickteller, its African digital payments platform originally built on ASP.NET Forms, was rebuilt in 2009 on a service-oriented architecture using Microsoft WCF to support multiple channels (ATM, POS, mobile, USSD, IVR, agent networks) without disruption. As transaction volume grew, they hit performance limits with LINQ-based ORM queries and moved to optimized stored procedures, kept business logic in reusable libraries to survive framework churn (ASMX to WCF to Web API to ASP.NET Core), and introduced an Enterprise Service Bus as an API gateway to simplify integration with third-party service providers. By the time of writing the platform was processing over 35 million transactions monthly and had migrated to ASP.NET Core on Linux with a microservices architecture in 2016.
what_they_built: A service-oriented architecture with a centralized business-logic service layer, no direct channel-to-database access, stored-procedure-based data access, reusable core libraries decoupled from the web framework, and an Enterprise Service Bus acting as an API gateway for third-party payment service providers.
evidence: "Processed and accounted for over 35 million transactions monthly from both our products and 3rd party re-sellers"; the platform later scaled to millions of transactions daily; ESB adoption described as keeping the codebase "insanely simple" while enabling flexible third-party integration.
why_it_matters: Shows how a payments platform PM/architect can sequence architectural investments (service layer, then data-access optimization, then an ESB integration layer) to keep scaling transaction volume without repeated rewrites.

title: The Secret Behind JD.com's Super Fast Delivery
publisher: JD Technology Blog (Medium, JD Smart Supply Chain team)
summary: JD.com's Smart Supply Chain team needed to hit same-day/next-day delivery promises across a huge, geographically spread product-SKU network while controlling stock-out and transportation costs. They built a two-step allocation system across their two-tier distribution network: Regional Distribution Centers (RDCs) serving large regions and Front Distribution Centers (FDCs) serving local areas. A network-flow model first computes the ideal per-product inventory allocation to each FDC to minimize combined stock-out and transportation cost, then a mixed-integer linear program (MILP) prioritizes which products get allocated when FDC capacity (unit and SKU-count limits) is constrained. The team reports the system drove visible improvement in stock-out rate at distribution centers and in the share of orders meeting same/next-day delivery standards, against a baseline of 90% same-or-next-day fulfillment.
what_they_built: A two-step inventory-allocation engine (network-flow optimization for ideal per-FDC stock levels, followed by MILP-based capacity prioritization) that governs how JD's RDC/FDC distribution network stocks products to hit delivery-time promises.
evidence: "JD is able to deliver 90% of orders same-day or next-day" as the baseline, with the team reporting "visible improvement in key performance metrics such as stock-out rate at the distribution centers and the proportion of orders meeting our same- and next-day delivery standards" after deploying the system — a qualitative before/after claim without a specific delta percentage.
why_it_matters: Shows how a delivery-time product promise can be decomposed into a two-stage optimization architecture (allocation, then constrained prioritization) that a PM can reuse whenever a service-level target has to be met under shared, capacity-limited infrastructure.

title: A journey to Progressive Web Apps
summary: Jumia Travel served users across sub-Saharan Africa where 75% of mobile connections were 2G and most devices were low-end with expensive data — conditions that made the standard mobile site slow and made native-app downloads a hard sell. Starting in early 2016, an engineer led a rebuild of the mobile experience as a Progressive Web App using Google's Polymer and Web Components, hitting and fixing a performance regression along the way by adopting the PRPL pattern (Push, Render, Pre-cache, Lazy-load) to shrink the initial app shell. They solved SEO and crawler compatibility by feeding search engines through their existing feature-phone site rather than the JS-rendered PWA. A year after launch, Google published the project's results showing large gains in conversion, bounce rate, and data efficiency.
what_they_built: A Progressive Web App for Jumia Travel built with Polymer/Web Components and the PRPL lazy-loading pattern, with SEO/crawler traffic routed to the existing feature-phone site for compatibility.
evidence: Per the post, "In May of 2017, Google published our results": 33% higher conversion vs. the previous mobile site, 50% lower bounce rate, 12x more users than the native Android/iOS apps, 5x less data used overall, 2x less data to complete a first transaction, and 25x less device storage required. An earlier four-week prototype had also shown 2x faster load on 2G and 6x less data than the native app; the PRPL rework "slim[med] down the app shell by 200%."
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete, metrics-backed case for treating "build for the worst network and device your users actually have" as a primary product requirement rather than an edge case, and for choosing a PWA over a native app to cut acquisition friction in low-bandwidth markets.

title: The story of the JET design system so far…
summary: Just Eat Takeaway's UX team faced the challenge of maintaining design consistency across a multi-brand operation spanning 20 markets and four business areas (Customer, Restaurants, Couriers, Operations). They built PIE (Principles for Interactions and Experiences), a design-token-based system with dark/light theming, staffed by 4 dedicated designers and 2 dedicated development teams (React and Vue.js), and drove adoption through early developer proof-of-concepts, monthly demos, bi-weekly clinics, and an active Slack support channel. The system grew to 53+ core components, 250+ illustrations, and 280+ icons, with a target of 95% of products powered by PIE.
what_they_built: A multi-brand design system (PIE) built on design tokens with themed variations, covering component libraries, illustrations, and icons across Just Eat Takeaway's business areas, supported by dedicated design and engineering teams and structured adoption mechanisms (demos, clinics, Slack support).
evidence: Reports 53+ core components, 250+ illustrations, 280+ icons, and a 95% target for products powered by PIE across 20 operating markets, alongside qualitative process detail (monthly demonstrations, bi-weekly clinics) as adoption mechanisms.
why_it_matters: Shows how a multi-brand org drives design-system adoption not just through component quality but through recurring social mechanisms (demos, clinics) and dedicated cross-functional staffing.

title: 카카오페이만의 AB 테스트 서비스를 만든 이유 (Why We Built Our Own A/B Test Service)
summary: Kakao Pay's Growth Platform team started with the open-source Wasabi A/B testing tool but hit walls once experimentation needed to scale company-wide: Wasabi couldn't integrate with internal user data or let teams freely define targeting logic, and mobile app release cadence made runtime experiment changes necessary. The team built an in-house A/B testing service with an ANTLR-based DSL for expressive, validated user-segmentation rules (e.g., OS=IOS AND OS_VER > 12.0.0), an actor-model bucket assignment layer to avoid duplicate/contended assignment, off-heap caching to control GC pressure at scale, snapshot isolation so experiments can be edited mid-flight without contaminating existing user data, and fallback buckets so outages or unmatched users degrade gracefully instead of breaking. Results and experiment data flow into Hadoop via batch ETL for statistical analysis, with an admin console fronting the whole pipeline.
what_they_built: An in-house A/B testing platform (DSL-based targeting, actor-model bucket assignment, off-heap caching, snapshot isolation, fallback buckets) replacing the open-source Wasabi experimentation tool across Kakao Pay.
evidence: Concrete architectural outcomes are reported rather than business-impact metrics: the off-heap cache achieves roughly an 8% hit rate in the distributed setup but keeps heap usage under 70MB, and the system is described as running in production supporting company-wide experimentation with fallback and snapshot-isolation safety mechanisms.
why_it_matters: Shows a PM/platform team the concrete tradeoffs (targeting flexibility, fault tolerance, safe mid-campaign edits) that separate an internal experimentation platform teams will actually adopt from an off-the-shelf tool that stalls at company scale.

title: AgentX: Towards Agent-Driven Self-Iteration of Industrial Recommender Systems
summary: Kuaishou's recommendation-algorithm iteration process was bottlenecked on engineers manually generating hypotheses, writing code, launching A/B tests, and attributing results — innovation scaled linearly with headcount. The team built AgentX, a production multi-agent system with a closed loop of a Brainstorm Agent, Developing Agent, Evaluation Agent, and a self-evolving "Harness Evolution" layer that learns from execution trajectories to improve the agents over time. Deployed for three weeks across Kuaishou's main-feed and life-service recommendation surfaces, three AgentX workers ran the equivalent of many manual engineers' worth of experimentation. The report frames this as a new division of labor: engineers collaborate with the agent system on strategy/model iteration while a separate layer evolves the agent framework itself.
what_they_built: A production-deployed multi-agent system (AgentX) that autonomously brainstorms, implements, A/B-tests, and learns from recommendation-algorithm experiments on Kuaishou's live app, replacing manual engineer-driven iteration.
evidence: In the three-week deployment, three AgentX workers turned 374 ideas into 10 launchable rollouts; per-worker throughput doubled weekly via self-evolution, delivering 8x concurrency and 3.7x business value versus a manual engineer, a 0.561% user app-time gain, and over RMB 100M in annualized revenue.
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete, measured pattern for turning an experimentation/iteration bottleneck into an internal agent-driven platform with tracked throughput and business-value multipliers versus the prior manual process.

title: VLM as Policy: Common-Law Content Moderation Framework for Short Video Platform (KuaiMod)
summary: Kuaishou's short-video platform faced content that harms user mental health (especially minors), and existing moderation systems couldn't adapt fast enough or reason transparently about nuanced policy violations. The team built KuaiMod, a vision-language-model-based moderation framework that treats policy as a "common law" system, dynamically updating rules via chain-of-thought reasoning over sparse user feedback (reports) rather than static rule lists. They ran a large-scale online A/B test putting KuaiMod into production, including using it to inform video recommendation ranking. The deployment cut the user reporting rate by 20% and lifted both Daily Active Users and App Usage Time on several Kuaishou surfaces.
what_they_built: A VLM-driven, chain-of-thought content moderation policy engine (KuaiMod) that dynamically evolves moderation rules from sparse user reports and feeds moderation signal into video recommendation ranking.
evidence: Large-scale online A/B test: 20% reduction in user reporting rate, plus increases in both DAU and AUT on several Kuaishou scenarios when applied to video recommendation.
why_it_matters: Shows a T&S/moderation system framed and measured as a product lever (engagement and complaint-rate metrics, not just offline classifier accuracy), which is the kind of trust-and-safety-with-product-framing case PMs at any platform with user-generated content need to reason about.

title: Takeaways from the closure of LINE LIVE
summary: LINE had to shut down its LINE LIVE streaming app, which had a large user base and significant in-app purchases, so an abrupt removal from the app store would have broken trust and left paying users without recourse. The team designed a structured four-step closure process — notification, compensation, ongoing support, then removal — and built a backend SERVICE_CLOSE_MODE status returned via an X-CastService-Meta-Type response header, paired with an Android OkHttp ApiInterceptor that detected the closure signal (even under network exceptions using cached data) and surfaced refund and inquiry information through a dedicated closure lifecycle event. The service closed on March 31, 2023, and the post documents the mechanism and process used to wind it down responsibly.
what_they_built: A server-side SERVICE_CLOSE_MODE flag delivered via response headers plus a client-side OkHttp ApiInterceptor and CloseServiceLifeCycleEvent in the Android app that detect the closure signal (online or from cached data during network failures) and route users to a screen with refund and support information.
evidence: Qualitative: the post documents the four-step closure plan actually executed (notification, compensation, support, removal) and the technical mechanism built to support it, with the service's actual closure date (March 31, 2023) as the real-world outcome; no quantitative adoption/refund metrics are given.
why_it_matters: Gives a PM a concrete, implementable pattern for winding down a paid product responsibly — a staged closure process backed by a server-driven feature flag and client interceptor rather than an abrupt app-store pull.

title: Detecting Interference: An A/B Test of A/B Tests
summary: LinkedIn's experimentation team noticed that standard A/B tests assume no interference between treatment and control users, an assumption that breaks on a social network where a treated user's connections can be indirectly affected. They built a detection method that runs a matched pair of experiments in parallel — one randomized at the individual level and one at the level of 10,000 network clusters produced by a graph-partitioning algorithm (reLDG) — and compares the two effect estimates using a Hausman-test-style check to see if they diverge. A large divergence signals that network interference is biasing the standard individual-level result.
what_they_built: A dual-randomization "test of tests" methodology — parallel individual-level and cluster-level A/B experiments on a 10,000-cluster network partition, compared statistically to detect interference bias, plus stratification and CUPED for variance reduction.
evidence: In one reported case ("experiment 2"), the cluster-based randomization measured an effect of 0.81 versus 0.24 for individual-level randomization on the same feature — a large divergence the team interprets as evidence of significant network interference biasing the standard individual-level estimate.
why_it_matters: PMs running experiments on any networked/social product should know that standard individual-randomized A/B results can be systematically wrong when a feature has spillover effects on connected users, and that cluster-randomized companion tests are a practical way to check for and quantify that bias.

title: Air Traffic Controller: Member-First Notifications at LinkedIn
summary: LinkedIn was sending notifications through disconnected, channel-specific systems that couldn't coordinate frequency or relevance across email, push, SMS, and in-app, driving member complaints and poor engagement. The team built Air Traffic Controller (ATC), a unified stream-processing platform on Apache Samza, Kafka, and RocksDB that partitions decisions by member ID for low-latency personalization. ATC applies a "5-Rights" framework (right message, right member, right channel, right time, right frequency) with ML-based relevance scoring, aggregation, ranking, spam filtering, and delivery-time optimization to decide what to send and when. The system now handles over a billion requests daily across 546+ million members.
what_they_built: A unified, real-time notification decisioning platform (ATC) built on Apache Samza/Kafka/RocksDB that centralizes channel selection, ranking, frequency capping, and ML relevance scoring across all of LinkedIn's notification types.
evidence: The post reports LinkedIn "cut member complaints in half and create[d] double digit increases in member engagement site-wide," plus a technical latency win: P90 latency for member messaging dropped from roughly 12 seconds to 1.5 seconds after RocksDB range-query and Samza async-processing optimizations.
why_it_matters: Shows a PM-transferable pattern for notification/engagement systems: centralizing fragmented, channel-siloed decisioning into one relevance- and frequency-aware platform can simultaneously cut complaints and lift engagement, rather than trading one off against the other.

title: Evolving LinkedIn's Analytics Tech Stack
summary: LinkedIn's analytics stack had grown into a costly, hard-to-maintain mess: it ran on third-party proprietary tools (Informatica/Appworx) and maintained redundant Teradata and Hadoop warehouses side by side, with 1,424 overlapping datasets and licensing constraints limiting scale. The team first built lineage and usage-tracking tooling (scraping Teradata logs and instrumenting MapReduce/Trino to emit Kafka access events) to understand actual usage before migrating, then consolidated datasets, switched file formats from Avro to ORC, moved Hive/Pig workflows to Spark, and de-normalized tables to cut expensive joins. They also stood up a new architecture (Unified Metrics Platform, Azkaban scheduling, DALI data-access API) to replace the legacy proprietary stack. The migration was completed with zero production impact while cutting costs and dataset sprawl substantially.
what_they_built: A re-architected analytics/ETL data platform migrating off proprietary Teradata/Informatica tooling onto Hadoop/Spark with a new Unified Metrics Platform, ORC-based storage, and a DALI data-access layer, guided by custom-built dataset lineage and usage-tracking tools.
evidence: Quantified before/after results: dataset count reduced from 1,424 to 450 (~70% reduction); Avro-to-ORC format migration increased read speed ~10-1000x and improved compression ratio ~25-50%; Hive/Pig-to-Spark transition cut runtime by roughly 80%; migration achieved zero production impact.
why_it_matters: Shows a PM-transferable pattern for large legacy-platform migrations: instrument and measure actual usage first (lineage/usage tracking) before consolidating or migrating, so the migration plan is evidence-driven rather than a guess.

title: Automated Fake Account Detection at LinkedIn
summary: LinkedIn's Anti-Abuse engineering team faced continuous attempts to create fake accounts for scraping, spamming, fraud, and phishing. They built a layered "funnel of defenses": a registration-time ML scoring model that allows, challenges, or blocks signups based on abuse risk; downstream cluster detection that groups accounts by shared attributes to catch coordinated fake-account creation; activity-based models that flag anomalous post-signup behavior; and human review from member reports and investigators that feeds signals back into the models. The team reports the registration model blocked five million fake accounts from being created in under a day during one observed attack.
what_they_built: A multi-stage automated defense pipeline combining a registration-time abuse-risk scoring model, cluster-based detection of coordinated fake-account creation, behavioral activity models, and human review loops that retrain the models.
evidence: Concrete production outcome cited: "the model blocked five million fake accounts from being created in less than a day" during one observed attack.
why_it_matters: Shows PMs how to design a tiered, self-reinforcing trust-and-safety funnel (score, challenge, block, review) rather than relying on a single detection layer.

title: Concourse: Generating Personalized Content Notifications in Near-Real-Time
summary: LinkedIn's earlier notification system only supported transactional (person-to-person) notifications; a prototype called Pepper proved that non-transactional, content-based notifications (e.g., "someone you follow posted") could drive a significant lift in key session metrics, but it needed to be rebuilt for scale and generality. The engineering team built Concourse, a near-real-time distributed platform with four stages: a Workflow Manager that standardizes requests and extracts features per content creator, a Fanout stage that resolves candidate recipients via the Edges API, an ML-based Scoring stage partitioned by recipient for distributed ranking, and a Feature Management layer that decorates notifications with actor/item/recipient/edge features from both online APIs and local RocksDB stores. The system is designed to score on the order of millions of candidate notifications per second while managing hundreds of billions of feature records across LinkedIn's 550M+ member base.
what_they_built: A near-real-time distributed notification-generation platform (Concourse) with staged Workflow Manager, Fanout, ML Scoring, and Feature Management components, backed by local RocksDB feature stores for low-latency personalization at scale.
evidence: Qualitative: the precursor prototype (Pepper) is reported to have driven "a significant lift in key session metrics" when non-transactional notifications were introduced, though no specific numeric lift is given. Quantitative scale evidence: the system is built to support scoring "on the order of millions of candidate notifications per second" and feature retrieval across "hundreds of billions of records and several terabytes in data size."
why_it_matters: Shows a PM-transferable pattern for scaling a proven-but-narrow prototype (Pepper) into a general, partitioned, real-time ranking platform once early metrics validate the underlying product bet.

title: Our approach to building transparent and explainable AI systems
summary: LinkedIn's ML models power recommender and anti-abuse systems, but non-technical stakeholders (sales, customer service, fraud reviewers) struggled to trust or act on raw model outputs like SHAP/LIME feature lists. LinkedIn built CrystalCandle, an internal platform with four components (Model Importer, Model Interpreter, Narrative Generator, Narrative Exporter) built on Apache Spark and integrated into their ProML pipeline, that turns model predictions into digestible narratives. By mid-2021 it covered 8+ predictive models across 5 lines of business and served over 5,000 employees, including anti-abuse and jobs-fraud review teams.
what_they_built: CrystalCandle, an end-to-end internal explainability platform that converts machine learning model outputs into customer- and reviewer-facing narrative explanations, used across sales/marketing, anti-abuse, and jobs-fraud workflows.
evidence: As of mid-2021, CrystalCandle was integrated with more than eight business predictive models across five lines of business and assisted more than 5,000 employees; the post states the jobs-fraud application "resulted in an increase in both reviewer accuracy and efficiency" (no specific percentage given).
why_it_matters: Shows how a PM can turn opaque ML outputs into an internal product (narrative explanations) that drives measurable adoption and operational trust across non-technical teams, not just a model quality improvement.

title: Practical Magic: Improving Productivity and Happiness for Software Development Teams
summary: LinkedIn's Developer Experience org faced the recurring question of how to measure and improve software developer productivity and happiness without falling into common measurement traps. They built and ran the internal DPH (Developer Productivity & Happiness) Framework, structured around a Goals/Signals/Metrics philosophy, developer personas with volunteer "persona champions" representing different engineering workflows, and explicit guidance on distinguishing raw data from actionable insights. LinkedIn open-sourced the framework's documentation, including hard-won lessons such as never using output volume alone to judge an individual engineer and avoiding collapsing multiple signals into a single aggregate score. The post is written for other engineering organizations to adopt or adapt the same approach.
what_they_built: An internal framework and process (Goals → Signals → Metrics, developer personas, persona champions) for defining and governing developer-productivity metrics at LinkedIn, now published as open-source documentation.
evidence: Qualitative: the post reports lessons learned from actually running the program internally, including explicit pitfalls identified in production use — that output volume is not a valid individual performance metric and that aggregating multiple signals into one score misleads decision-making — rather than quantitative before/after metrics.
why_it_matters: Gives a PM or eng-platform leader a battle-tested vocabulary and governance model (goals/signals/metrics, personas) for standing up a developer-productivity measurement program without repeating known failure modes.

title: Less Is More: Optimizing Email Volume - Part 1
summary: LinkedIn found excessive email was driving engagement but also causing member fatigue, and needed a principled way to decide which emails to drop. They ran a random-drop vs. send-all experiment to quantify the tradeoff between page-view lift from email and complaint growth, then formulated volume control as a Multi-Objective Optimization (MOO) problem: maximize downstream sessions while minimizing emails sent and complaints, solved via a linear program over per-email send probabilities using logistic-regression response predictions. The system reduces to a small set of per-type coefficients that convert response predictions into real-time send/drop decisions across millions of weekly email candidates.
what_they_built: A Multi-Objective Optimization (MOO) system that scores each candidate email with logistic-regression response predictions and applies learned per-type coefficient thresholds to decide, in real time, whether to send or drop it, balancing downstream engagement against complaint rate and volume.
evidence: Random-drop (~50% of emails dropped) vs. send-all A/B test over one week: send-all group had 2.6% more total page views (Jobs -4%, Profile -4.5%, PYMK -4.5%, Search -4%, Homepage -1.4% for random-drop), but generated 45% more negative responses (unsubscribes/spam reports) than the random-drop group.
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete pattern for turning an engagement-vs-fatigue tradeoff into a constrained optimization with measurable objective and complaint-rate constraints, rather than tuning volume by gut feel.

title: Less Is More: Optimizing Email Volume Part 2
