They restructured around "full cycle developers" who own design through operate/support for their own services, and backed this with centralized platform teams (Cloud Platform, Performance & Reliability Engineering, Engineering Tools) building shared tooling like Spinnaker for deploys and Atlas for monitoring. The piece walks through why the earlier hybrid model (devs could push code but still deferred to ops specialists) only partially worked, and lays out the tradeoffs of local versus centralized tooling investment. It closes by describing the mindset and skills shift required of developers under the new model.
what_they_built: An organizational model ("full cycle developers") where each development team owns its full software life cycle including deploy and operate, supported by centralized internal platform teams building shared deployment (Spinnaker) and monitoring (Atlas) tooling.
evidence: Qualitative before/after: under the old specialist-split model, "the gap between code complete and deployed was much longer than today, with releases happening on the order of weeks rather than days," with higher time-to-detect and time-to-resolve for deployment problems due to ops teams lacking direct knowledge of changes; no quantified metrics are given post-change, but the piece attributes tighter feedback loops and faster releases to the full-cycle model.
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete tradeoff PMs and eng leaders face when scaling a platform org: centralizing tooling investment to make distributed operational ownership viable, rather than choosing between silo'd specialists or unsupported generalists.

title: The Netflix Hermes Test: Quality Subtitling at Scale
summary: Netflix's localization scale (from 3 languages to 20+) exposed a gap: no industry-standard way to vet or track subtitlers/translators working through third-party vendors, making it impossible to measure individual quality or match talent to work. The Content Localization and Media Engineering teams built HERMES, an online five-part randomized subtitling/translation test developed with academic collaborators, which issues each candidate a persistent "H-Number" identifier tied to their work for the rest of their career supplying Netflix translations. Starting the following summer, all delivered subtitles would require a valid H-Number, letting Netflix correlate quality metrics to individual translators and eventually build a recommendation system matching subtitlers to content types. Two weeks after launch, thousands of candidates worldwide had completed the test across all supported languages.
what_they_built: HERMES: an online, randomized five-part subtitler certification test that issues each candidate a persistent unique identifier (H-Number) used to track quality metrics and route/match translation work across Netflix's global vendor network.
evidence: "Since we unveiled our new HERMES tool two weeks ago, thousands of candidates around the world have already completed the test, covering all represented languages" -- an adoption figure reported two weeks post-launch, plus the stated forward requirement that all delivered subtitles carry a valid H-Number starting that summer.
why_it_matters: Shows how to convert an unmeasurable, fragmented third-party vendor pool into a first-party quality system by issuing a persistent identity and standardized assessment -- a pattern transferable to any PM managing quality across a distributed contractor/marketplace supply chain.

title: How We Build Code at Netflix
summary: Netflix's engineering tools team describes the centrally-supported "paved road" toolchain that carries code from a developer's laptop to a live multi-region deployment: Nebula (a Gradle plugin suite) for local build/test/package, Jenkins (25 masters running in AWS) for CI, the Bakery/Aminator for building immutable Amazon Machine Images, and Spinnaker for continuous delivery pipelines including canary and red/black deployments. The post explains why each stage exists — rooted in Netflix's culture of engineer freedom-with-responsibility, its 2008 move to AWS microservices, and an Immutable Server deployment philosophy — and walks through a concrete build.gradle example. It closes by naming an active problem the team is still solving: binary dependency management across Nebula-based builds.
what_they_built: A centrally-maintained CI/CD toolchain (Nebula, Jenkins, Bakery/Aminator, Spinnaker) that every Netflix microservice team can use as an optional but heavily-adopted "paved road" from code commit to multi-region cloud deployment.
evidence: Reports it takes just 16 minutes to move the Janitor Monkey service from code check-in to a multi-region production deployment using this pipeline, and notes Jenkins has scaled from a single datacenter master to 25 masters running in AWS.
why_it_matters: Shows how a platform team earns adoption without mandates — by making the paved-road tooling compelling enough that teams choose it over building their own, while still allowing opt-out with the tradeoff of owning maintenance themselves.

title: Innovating Faster on Personalization Algorithms at Netflix Using Interleaving
summary: As Netflix's product matured, detecting real improvements from new ranking algorithms via standard A/B tests required ever-larger sample sizes and longer experiment durations, slowing iteration. The team built a two-stage process: first, team-draft interleaving exposes a single user group to a blended ranking from algorithm A and algorithm B simultaneously, using viewing-hours attribution to cheaply and quickly identify the most promising candidates within days; only the surviving candidates then go through a traditional A/B test to confirm impact on longer-term behavior like retention. A bootstrap subsampling analysis showed interleaving needed far fewer subscribers than even their most sensitive A/B metric to reach the same statistical power.
what_they_built: A team-draft interleaving system that alternates algorithm-selected videos within a single ranked feed shown to one user group (instead of splitting users into separate A/B arms), used as a fast first-pass filter before a standard A/B test on surviving candidates.
evidence: Quantified sensitivity result: "interleaving can require >100x fewer subscribers to correctly determine ranker preference even compared to the most sensitive A/B metric," at 95% statistical power, derived from bootstrap subsampling analysis comparing the two methods.
why_it_matters: A PM evaluating a slow, low-power experimentation pipeline can transfer the pattern of inserting a cheap, highly-sensitive preference-detection stage (paired/within-subject comparison) ahead of a traditional between-subjects A/B test to cut the sample size and time needed to prune bad candidates before committing to a full-scale test.

title: Netflix Third Quarter 2023 Letter to Shareholders
summary: Netflix had spent prior quarters rolling out its paid-sharing crackdown (converting password-sharing borrower households into paying members or extra-member add-ons) region by region. In this Q3 2023 shareholder letter, Netflix reports the rollout is now complete in every region it operates and states the results: cancel reactions were lower than expected, borrower households converting to full memberships showed healthy retention, and the company is now revenue-positive in every region from the change. Paid net additions hit 8.8M in the quarter versus 2.4M a year earlier, driving 9% year-over-year membership growth and 8% revenue growth to $8.5B.
what_they_built: A global paid-sharing enforcement and monetization program that converts password-sharing "borrower" households into full paying memberships or paid "extra member" add-ons.
evidence: 8.8M paid net additions in Q3'23 vs 2.4M in Q3'22 (9% YoY membership growth); revenue-positive in every region after accounting for extra-member fees, churn, and plan-mix changes; qualitatively "low" cancel reaction and "healthy retention" of converted borrower households; Q3'23 revenue of $8.5B (up 8% YoY) and operating margin of 22.4%.
why_it_matters: Shows a PM-relevant playbook for monetizing free-riding usage of a product without triggering the churn spike leadership feared, backed by real region-by-region rollout data rather than a single market pilot.

title: Netflix Q2 2023 Shareholder Letter (Form 8-K Exhibit 99.1)
summary: Netflix faced a long-standing revenue leak from widespread password sharing outside paid households. In May 2023 it completed the global rollout of its paid-sharing initiative — extending an account to non-household members for an extra fee — to over 100 countries representing more than 80% of its revenue base. The Q2'23 shareholder letter reports the initial financial outcome of that rollout: paid net additions swung to 5.9M (with over 1M in every region) versus -1.0M in the same quarter a year earlier, revenue in each region already exceeded pre-launch levels, and sign-ups were outpacing cancellations. Operating margin also expanded to 22% from 20% in Q2'22, partly attributed to the paid-sharing launch alongside expense management.
what_they_built: A global paid password-sharing (account-sharing monetization) program that converts non-paying household sharers into paying "extra member" add-ons or new standalone subscriptions, rolled out to 100+ countries by end of Q2 2023.
evidence: Paid net additions of 5.9M in Q2'23 vs -1.0M in Q2'22 (over 1M net adds in every region); revenue in each region above pre-launch levels; Q2'23 revenue of $8.2B and operating margin of 22.3% (vs 19.8% in Q2'22); full-year 2023 operating margin still targeted at 18-20%.
why_it_matters: Shows how a company can convert a long-tolerated product loophole (shared logins) into a monetization lever without cratering engagement, using phased regional rollout and near-real-time net-add/revenue tracking to validate the change quarter by quarter.

title: Netflix Q4 & FY 2023 Shareholder Letter
summary: Netflix faced a mature core subscription business with limited pricing headroom and needed new revenue pools, so in 2023 it pushed two monetization changes into steady-state operation: an ad-supported tier and a paid-sharing (password-sharing crackdown) program. The Q4'23 shareholder letter reports these are no longer launch experiments but "normal course of business" — the ads plan drove nearly 70% quarter-over-quarter membership growth for two consecutive quarters, helped along by phasing out the cheaper ad-free Basic plan for new/rejoining members in ads markets, and paid sharing has been "successfully addressed" via features like Transfer Profile and Extra Member.
what_they_built: An ad-supported subscription tier (with feature parity improvements like downloads) combined with retirement of the legacy Basic plan in ads markets, plus paid-sharing mechanics (Transfer Profile, Extra Member) that convert shared-account users into payers.
evidence: Ads membership grew nearly 70% quarter-over-quarter in both Q3'23 and Q4'23; the ads plan reached 40% of all Netflix sign-ups in ads markets; Q4'23 revenue grew 12% YoY (13% FX-neutral) with paid net adds of 13.1M vs 7.7M in Q4'22, which the letter attributes partly to paid sharing and price changes; FY23 free cash flow reached $6.9B.
why_it_matters: Shows a PM-relevant pattern of pairing a monetization change (paid sharing crackdown) with a demand-funnel change (retiring a cheaper ad-free tier to push signups toward ads) and reporting the compounding effect with hard adoption numbers rather than a one-time launch announcement.

title: Quasi Experimentation at Netflix
summary: Netflix's experimentation team explains that some hypotheses can't be tested with classic individual-level A/B tests — either randomization is infeasible or interference between users (word-of-mouth, ranking systems, mass media) violates SUTVA. Their example: measuring the effect of out-of-home billboard marketing, where they instead randomize which cities show billboards versus not, comparing test and control regions over time, and iteratively toggling exposure on/off in the same regions to get more comparisons. The same geo/cluster-randomization idea extends to server-level tests on their Open Connect CDN, where they discuss whether blocking, matching, or covariate-control designs would improve precision. Having scaled quasi-experiments across marketing and other teams, they describe building "Quasimodo," a new product inside their wider Experimentation Platform, to automate the operational mechanics so scientists can focus on hypothesis generation and run more quasi-experiments in parallel.
what_they_built: A quasi-experimentation methodology (geo-level and server-level randomized designs with iterative on/off exposure) layered on Netflix's existing A/B testing platform, plus an in-development platform product called Quasimodo to automate the workflow.
evidence: Qualitative: the marketing team "has started to run a lot more quasi experiments to measure the business impact of marketing movies & TV shows across various canvases," and success there "spurred interest across many other teams to scale their own quasi experiment efforts." No specific lift/precision numbers are given in this post.
why_it_matters: Shows PMs a concrete pattern for measuring causal impact when individual randomization is impossible or contaminated by network effects — randomize at the geography/cluster level instead and invest in test design before reaching for fancier statistical models.

title: Return-Aware Experimentation
summary: Netflix's experimentation team faced the problem of running many A/B tests as isolated hypothesis tests rather than as a portfolio competing for limited resources, and of the "winner's curse" inflating estimated returns when the same data both selects and measures a winning treatment. They reframed experimentation as a resource-allocation problem, deriving a dynamic-programming policy for maximizing returns across a portfolio of tests, and proposed splitting data (e.g. 90% to select the winner, 10% to evaluate its returns) to get unbiased estimates. Netflix then applied this methodology in a real case study to pick a new proxy metric and decision rule, which now govern how the company sets OKRs and decides every new personalization A/B test.
what_they_built: A dynamic-programming framework that treats a company's experimentation program as a resource-allocation problem, plus a sample-splitting technique to remove winner's-curse bias when estimating a chosen treatment's returns.
evidence: The methodology was put into production use: Netflix used it to select a new proxy metric and decision rule that are now used to set OKRs and to decide all new personalization A/B tests at the company — an organizational adoption outcome rather than an offline benchmark.
why_it_matters: Shows a PM-relevant reframing of experimentation programs as a portfolio-allocation and decision-rule problem, not just a statistics problem, with a concrete, adopted mechanism (sample splitting) for avoiding winner's-curse bias in the metric an org optimizes.

title: Thinking Fast & Slow for a Personalized Notification System
summary: Netflix's messaging platform previously used a single causal model that predicted the short-horizon effect of each notification, which optimized near-term engagement but implicitly coupled frequency control with message-selection quality and missed cumulative fatigue/opt-out effects. The team rebuilt the system as a two-layer 'Slow/Fast' architecture: a weekly 'Slow' policy sets a personalized cross-channel pacing plan by maximizing a utility function that trades off engagement rewards against an explicit messaging cost, while a real-time 'Fast' policy consumes that plan from a feature store to pick the specific message to send at each opportunity. This decoupling let the team iterate on pacing strategy and content ranking independently and A/B test each layer separately. Netflix reports the change produced one of its largest production metric lifts to date, concentrated among lower-frequency ('casual viewer') members.
what_they_built: A hierarchical two-policy notification system — a weekly 'Slow' policy that sets personalized per-channel send-frequency and pacing plans via a utility-function optimization, and a real-time 'Fast' policy that reads that plan from a feature store to make tactical per-message send decisions.
evidence: Netflix states the new architecture delivered "one of our largest production metric lifts to date," with gains most significant among less-frequent ("casual viewer") members; no specific percentage figures are given, so the outcome is reported qualitatively/directionally by the company.
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete pattern for PMs managing any engagement-vs-fatigue tradeoff: separate a strategic 'how much/how often' budget decision from a tactical 'what exactly' decision so each can be independently modeled, tuned, and A/B tested.

title: Nextdoor 2023 Transparency Report
summary: Nextdoor faced the standard challenge of a hyperlocal social platform: keeping neighbor-to-neighbor discourse civil at scale while relying heavily on volunteer moderators. In 2023 it rolled out a generative-AI-assisted "Kindness Reminder" that intervenes when a post looks likely to violate community guidelines, alongside its existing rule-based version, and paired this with a large volunteer moderation program (including a new training course built with The New Quo). The transparency report quantifies both the AI intervention's effect on user behavior and the scale/speed of human moderation across the platform.
what_they_built: A generative-AI-powered "Kindness Reminder" prompt that flags potentially guideline-violating posts before submission and suggests revisions, run alongside a traditional rule-based Kindness Reminder and a ~200,000-strong volunteer moderator program.
evidence: 36% of neighbors prompted by either the AI or traditional Kindness Reminder revised or withheld their message; 26% of those shown AI-revised text adopted the suggestion; overall a 15% reduction in content violating Community Guidelines; volunteer moderators reviewed 90% of reported content with a median removal time of 5.3 hours; only 0.16% gave negative feedback on AI suggestions.
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete, measured way to pair a generative-AI nudge with human moderation capacity in a trust-and-safety product, giving PMs a template for instrumenting behavioral-intervention adoption and downstream policy-violation impact.

title: Backend Driven Content: why we developed a Server Driven UI framework at Nubank
summary: Nubank's mobile teams were bottlenecked by app store release cycles of one to two weeks whenever they wanted to change a UI screen or run an experiment. They built Backend Driven Content (BDC), a Clojure-based server-driven UI framework where the backend sends instructions for building and rendering UI components rather than hardcoding interfaces into the app. A dedicated BDC engineering team now supports the framework across the company, and adoption has grown to cover a large share of new product surfaces.
what_they_built: An internal Server Driven UI platform (Backend Driven Content) that lets the backend push UI component instructions and data to the app, avoiding app-store releases for most UI changes, backed by a dedicated engineering team and design-system compliance.
evidence: Nearly 70% of new screens and flows at Nubank now use BDC, and 43% of the app already incorporates the framework; the company reports production changes can ship within a day versus the usual one-to-two-week app store cycle, plus same-day A/B test execution and controlled/gradual rollouts.
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete pattern for decoupling product iteration speed from mobile app-store release cadence via a server-driven UI platform, with real adoption and cycle-time metrics a PM can cite when building the case for similar internal tooling investment.

title: How we designed Nubank's Bank Account — Part II
summary: Nubank's design team faced the challenge of designing NuConta, a hybrid account combining checking, savings, and investment features, in a way that avoided expensive late-stage rework. They ran co-design sessions assigning different personas to stakeholder groups to surface cross-disciplinary constraints early, spent significant time whiteboarding before touching digital tools, used Figma to make UI work visible and collaborative in real time, and built a staged sequence of prototypes (paper for copywriting, Keynote for engineers, Principle for flow, and a full Framer + JavaScript prototype with dynamic financial data) each matched to a specific learning goal.
what_they_built: A hybrid checking/savings/investment bank account (NuConta) UI, developed through structured co-design sessions and a staged, tool-matched prototyping pipeline from paper through Framer/JavaScript.
evidence: Qualitative process evidence rather than metrics: explicit rationale that co-design "maximizes the possibilities of finding problems that would appear later, when they would be more expensive to solve," plus a documented staged prototyping approach tied to distinct learning goals per fidelity level.
why_it_matters: Shows PMs and designers a concrete, replicable method for sequencing collaborative design exploration and prototype fidelity to surface cross-functional problems before they become costly.

title: From feature to platform: how Nubank reinvented its conversation architecture
summary: Nubank's chat system had grown into a monolithic client where all conversational logic lived in one codebase, coupling the platform team to every product team's business rules. They re-architected it into a platform that owns only shared concerns (session management, conversation history, message delivery, multi-channel support, observability) and routes events through a new agent-selector component to independent, product-team-owned agents that all conform to standardized contracts. The platform team also redefined its own success metrics, moving from counting feature deliveries to tracking adoption, stability, scalability, onboarding experience, contract standardization, observability, and the autonomy of consuming teams. The post notes the initiative only gained traction once it was reframed from a technical refactor into a strategic initiative tied to company growth, with data governance and organizational alignment proving as important as the architecture itself.
what_they_built: An internal conversation platform with an agent-selector routing layer and standardized agent contracts, splitting shared conversational infrastructure from product-team-owned business-logic agents.
evidence: Qualitative organizational evidence: the platform team changed its own success metrics from feature-delivery counts to adoption, stability, scalability, onboarding experience, contract standardization, observability, and consuming-team autonomy; no numeric before/after figures were given in the retrieved text.
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete pattern for platform PMs: redefine your team's success metrics around what consuming teams can do autonomously, not around features you ship yourself.

title: Nubank Credit Card: from Product to Platform mindset
summary: Nubank's credit card org faced separate product teams each rebuilding similar credit logic, inconsistent calculations across operations, rising funding costs, and tightening Central Bank regulatory demands. Over 30 people across five cross-functional teams (business, product, engineering, data, operations) restructured around a shared credit platform, splitting solutions into a generic reusable part and a product-specific part, guided by four tenets (Time, Experience, Risk, Scalability) and documented decision records. They used story mapping, weekly "walk-the-board" sessions, and confidence check-ins to manage delivery. The shift unlocked more than 10 new credit products and let Nubank stand up financing options in Mexico quickly on the same platform.
what_they_built: A shared internal credit platform separating generic reusable credit logic from product-specific logic, governed by written decision records and cross-functional team chartering across roughly 30 people on five teams.
evidence: More than 10 new credit products were unlocked on the platform, and Nubank was able to launch financing options in Mexico in a notably short timeframe using the same infrastructure.
why_it_matters: Shows a PM-relevant playbook for when to convert a product org into a platform org: shared decision tenets, written decision records, and cross-functional chartering rather than just a tech rewrite.

title: 3 Lessons from Implementing Controlled-Experiment Using Pre-Experiment Data (CUPED) at Nubank
summary: Nubank's Experimentation Platform Data Science team implemented CUPED (Controlled-experiment Using Pre-Experiment Data) to reduce variance in A/B test metrics across a diverse product ecosystem spanning credit cards, marketplace, and crypto. They found the standard pooled theta estimator lost precision when treatment and control groups had unequal sizes or the treatment altered metric serial correlation, so they switched to a weighted average estimator. Through simulation they standardized on a 42-day pre-experiment lookback window as a practical balance of variance reduction and compute cost, and had to educate stakeholders on why CUPED-adjusted significant results often shrink toward zero (mitigating Type M errors) versus unadjusted estimates.
what_they_built: A CUPED variance-reduction layer for Nubank's internal experimentation platform, using a weighted theta estimator and a standardized 42-day pre-experiment observation window applied across product verticals.
evidence: About 40% of metric comparisons had variance reduced by more than 20%, with some specific populations/metrics seeing reductions near 99%; roughly 12% of comparisons lacked sufficient pre-experiment data to apply CUPED.
why_it_matters: Shows PMs running experimentation platforms the concrete estimator and window-selection tradeoffs behind variance reduction, plus the stakeholder-trust cost of adjusted effect sizes shrinking toward zero.

title: More productive data analytics with Data Quality Governance
summary: By early 2020 Nubank's fully self-service analytics environment had scaled to ~20,000 raw and ~10,000 transformed tables with roughly 500 contributors across ~100 autonomous teams, and unconstrained read-write access was undermining data trust and consistency. Nubank formed an Analytics Productivity squad in January 2020 to fix this without killing the flexibility that had enabled growth. The team introduced a "core" data layer with stricter naming conventions, mandatory documentation, designated table owners, and code-repository-enforced contribution rules for that layer, while leaving the rest of the environment low-constraint and self-service. They tracked adoption (core-table usage vs. duplicate/competing tables), execution-time and issue-resolution performance, and ran proxy productivity surveys to gauge impact.
what_they_built: A "core" data layer with enforced naming conventions, documentation requirements, designated table owners, and repository-level contribution controls, layered on top of an otherwise open self-service analytics environment.
evidence: Reports scale (20K raw tables, 10K transformed tables, ~500 contributors, ~100 teams) and describes tracking adoption of core tables versus alternative/competing tables, execution-time monitoring, and proxy productivity surveys as their measurement approach — qualitative/adoption-based evidence rather than a reported before/after metric delta.
why_it_matters: Shows how a platform team can layer governance onto a runaway self-service data environment by constraining just a "core" subset rather than re-centralizing everything, a pattern transferable to any org where flexibility scaled faster than trust.

title: Scaling fraud defense: How Nubank evolved its risk analysis platform
summary: Nubank's fraud defense system had grown into a sequential, layer-by-layer flow that was slow and hard to extend as the company scaled across Brazil, Mexico, and Colombia. The team rebuilt it as a unified Defense Platform using a DAG-based flow orchestrator (built on the open-source Nodely library) so each rule or model component only waits for the data it actually depends on, plus a shadow-testing mechanism that runs new rules and models against real production traffic without affecting real users before they go live. The platform now handles roughly 450 million events per day and 5 million internal requests per minute with over 99.98% availability, and processing time in complex flows dropped from 550ms to about 350ms.
what_they_built: A unified "Defense Platform" replacing a sequential fraud-rule pipeline with a DAG-based orchestrator (via the Nodely library) plus a shadow-testing framework for safely validating new rules/models against live traffic.
evidence: Processing time in complex flows dropped from 550ms to ~350ms; the platform sustains ~450M events/day and ~5M internal requests/minute with over 99.98% availability across Brazil, Mexico, and Colombia, using 20 shards in Brazil alone.
why_it_matters: Shows how reframing a rule engine's execution model around data dependencies (DAG) rather than fixed sequential layers can cut latency while making a safety-critical system easier for non-engineering risk analysts to extend.

title: Nu Holdings F-1 Registration Statement — Branchless Cost-to-Serve Model
summary: Nubank's F-1 registration statement (filed with the SEC ahead of its IPO) lays out why Latin American incumbent banks are structurally expensive to run — branch networks of 2,000-5,000 locations and ~80,000 employees per major incumbent bank in Brazil — and quantifies the cost advantage of Nubank's branchless, mobile-first model built to avoid that overhead. The filing states Nubank's cost to serve plus G&A per active customer in Brazil is roughly 85% lower than incumbents, whose comparable monthly figure was about US$15.7 for the six months to June 30, 2021. It also reports operating scale reached by that point: 48.1 million total customers by Q3 2021 (up from 3.7 million in Q1 2018), 90+ NPS platform-wide, and in the newly launched Mexico market, 760,000 customers and an NPS of 94 as of September 30, 2021.
what_they_built: A branchless, mobile-only retail banking and financial-services platform (accounts, cards, loans, insurance, investing) built without physical branches or the legacy back-office infrastructure that Latin American incumbent banks rely on.
evidence: Self-reported estimate: cost to serve + G&A per active customer in Brazil ~85% lower than incumbents (incumbent average ~US$15.7/month per active customer for H1 2021); 48.1M total customers by Q3 2021 vs 3.7M in Q1 2018 (110% 3-year CAGR); NPS 90+ platform-wide; Mexico market at 760,000 customers and NPS 94 as of Sept 30, 2021.
why_it_matters: Shows how a company translates a structural product decision (no branches) into a quantified, investor-facing unit-economics claim rather than a vague efficiency narrative — a template for PMs building cost-to-serve arguments for leaner service models.

title: How Nubank Built its in-house log platform
summary: Nubank's external logging vendor became prohibitively expensive and offered poor observability and control, to the point where the team calculated they could hire Lionel Messi as an engineer for the same cost. Facing unpredictable pricing, tight vendor coupling, and gaps where logs weren't retained end-to-end (hurting incident response), the team built an in-house platform in two phases: an ingestion "Observability Stream" (Fluent Bit, a custom Data Buffer Service, and a custom Filter & Process Service) decoupled from a "Query & Storage Platform" (Trino querying Parquet files on S3). The system now runs in production at large scale with substantial cost savings over the prior vendor solution.
what_they_built: An in-house log ingestion and query platform combining Fluent Bit collection, custom buffering/filtering microservices, and a Trino-on-S3-Parquet query and storage layer, replacing a third-party logging vendor.
evidence: Ingests 1 trillion logs/day (1 PB), retains 45 days for 45 PB of searchable data, serves ~15,000 queries/day scanning 150 PB, achieves ~95% average Parquet compaction, and costs 50% less than market solutions per their own benchmarks.
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete framework (decouple ingestion from query/storage, migrate incrementally, instrument cost/observability metrics before committing) for when a platform team should build vs. buy critical infrastructure at hyperscale.

title: Leveraging Internal Platforms through InnerSource
summary: Nubank's mobile platform team faced a bottleneck: all mobile development depended on its Backend Driven Content (BDC) framework, and every change had to route through a small core team, slowing delivery for the rest of the org. They adopted InnerSource practices — standardized contribution processes, CLI tools, templates, dedicated Tech Writer and Developer Advocate roles, and treating internal developers as customers — to let other teams contribute directly to BDC and are now extending the same model to the Experimentation Platform. The community grew to roughly 1,000 developers with over 85% actively engaging in discussions and over 90% organic adoption across teams, while support tickets for bugs/crashes/questions declined as contribution-related tickets rose.
what_they_built: An InnerSource operating model (contribution processes, CLI tooling, templates, and dedicated advocacy/writer roles) applied first to the BDC mobile platform and now being extended to the Experimentation Platform.
evidence: Roughly 1,000 developers in the BDC community, over 85% actively engaging in discussions, over 90% organic adoption across teams, and a consistent decrease in bug/crash/question tickets to the core team as contribution volume rose.
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete, metric-backed playbook for turning a bottlenecked core-team-owned internal platform into a broadly adopted internal product by borrowing open-source contribution mechanics.

title: A/B Testing for Decision of Scaling or Decommissioning a Human Resources Product
summary: Nubank's People team built "Noodles," a Slack-integrated peer-to-peer recognition tool, on the hypothesis that embedding appreciation into everyday communication would increase how recognized employees felt. They tested it with a proper A/B design — treatment/control split, calculated minimum sample size, pre/post surveys, and structured metrics (a Recognition Index and a Product-Market Fit score) — treating an internal HR initiative with the same experimental discipline as a customer-facing feature. The results were mixed and counterintuitive: adoption was strong and PMF hit 43%, but the Recognition Index actually declined in specific groups, and the tool resonated more with people giving recognition than those receiving it, meaning more usage didn't translate to feeling more recognized. Rather than scale on partial signals, they paused Noodles in its current form and shifted to qualitative research to understand the gap.
what_they_built: A Slack-integrated peer-recognition product ("Noodles") evaluated through a controlled A/B experiment with power-calculated sample sizes and pre/post surveys tracking a custom Recognition Index and PMF score.
evidence: 43% Product-Market Fit score and strong tool adoption, but the Recognition Index declined among specific employee groups, and increased usage did not correlate with feeling more recognized — a giver/receiver asymmetry the team says would likely have been missed without the experimental design. Outcome: Nubank paused the product rather than scaling it.
why_it_matters: Shows a PM playbook for applying rigorous experimentation (control groups, sample-size math, named leading/lagging metrics) to internal, non-revenue products, and for accepting a "pause" decision when adoption metrics diverge from the outcome metric that actually matters.

title: Design experiences based on storytelling: Nubank's framework
summary: Nubank's NuConta Design Manager Cris Luckner recapped, in an internal Design Center of Excellence masterclass written up on Nubank's engineering/design blog, a three-phase storytelling framework (Understanding, Defining, Designing) for shaping product experiences as narratives with the user as protagonist. She walked through two applications: NuConta had low account activation because its messaging was abstract, so the team rewrote the "resolution" of the story to be a concrete next action (deposit via Pix) instead of a vague promise of simplicity. For the Pix feature, where every Brazilian bank launched an identical underlying technology, cross-functional collaboration between Content Design, UX Research, and Product Design mapped each narrative beat to a specific screen to build user trust around security and registration, resulting in what the post describes as the highest number of Pix registrations of any bank in the country.
what_they_built: A three-phase (Understanding / Defining / Designing) storytelling framework used cross-functionally by Content Design, UX Research, and Product Design to sequence product screens as a narrative, applied to the NuConta activation flow and the Pix registration flow.
evidence: Qualitative/ranking claim, not a precise metric: the post states Nubank achieved "the highest number of Pix registrations" of any bank in Brazil after redesigning the Pix flow around the storytelling framework, and separately notes NuConta activation had been low before the messaging was reworked around a concrete next action.
why_it_matters: Shows how a lightweight narrative-structure framework (beginning/conflict/resolution mapped to screens) can be used as a cross-functional design coordination tool to fix an activation problem and differentiate an undifferentiated feature.

title: Introducing a new patron billing option
summary: Patreon's legacy billing model charged every patron on the first of the month regardless of when they joined, which meant a patron who joined late in a month could be billed again just days later — creators reported this drove confusion, refund requests, and a 60% spike in cancellations during the first week of each month. Patreon built an opt-in 'anniversary billing' option so new patrons are charged on the date they join and then monthly on that same date going forward, while existing patrons stay on first-of-month billing. Early-adopter creators reported the change eliminated the recurring stream of 'charged twice' refund complaints and gave them confidence to promote memberships at any point in the month instead of clustering asks around the 1st.
what_they_built: An opt-in subscription billing mode where new patrons are billed on their join date and monthly thereafter on that anniversary, instead of everyone being billed on the first calendar day of the month.
evidence: Quantified rationale: 60% more patron cancellations occur in the first week of the month than other weeks under first-of-month billing, and creators who promote membership weekly earn about 31% more than those promoting only monthly. Reported outcome after rollout: creator testimonial that they went a full month without a single refund request for being 'charged twice,' versus previously having to manually refund late-month subscribers every month to avoid duplicate near-term charges.
why_it_matters: Shows how a PM can use a single lifecycle-timing metric (cancellation spikes tied to billing-date mismatch) to justify a subscription billing redesign and validate it with qualitative before/after signal from the affected users.

title: Discovery on Patreon is driving $200M+ to creators per year
summary: Patreon built its discovery and recommendation system around a monetization metric rather than engagement, deliberately prioritizing posts from creators fans already follow before surfacing any recommendations. The company reports that discovery tools now drive over $200 million per year to creators, with creator recommendations generating more than 2 million new memberships since launch. A named creator case study (The Fluffy Folio) attributed 12% of new paid members and 37% of new free members to recommendations over six months. The post explicitly contrasts Patreon's conversion-optimized ranking with engagement-optimized feeds elsewhere, noting a post with negative comments but no new subscribers would not get boosted on Patreon.
what_they_built: A creator-recommendation and home-feed ranking system that surfaces followed creators first and optimizes recommendation ranking for membership conversion rather than engagement signals.
evidence: Discovery tools drive over $200 million/year to creators; creator recommendations have generated 2M+ new memberships since launch; one case-study creator attributed 12% of new paid members and 37% of new free members to recommendations over six months.
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete example of choosing and optimizing a feed-ranking metric (conversion) deliberately over the default engagement metric, with measurable platform and creator-level outcomes.

title: Deploying Large-scale Fraud Detection Machine Learning Models at PayPal
summary: Before building Quokka, PayPal's data scientists had to bundle fraud-detection models into fixed release schedules and push them through a manual, multi-team deployment process, which meant weeks of waiting and frequent score-drift or data-parity surprises when models hit production, sometimes forcing rollbacks. Quokka gave modelers a self-service UI for scheduling deployments and change requests, an automated CI/CD pipeline for packaging and deploying model artifacts, and a shadow environment that mirrors production so new models can be tested against live traffic without affecting real decisions. The platform is built on Node.js, React.js, Python, Java, Hadoop, and BigQuery, with ONNX and TensorFlow for model execution. Since launch, adoption has grown roughly 50% per quarter and support scaled from 8x5 to 24x7 without added headcount.
what_they_built: Quokka, an internal self-service platform combining CI/CD automation and a shadow production-mirror environment that lets PayPal data scientists deploy, test, and monitor fraud-detection models before full release.
evidence: Model development and deployment time reduced by 80%, zero system rollbacks due to model execution failure since launch, user productivity improved 3x, and adoption has grown at roughly 50% per quarter.
why_it_matters: Shows how a platform team can turn a slow, risky, manually-gated ML release process into a self-service pipeline with a shadow-testing safety net, cutting both time-to-production and rollback risk simultaneously.

title: How PayPal Uses Real-time Graph Database and Graph Analysis to Fight Fraud
summary: PayPal's risk teams needed a way to surface hidden relationships between accounts, devices, and IP addresses fast enough to catch fraud rings before they could act, which relational queries couldn't do at the needed speed and freshness. The team built a custom real-time graph database platform combining sub-second real-time, interactive, and analytics query stacks, using Gremlin as the unified query language over an Aerospike NoSQL backend, with both offline batch loading and event-based real-time updates. They illustrate the payoff with a fraud case where a new account linking to shared device/IP assets already tied to seven restricted accounts could be flagged rapidly. The post reports that almost all PayPal risk models now consume graph features, with 2-hop queries averaging ~10ms and near-real-time (seconds-level) data freshness at scale.
what_they_built: A custom real-time graph database and query platform (Gremlin over Aerospike) unifying real-time, interactive, and analytics graph access for PayPal's fraud/risk models.
evidence: Reports adoption ("almost all PayPal risk models are using graph features"), production performance (2-hop queries ~10ms average latency, seconds-level data freshness, million-QPS throughput capability), and a concrete fraud pattern (new account linked via shared IP/device to seven already-restricted accounts) as the mechanism the platform catches.
why_it_matters: Shows how a shared internal graph-query platform, not per-model bespoke engineering, became the default substrate for fraud/risk models across an org, a pattern any PM building internal risk or trust-and-safety tooling can borrow.

title: Planning a roadmap for developer experience at Paystack: Insights from 200+ African developers
summary: Paystack's Developer Relations team wanted to improve developer experience but lacked systematic insight into what African developers across Nigeria, Ghana, South Africa and Kenya actually needed. They ran 15 interviews plus a 194-respondent survey, then built three developer personas and applied a five-stage Developer Journey Map to audit documentation, SDKs, and community programs. The research surfaced a content gap for senior developers, a local hiring catch-22, and strong unmet demand for developer community, and directly drove roadmap actions: updated Node.js/Python libraries, new Android/iOS SDKs, expanded docs coverage for post-payment flows, and new in-market community events (Accra, Lagos, Nairobi meetups; DevConf ZA and GirlCode ZA sponsorships).
what_they_built: Paystack's Developer Relations team ran a mixed-methods research exercise (15 one-on-one interviews plus a survey of 194 developer-newsletter subscribers across Nigeria, Ghana, South Africa and Kenya) to understand developer needs, then synthesized findings into three developer personas (The Learner, The Journeyman, The Manager) and applied the "Developer Journey Map" framework (Discovery to Evaluation to Learning to Building to Scaling) to audit their docs, SDKs, and community touchpoints and prioritize a concrete roadmap.
evidence: Survey of 194 developers plus 15 interviews found 46% had 0-3 years experience vs 25% at 3-5 years and 26% over 5 years, and 54% of respondents were not active in any developer community but wanted to be — findings that directly shaped Paystack's persona-based roadmap and led to shipped SDK updates and new regional community programs.
why_it_matters: Gives platform/DevRel PMs a replicable, evidenced method (persona + journey-map audit built on real interview/survey data) for turning developer research into a prioritized DX roadmap, rather than acting on anecdote alone.

title: Peloton S-1 Registration Statement — Connected Fitness Subscription Model
summary: Peloton's S-1 filing lays out the hardware-plus-subscription "Connected Fitness" model it had scaled by fiscal 2019: bikes and treadmills sold with mandatory recurring subscriptions, sold at a low acquisition cost offset by hardware gross profit. The filing reports three years of audited operating metrics rather than projections — subscriber counts, churn, and revenue growth — to demonstrate the model's durability ahead of the IPO. It explicitly frames low churn plus hardware-margin-funded acquisition as the mechanism behind the unit economics.
what_they_built: A hardware-plus-recurring-subscription business model (Connected Fitness Products bundled with mandatory monthly subscriptions) and the operating-metric definitions (Connected Fitness Subscribers, Average Net Monthly Connected Fitness Churn, Subscription Contribution Margin) used to report and evaluate it.
evidence: Total revenue grew from $218.6M (FY17) to $435.0M (FY18) to $915.0M (FY19), 99.0% then 110.3% YoY. Connected Fitness Subscribers grew from 107,708 (FY17) to 245,667 (FY18) to 511,202 (FY19) — 35,135 as of June 2016 to 511,202 by June 2019, ~144.1% annualized. Average Net Monthly Connected Fitness Churn held at 0.70%, 0.64%, and 0.65% across FY17-19.
why_it_matters: Shows a PM how to instrument and report a hardware-subscription hybrid model with a small set of durable metrics (subscriber growth, net churn, contribution margin) that investors and internal teams can track together as one unit-economics story.

title: Engineering Agent Hub: How PhonePe Built a Production-Grade Internal AI Platform
summary: PhonePe hit limits with external AI tools (request caps, document limits, integration constraints) that couldn't meet its regulatory-compliance and large-scale internal knowledge base needs. So it built Agent Hub, an internal platform running agents inside ephemeral, network-isolated Docker containers with an air-gapped MCP architecture (local MCP servers as subprocesses, no public internet dependency) and a unified semantic discovery engine indexing agents, knowledge bases, and tools. Teams across engineering and ops adopted it for tasks like Product Readiness Assessments, lending audits, and fraud investigation. The post reports adoption numbers and measured time/cost savings from production usage.
what_they_built: An internal AI agent platform (Agent Hub) with sandboxed/air-gapped execution, semantic tool and knowledge discovery, and bulk-execution workflow capabilities (e.g., fan-out across CSV rows, document parsing from email/Drive).
evidence: 200+ internal agents handling 1,600+ daily queries for 759 Weekly Active Users, averaging 5 minutes saved per automated task; Product Readiness Assessments went from weeks to days (7x faster); lending audits dropped from 3-4 days to 2-3 minutes; fraud investigations became 80% autonomous (instant vs. 90 minutes) with cost per case falling from ₹50 to ₹5-₹15, covering 800 previously-unaddressed cases.
why_it_matters: Shows how an internal platform team can win adoption for AI tooling by solving for compliance/data-isolation constraints (air-gapped MCP, sandboxed execution) rather than just wrapping an external model API.

title: Central Platforms: Building Blocks for the PhonePe Group
summary: PhonePe's CTO describes how the company, rather than rebuilding core capabilities for each new app, invested over years in a suite of internal SaaS platforms spanning onboarding/identity, catalog, CRM, transaction processing, ad serving, customer support, pricing/reconciliation, and risk/fraud detection. These platforms let newer consumer apps in the PhonePe Group — Pincode (e-commerce), Share.Market, and Indus Appstore — launch fast by composing existing, battle-tested services instead of building from scratch. The piece walks through Pincode's actual dependency list (User Service, Atlas location services, Growth Solutions, CRM Services, FRA fraud detection, Primus merchant integration) and how platform maturity let Pincode ship rapid experimentation, referrals, and geo-targeted promotions. It closes by noting one of these platforms, Guardian (risk/fraud detection), now runs 40 crore (400 million) evaluations a day internally and is being externalized as a licensed product.
what_they_built: A portfolio of dozens of in-house SaaS platforms (identity/onboarding, catalog, CRM, transaction processing, ad server, support tooling, pricing/reconciliation, and Guardian — a risk and fraud detection platform) shared across all PhonePe Group consumer apps.
evidence: Guardian conducts over 40 crore (400 million) fraud/risk evaluations daily and is now licensed to external/global markets; Pincode, a newly launched e-commerce app, was able to compose six named existing platform services (User Service, Atlas, Growth Solutions, CRM Services, FRA, Primus) to ship quickly with referral programs, geo-targeted offers, and resilient offer disbursement during downtime.
why_it_matters: Shows a PM-relevant pattern for scaling a multi-product company: treat shared capabilities (identity, risk, CRM, pricing) as internal products with their own roadmap and external-licensing upside, not one-off features rebuilt per app.

title: The Tale Before the Design System
summary: PhonePe grew from a small team of 3 Android, 3 iOS developers and 2 designers into a multi-app organization (PhonePe, Pincode, Share.Market) where informal styling conventions started producing visible visual inconsistency across screens. Rather than force one shared design system across apps with very different audiences (utilitarian payments vs. shopping vs. investing), they built separate design systems sharing common base protocols, and stood up four dedicated teams (design system, UI/UX, library development, app development) to own the work full-time instead of as a side project. Execution involved migrating to declarative UI (Compose, SwiftUI), using Figma Variables and Dev Mode for token management and design handoff, and adding configuration support to their existing Liquid UI server-driven UI framework so remote UI stayed compatible with the new design tokens.
what_they_built: A federated, per-app design-system operating model (four dedicated team functions) plus a Figma-token-to-code pipeline (Variables/Dev Mode into Compose and SwiftUI component libraries) with SDUI (Liquid UI) configuration support for remote-UI compatibility.
evidence: Qualitative only: no adoption metrics or before/after numbers are given. The evidence is organizational/process -- the explicit decision to run separate-but-aligned design systems per app, the need for a dedicated (not side-project) team, and named integration problems solved (SDK case-by-case treatment, SDUI config support, training/workshops for the learning curve).
why_it_matters: Shows a PM/design leader how to decide between one shared design system versus per-product systems with shared protocols when a company's sub-apps serve genuinely different audiences.

title: Powering Real-Time Decisions with PhonePe's Garfield Platform and Apache Druid
summary: PhonePe needed an analytics platform that could turn hundreds of millions of daily transaction and interaction events into instant, actionable insight for incident detection and product decisions, since batch processing was too slow at their scale. They built Garfield, a custom event-ingestion and analytics platform on top of a tuned Apache Druid cluster, with a governance/caching proxy layer called Armor sitting in front of query traffic. The platform now processes 500M+ messages a day (peak 40,000 msgs/sec), runs active-active across two data centers with cross-region Kafka Mirror Maker replication, and enforces strict dimensionality controls (20 custom dimensions per event) to keep query performance and cardinality under control. It underpins real-time operational monitoring, product/customer journey analytics (funnel optimization, A/B test evaluation, feature adoption tracking), and fraud/security detection.
what_they_built: Garfield, a custom-built high-throughput event ingestion and analytics platform on Apache Druid, fronted by a custom query-governance proxy (Armor) and visualized via Superset.
evidence: 500+ million messages processed daily with peak ingestion of ~40,000 messages/second, a 5:1 data rollup target, 1M-row segment caps, active-active deployment across two data centers with dual HDFS copies and Kafka Mirror Maker cross-region replication, and a hard cap of 20 custom dimensions per event to control cardinality.
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete pattern for building an internal real-time analytics platform that serves both product decision-making (funnels, A/B tests, feature adoption) and operational incident response at very large transactional scale, including the governance tradeoffs (dimensionality limits, query-blocking proxy) needed to keep it usable by many internal teams.

title: PhonePe's Guardrails: Real-Time Fraud Detection
summary: PhonePe's Trust & Safety team describes the real-time fraud detection engine underpinning its payments platform, which evaluates over 31+ crore (310 million) transactions a day against risk profiles for 59+ crore users and 4+ crore merchants. The system combines four signal layers -- connected intelligence (network mapping), action intelligence (device/activity aggregation), profile intelligence (sender/receiver/instrument risk scores), and behavioral biometrics (typing patterns, new-device logins) -- to either stop a transaction outright or prompt for additional authentication, all within milliseconds. The post also covers what happens after detection: PhonePe reverses funds to victims where still held by the company, runs a dedicated 24/7 cyber-cell portal for law enforcement request automation, and maintains a shared blocklist of fraudulent users with government entities.
what_they_built: A real-time, multi-layer fraud detection and risk-scoring engine (connected/action/profile intelligence plus behavioral biometrics) that triggers graduated responses -- blocking or step-up authentication -- during live transactions, paired with a victim-refund process and a dedicated law-enforcement request portal.
evidence: Concrete production scale figures given: 31+ crore (~310 million) transactions evaluated per day, 59+ crore user risk profiles, 4+ crore merchant risk profiles, decisions made "within a few milliseconds during transactions." Qualitative evidence: describes graduated response (stop vs. prompt for authentication) rather than blanket blocking, an operating victim-fund-reversal process, and a live law-enforcement cyber-cell portal running 24/7 -- claimed as an industry first for the company.
why_it_matters: Shows a PM building trust-and-safety systems how to frame a fraud engine as a layered, real-time product decision (block vs. step-up-auth) with downstream operational commitments (refunds, law-enforcement tooling) rather than a pure ML classification problem.

title: Introducing LiquidUI – PhonePe's Server-Driven UI Framework – Part 1
summary: PhonePe needed to ship UI changes and new product flows without waiting for app-store release cycles or forcing users onto newer app versions. They built LiquidUI, an in-house server-driven UI framework with a drag-and-drop web console, a backend that validates and publishes JSON UI specs through a maker-checker approval flow into PhonePe's config store, and native iOS/Android SDKs that render those specs into functional screens using 40+ predefined widgets, layout renderers, actions, and expressions. The framework has been used to build around 9 products spanning health insurance, personal loans, and credit card distribution, totaling over 130 screens inside the PhonePe app. Two recent products launched directly on older app versions with no code changes, which the team cites as evidence of backward compatibility and rapid delivery.
what_they_built: An internal server-driven-UI (SDUI) developer platform at PhonePe consisting of a web console, backend spec/publishing service with maker-checker approvals, and native SDKs that render JSON UI specs into app screens without requiring app updates.
evidence: Adoption across roughly 9 products and over 130 screens inside the PhonePe app; two recent products launched without code changes directly on older app versions, cited as validation of backward compatibility and delivery speed.
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete internal-platform pattern (SDUI with approval workflow and versioned rendering SDKs) that lets a product org decouple UI shipping cadence from app-store release cycles while preserving compatibility across old app versions.

title: Building PhonePe's Real-Time Transaction Model to prevent Frauds: A Journey
summary: PhonePe's Trust & Safety team faced a P2P UPI fraud problem complicated by the fact that many "fraud" complaints were actually product disputes, polluting model training data. They built a segmentation step to separate disputes from true fraud, then trained a real-time classification model on sender/receiver/transaction features, moving from batch to real-time scoring after testing showed immediate decisions worked better. Two systems support production: Yoda, a real-time knowledge store for feature aggregates, and Kratos, an evaluation engine returning model outputs within millisecond latency budgets during the transaction flow.
what_they_built: A real-time fraud-decisioning pipeline for P2P UPI transfers combining a dispute-vs-fraud segmentation step, a classification model, and two supporting systems (Yoda for real-time feature aggregation, Kratos for millisecond-latency model scoring during transactions).
evidence: Reported outcomes: 40% reduction in fraud rate on consumer-to-consumer transactions, 38% decrease in overall daily fraud complaints (50% reduction in the targeted high-value transaction segment), and a roughly 1% reinstatement rate on accounts blacklisted by the model.
why_it_matters: Shows how separating "customer dissatisfaction" signal from true fraud signal in noisy complaint data can materially improve a production risk model — a data-quality lesson that transfers to any PM building trust/safety systems on top of messy user reports.

title: Rollup: Managing 300 Billion Daily Metrics at PhonePe
summary: PhonePe's metrics platform ingests roughly 330 billion metrics per day into a 1.65 petabyte HBase cluster, making raw-resolution queries over long time windows slow and storage-intensive. The team built Rollup, a feature that aggregates raw data into lower-resolution (e.g. hourly) summaries so queries over long periods scan far fewer data points. Applied org-wide, it cut annual data points from 525k to 8,760 for a given series, compressed a day of raw data from ~2.54TB to ~40GB, and made rolled-up queries roughly 10x faster than raw ones, with the full daily rollup job completing in about 3 hours.
what_they_built: Rollup, an internal feature of PhonePe's metrics platform that periodically aggregates raw high-resolution metric data into lower-resolution (e.g., hourly) summaries for storage and querying.
evidence: Queries against rolled-up data are ~10x faster than against raw data; a day of raw data (~2.54TB) compresses to ~40GB (~1/64th the size); the daily rollup job over the full dataset completes in ~3 hours; annual data points for a metric drop from 525k to 8,760.
why_it_matters: Shows how an internal data/observability platform team can make a targeted resolution-tradeoff feature (not a new database or vendor tool) deliver order-of-magnitude cost and speed wins at massive scale, a pattern any PM running internal data infrastructure can borrow.

title: Beyond IaC: Engineering a Solution to Guarantee What Actually Is in Production (TestInfra)
summary: PhonePe's infrastructure team found that Infrastructure-as-Code only describes intended state, not what is actually running in production, given configuration drift and manual emergency interventions at scale. They built TestInfra, an internal validation platform on top of Pytest-Testinfra integrated with ReportPortal, that runs assertion-based checks against real servers across many teams' environments. When the underlying SaltStack event bus became a bottleneck at scale, they built a dedicated multi-master/multi-minion "Salt Exec" execution path that cut average check time from about 1,800 seconds to about 115 seconds, and the effort spun off an internal governance framework (SRE Forge) for cross-team tooling collaboration.
what_they_built: TestInfra, a Pytest-Testinfra-based infrastructure validation platform (with a dedicated Salt Exec execution path and ReportPortal reporting) that asserts actual production server state against expectations across PhonePe's environments.
evidence: Reported production usage: 25+ active environments, 20+ active contributors, 390+ logical test cases, 15,000+ lines of custom code, roughly 1 million single-environment runs within months, and average check execution time reduced from ~1,800 seconds to ~115 seconds after the Salt Exec redesign.
why_it_matters: Shows a PM/platform-owner pattern for internal tooling: pairing a declarative config system with an independent, continuously-run verification layer closes the gap between "intended" and "actual" state, and the tooling itself can seed broader cross-team platform governance.

title: Demystifying TStore: The Backbone of Billions of Transactions at PhonePe
summary: PhonePe needed a single system to store and serve billions of payment transactions consistently across services, geographies, and read patterns (user statements, merchant analytics, fraud detection) while write and read loads scaled independently. They built TStore: a write path (Entity Ingestor) that logs to Kafka then persists to HBase as source of truth while building field indices, and a read path (Feed Service) that serves history feeds and dashboards using HBase indices plus Elasticsearch for filtering/aggregation, with an Avro-based Schema Registry allowing schema evolution without code changes to TStore. The architecture models transactions as entities grouped under a Unit ID into a Unit, with Views enforcing per-stakeholder privacy filtering. In production it ingests at 45,000 RPS (<10ms) and serves reads at 100,000 RPS (<20ms), handling 8,000+ transactions/second at peak.
what_they_built: TStore, an internal transaction storage and retrieval platform at PhonePe with a decoupled Kafka/HBase write path (Entity Ingestor) and an Elasticsearch/HBase-backed read path (Feed Service), fronted by an Avro schema registry and client abstraction layer.
evidence: Reported production figures: write ingestion at 45,000 RPS with <10ms latency, read/Feed Service at 100,000 RPS with <20ms latency, and 8,000+ transactions/second processed during peak hours.
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete pattern for platform PMs building internal data infrastructure: separating write-heavy and read-heavy paths lets each scale and evolve independently, and a schema registry can decouple producer schema changes from platform code changes.

title: Building Pinterest's A/B Testing Platform
summary: Pinterest was running roughly 1,000 concurrent experiments and needed a system to stop experimenters from making predictable, costly mistakes at that scale. The Data team built a platform with three parts: a configuration system that pushes serialized experiment settings to all services within sub-minute intervals without code deploys, cross-platform client APIs (Python, JVM, JS, mobile), and an analytics pipeline that processes activation logs via Kafka/Singer. They paired this with a lightweight, peer-reviewed setup workflow where cross-functional helper groups check hypothesis validity, trigger logic, and group configuration before an experiment ships.
what_they_built: An internal A/B testing platform combining a real-time configuration/publishing system, multi-language client SDKs for assignment and activation, and a Kafka-based analytics pipeline, wrapped in a mandatory peer-review workflow for experiment setup.
evidence: Operating scale is cited directly (~1,000 concurrent experiments), and the piece describes the review/QA workflow (cross-functional helper groups, PR-based audit trails) as the mechanism for catching setup errors before launch; no before/after metrics or lift numbers are given, so the evidence is scale plus process description rather than a quantified outcome.
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete pattern for scaling experimentation governance — real-time config control plus mandatory cross-functional review — that a PM building or overseeing an internal experimentation platform can borrow directly.

title: Four lessons in making Pinterest faster on Android
summary: Pinterest engineering found the app was consistently slower on Android than iOS despite Android being ~90% of smartphones globally and most new signups coming from outside the US. They introduced Pinner Wait Time (PWT), a user-perceived latency metric spanning home feed, Pin closeup, and search, and paired it with NimbleDroid for automated cloud-based regression testing on every pull request. Concrete fixes included replacing reflective Gson type adapters with compile-time generation and reusing data already fetched in the feed response to avoid redundant network calls on Pin closeup. The team tied these speed gains directly to behavioral signals like deeper feed scrolling and fewer accidental double-taps.
what_they_built: A user-centric performance metric (Pinner Wait Time) combined with NimbleDroid-based automated regression testing on every PR, plus targeted Android optimizations (compile-time Gson adapters, feed-data reuse for Pin closeups).
evidence: Pin closeup loads got 60% faster by reusing feed-response data instead of re-fetching; compile-time Gson adapters saved ~30ms at cold start; NimbleDroid caught ~30 slowdown regressions over six months that would have "accounted for at least three seconds of additional wait time," and regression resolution time dropped from multiple days to about 21 hours.
why_it_matters: Shows how a PM/eng team can turn perceived latency into a first-class, engagement-linked metric and enforce it with continuous regression testing rather than one-off optimization sprints.

title: How Pinterest's design systems team measures adoption
publisher: Figma Blog (authored by Ravi Lingineni, Pinterest Design Technologist)
summary: Pinterest's Gestalt design systems team found that code-based component adoption metrics only covered web and lagged behind what designers actually used, so they built FigStats, a nightly service that hits the Figma REST API to compute a design adoption score per file. The score is simply the ratio of Gestalt-library layers to total layers on "handoff" pages of recently edited files (e.g. 7 of 15 layers, 46.6%), with hidden layers and experimental components excluded to keep the number honest. The team also tracks text-style and color-style usage to flag off-brand hex codes and non-standard fonts, and open-sourced the adoption-calculation logic as an npm library.
what_they_built: FigStats, an automated nightly Figma-REST-API service that computes a per-file design-adoption percentage (Gestalt component layers ÷ total layers) plus style-usage checks, separate from their existing code-adoption tracking.
evidence: Worked example given: "7 Gestalt layers ÷ 15 total layers, or 46.6%"; scoping rules (last-two-weeks files, handoff pages only, hidden/experimental layers excluded) described as deliberate accuracy fixes; the methodology was open-sourced as an npm library for external reuse.
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete pattern for defining a design-adoption metric that catches usage code metrics miss, and for scoping a metric (time window, page type, exclusions) to keep it honest rather than vanity.

title: Personalizing Pinterest's New User Experience Abroad
summary: Pinterest's U.S.-centric onboarding flow showed new international users the same set of topic boards regardless of country, which meant it wasn't surfacing locally relevant content during the critical first-session activation moment. The team built a pipeline that mined board topics and search query logs by country to generate candidate topics, ranked and fuzzy-matched relevant local boards using engagement signals, relevance, recency and language alignment, then routed those candidates through in-country teams to vet quality before shipping. They rolled this out for French, German, Japanese, Portuguese, and Spanish speakers. Users who went through the localized onboarding were 5-10 percent more likely to return and use Pinterest again.
what_they_built: A country-specific topic-selection pipeline for new-user onboarding that combines automated signal analysis (search logs, board engagement, fuzzy text matching) with human in-country review to surface locally relevant boards instead of a single U.S.-centric topic set.
evidence: Users who signed up through the localized onboarding were "5-10 percent more likely to come back and use Pinterest" — a measured retention/activation lift reported directly on the company's engineering blog.
why_it_matters: Shows a PM how to instrument an onboarding funnel for international expansion by pairing quantitative signal mining with lightweight human-in-the-loop localization review, rather than either pure automation or pure manual curation.

title: Trusting Metrics at Pinterest
summary: Pinterest engineering found that key metrics like Daily Active Users were prone to definitional drift and implementation bugs, making them untrustworthy for decision-making. They built a formal metric certification process requiring a written product/technical spec for each metric, automated data checkers and UI tests to validate the implementation before release, and recurring quarterly health checks to catch regressions afterward. Running the process against their existing metrics surfaced that every single metric audited had definitional issues, underscoring how widespread the problem was even at a mature data organization. The piece frames this as a governance workflow rather than a one-off fix, meant to be applied continuously as new metrics are created.
what_they_built: A three-step metric certification workflow: (1) write a product/technical spec defining correct metric behavior, (2) implement the metric with automated data checkers and UI tests, (3) run recurring tests and quarterly health checks to keep it accurate over time.
evidence: Qualitative rather than quantitative: no adoption numbers or before/after metrics are given, but the piece reports a concrete failure-mode finding from running the process — "Every metric we've audited has had definitional issues" — plus the stated lesson that catching metric errors before release is far cheaper than debugging them once they've shipped to production.
why_it_matters: A PM can borrow the pattern of treating metric definitions as a spec'd, tested, and periodically re-validated artifact rather than a one-time SQL query, which prevents silent metric drift from undermining product decisions.

title: How Pinterest fights misinformation, hate speech, and self-harm content with machine learning
summary: Pinterest needed to detect and remove policy-violating content — misinformation, hate speech, self-harm material, graphic violence — across hundreds of billions of Pins without relying solely on manual review. The Trust and Safety ML team built a layered enforcement system: image-signature grouping for consistent decisions on duplicate content, a daily-batch Pin Batch Model (Spark) scoring the full Pin corpus against six violation categories, a low-latency Pin Online Model (Flink + TensorFlow Java) for immediate action on new Pins, and a Board Batch Model that aggregates Pin-level embeddings to score boards without separate training. The team reports declines in both overall policy-violation reports and self-harm content specifically since rolling the system out.
what_they_built: A multi-model content-safety pipeline combining batch (Spark) and real-time (Flink/TensorFlow Java) scoring models plus image-signature grouping and board-level aggregation to detect and enforce against policy-violating Pins at platform scale.
evidence: "Policy-violating content reports per impression have declined by 52% since the fall of 2019" and reports for self-harm content decreased by 80% since April 2019, per the team's own reporting.
why_it_matters: Shows how to architect trust-and-safety enforcement as a layered product system (batch + real-time + aggregation) rather than a single model, and how to define enforcement-outcome metrics (reports per impression) that a PM can actually track.

title: NEP: Notification System and Relevance
summary: Pinterest's notifications team replaced a daily-budget-based send model with NEP, a near-real-time decisioning system that scores each candidate notification with a multi-task transformer model (predicting engagement, session activity, and unsubscribe likelihood) and gates sends through a policy layer with a PID controller that auto-tunes volume thresholds per user segment. The rebuild moved candidate generation and ranking onto a streaming platform (Pacer) with a GPU inference cluster, replacing manual threshold management with a closed-loop control system. Over 100 production experiment iterations were run to validate the new pipeline before full rollout. The launched configuration produced large lifts in email and push engagement and meaningful WAU growth, but also raised unsubscribe rates, which the team attributes candidly to notifications now reaching previously under-served user cohorts.
what_they_built: A real-time notification decisioning system (event pool + multi-task transformer ranker + PID-controlled policy layer) that replaced Pinterest's prior daily-budget allocation model for push and email sends.
evidence: Reports "large user email and push engagement lift and significant WAU gains" from the launched experiment group, alongside an explicitly disclosed tradeoff of increased unsubscribe rates, validated across 100+ production experiment iterations.
why_it_matters: Shows how to pair a ranking-model rebuild with an automated control mechanism (PID-tuned thresholds) so volume policy doesn't require constant manual tuning, and models honest tradeoff disclosure (engagement up, unsubscribes up) rather than cherry-picked wins.

title: Notification Volume Control and Optimization System at Pinterest
summary: Pinterest's previous notification system tightly coupled per-type frequency rules to CTR predictions, which made it nearly impossible to tell whether engagement gains from a new notification type or model were real or just came from sending more volume — a problem that got worse as more teams shipped new notification types. The team rebuilt the system around a single weekly per-user "notification budget," computed by a separate volume-optimization layer and enforced independently of the content-ranking/type-selection layer, so teams could add or improve notification types without silently changing total volume. The volume optimizer models the long-term effect of notification count on daily activity and unsubscribe risk (avoiding the independence assumption of prior send-by-send methods) and solves a scalable Map-Reduce budget-allocation algorithm across hundreds of millions of users. It shipped to production in mid-2017 and was validated with A/B tests across three user segments.
what_they_built: A production notification-volume-control platform that computes a weekly per-user notification budget via machine-learned activity/unsubscribe models, deliberately decoupled from the notification-type ranking system so other teams can experiment independently.
evidence: A/B test results reported in Table 1: email-only users saw volume -24%, notification CTR +31%, DAU +0%; push-only users saw volume -6%, CTR +11%, DAU +1%; email&push users saw email volume -7%/CTR +10% and push volume -4%/CTR +21%, with DAU +3%. The system also reallocated budget toward less-active "marginal" users, increasing their volume, clicks, DAU and WAU, while cutting volume for already-active "core" users without hurting their DAU/WAU.
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete architectural pattern — decoupling a volume/fatigue-control layer from content ranking — that lets a growing org test new engagement channels without volume changes confounding every experiment's results.

title: User State-Based Notification Volume Optimization
summary: Pinterest was sending notifications with a single universal model that didn't account for differences in how users at different engagement levels respond, risking either under-notifying (missed re-engagement) or over-notifying (unsubscribes/fatigue).
