what_they_built: A set of build-infrastructure changes to Shopify's CI system: optimized Docker agent provisioning (disk sizing plus read-only cache mounts), a compilation-result caching layer keyed on MD5 hashes of DB/asset files, and an improved test-selection/mapping system to skip full suite runs when unaffected.
evidence: CI p95 build time dropped from 45 minutes to 18 minutes; Docker container startup p95 improved from 90s to 25s; dependency preparation time fell from ~5 minutes to ~3 minutes; builds able to skip the full test suite rose from 45% to over 60%; test stability rose from 88% to 97%.
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete, measured playbook for treating internal CI/build tooling as a product with its own funnel of bottlenecks, cache strategy, and reliability metrics -- directly transferable to any PM or eng lead owning developer-platform experience.

title: How to Build an Experiment Pipeline from Scratch
summary: Shopify's Marketing Data Science team faced ad hoc, manually randomized email experiments stored in local files, leading to data loss, poor discoverability, cross-contamination between concurrent tests, and no way to manage many-to-many email-to-shop relationships. They built an automated PySpark pipeline with three stages -- SQL-based experiment definition, consolidation of multiple experiment definitions into continuous assignment, and deduplication to prevent cross-experiment contamination -- outputting a single table of email/shop/experiment assignments. The pipeline extended Shopify's existing experimentation and analysis framework to the email channel.
what_they_built: An automated experiment assignment and deduplication pipeline (built in PySpark) that lets data scientists define email A/B tests via SQL criteria files and safely run them without cross-contamination.
evidence: Adoption evidence: used not only by Marketing Data Science but across the data organization by teams focused on shipping, retail, and international growth to ship email experiments over the past year (as of the Oct 2020 post).
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete pattern for extending a web-oriented experimentation platform to a new channel (email) while solving assignment overlap and deduplication -- a reusable design for any PM building internal experimentation infrastructure.

title: Lost in Translations: Bringing the World to Shopify
summary: After twelve years of hardcoding English strings across its codebase, Shopify needed to internationalize its commerce platform for roughly 175 countries. The team built linters to stop new hardcoded strings, automated extraction tooling to pull existing strings into dictionary files, a pseudolocalization backend on top of Rails I18n to visually test layouts before real translations existed, migrated MySQL tables to full UTF-8 to support emoji and non-BMP characters, and shipped an internal Translation Platform that watched GitHub for language-file changes and auto-created translation requests with a professional TMS. The piece frames localization as ongoing infrastructure work rather than a one-time project.
what_they_built: An internal i18n toolchain: custom Rails linters/cops to catch new hardcoded strings, an automated string-extraction pipeline, a pseudolocalization testing backend, and a Translation Platform integrating GitHub with a translation management system.
evidence: Qualitative and mechanism-level: pseudolocalization simulated German text running ~30% longer and French ("Save" to "Sauvegarder") running roughly 200% longer to catch layout breakage before real translations landed; MySQL tables were migrated to full UTF-8 after the default 3-byte encoding was found to exclude emoji and non-BMP characters; the author states the initiative made Shopify "available in a variety of languages" within about six months, with localization framed as continuous, security-like maintenance rather than a finished project.
why_it_matters: Shows a PM/platform team how to sequence an i18n retrofit on a mature codebase — stop the bleeding with linting, automate extraction for the easy cases, and build synthetic testing (pseudolocalization) before spending translator budget on real content.

title: How Shopify Payments Uses Machine Learning to Boost Payment Success Rates by 0.26% and Cut Fraud Chargebacks by 20%
summary: Shopify Payments faced the standard fraud-prevention tradeoff: blanket 3D Secure (3DS) challenges reduce fraud but also reject legitimate buyers, costing conversion (the piece cites an industry-wide $443B lost annually to incorrectly rejected legitimate transactions). In January 2025 they shipped a machine learning preauthorization model, trained across millions of merchants and billions of transactions on the full buying journey, that decides per-transaction whether to force 3DS only on high-risk checkouts rather than applying uniform rules, with no merchant configuration required. The system lifted payment success rates by 26 basis points and cut fraud-categorized chargebacks by 20% (8 bps off a 41-bps base). Shopify frames this as evidence that security and conversion don't have to trade off when routing decisions are made intelligently per transaction.
what_they_built: A machine learning preauthorization/routing model inside Shopify Payments that predicts transaction risk across the full buyer journey and selectively forces 3D Secure authentication only on high-risk checkouts instead of applying blanket rules.
evidence: "26-basis-point increase in payment success rates alongside a 20% reduction (8 bps out of a 41-bps base) in chargebacks categorized as fraudulent"; extrapolated to 2024 volumes, "$471 million in additional annual gross payments volume" and "$62 million" saved in chargeback-related costs; accounting for the full $4.41-per-$1-of-fraud cost multiplier, the piece claims "$273 million in true costs" saved.
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete pattern for reframing a classic false binary (security vs. conversion) as a routing/segmentation problem solved with per-transaction risk scoring rather than uniform policy.

title: Putting the system back in our design system
summary: Shopify's admin surface runs 2.5 million lines of TypeScript for UX, and while its Polaris design-system components were used in 70% of the admin, the underlying design tokens only reached 44% of components. Tokens were scattered across CSS custom properties, Sass functions, and mixins in different repos with incomplete, fragmented documentation, so product teams often hardcoded values or forked components instead of reusing tokens. The design systems team consolidated the styling code into a unified token set and built centralized documentation on polaris.shopify.com, shipping this as part of Polaris v9.0.0 to make large-scale design changes cheaper and token values easier to discover.
what_they_built: A consolidated, unified design-token system (replacing fragmented CSS custom properties, Sass functions, and mixins) plus centralized token documentation, shipped in Polaris v9.0.0.
evidence: Reports the gap that motivated the work: Polaris components used in 70% of the admin vs. tokens used in only 44% of admin components, in a 2.5-million-line TypeScript admin codebase.
why_it_matters: Shows PMs/design leads how to make a design system's health legible and prioritizable by measuring component adoption separately from token adoption, rather than treating "system usage" as one number.

title: Uplifting Shopify Polaris: The process of evolving a large scale design system
summary: Shopify's admin surface had grown "dull" and "bland" on a flat, minimalist visual language, and the design team set out to modernize Polaris — its shared component library — without forcing every team to manually redo their UI. They redesigned core tokens and components (buttons went through 10 iterations to get the right "plastic, not glass" tactility, icons moved to 1.5px outline weight with filled nav variants, typography switched to Inter, primary color shifted from green to neutral black, and a new "Snappy" motion curve was introduced) and pushed the changes centrally through Polaris. Because the admin already had high mainline adoption of the shared library, most of the surface updated automatically once the library shipped, with only a minority of screens needing manual rework. The full redesign shipped in 10 weeks to coincide with Summer Edition 2023, and merchant testing described the result as more "refined," "denser," and "useful."
what_they_built: A systemic visual and interaction "uplift" to Shopify's Polaris design system — new button styling, iconography, typography (Inter), color primitives, and a custom motion curve — engineered to propagate through the shared component library rather than require per-surface rebuilds.
evidence: The admin had 86.6% Polaris mainline coverage going in; once the uplifted Polaris library was updated (about 3 weeks of work), roughly 86% of the admin changed automatically, leaving only ~14% needing manual updates. The full transformation, including design and rollout, shipped in 10 weeks for Summer Edition 2023.
why_it_matters: Shows a PM-relevant lever for platform-scale redesigns: investing upfront in shared-component adoption (mainline coverage) turns a redesign into a centrally-pushed update instead of a team-by-team migration project, dramatically cutting rollout time and coordination cost.

title: How to Use Quasi-experiments and Counterfactuals to Build Great Products
summary: A Shopify data scientist lays out a hierarchy of causal-inference methods for product teams to use when a clean A/B test isn't possible: randomized A/B tests as the gold standard, then quasi-experiments (fixed-effects regression, difference-in-differences), then time-series counterfactual models when no control group exists at all. The piece walks through a real internal case: a mandatory security update that added user friction, where the team had no control group to compare against. They trained a time-series model on unaffected features and global activity trends to predict what usage would have been absent the update, then compared prediction to actuals.
what_they_built: A "levels of evidence" decision framework for choosing between A/B tests, quasi-experimental designs, and counterfactual time-series models, applied to a real rollout where a counterfactual model estimated feature usage without a control group.
evidence: Comparing the counterfactual model's predicted usage to actual usage after the security update rollout, the team "found that there was no lift," concluding the update did not negatively affect feature usage despite introducing friction — a qualitative/directional null result rather than a headline metric swing.
why_it_matters: Gives a PM a concrete fallback playbook for measuring impact when randomization is impossible (ethical, technical, or retroactive constraints) rather than shipping blind or giving up on measurement.

title: 2,000 Robots Walk Into a Shop: Simulated A/B Testing
summary: Shopify merchants with low traffic can't reach statistical significance on traditional A/B tests, so Shopify built SimGym, a platform that runs hundreds to thousands of AI browser agents with distinct personas, budgets, and shopping intents against a merchant's storefront in a contained environment to simulate outcomes in minutes. The system serves 400,000 simulated shopping sessions per day, backed by 48 dedicated NVIDIA B200 GPUs running up to 2,048 concurrent sequences. The team then optimized the serving stack (async scheduling, reduced HTTP/gRPC overhead, speculative decoding, MIG GPU partitioning), cutting average LLM latency by roughly 20%, which lowered cost per merchant run by about 10% and raised daily throughput by about 12% (from 1,311 to 1,463 merchant runs/day, with per-session duration dropping from 7.3 to 6.6 minutes).
what_they_built: SimGym, an internal simulated-testing platform that substitutes concurrent AI browser agents for real customer traffic to give low-traffic merchants fast, statistically usable A/B-test-like results.
evidence: 400,000 simulated sessions/day; ~20% average LLM latency reduction from serving optimizations translated into ~10% lower cost per merchant run and ~12% higher daily throughput (1,311 to 1,463 merchant runs/day); session duration fell from 7.3 to 6.6 minutes; speculative decoding added ~6% improvement at 100-200 concurrent sessions; B200 vs H200 gave a 5.2x tokens/second speedup.
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete pattern for giving low-traffic customers/products a usable substitute for statistical experimentation by simulating demand with agents rather than waiting for real traffic to accumulate.

title: Adopting Sorbet at Scale
summary: Shopify's 37,000-file, 622,000-method Ruby monolith was untyped, making refactors risky and production NameError/NoMethodError crashes common. The team rolled out Sorbet gradual typing across the codebase and built three open-source tools (RuboCop Sorbet, Tapioca, Spoom) to drive and enforce adoption at scale, including generating RBI files automatically for gems and Rails DSLs. They ran controlled experiments showing Sorbet's runtime checks eliminated whole classes of production errors, and pushed adoption to 80% of files typed and roughly half of methods signed, while extending usage to 60 internal projects beyond the monolith.
what_they_built: A gradual-typing rollout of Sorbet across Shopify's Ruby monolith plus three open-sourced supporting tools (RuboCop Sorbet for enforcement, Tapioca for auto-generating RBI type files, Spoom for programmatic Sorbet tooling/reporting).
evidence: 80% of files (including tests) are typed:true or higher; ~half of method calls are typed and ~half of methods have signatures; Sorbet in use on the main monolith plus 60 internal projects; a controlled experiment on their internal dev tool showed production NameErrors dropped to zero after enabling Sorbet, and typed:true files showed measurable NoMethodError reductions even before signatures were added.
why_it_matters: Shows a PM/platform team how to sequence a large-scale, low-friction internal-tooling migration (gradual, file-by-file typing plus purpose-built enforcement/automation tooling) and how to prove its value with a controlled before/after error-rate experiment rather than just adoption counts.

title: Speculation Rules at Shopify
summary: Shopify's performance team rolled out the browser-native Speculation Rules API across all Liquid-based storefronts in June 2025 to speed up navigation without custom JavaScript. They shipped the ruleset via a Speculation-Rules HTTP header pointing to an external JSON file, using a conservative prefetch strategy triggered on mousedown/touchdown for same-site links in Chromium browsers. The team weighed trade-offs between wasted bandwidth from over-aggressive prefetching and analytics overreporting from prerendering, opting for cautious eagerness. The rollout produced measurable navigation speedups across TTFB, FCP, and LCP.
what_they_built: A platform-wide, header-delivered Speculation Rules configuration (external JSON referenced via the Speculation-Rules HTTP header) that prefetches likely next-page navigations on pointer-down events across all Shopify Liquid storefronts.
evidence: Average 130ms improvement on desktop and 180ms on mobile across all percentiles and across all loading metrics (TTFB, FCP, LCP).
why_it_matters: Shows how a platform team can ship a single, centrally-controlled performance primitive (via an HTTP header) across thousands of independently-built storefronts, trading conservative defaults for safety against bandwidth waste and analytics distortion.

title: Faster storefront navigations with moderate speculation rules
summary: Shopify's performance team had all Liquid storefronts running the browser Speculation Rules API at a conservative eagerness setting, which limited how proactively Chromium browsers prefetched/prerendered likely next navigations. They switched the default eagerness to "moderate" across all storefronts on Chromium-based browsers and measured the before/after impact on real traffic. The change produced a large drop in desktop TTFB and LCP with no observed regressions, at the cost of a meaningful increase in total speculative HTML requests. Firefox support is still in development and Safari is expected via an upcoming beta.
what_they_built: A platform-wide switch of the Speculation Rules eagerness setting from conservative to moderate on all Liquid storefronts, increasing how aggressively browsers prefetch/prerender likely next-page navigations.
evidence: Desktop: TTFB -285ms median, FCP -224ms median, LCP -228ms median; about 10% of speculated desktop navigations hit a TTFB of exactly 0 (already cached). Mobile: TTFB -25ms, FCP -20ms, LCP -24ms median. Trade-off: nearly 4x more speculated requests on desktop (14% increase in total HTML requests); mobile request volume stayed roughly stable.
why_it_matters: Shows a PM/platform team how a single low-risk config change to a browser API, rolled out broadly and measured with real percentile data, can move core perceived-speed metrics without a UI redesign.

title: Store Speed and Conversion: What the Data Shows
summary: Shopify analyzed 28 days of Core Web Vitals data (LCP, INP, CLS) against conversion rates across its actively-selling merchant base, using a stated methodology: excluding the slowest 5% of stores as outliers, using the 90th percentile for interactivity and layout-stability metrics, controlling for the other two metrics when isolating each one, and grouping stores into performance tiers to average out confounders. They found conversion drops roughly 3.5% per 100ms of added LCP and about 1.5% per 32ms of added INP, with stores at 2.5s LCP converting roughly 30% worse than stores at 1.5s LCP. CLS showed no consistent correlation with conversion, which they note plainly rather than forcing a story.
what_they_built: A platform-scale correlational analysis of Core Web Vitals (LCP, INP, CLS) against store conversion rates, run across Shopify's own merchant base with an explicit outlier-handling and confound-control methodology.
evidence: Reports ~3.5% lower conversion per 100ms slower LCP, ~30% lower conversion comparing 2.5s vs 1.5s LCP stores, ~1.5% lower conversion per 32ms slower INP, and an explicit null result for CLS versus conversion.
why_it_matters: Gives a PM a concrete, methodologically transparent benchmark for how much page-speed regressions cost in conversion, and a reminder that not every UX metric will show a clean signal — worth reporting the null result rather than hiding it.

title: Shopify Storefronts Have Gotten More Than 35% Faster
summary: Shopify's storefront platform team faced slow-loading storefronts hurting conversion and set out to improve Core Web Vitals across the whole platform. They consolidated static assets from a separate cdn.shopify.com domain onto a /cdn path prefix on the same hostname to leverage HTTP/3 prioritization, introduced section-aware lazy-loading in the theme engine (using section index as a heuristic so hero/product images aren't lazily loaded), and expanded points of presence with more distributed database placement to cut geographic latency. The result was a 35% increase in sessions passing the TTFB threshold and nearly 400ms shaved off LCP.
what_they_built: A CDN/hostname consolidation (cdn.shopify.com to a /cdn path prefix) plus section-aware lazy-loading logic built into the theme engine's image_tag/metafield_tag filters, alongside expanded global points of presence and distributed database placement.
evidence: 35% more sessions passing the TTFB Core Web Vitals metric over twelve months; nearly 400ms improvement to LCP from the CDN path change; cited industry research that a 10% faster first experience correlates with a 7% conversion increase.
why_it_matters: Shows a platform team how consolidating a CDN onto the primary hostname and building rendering-priority heuristics directly into a theme/template engine can move measurable, storefront-wide performance and conversion metrics rather than relying on individual developers to optimize each site.

title: Test Budget: Time Constrained CI Feedback
summary: Shopify's Test Infrastructure team faced a 170,000+ test suite in their core monolith that was too slow to run in full on every CI build, so they explored a time-constrained "test budget" that prioritizes which tests to run within a fixed window. They evaluated several prioritization criteria (default, failure_rate, churn, complexity) against metrics like time-to-first-failure (TTFF) and average percentage of faults detected (APFD), run against historical build data. The failure_rate-based prioritization consistently outperformed others, detecting a higher share of failures earlier while running a smaller fraction of the suite.
what_they_built: A test-selection and prioritization system for Shopify's monolith CI pipeline that ranks and runs a subset of a 170,000+ test suite within a time budget, ordering tests using criteria such as historical failure rate, code churn, and complexity.
evidence: Using the failure_rate criterion, they found failures after running only 70% of the test-selection suite; at 60% of the suite run, failure_rate prioritization detected 80% of faults, versus default prioritization needing 50% of the suite to detect only 50% of faults. Median time-to-first-failure was under 5 minutes for failure_rate and default criteria, while churn and complexity criteria performed far worse (median APFD near zero, TTFF up to ~16 minutes at the third quartile).
why_it_matters: Shows PMs and platform teams a concrete, metrics-driven way to trade off CI feedback speed against test coverage confidence when a test suite has outgrown "just run everything."

title: Case Study → Visitor Surveys: Generative research to understand customers
summary: A Shopify Design team member ran a four-month generative-research project on a live Shopify store, embedding an open-ended online survey (via Pixelpop popup, hosted in Wufoo) with conditional logic that tailored questions to new, repeat, and fanatic customer segments. The team collected 133 responses, consolidated them in Excel, and cross-referenced results against actual Shopify order data in SPSS to connect qualitative feedback to real purchase behavior. The research surfaced concrete findings: email was the strongly preferred support channel across segments, photography-related requests were a top unmet feature need, and customers who redeemed a $25 incentive code spent meaningfully more per order than non-redeemers. The piece documents specific method choices such as neutral/open-ended question wording to reduce bias and the design of the incentive itself.
what_they_built: A segmented, conditional-logic online visitor survey (popup-triggered, 133 responses over 4 months) whose results were merged with Shopify's own order-history data for cross-referenced analysis.
evidence: Customers who redeemed the $25 discount incentive had an average order value about $37 higher than non-redeemers ($225 vs. $188, roughly a 20% lift), about one-third of respondents redeemed the incentive, email was the clear preferred contact channel, and photography-related feature requests ranked as the second most-requested missing feature.
why_it_matters: Shows PMs and designers a lightweight, replicable way to run generative customer research that stays connected to real transactional outcomes rather than staying purely qualitative.

title: How we scaled our design system to unleash Skyscanner's new brand
summary: Skyscanner needed to roll out a completely new brand across more than 40 engineering squads without disrupting their work, while testing the new look via controlled experiments and keeping the unreleased branding hidden in their open-source design system. Instead of adding one-off props or duplicating components, the team extended Backpack's existing partner-theming mechanism to cover more design properties (e.g., button corner-radius) and previously unthemed components, using a deliberately garish "London" test theme to surface gaps and a covertly named "Azure" theme to carry the real new brand. They wired the new theme into Dr Jekyll, Skyscanner's internal experimentation platform, to allow regional and percentage-based rollout ahead of a coordinated global launch.
what_they_built: Extended the Backpack design system's theming layer to support a full brand refresh (new theme-able properties and components), integrated with the Dr Jekyll experimentation platform for staged, regional rollout, then flipped a single switch for global launch.
evidence: Over 40 squads achieved 100% design system adoption ahead of launch, and on launch day the new brand was enabled for "tens of millions of travellers" by toggling a switch in Dr Jekyll's rollout interface.
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete pattern for treating a design system as a platform capability — building theming and experimentation hooks in advance so a company-wide brand change can be coordinated and rolled back safely instead of shipped as a risky big-bang release.

title: Building products for large scale experimentation
summary: Skyscanner's original Dr Jekyll experimentation platform tied experiment variants directly to hard-coded sections of the codebase, forcing engineers to hand-build every variant they wanted to test. The team layered in "configurables" — reusable links between code and data (booleans, strings, or whole code modules) that could be combined like Lego blocks into experiment variants without new development. Testing a booking-panel redesign (four independent changes: collapsing itinerary info, adding star ratings, repositioning itinerary, expanding the provider list) via configurables let them explore combinations beyond the single design they had planned to build.
what_they_built: A "configurables" layer on top of Skyscanner's Dr Jekyll experimentation platform that decouples experiment variants from code, letting teams combine independently-built modules into many testable variants.
evidence: Qualitative: an unplanned combination of the four booking-panel configurables was discovered in-browser and shipped to production "with no additional development effort due to the available combinations" — no quantitative metrics given.
why_it_matters: Shows a PM/platform pattern where investing in a combinatorial experimentation abstraction can surface winning designs the team never explicitly planned to build, at effectively zero marginal dev cost.

title: 3 Techniques We Used to Improve Skyscanner for iOS Performance
summary: Skyscanner's iOS team found the flight-results scroll experience was janky, especially on the non-flagship devices roughly half their users owned (iPhone 5 alone was ~10%). Using Xcode Instruments (Time Profiler, Core Animation) to actually measure rather than guess, they identified two hot spots: repeated recomputation of accessibility labels and repeated date calculations. Caching both cut CPU time for localized-resource calls from 13.5% to 4.6% (an 8.9% gain) and date-function CPU time from 3.9% to 0.4% (a 3.2% gain). Combined with low-end-device testing, this took the share of users seeing 30+ FPS while scrolling from 61% to 97.47%.
what_they_built: Instrumented profiling workflow plus two caching layers (accessibility-label cache and a date cache) in the Skyscanner iOS app's scroll rendering path, validated on low-end devices like the iPhone 5.
evidence: Accessibility-label caching cut CPU for localized-resource calls from 13.5% to 4.6% (8.9% perf gain); date caching cut date-function CPU from 3.9% to 0.4% (3.2% perf gain); overall users experiencing 30+ FPS rose from 61% to 97.47%.
why_it_matters: Shows a PM/eng team turning a vague "app feels slow" complaint into targeted, measured fixes by profiling first and tying the fix to a user-facing perceived-smoothness metric rather than a proxy.

title: How Recursion Brought Down Flight Search at Skyscanner
summary: On September 14, 2023, a bug in Skyscanner's daily geo-data "materialisation" pipeline made some locations (like Scotland) reference themselves as their own parent, creating circular loops in what should have been a hierarchical parent-child structure. When flight-search requests touched these locations, threads entered infinite loops traversing the corrupted hierarchy, burning CPU across hundreds of Kubernetes pods; the resulting throttling triggered autoscaling that added more pods to handle what looked like demand, while an upstream warmup service using an affected location even prevented some systems from starting. Diagnosis was slower than it should have been because the team's existing telemetry couldn't reveal an infinite loop and initial instinct was to treat it as a performance problem, until thread dumps examined with JVM profilers exposed the real cause; rerunning the corrected pipeline restored normal operations.
what_they_built: A postmortem write-up detailing the failure mechanism in Skyscanner's geo-data materialisation pipeline and flight-search serving stack, plus the diagnostic and remediation steps taken during the incident.
evidence: "In total, we served over one million errors to travellers searching for flights." The team also names concrete fixes/lessons: treat data changes with the same rigor as code changes, avoid recency bias during incident investigation, run large-scale incident scenario drills, validate actual demand signals before autoscaling, and take regular timeouts during extended incidents to reassess.
why_it_matters: A reminder that data-pipeline changes need the same review/testing rigor as code deploys, and that autoscaling on symptom metrics (CPU/queue depth) without validating true demand can amplify a bad rollout rather than absorb it.

title: WISE: Skyscanner's Bayesian AB Experimentation Library and Decision Engine
summary: Skyscanner's experimentation squad faced inconsistent, manual interpretation of A/B test results across teams using their Dr Jekyll experimentation platform. They built WISE, a centralized Python library that applies Bayesian models (beta-binomial for conversion metrics, hurdle gamma-exponential for zero-inflated revenue metrics) to automatically compute credible intervals and recommend a winning variant against guardrail metrics, rather than relying on manual p-value interpretation. WISE was integrated into Dr Jekyll as the standard decision engine. The team reports widespread adoption across teams and that it standardized experiment analysis and decision-making company-wide.
what_they_built: A Python statistics library (WISE) using Bayesian beta-binomial and hurdle gamma-exponential models, integrated into Skyscanner's Dr Jekyll experimentation platform, that automatically analyzes A/B tests and recommends a winning variant based on 90% highest-density-interval lift over control plus guardrail-metric checks.
evidence: Qualitative adoption claim: "WISE has seen widespread adoption across teams at Skyscanner" and it "eliminates the need for manual analysis of experimentation results, so we can ship faster," and "helped standardize experiment analysis and product decision-making across the company." No hard usage numbers given.
why_it_matters: Shows how a PM-adjacent platform team can turn ad hoc statistical judgment calls into a repeatable, guardrail-aware decision engine that speeds shipping without sacrificing rigor.

title: How to Fail at Accessibility
summary: A Slack senior engineer recounts shipping an inaccessible feature and getting flagged by Slack's internal accessibility team, then walks through the concrete testing and building practices Slack adopted afterward: color contrast checks, keyboard navigation, screenreader testing (VoiceOver/NVDA), semantic HTML and ARIA usage, and refactoring components like a combobox into a single accessible unit rather than two separate pieces. At the org level, Slack moved from having one accessibility expert cover 75 engineers to training the whole frontend org, mandating accessibility considerations in tech specs, centralizing accessible components in its Slack Kit design system, and running dedicated QA and third-party audits. The piece treats accessibility bugs as launch blockers, not polish items.
what_they_built: A company-wide accessibility process at Slack — accessibility training for all frontend engineers, mandatory accessibility sections in tech specs, a centralized accessible component library (Slack Kit), and dedicated QA plus third-party accessibility audits.
evidence: Slack Kit's accessible components reached 75% adoption across the applicable codebase, and accessibility knowledge scaled from a single dedicated expert to training the entire 75+ person frontend engineering team; the rest of the evidence is qualitative — specific failure modes (unlabeled icons, broken keyboard focus, split components confusing screenreaders) and the fixes that resolved them.
why_it_matters: Shows a PM how to convert accessibility from an individual-expert bottleneck into a scaled, blocking-by-default process: training plus tooling (design system, tech-spec requirements, QA gates) rather than relying on one specialist to catch everything.

title: What We've Learned About Designing for Accessibility From Our Users
summary: Slack's design team describes how they moved from ad hoc accessibility fixes to a structured "Champions" program of screen-reader and keyboard-navigation users who gave ongoing feedback on the product. That feedback drove a redesign of core interface navigation: a new toolbar pattern with standard Home/End keyboard shortcuts, a new F6 (or Cmd+Ctrl+Left/Right) paradigm letting users jump between UI regions, tab-trapping to bound navigation within a region, and more consistent focus placement when opening threads. The piece is written as lessons learned directly from running these changes past real assistive-technology users rather than as a product launch pitch.
what_they_built: A formal recurring feedback program ("Champions") with assistive-technology users, plus a redesigned keyboard/screen-reader navigation model for Slack's UI centered on F6 region-jumping and toolbar/tab-trapping conventions.
evidence: Qualitative, not quantitative: the team reports that "traversing Slack's UI was made much more efficient" and that screen-reader users had better orientation and hierarchy/labeling clarity after the changes, based on direct feedback from the Champions user group — no adoption numbers or before/after metrics are given.
why_it_matters: Shows a PM how to operationalize accessibility feedback into an ongoing structured research loop (not a one-off audit) and how to ship navigation-paradigm changes validated directly by the affected user population.

title: How We Design Our APIs at Slack
summary: Slack's platform team describes the internal process it actually runs for shipping public APIs: a four-stage pipeline of writing an API specification, cross-functional review in an internal #api-decisions Slack channel, early feedback from partner developers, and a beta phase with selected partners before general release. The post lays out six design principles governing that process, including a hard target of getting a new developer to a working "Hello World" in about 15 minutes and a strict avoid-breaking-changes philosophy summed up as "what worked yesterday should work tomorrow." It grounds the principles with a concrete example: refactoring the rtm.start method into rtm.connect to strip out an unnecessarily large data payload while preserving compatibility.
what_they_built: An internal four-stage API design and review workflow (spec -> #api-decisions channel review -> partner early feedback -> beta testing) governed by six documented design principles.
evidence: Qualitative: names the specific internal review channel (#api-decisions), a concrete implemented example (rtm.start refactored to rtm.connect to remove unneeded payload data without breaking compatibility), and a specific target metric (~15 minutes to first successful "Hello World" call). No adoption or usage-lift numbers are given.
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete, lightweight internal governance pattern (spec + named review channel + partner beta) a platform PM can copy to keep external API changes consistent and non-breaking without heavyweight process.

title: Automated Accessibility Testing at Slack
summary: Slack's engineering team needed a scalable way to catch accessibility regressions without relying solely on manual audits. They built an automated testing layer using @axe-core/playwright integrated into their existing Playwright E2E framework, checking against WCAG 2.1 Levels A and AA and filtering/deduplicating violations with screenshots of affected pages. The initial rollout shipped 91 non-blocking tests running as daily regression checks in Buildkite, with results piped to alert channels and a Jira ticket-triage workflow feeding an internal accessibility team's backlog. The team explicitly frames this as a complement to, not a replacement for, manual and screen-reader-based testing.
what_they_built: An automated accessibility-testing pipeline that runs Axe Core checks inside Slack's Playwright E2E suite, with a controllable rollout flag, CI integration, and automatic Jira ticket creation for triaging discovered violations.
evidence: Initial suite of 91 non-blocking accessibility tests running as scheduled daily regressions in CI (Buildkite), feeding results to alert channels and generating auto-labeled Jira tickets placed in a dedicated triage epic.
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete pattern for PMs/platform teams to bootstrap accessibility coverage non-blockingly first, then use triage data to decide where to invest manual/screen-reader testing.

title: The Case of the Recursive Resolvers: Slack's DNSSEC Rollout Incident
summary: Slack tried three times to enable DNSSEC on slack.com for FedRAMP Moderate compliance, and the third attempt on September 30, 2021 caused a 24-hour outage for under 1% of online users. After publishing then withdrawing the DS record, the team disabled DNSSEC signing without realizing the .com zone's 24-hour TTL kept the withdrawn DS record cached at resolvers, so validation kept failing even after rollback. The actual mechanism was subtler still: Route 53's NSEC responses for the wildcard *.slack.com domain omitted existing record types in their bitmap, and resolvers like Google Public DNS cached this as proof nothing existed there, effectively poisoning the cache for app.slack.com. Managing the rollout via infrastructure-as-code meant the team missed an AWS console warning dialog about the risk of disabling DNSSEC signing prematurely.
what_they_built: Not a product build but a postmortem/incident report of Slack's staged DNSSEC signing rollout on slack.com and the coordinated recovery involving AWS Route 53 fixes and DNS-resolver cache purges.
evidence: A 24-hour outage affecting less than 1% of online users; root cause traced to an NSEC bitmap bug in Route 53 that Amazon subsequently fixed; recovery required coordinated cache purges across major public resolvers including Google Public DNS and Cloudflare.
why_it_matters: Shows a PM/platform team why infra-as-code tooling can silently strip away vendor safety warnings, and why rollback plans for DNS-adjacent changes must account for caching/TTL behavior across third-party resolvers you don't control.

title: A Terrible, Horrible, No-Good, Very Bad Day at Slack
summary: On May 12, 2020, Slack suffered a major outage that began at 4:45pm Pacific when a morning database incident triggered an eight-hour buildup of stale state in the HAProxy load-balancer configuration pipeline. A bug in the program syncing Consul-template host lists with HAProxy always tried to add new webapp backend slots before freeing old ones, so as the day's autoscale-down kicked in, HAProxy instances were left routing only to a shrinking pool of stale backends and users saw a spike in HTTP 503 errors. The team found that monitoring for this exact failure mode had silently broken because the system had run untouched for so long. Slack committed to replacing the custom HAProxy state-manipulation pipeline with Envoy's native xDS control-plane integration for endpoint discovery.
what_they_built: A postmortem-driven architectural response: replacing a custom, bug-prone HAProxy backend-state synchronization pipeline with Envoy plus an xDS control plane for endpoint discovery.
evidence: Concrete production failure mode described (HAProxy backend-slot allocation bug, silently-broken monitoring for that scenario) and a resulting spike in HTTP 503 errors; company committed publicly to a multi-quarter migration to Envoy/xDS as the fix.
why_it_matters: Shows a PM/platform lesson that infrastructure "glue" code which nobody touches because it "just works" is exactly where monitoring blind spots hide, and that a production failure can justify a multi-quarter platform migration decision.

title: Ways We Make the Slack iOS App Accessible
summary: Slack's iOS engineering team needed the app to work for users with vision, motion-sensitivity, and other accessibility needs rather than treating accessibility as an afterthought. They shipped five concrete changes: a setting to disable animated GIFs/emoji (using the FLAnimatedImage framework, synced across devices via websocket), Dynamic Type-based font scaling, custom VoiceOver labels/traits/values on UI elements via Apple's Accessibility API, a full color-contrast audit targeting 7:1 ratios (exceeding WCAG 2.0 AAA's 4.5:1), and accessibility-identifier-based automated UI tests using the Kif framework to catch layout regressions. The contrast work is evidenced with a concrete before/after number on timestamps.
what_they_built: Five shipped iOS accessibility features: animation-disabling toggle, synced Dynamic Type font scaling, VoiceOver-compatible custom UI labeling, an AAA-contrast color palette, and Kif-based automated accessibility UI tests.
evidence: Concrete before/after metric: timestamp text contrast ratio improved from 2.2:1 to 4.8:1, as part of a broader push to hit 7:1 contrast (above WCAG 2.0 AAA's 4.5:1 minimum) across the interface; plus qualitative implementation detail for each of the five features.
why_it_matters: Shows a PM/design team how to operationalize accessibility as measurable design-system work (contrast targets, synced settings, automated regression tests) rather than a one-off compliance checkbox.

title: The Gradual Design System: How We Built Slack Kit
summary: As Slack grew rapidly, UI components across teams became inconsistent, and the team faced pressure to standardize without stalling product work. Rather than attempting a comprehensive design system upfront, they started small and concrete: pruning the color palette down to just 16 colors that could satisfy varied use cases, then rebuilding React components one at a time to meet "carrier-grade" standards of robustness, accessibility, and flexibility. Over time this incremental approach produced Slack Kit, a library that eventually grew to 40+ components. The team measured the payoff directly in build velocity — the first component (a multi-select) was expensive to build, but the investment in the underlying system paid off on every component built afterward.
what_they_built: Slack Kit, an internal React-based design system/component library built incrementally rather than all at once, starting with a pruned 16-color palette and expanding to 40+ standardized, accessibility-focused UI components.
evidence: Direct before/after metric on component build time: "Our first multi-select took almost two months to build, but subsequent feature implementations took just hours."
why_it_matters: Shows a PM/design-eng leadership pattern for justifying design-system investment: start with a narrow, high-friction real problem (not a comprehensive spec), and use build-time-per-component as the ROI metric to prove out and expand the system.

title: Slack's Incident on 2-22-22
summary: On February 22, 2022, a routine percentage-based rollout of Consul agent upgrades across Slack's fleet caused cache nodes to deregister and re-register in rapid succession. Slack's newer Mcrib component (which generates Mcrouter configs from Consul service discovery) reacted to this churn faster and more efficiently than its predecessor, which paradoxically increased cache churn and emptied cache nodes right as peak traffic hit. That drove a flood of scatter queries against a Vitess keyspace sharded by user, overloading the database tier and triggering a cascading failure across the application. Engineers mitigated by throttling client boot operations to relieve database pressure while the cache refilled, and drew broader lessons about how a "better," faster subsystem can make the overall system less safe.
what_they_built: A retrospective post-mortem analyzing how Mcrib (a Consul-driven Mcrouter config generator), Memcached/Mcrouter caching, and Vitess sharded MySQL interacted to cause a cascading production outage, paired with concrete fixes like throttling client boot queries.
evidence: Explicit failure-mode narrative: cache node churn from Consul agent restarts emptied the cache tier at peak traffic, causing "nearly all users" to query every shard of a channel-membership keyspace, overloading Vitess; mitigated via throttling boot operations, with stated follow-up risks (e.g., a brief network partition could trigger the same cascade).
why_it_matters: Shows a PM/platform builder why component-level improvements (a faster, more efficient subsystem) can degrade system-level safety, and why rollout procedures and cross-system interaction effects deserve explicit review, not just individual component correctness.

title: How Our Biggest Redesign Yet Came to Be
summary: Slack's VP of Product Design and Sr. Principal PM describe how the team approached its largest-ever navigation redesign: starting with deliberately provocative, non-final prototypes meant to spark stakeholder and user discussion rather than lock in a solution early, then organizing execution in a hub-and-spoke model that let feature teams move autonomously under central coordination. They ran ongoing research workstreams, cross-functional workshops, and pulse surveys with both internal staff and external users throughout the rollout. One concrete reversal: an early version moved search into the left-hand navigation, but research, pilot feedback, and usage data showed search became unfindable there, so the team moved it back to the top of the product.</summary> <parameter name="what_they_built">A large-scale navigation redesign for Slack's product, executed via provocative early prototyping, a hub-and-spoke cross-team operating model, and layered feedback loops (research, workshops, pulse surveys) during a staged pilot rollout.
what_they_built: Slack redesigned its core product navigation and interface — restructuring the left-hand navigation and visual language beyond the original messaging-only design — to accommodate newer features like huddles, canvases, and lists, testing changes such as moving search placement in and out of the left nav based on user feedback.
evidence: Qualitative, not metrics-based: the piece reports a specific discovered failure mode — search placed in the new left-hand nav was found unfindable via user research, pilot feedback, and internal usage data — which caused the team to revert search's position to the top of the UI. No quantitative before/after numbers are given.
why_it_matters: Shows a PM-transferable pattern: use intentionally imperfect, provocative prototypes early to surface disagreement cheaply, and treat pilot usage data as a trigger to reverse a design decision rather than defend it.

title: How Slack Rebuilt Notifications
summary: Slack's notification system had accumulated four conflicting preference models across desktop and mobile, with hidden coupling between what users were notified about and how they received notifications, and it was one of the top three drivers of customer support tickets. The team rebuilt the architecture around a unified model: three clear per-channel options (all posts, mentions, mute) plus a separate push toggle decoupling interruptions from awareness, migrated using a read-time strategy rather than risky database rewrites, and rewrote legacy iOS code to match desktop for true cross-platform parity. Post-launch, settings engagement rose 5x and stayed elevated for weeks, and the share of users needing per-channel overrides dropped.
what_they_built: A unified notification preference architecture across desktop and mobile that decouples "what you're notified about" from "how you're notified," using a read-time migration strategy (new desktop_push_enabled pref, backfilled without disrupting existing behavior) plus auto-save UI and rewritten cross-platform (iOS) client code.
evidence: Settings engagement increased 5x post-launch and remained elevated for weeks afterward; the percentage of users needing per-channel overrides decreased; the piece also states notifications were one of the top three drivers of Customer Experience tickets pre-rebuild, framing the problem's scale.
why_it_matters: Shows how decoupling two conflated preference dimensions (content vs. delivery channel) and choosing a read-time migration over a risky database rewrite let a team ship a breaking architectural change to millions of users with rollback safety intact.

title: Slack's Outage on January 4th, 2021
summary: On January 4, 2021 — the first Monday back from the holidays — Slack's web tier degraded when an AWS Transit Gateway became overloaded by an unusually large mini traffic-peak of clients reconnecting with cold caches. Message send success dropped from over 99.999% to 99% at the worst point, and Slack's attempt to add 1,200 servers to its web tier failed because provision-service hit a Linux open-files limit and an AWS quota limit simultaneously, leaving most new instances unprovisioned and stuck against autoscaling-group size caps. The team details how monitoring dashboards sharing the same degraded network path masked visibility during the incident, and full recovery took until roughly 10:40am PST. They committed to moving dashboards off the shared network path, load-testing the provisioning pipeline, revisiting autoscaling health-check configuration, and pre-emptively requesting AWS Transit Gateway capacity increases ahead of future holiday seasons.
what_they_built: A public engineering post-mortem of Slack's January 4, 2021 outage, detailing the AWS Transit Gateway saturation, the failed emergency web-tier scale-up, and the resulting infrastructure and process changes to provisioning, monitoring isolation, and AWS capacity planning.
evidence: Message send success rate fell from a normal >99.999% to about 99% at 6:57am PST; an emergency addition of 1,200 servers between 7:01-7:15am PST failed due to a Linux open-files limit and an AWS quota limit on provision-service; service was substantially degraded until roughly 9:15am PST with full recovery around 10:40am PST.
why_it_matters: Shows a PM/platform lead why capacity headroom, dependency isolation for observability tooling, and load-testing of scaling/provisioning paths themselves (not just steady-state services) matter before a predictable seasonal traffic pattern hits.

title: Slack Technologies, Inc. Form S-1 — Free-to-Paid Conversion Mechanics
summary: In its April 2019 IPO registration statement, Slack disclosed the specific product levers it believes drive free-to-paid conversion: a 10,000-message search/access cap on the Free plan and single-sign-on gated behind the paid Plus plan. The filing quantifies the resulting revenue impact directly, stating that organizations that converted from Free to Paid in a given fiscal year accounted for a measurable share of that year's total revenue. This gives a rare, company-disclosed link between a specific freemium feature limit and downstream monetization, rather than a vague claim about "freemium working."
what_they_built: A tiered Free/Plus subscription structure where message-history search beyond 10,000 messages and SSO are withheld from Free-tier organizations specifically to create upgrade pressure toward paid plans.
evidence: Approximately 10% of Slack's revenue in fiscal year ended January 31, 2018 came from organizations that had been on the Free plan prior to FY2018 and converted to Paid Customers during FY2018; approximately 8% of revenue in fiscal year ended January 31, 2019 came from the equivalent FY2019 cohort conversion.
why_it_matters: Shows a PM how to instrument and report a freemium-to-paid gating decision as a quantified revenue-attribution metric, not just an activation funnel number.

title: Inside Snap's Experimentation Platform: Leveraging NVIDIA GPUs for Accelerated Data Processing
summary: Snap's AB Console is the internal experimentation platform used by 1,600+ engineers and data scientists to configure studies, monitor performance, and analyze results across 1,200+ concurrent studies and 6,000+ metrics, with built-in CUPED variance reduction and sample-ratio-mismatch diagnostics. As the platform scaled, its daily and hourly aggregation pipelines became a major CPU compute and cost burden. The team migrated core data processing to NVIDIA L4 GPUs on GCP, replacing a fleet of 45,600 e2-highmem-8 CPU VMs with 2,850 g2-standard-48 GPU-backed VMs. The migration cut core count by 62.5% and memory footprint by over 81%, while cutting daily pipeline costs by more than 76% and improving runtime.
what_they_built: A GPU-accelerated rewrite of the data aggregation pipelines behind Snap's internal AB Console experimentation platform, moving daily/hourly metric computation from CPU VMs to NVIDIA L4 GPU instances on Google Cloud.
evidence: Reported infrastructure changes: VM count dropped from 45,600 e2-highmem-8 to 2,850 g2-standard-48 (11,400 L4 GPUs), cores fell 62.5% (364,800 to 136,800), memory fell ~81.25% (2.9M GB to 547.2K GB); daily/hourly pipeline costs fell over 76%; GPU job success rate held at 96-98% daily with a 3-8% CPU fallback rate; initial benchmarks showed 1.5-3x faster runtime at identical machine counts or 30-60% smaller clusters at equivalent performance.
why_it_matters: Shows a PM-relevant pattern for scaling an internal experimentation platform: when compute cost and pipeline runtime become the bottleneck for a widely-adopted internal tool, hardware-level re-architecture (not just software optimization) can deliver step-change cost and speed gains.

title: SoundCloud Echo: Next-Level Humane Registry with Backstage
summary: SoundCloud had built a homegrown Services Directory so engineers could find which team owns which system, but the maintenance burden of keeping it current became unsustainable. The team evaluated alternatives, chose the open-source Backstage developer portal, and rebuilt the registry as "SoundCloud Echo" — migrating the underlying database from MySQL to PostgreSQL on Google Cloud SQL and converting catalog descriptors from JSON to YAML, while adding backend integrations and custom plugins. Two years into the effort, the platform is tracked with page views and DAU/MAU, and SoundCloud's open-sourced plugins see over 1,000 downloads a week.
what_they_built: A migration of SoundCloud's internal service/ownership registry from a homegrown "Services Directory" to a customized, org-wide instance of the open-source Backstage developer portal, branded "SoundCloud Echo."
evidence: Two years of iteration since the initial migration; platform usage tracked via page views and DAU/MAU; SoundCloud's open-sourced Backstage plugins receive more than 1,000 downloads weekly; reported qualitative benefit that feature-toggle management and dependency visualization "has been helpful in bringing engineering managers, product managers, and engineers closer."
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete build-vs-adopt decision for internal developer tooling — when to retire a homegrown platform product in favor of an open-source alternative, and how to measure adoption of an internal developer platform like any other product.

title: How (Not) to Build Datasets and Consume Data at Your Company
summary: SoundCloud engineers found that teams were consuming internal data three problematic ways: subscribing to raw events (scales poorly, leaks domain complexity), snapshotting other teams' databases (creates brittle coupling, loses history), or calling live production APIs (adds load and hurts scalability). They introduced "published datasets" — data explicitly produced by the owning team under an agreed contract, built either by rolling up events or by transforming/rolling up database snapshots, with validation and a formal publication step. Adoption required data owners to take on extra work (defining update frequency, time resolution, historical depth, and handling retrospective corrections) but shifted consumption org-wide toward shared, well-specified, well-maintained datasets.
what_they_built: A standardized "published datasets" convention and tooling at SoundCloud — owner-produced, contract-based datasets built from event rollups or database snapshots, with validation and publication steps replacing raw event/snapshot/live-API consumption.
evidence: Qualitative outcome stated directly: "The additional effort and the initial learning curve on the part of data owners led to the organization-wide benefits of shared, well-specified, and well-maintained datasets." No hard adoption metrics given, but the piece frames it as a completed, working org-wide practice contrasted against the three failure modes it replaced.
why_it_matters: Shows a PM/platform-team pattern for turning data quality into an internal-product problem — define ownership and a contract before consumption scales, rather than letting teams grab raw events, snapshots, or live APIs ad hoc.

title: How We Improved the Development Experience for our Client Developers
summary: Spotify's Client Platform team ran an Engineering Satisfaction survey of 318 engineers and found long build times were hurting productivity. They hypothesized that moving client developers from Intel-based Macs to Apple silicon would cut build times, tested it, and found meaningful speedups on both Android and iOS builds. They then ran a cost-benefit analysis and coordinated a global hardware rollout across 11 countries, followed by a post-upgrade satisfaction survey to validate the change.
what_they_built: A measured hardware transition program moving Spotify client engineers from Intel to Apple silicon Macs, validated with before/after build-time benchmarks and a company-wide procurement rollout across 11 countries.
evidence: Apple silicon machines were 43% faster overall, up to 50% faster for Android builds and 40% faster for iOS builds versus Intel Macs; breakeven on cost within three months with up to 36% potential engineering-cost savings per team; post-upgrade survey of 100 developers found 98.8% rated experience 4-5/5, 91.6% rated productivity 4-5/5, and 83% reported positive task-completion change.
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete pattern for justifying and validating a developer-experience hardware/tooling investment with a survey-hypothesis-measurement loop rather than anecdote.

title: How Backstage Made Our Developers More Effective — And How It Can Help Yours, Too
summary: Spotify's autonomous, highly distributed team structure left engineers navigating dozens of disconnected tools, causing heavy context-switching and duplicated work because services and ownership were hard to discover. Spotify built and rolled out Backstage internally as a single portal where developers could "find stuff, manage stuff, and create stuff" — a service catalog, documentation hub, CI/CD visibility, and Golden Path templates for scaffolding new services. Adoption of Backstage cut the time for new engineers to merge their tenth pull request from over 60 days to 20 days. The post argues this kind of before/after metric is what makes it easy to secure continued investment in developer experience.
what_they_built: Backstage, Spotify's internal developer portal unifying service catalog, documentation, CI/CD status, and scaffolding templates (Golden Paths) into one interface.
evidence: Time for new engineers to merge their tenth pull request dropped from over 60 days to 20 days after Backstage adoption.
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete, defensible metric (time-to-nth-PR) that platform PMs can use to justify internal developer-platform investment to leadership.

title: Supercharged Developer Portals
summary: Spotify built Backstage, an open-source internal developer portal, to counter the fragmentation and cognitive load created by microservices, cloud tooling, and expanding DevOps responsibilities. The post introduces three new Backstage-based offerings -- Spotify Plugins (including a Soundcheck quality-tracking tool and role-based access control), Enterprise Support, and a pre-built Spotify Portal -- and backs the platform's value with an internal comparison of frequent versus infrequent Backstage users. It also reports broader ecosystem adoption figures for the open-source project.
what_they_built: Backstage, an open-source internal developer portal/platform, plus new commercial-adjacent offerings on top of it (Spotify Plugins, Enterprise Support, and a ready-to-use Spotify Portal for Backstage).
evidence: Internal research found frequent Backstage users were "2.3x more active in GitHub, create 2x as many code changes in 17% less cycle time, and deploy software 2x as often," with software "deployed for 3x as long" at higher quality; the open-source project has over 2,200 contributors and roughly 3,000 adopting companies.
why_it_matters: Shows a PM/platform team how to justify internal-tooling investment by tying developer-portal adoption directly to measurable engineering-velocity outcomes rather than qualitative satisfaction alone.

title: How we measure Backstage success at Spotify
summary: Spotify's engineering org saw productivity stagnate despite rapid hiring, tracing the problem to onboarding friction: their "time-to-10th-PR" metric had ballooned to 60 days as the software ecosystem fragmented. They built Backstage as an internal developer platform to unify tooling and service discovery, then instrumented it with a measurement framework spanning productivity, efficiency, and effectiveness (including quarterly EngSat surveys and search/DAU proxy metrics). Author Helen Greul reports the onboarding metric dropped to under 20 days, along with strong satisfaction and broad organic contribution to the platform.
what_they_built: An internal developer platform (Backstage) with an accompanying multi-metric evaluation framework combining hard productivity metrics, proxy metrics (search success, DAU, context-switching), and qualitative EngSat satisfaction surveys.
evidence: Time-to-10th-PR onboarding metric fell from 60 days to under 20 days; 86% of internal users report satisfaction with Backstage in quarterly EngSat surveys; 80% of plugin contributions come from outside the core team, with 150+ plugins from 100+ squads.
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete, reusable framework (productivity vs. efficiency vs. effectiveness, plus proxy metrics and recurring satisfaction surveys) for treating an internal platform as a product with measurable adoption and outcomes.

title: Accelerating Creator Audience Building through Centralized Exploration
summary: Spotify's individual recommender systems (Discover Weekly, Daily Mix, etc.) each had to independently explore new/creator content to learn how to recommend it, which was inefficient and made it hard for new content to build an audience — a classic cold-start problem replicated many times over across surfaces. Spotify Research built a centralized exploration component that coordinates exposure allocation (impression budgets) across surfaces in a dedicated exploration phase, then distributes what it learns — as raw signals, fixed-size feature vectors, and personalized candidate pools — to the existing distributed recommendation subsystems for exploitation. They validated the approach with online A/B testing rather than offline metrics alone. The centralized approach let new creator content reach listeners faster without each downstream recommender having to independently rediscover it.
what_they_built: A centralized exploration system that allocates cross-surface impression budgets to new/creator content and propagates the resulting learning (signals, features, candidate pools) to Spotify's existing recommenders (Discover Weekly, Daily Mix, and others) instead of each one exploring independently.
evidence: Online A/B testing showed a 10x increase in the number of listeners for explored content, with no negative impact on the performance of the individual downstream recommendation systems.
why_it_matters: Shows a pattern for solving cold-start once centrally and distributing the learning to many downstream ranking/recommendation systems, rather than paying the exploration cost redundantly in each one — transferable to any platform PM managing multiple surfaces that each need to learn about new inventory.

title: Discovery Mode
summary: Spotify built Discovery Mode as a mechanism letting artists flag priority tracks that get extra weight in Spotify's personalized recommendation algorithms (radio, autoplay, Discover Weekly-type surfaces), in exchange for Spotify taking a commission on streaming revenue earned specifically within those Discovery Mode contexts rather than an upfront fee. The feature is opt-in, toggleable per track, and Spotify says it filters out tracks that don't resonate with listeners to protect recommendation quality. It has rolled out to 90+ markets for eligible artists and labels, with the company reporting adoption/performance data directly on the product page.
what_they_built: An opt-in, commission-based boosting mechanism ("Discovery Mode") where artists trade a cut of streaming royalties earned in algorithmic-recommendation contexts for increased weighting of their flagged tracks in Spotify's personalized recommendation signals.
evidence: Spotify reports artist teams have seen an average +106% increase in monthly listeners for songs in Discovery Mode (+121% when activated within the first month of eligibility), +186% average increase in monthly playlist adds, +181% average increase in monthly saves, and 2x greater monthly active listener growth when combined with display campaigns; available in 90+ markets.
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete pattern for monetizing algorithmic distribution/recommendation surfaces via a performance-linked commission rather than a flat pay-to-play fee, a model transferable to any platform PM designing paid-boost or sponsored-discovery mechanics on top of an organic ranking system.

title: Encore x Accessibility: A Balancing Act
summary: Spotify's Encore design system team paired with the company's Mandalorian accessibility squad to figure out how to make shared UI components accessible by default across a federated product org where components get reused unpredictably. The teams ran iterative pairing sessions, shared code review over VS Code, and even adopted sound-cue tooling so sighted and blind collaborators could track cursor position together. Rather than relying on documentation alone, they leaned toward baking accessibility defaults directly into components so downstream teams couldn't easily misuse them, and they launched accessibility training (via Fable Tech Labs) to shift the org from reactive compliance checks to proactive practice.
what_they_built: A cross-team accessibility collaboration process for Spotify's Encore design system, including component-level accessibility defaults, remote pairing workflows adapted for assistive-tech users, and an internal accessibility training program.
evidence: Qualitative lessons learned rather than metrics: the team found accessibility exists in gray areas rather than binary pass/fail, that automated scanning is insufficient without context, that baking defaults into components prevents well-intentioned misuse better than documentation alone, and that direct testing with assistive technology users surfaced issues code review missed.
why_it_matters: Shows a PM how to run accessibility as an embedded, iterative collaboration with a design system team rather than a late-stage compliance audit, and how to decide when to enforce behavior via defaults versus guidance.

title: Can I get an Encore? Spotify's Design System, Three Years On
summary: Three years after launching its "family of systems" design system Encore (replacing 22 grassroots design systems across 45+ platforms), Spotify Design staff recount what changed. Adoption tracking moved from a manual Coda table of box-ticking to daily automated data pipelines and dashboards that query company repositories for token/component usage counts and upgrade cadence. They also describe a concrete failure: because their original color tokens were non-semantic, they couldn't safely change the Spotify Green token's value for an accessibility contrast fix since they didn't know where or how many times (thousands, across iOS/Android/web) it was being used — the fix was introducing semantic token layers plus a color-theming algorithm guaranteeing accessible contrast. The post also covers native-vs-web component reusability tradeoffs and a Figma-sync bot ("Figgy").
what_they_built: Automated daily data pipelines/dashboards that query repositories across the company to measure design token and component usage, upgrade adoption, and trends; plus a semantic design-token layer system and a color-theming algorithm to enable safe token changes.
evidence: Concrete before/after: 2019 adoption tracking was a manual box-ticking Coda table; by 2022 it's daily automated pipelines/dashboards. Concrete failure mode: the Spotify Green token was used "thousands of times across iOS, Android, and web" but its non-semantic nature meant they "didn't know where or how," blocking a planned accessibility contrast change until semantic tokens were introduced.
why_it_matters: Shows PMs/design-system owners that adoption metrics and semantic token architecture aren't nice-to-haves — without traceable usage data, even a simple brand-color accessibility fix becomes too risky to ship.

title: Spotify's New Experimentation Platform (Part 1)
summary: Spotify's legacy A/B testing system, ABBA, mapped each experiment 1:1 to a feature flag, making restarts costly, generated event logging that grew to ~25% of total event volume (driving cost and incidents), and offered too few built-in metrics, pushing data scientists into ad hoc notebook analysis. In late 2017 engineers began designing a replacement, and this post (part 1 of 2) describes the resulting Experimentation Platform: a Remote Configuration service (property-based, YAML-defined, policy-driven, replacing feature flags), a Metrics Catalog (managed SQL pipelines feeding an OLAP cube served via a sub-second-latency API), and an Experiment Planner orchestration layer built into Spotify's Backstage developer portal that any internal team can use to create and run experiments. The piece focuses on architecture and rationale rather than post-launch results, noting the platform is a "step change in ease of use and capabilities" but that it's "still... early for experimentation at Spotify."
what_they_built: An internal experimentation platform composed of three integrated services — Remote Configuration (property-based config replacing feature flags), Metrics Catalog (SQL-pipeline-fed OLAP metrics store with an API), and Experiment Planner (a Backstage-hosted UI for creating, running, and analyzing experiments) — replacing Spotify's original ABBA A/B testing system.
evidence: Quantified problem evidence from the old system: A/B testing event logging had grown to almost 25% of total event volume, driving up processing cost and causing delivery-system incidents; the 1:1 experiment-to-feature-flag mapping meant any experiment restart required creating a new flag and shipping new software. No post-launch adoption/outcome metrics for the new platform are given in this part; the team states it is "early for experimentation at Spotify."
why_it_matters: Shows how outgrowing a scrappy in-house A/B testing tool forces a rebuild around configuration-as-data, centralized metrics governance, and self-serve tooling — a pattern any PM scaling experimentation across many teams will eventually hit.

title: Beyond Winning: Spotify's Experiments with Learning Framework
summary: Spotify's experimentation team noticed that win rate — the traditional headline metric for A/B testing programs — badly undersells how much value experimentation actually delivers, especially in a mature product where most changes shouldn't ship. They built the Experiments with Learning (EwL) framework on top of their Confidence platform, which classifies every experiment as invalid, unpowered, or learning (valid and decision-ready, regardless of whether it shipped, regressed, or was neutral). Rolled out across Spotify R&D, the framework shows an average learning rate of 64% against a win rate of only about 12%, with learning rates ranging from 16% to 76% across teams, and adoption growing from roughly 40 teams in 2018 to about 300 by 2022.
what_they_built: A classification framework (Experiments with Learning) layered on Spotify's internal Confidence experimentation platform that labels each experiment as invalid, unpowered, or learning based on validity and decision-readiness, replacing win rate as the primary success metric for experimentation.
evidence: Reports concrete production metrics: average learning rate of 64% versus a win rate of roughly 12%, team-level variation from 16% to 76%, and adoption growth from about 40 teams in 2018 to about 300 teams by 2022.
why_it_matters: Shows a PM how to redefine what "successful experimentation" means for a mature product — reframing negative and neutral results as learning rather than failure — and gives a concrete metric design (with guardrails against gaming it) for measuring an experimentation program's health beyond raw win rate.

title: How We Use Golden Paths to Solve Fragmentation in Our Software Ecosystem
summary: As Spotify's engineering org grew, teams built systems in wildly different ways, creating fragmentation that made it hard to move between teams, hire, and maintain infrastructure. Spotify's answer, born from a Hack Week project, was "Golden Paths" -- an opinionated, supported way to build a given type of system, paired with a step-by-step tutorial covering backend, client, data engineering, data science, ML, web, and audio disciplines. The tutorials became core to onboarding, with nearly every new engineer completing one during their first two weeks via the Engineering Bootcamp. The piece reports the tutorials are the company's most-read and most-used technical documentation, driven by clear target-audience definition and continuous feedback loops.
what_they_built: An internal developer platform pattern -- "Golden Path" tutorials and supported tooling per engineering discipline -- meant to give new and existing engineers a single, opinionated, well-supported way to build systems rather than everyone reinventing infrastructure.
evidence: Reports the Golden Path tutorials are "Spotify's most read and most used technical documentation," and that nearly all new engineers complete a tutorial in their first two weeks through the mandatory Engineering Bootcamp -- qualitative adoption evidence rather than hard usage numbers.
why_it_matters: Shows how a platform team can fight tooling fragmentation not by mandating standards top-down but by making the supported path the easiest, best-documented one -- a pattern any PM running an internal platform can borrow.

title: How We Improved Developer Productivity for Our DevOps Teams
summary: Spotify's autonomous, self-service engineering teams faced slow, manually-configured project setup involving GitHub, Google Cloud, and Jenkins pipeline creation. The Platform Developer Experience team built three tools — Golden Paths for guided project scaffolding, Tingle as a centralized CI/CD system, and a gamified test-certification program — to remove this friction without sacrificing team autonomy. Tingle replaced over 200 standalone Jenkins machines previously managed by individual feature teams and now runs tens of thousands of builds weekly. Setup time for a new service dropped from 14 days to under 5 minutes, and teams that adopted test certification saw a marked drop in blocking bugs and reactive work.
what_they_built: Spotify's Platform Developer Experience team built Golden Paths (a wizard-style tool to scaffold new projects), Tingle (a centralized, zero-config CI/CD system replacing 200+ standalone Jenkins machines), and a gamified test-certification program for autonomous feature teams.
evidence: Setup time for a basic service fell from 14 days to under 5 minutes; Tingle replaced 200+ standalone Jenkins machines and now runs tens of thousands of builds per week; teams that invested in test certification saw a "drastic drop in blocking bugs and reactive work" (qualitative).
why_it_matters: Shows how a platform team can measure and productize internal developer experience (golden paths + centralized CI/CD + gamified quality gates) the same way a product team measures user-facing features, without recentralizing decision-making away from autonomous teams.

title: The Rise (and Lessons Learned) of ML Models to Personalize Content on Home (Part I)
summary: Spotify's Home page personalization runs on a two-stage ML pipeline (candidate generation, then ranking) feeding models for podcasts, shortcuts, and playlists. As the team moved from batch offline predictions to real-time serving, they discovered they had been transforming one of the model's features slightly differently at training time than at serving time -- a training/serving skew bug that potentially degraded recommendations for four months before it was caught. They fixed it by unifying training and serving feature transformation code paths and added TensorFlow Data Validation to monitor feature distribution drift daily via Chebyshev distance, catching similar issues going forward.
what_they_built: A two-stage ML personalization system for Spotify's Home page (candidate generation plus ranking) with automated feature logging via Scio pipelines and weekly retraining via Kubeflow, later hardened with shared training/serving feature-transformation code and daily automated feature-drift monitoring.
evidence: A concrete production failure mode: a training/serving skew bug in the Podcast Model went undetected for four months, potentially degrading recommendations, until the team unified the transformation code paths and added TensorFlow Data Validation drift checks.
why_it_matters: Shows PMs running ML-powered features why silent training/serving skew is a real, hard-to-detect risk, and why investing in drift monitoring infrastructure (not just model quality) is part of shipping a recommendation system responsibly.

title: Coding Is No Longer the Constraint: Scaling Developer Experience to Teams and Agents at Spotify
summary: Spotify's engineering org faced fleet-wide maintenance and migration work that scaled poorly when done component-by-component by individual teams.
