They built Honk, a background coding agent on Claude's Agent SDK running in Kubernetes with CI/build verification access, paired it with Fleetshift (an orchestration system for target identification, scheduling, and progress tracking across their whole component fleet), and integrated both with their existing Backstage developer portal and Soundcheck standards tooling. The result was a shift from manual, team-by-team migrations to fleet-wide automated mutation, with a large share of maintenance PRs now auto-merged. A recent backend Java migration that historically would have taken hundreds of teams weeks to months was completed by a single engineer in three days.
what_they_built: An internal developer platform combining Honk (a Claude Agent SDK-based background coding agent deployed in Kubernetes) with Fleetshift (a migration/maintenance orchestration system), integrated into Spotify's existing Backstage portal and Soundcheck standards system.
evidence: 99% of engineers use AI coding tools weekly, 94% report increased productivity, a 76% increase in pull request frequency, 2.5 million automated maintenance PRs merged via Fleetshift (majority auto-merged), and a Java migration that used to take weeks/months across hundreds of teams completed by one engineer in three days.
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete pattern for platform teams: pairing a background coding agent with a fleet-wide orchestration layer turns component-by-component migrations into a single automated sweep, with measurable adoption and velocity gains.

title: Better Experiments with LLM Evals — A Funnel, Not a Fork
summary: Spotify's experimentation team faced a familiar problem: most A/B tests don't ship a positive result, and nearly half of the ones that do get shipped still have to be rolled back to protect metrics nobody was optimizing for. Rather than let LLM-as-judge evals replace A/B testing, they built an "evaluation funnel" where evals filter and rank candidate treatments before they consume experiment bandwidth, raising the hit rate of what gets tested. After launch, they close the loop by running the same LLM judges against live A/B test data to check whether what the judge preferred actually performed better with real users, using any gap between eval scores and experiment outcomes as a calibration signal. The piece frames evals as verification (does the output meet quality standards) and experiments as validation (do real users and guardrail metrics respond as predicted), keeping human-run experimentation as the final arbiter.
what_they_built: An "evaluation funnel" process that places LLM-judge evals as a pre-experiment filtering/hypothesis-generation stage ahead of A/B tests, paired with a post-experiment calibration loop that re-runs the same evals against A/B test data to check eval-outcome alignment.
evidence: Reports baseline experimentation stats at Spotify: only about 12% of A/B tests end in a shipped positive result, around 64% produce valid learning (regression caught, idea ruled out, hypothesis refined), and roughly 42% of launched experiments are rolled back to prevent regressions in guardrail metrics like session length, crash rate, and retention that no eval had flagged.
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete pattern for using LLM evals to raise experiment hit-rate without letting them replace human A/B validation of real-world, guardrail-metric impact.

title: Loud & Clear 2026 — Takeaways
summary: Spotify's annual Loud & Clear report documents outcomes of its royalty-payout marketplace mechanism at multiple artist-earning tiers over a decade. It shows total industry payouts crossing $11 billion in 2025 (nearly $70 billion lifetime), a widening base of artists clearing $100K and $1M annual thresholds, and a twentyfold increase in earnings for the 100,000th-ranked artist between 2015 and 2025. It also reports that roughly half of royalties flow to independent artists and labels, giving a concrete evidence trail for how the payout system has evolved.
what_they_built: Spotify's royalty-payout system that distributes streaming revenue to artists, labels, and publishers, tracked and reported annually via the Loud & Clear transparency report.
evidence: Paid over $11B to the music industry in 2025 (nearly $70B lifetime); 13,800+ artists generated $100K+ in 2025 (up ~1,400 YoY); 1,500+ artists earned $1M+; the 100,000th-ranked earning artist made over $7,300 in 2025 vs about $350 in 2015 (20x increase); roughly half of royalties went to independent artists/labels.
why_it_matters: Shows how a PM running a two-sided marketplace can use tiered payout thresholds and decade-long before/after comparisons to evidence and communicate the health of a creator-payout mechanism.

title: The Process (Loud and Clear)
summary: Spotify runs a pro-rata "streamshare" royalty model: total subscription and ad revenue is pooled and each artist is paid in proportion to their share of total platform streams — 1% of streams roughly equals 1% of the royalty pool. Loud & Clear is Spotify's own transparency report walking through how money flows from listeners to rightsholders, and it publishes real production numbers rather than just describing the mechanism in the abstract. For 2025 it reports that artists whose music accounted for 1-in-a-million streams generated over $11,000 on average, and that more than 400,000 songs crossed the one-million-stream mark that year. It's Spotify explaining and defending a live payment system it operates at scale, not a hypothetical or a product pitch.
what_they_built: A public, self-published breakdown (with explainer videos and an interactive comparison tool) of Spotify's pro-rata streamshare royalty pooling mechanism used to pay out artists and songwriters.
evidence: In 2025, artists accounting for 1-in-a-million streams generated over $11,000 on average, and over 400,000 songs exceeded one million streams that year — concrete production-scale payout metrics, not just a description of the mechanism.
why_it_matters: Shows a PM how a platform can make a contentious, structurally complex payout mechanism (pro-rata pooling vs. user-centric alternatives) legible to stakeholders by pairing a plain-language mechanism explanation with real distribution numbers.

title: Your Questions, Answered (Loud & Clear FAQ)
summary: This FAQ page explains, in Spotify's own words, how its per-market streamshare royalty pool works: an artist's payout in a market is their share of streams in that market divided by total streams in that market, applied against a pool funded by that market's subscription and ad revenue. It backs the mechanism with concrete 2025 payout data broken out by artist earnings tier and shows the trend since 2017. It is a companion FAQ to Spotify's Loud & Clear /process and /takeaways pages, focused specifically on explaining the mechanics and answering common artist questions about how royalties are calculated.
what_they_built: Spotify's pro-rata, per-market "streamshare" royalty pool mechanism, where each market's ad and subscription revenue funds a pool distributed to rights holders proportional to their share of streams in that market.
evidence: In 2025, one-in-a-million share of Spotify streams generated about $11,000; 81,000+ artists earned $10K+, 13,800+ earned $100K+, and 1,500+ earned $1M+ — figures Spotify says have roughly tripled since 2017 (the 10,000th-ranked artist earned $147K in 2025 vs. $34K in 2017), with total payouts exceeding $11B annually.
why_it_matters: Shows a PM how to communicate a two-sided marketplace's payout mechanism transparently to a skeptical creator base using concrete, tiered outcome data rather than averages.

title: Spotify 2022 Investor Day Transcript — Marketplace and Podcasting Economics
publisher: Spotify Technology S.A. (self-published investor day transcript)
summary: At its 2022 investor day, Spotify walked through the multi-year financial track record of two strategic bets it made starting in 2018: building a Marketplace of paid tools for artists/labels, and aggressively investing in podcasting. Management showed Marketplace's contribution to gross profit growing 8x from €20M (2018) to over €160M (2021), directly driving music gross margin from roughly 26.8% toward a 30-35% long-term target. Podcasting revenue grew over 300% YoY to nearly €200M in 2021 but came with a €103M negative gross-profit impact, which management framed as a temporary drag expected to peak in 2022 before turning margin-accretive within 3-5 years. The transcript ties the investment thesis (build tools/content that benefit both creators and Spotify) to specific, reported financial outcomes by vertical.
what_they_built: Spotify built its "Marketplace" suite of paid artist/creator tools (starting 2018) and made a multi-year investment in original/exclusive podcasting content and ad monetization (SPAN).
evidence: Marketplace gross-profit contribution: €20M (2018) to more than €160M (2021), an 8x increase, with an expected 30%+ further increase in 2022. Podcasting: revenue grew over 300% YoY to nearly €200M in 2021, but with a €103M negative impact on Gross Profit; music gross margin reached 28.3% in 2021, ~150bps above the 26.8% total consolidated margin.
why_it_matters: Shows a PM-relevant pattern for justifying a multi-year platform/monetization bet to leadership: report the vertical's isolated margin contribution over time rather than blending it into consolidated numbers, and explicitly separate temporary investment drag from long-term accretive potential.

title: Bringing Sequential Testing to Experiments with Longitudinal Data (Part 1): The Peeking Problem 2.0
summary: Spotify's data science team wanted faster feedback from experiments, which meant analyzing longitudinal data — multiple repeated measurements per user over time — rather than a single end-of-experiment observation. They identified a new failure mode they call the "peeking problem 2.0," which occurs when a unit's results are analyzed before all of that unit's measurements have been collected ("within-unit peeking"), and showed it can silently invalidate standard sequential tests. A Monte Carlo simulation (1,000 replications, varying max measurements per unit K=1/5/10/20 and within-unit autocorrelation AR(1)=0/0.5) demonstrated that both a naive IID treatment of repeated measurements and "open-ended" metrics (which use all available data per unit as it accrues) can produce substantially inflated false positive rates despite using group sequential tests meant to control them.
what_they_built: A Monte Carlo simulation study comparing group sequential tests (GSTs) under an IID assumption versus open-ended-metric formulations, across varying numbers of repeated measurements per experimental unit and varying within-unit autocorrelation.
evidence: Across 1,000-replication simulations, false positive rates for open-ended metrics inflated as the number of measurements per unit (K=1,5,10,20) increased, and the IID method's FPR inflated once within-unit correlation (AR(1)) was nonzero; results are presented graphically (Figure 4) across K and AR(1)=0/0.5 rather than as precise text-stated percentages in the article body.
why_it_matters: PMs and analysts who rely on continuously-refreshing ("open-ended") metrics in continuously-monitored experiments should know that repeated per-user measurements can silently break the false-positive guarantees of standard sequential significance tests.

title: How Spotify's Rebuilt Ad Platform Is Delivering New Value for Brands
summary: Spotify rebuilt its advertising business around two tracks: high-impact sponsorships (live events, sponsored playlists, podcast host reads) and scaled biddable channels through a self-serve Spotify Ads Manager and a programmatic Spotify Ad Exchange. The post, published around Spotify's 2026 Investor Day, reports that biddable channels now make up more than a third of the ads business and that active advertisers grew 68% year-over-year in Q1, with regional breakouts (EMEA nearly 10% YoY, Latin America 25% YoY). It also credits Gen AI ad-creation tools with lowering the barrier to entry for audio ad creation, citing adoption by thousands of advertisers.
what_they_built: A self-serve programmatic ads stack (Spotify Ads Manager plus the Spotify Ad Exchange) combined with Gen AI tools that let advertisers generate audio ads directly.
evidence: Biddable channels now represent more than a third of the ads business; active advertisers grew 68% year-over-year in Q1; EMEA grew nearly 10% YoY and Latin America 25% YoY; Gen AI tools produced more than 20,000 ads generated by over 7,000 advertisers to date.
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete pattern for lowering the barrier to a supply-side self-serve product (ad creation) with generative AI while tracking adoption and channel-mix metrics as the primary success signal.

title: Modernizing Our Royalty System to Drive an Additional $1 Billion toward Emerging and Professional Artists
summary: Spotify's royalty pool was being diluted by three systemic issues: distributor minimum-payout thresholds and banking fees meant many small per-track payments never actually reached artists, artificial streaming was siphoning royalties away from real listening, and short "noise" tracks (white noise, ASMR) were being fragmented to farm streams at music-level payout rates. Spotify announced three mechanism changes effective April 1, 2024: a 1,000-stream-per-12-months threshold a track must clear to generate royalties, per-track financial penalties charged to labels/distributors when flagrant artificial streaming is detected, and a 2-minute minimum length plus reduced payout rate for functional noise recordings. Spotify states 99.5% of streams already come from tracks that clear the 1,000-stream threshold, so the redirected pool (roughly $40 million/year from that change alone) comes from the long tail of near-zero-payout tracks.
what_they_built: A redesigned royalty-eligibility and anti-fraud mechanism inside Spotify's payout system: a minimum-stream threshold, distributor-facing artificial-streaming fines, and differentiated payout rules for noise-genre recordings.
evidence: Spotify quantifies the impact: the 1,000-stream threshold redirects roughly $40 million per year to eligible artists, with 99.5% of total streams unaffected because they already come from tracks above that bar; combined with the other two changes, Spotify projects an additional $1 billion in revenue toward emerging and professional artists over the next five years.
why_it_matters: Shows how a marketplace operator can redesign a payout/take-rate mechanism (minimum thresholds, fraud penalties, category-specific rate rules) to fix long-tail value leakage without changing the headline revenue-share number.

title: (Right to Left) The Mirror World
summary: When Spotify's desktop and web player teams shipped Arabic-language support, they had to solve right-to-left layout mirroring, mixed-direction text, and Arabic-specific typography problems beyond simple translation. They built tooling using the HTML dir attribute plus PostCSS-RTL to auto-generate mirrored CSS, used dir="auto" with the Unicode Bidirectional Algorithm for mixed English/Arabic strings, and made typography changes (Noto font, larger minimum font size, no letter-spacing, increased line-height) tuned to Arabic glyph legibility. They also built a GitHub bot that flags PRs touching CSS/JS likely to break RTL rendering, catching regressions without blocking merges.
what_they_built: A CSS-mirroring pipeline (dir attribute + PostCSS-RTL), bidi-aware markup conventions (dir="auto"), Arabic-tuned typography rules, a getSeparator() locale utility, and an automated GitHub PR-review bot for RTL regressions.
evidence: Concrete before/after design decisions reported: minimum font size raised from 11px to 12px for Arabic legibility, switch to Google Noto font, letter-spacing removed, and a custom getSeparator() method to use Arabic comma "،" instead of English comma — presented as lessons learned from shipping RTL support in production.
why_it_matters: Shows a PM/platform team the concrete engineering and process surface (tooling, typography rules, automated review bot) needed to ship a new writing-direction market beyond just translating strings.

title: Scaling Translations at Spotify
summary: Spotify's localization team faced a fragmented, hard-to-scale translation pipeline that left many users without support in their native language, capping the platform at 26 supported languages. A dedicated team spent two months assessing the stack, then over six months defragmented the tech stack, automated linguistic QA, and mass-updated more than 30 backend services to support new locales. The initiative expanded supported languages from 26 to 62, adding 36 new languages, and drastically cut the share of users not served in their preferred language.
what_they_built: A rebuilt, defragmented translation/localization pipeline with automated linguistic QA, deployed across 30+ backend services to support many more languages.
evidence: Supported languages grew from 26 to 62 (36 added) in a project that ran roughly 6 months from team hire to launch, following a 2-month assessment phase; the team reports this "drastically reduced the percentage of users not served their preferred language."
why_it_matters: Shows how a PM can turn an internal, unglamorous platform capability (localization infrastructure) into a measurable market-expansion lever by treating it as a product with an MVP, vendor partnerships, and cross-functional buy-in.

title: Search Journey Towards Better Experimentation Practices
summary: Spotify's Search organization already had a mature internal experimentation platform, but found that having the tooling wasn't enough — teams of engineers, PMs and data scientists with varying statistical fluency weren't consistently turning it into a habit of evidence-based decisions. The Search data science team built a three-step maturity roadmap (standardize individual experiment quality via shared templates and naming conventions, coordinate overlapping experiments to avoid user contamination, then measure total quarterly business impact via a holdback methodology) plus ongoing cultural mechanisms like a weekly forum and a "Champion program" to spread practice across teams. The piece is a first-person account of the specific actions taken over 2021 to convert platform capability into a genuine experimentation culture.
what_they_built: A three-step experimentation-maturity roadmap (individual experiment quality standards, cross-experiment coordination, and quarterly holdback experiments to measure total product-initiative impact) layered on top of Spotify's existing internal Experimentation Platform, supported by templates, a weekly forum, naming conventions, and a Champion program.
evidence: Evidence is largely qualitative/cultural rather than quantitative: the team reports that "most of the new features we developed for users were tested with an experiment" and that "it is now common to see engineers discussing if they should stop a product change from being launched," describing improved cross-team alignment and transparency rather than citing hard before/after metrics.
why_it_matters: Shows a PM/data-org pattern for converting an experimentation platform into an actual decision-making culture: sequence individual-experiment rigor, then cross-experiment coordination, then quarterly holdback measurement of total program impact.

title: Why We Use Separate Tech Stacks for Personalization and Experimentation
summary: Spotify's ML and experimentation platform teams explain why they deliberately keep the ML personalization stack (which serves recommendations, requiring low-latency feature access and support for many model types like neural nets, LLMs, and contextual bandits) architecturally separate from their experimentation platform, Confidence (which evaluates those systems via A/B testing). They argue merging the two would create hidden technical debt and slow teams down, whereas separation lets each stack specialize and lets the experimentation layer treat personalization systems as clean, swappable treatment variants. The post is grounded in Spotify's actual operating scale: 58 teams ran 520 experiments on just the mobile app's home page in the past year, and 300+ teams run thousands of experiments company-wide using the shared, simpler A/B testing tooling rather than more theoretically sophisticated but complex alternatives.
what_they_built: Two deliberately decoupled internal platforms: an ML serving stack for personalization/recommendations and a separate experimentation platform (Confidence) for A/B testing, with personalization systems integrated into experiments only as treatment variants.
evidence: 58 teams ran 520 experiments on the mobile app's home page alone in the past year; 300+ teams run thousands of experiments overall using the shared A/B testing platform.
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete, adoption-scale rationale for resisting the urge to unify adjacent internal platforms (ML serving vs. experimentation) even when technically related, prioritizing broad usability over theoretical elegance.

title: It's OK to be Human in a Machine-Learned World
summary: Spotify's research team faced an inconclusive A/B test when naming a new Home-tab feature, torn between generic labels like "Listen Now" and a daypart greeting ("Good morning") for the eventual "Shortcuts" feature. With the quantitative test "too close to call," the designer pushed to go with the human, emotionally warmer daypart-greeting option anyway. A month-long post-launch diary study then showed users quickly understood the feature and became emotionally attached to the greetings, validating the qualitative call the quant data couldn't make.
what_they_built: A named, ML-powered Shortcuts grid on the Spotify Home tab using daypart greetings (e.g., "Good morning") instead of a generic label, chosen via a combination of A/B testing and qualitative diary-study research.
evidence: The A/B test between naming options came back statistically neutral ("too close to call"); a subsequent month-long post-launch diary study found users understood Shortcuts quickly and became "emotionally attached to and frequently delighted by the daypart greetings" — a qualitative, not quantitative, signal of success.
why_it_matters: When quantitative experiments return inconclusive results, well-run qualitative research (like a longitudinal diary study) can be a legitimate, evidence-based basis for a product decision rather than defaulting to the "safe" generic option.

title: Redesigning Your Library with Mixed Methods Insights
summary: Spotify needed to redesign the "Your Library" surface to accommodate podcasts and audiobooks alongside music without breaking users' existing mental models and organizational habits. The research team ran an ethnographic study visiting 18 representative US users at home early in the design cycle to understand how people actually organize and think about their library, then iterated through prototypes and usability studies before running a staged opt-in beta and a large-scale A/B test. For the final rollout decision they used non-inferiority testing to prove the new Library was not unacceptably worse than the old one on guardrail metrics like listening time and retention, rather than requiring it to beat the old version outright. The redesign shipped with substantially lower negative sentiment than Spotify's prior Library update, and users adopted the new retrieval features while guardrail metrics held.
what_they_built: A mixed-methods research and validation pipeline — home ethnography, iterative prototyping/usability testing, an opt-in beta, and a non-inferiority A/B test — used to design and ship a redesigned "Your Library" feature spanning music, podcasts, and audiobooks.
evidence: Reported outcomes: negative sentiment after launch was substantially lower than after Spotify's previous Library update; guardrail metrics for listening time and retention held under non-inferiority margins; users adopted the new retrieval features and retained access to their existing collections.
why_it_matters: Shows PMs a concrete alternative to superiority-only A/B testing — non-inferiority testing lets a team ship a necessary structural redesign by proving it doesn't hurt core metrics, rather than demanding it beat the incumbent on every number.

title: Experimentation with Resource Constraints
summary: Stitch Fix's data science team explains why standard A/B tests of algorithmic variants (e.g. recommendation algorithms) break down when the variants compete for the same shared inventory: an item shipped to a client in variant A is no longer available to clients in variant B, violating the independence assumption experiments rely on. Since building a second physical warehouse is prohibitively expensive and a crossover design doesn't work because inventory evolves too slowly to reach a new equilibrium, they built the "virtual warehouse": each experimental variant is allocated its own enforced slice of inventory (e.g. a fixed split of otherwise-identical stock), cutting the dependency that ran through shared inventory. This lets them causally test questions like optimal product mix or inventory depth-vs-breadth without one variant contaminating the other's results.
what_they_built: A "virtual warehouse" mechanism that partitions shared inventory into isolated pools per experimental variant so that A/B tests of recommendation-algorithm variants are not confounded by cross-variant inventory depletion.
evidence: Qualitative/methodological: the post does not report a specific experiment's outcome metrics, but explains the mechanism (e.g. partitioning "100 identical shirts" proportionally across variants) and states it lets them "answer these questions causally" about inventory strategy — evidence here is a described-and-implemented solution to a documented bias problem, not a quantified before/after result.
why_it_matters: Any PM running experiments over a shared, depletable resource (inventory, budget, matches in a marketplace) needs to isolate resource pools per variant or risk experiment results that don't predict what happens at full rollout.

title: How We Design for Growth At Strava
summary: Strava's Growth team describes its six-step process for running product experiments: define a hypothesis, design a bite-sized experiment against a specific metric, release via controlled A/B or multivariate rollout (often language-limited first), evaluate after two weeks or at statistical significance, graduate winners platform-wide, and feed qualitative research back into the next round. They illustrate the process with an activity-tagging invite feature that went through three design iterations, from a simple native-share invite to full Strava-athlete tagging.
what_they_built: A three-phase activity-tagging feature that lets athletes invite friends or tag other Strava athletes directly from an activity, run through Strava's structured growth-experimentation pipeline.
evidence: During testing, tagging produced "a 5x increase in invites sent, compared to standard invitations."
why_it_matters: A concrete template for running lightweight, metric-tied growth experiments (hypothesis to graduation) that a PM can reuse for any invite/referral feature.

title: Building Activity Tagging With an Iterative Approach
summary: Strava's Growth Engineering team wanted to let athletes tag other people (including non-Strava users) into an activity as a way to drive both engagement and signups. They shipped a two-week MVP using Branch Metrics deep links, validated demand (tags sent at 5x the rate of regular invites with no harm to core metrics), then invested four more weeks building a proper Version 2.0 with in-app notifications and a friend-picker UI. Along the way they ran button-color and copy A/B tests that meaningfully lifted invite volume, and the v2.0 rebuild produced large gains in both the number of athletes inviting and total invites sent.
what_they_built: An activity-tagging feature letting athletes tag friends — including people not yet on Strava — into their activities, first as a lightweight MVP using Branch Metrics links, then rebuilt as a native v2.0 flow with in-app notifications and a friend list.
evidence: MVP: tags sent at 5x the rate of regular invites. Button color test (white/orange-border to solid orange): iOS tap rate 6.5%→7.6%, invites sent +11%. Copy test (generic prompt to "With someone who didn't record?"): +36% invites sent. V2.0: invites per athlete rose from 0.42 to 1.12 (2.75x total increase), athletes sending invites up 1.7x, and 9% of tags targeted non-Strava users.
why_it_matters: Shows a disciplined MVP-then-invest pattern for growth features: validate a viral loop cheaply before committing engineering to a polished native version, and layer in cheap UI experiments (button color, copy) along the way.

title: Testing the impact of Adaptive Pricing across 1.5M subscription checkout sessions
summary: Stripe wanted to know whether showing subscription prices in a customer's local currency (Adaptive Pricing), rather than the merchant's default currency, actually moves the numbers merchants care about. They ran a randomized controlled experiment across 1.5 million subscription checkout sessions, holding back 1% of traffic as a control group, and measured effects on signup conversion, payment authorization, and subscription lifetime value both at initial signup and through renewals. The experiment showed statistically meaningful lifts across all three metrics, and they cite a specific customer (Runway) whose subscriptions saw a much larger LTV gain than the average. The post lays out the experiment design (sample size, holdback structure, what was measured) alongside the results, rather than just asserting the feature works.
what_they_built: Adaptive Pricing, a Stripe Billing feature that automatically displays and charges subscription prices in the customer's local currency instead of the merchant's default currency, tested via a randomized controlled experiment with a 1% holdback control.
evidence: Across 1.5M subscription checkout sessions with a 1% randomized holdback control: conversion at signup increased 4.7% on average, authorization rate improved 1.9% on average, and subscription LTV per session rose 5.4% on average; one customer (Runway) saw a 14% increase in LTV per session, with some subscriptions generating 17.7% more LTV.
why_it_matters: Shows a PM how to structure a rigorous holdback-based experiment to prove a pricing/localization change's causal impact on conversion and downstream revenue metrics, not just surface-level adoption.

title: APIs as infrastructure: future-proofing Stripe with versioning
summary: Stripe's API needs to keep evolving without ever breaking the thousands of integrations already built against it. Their solution, running since 2011, pins each new integration to the API version live at signup and then applies a chain of small, self-contained "version change" modules that transform requests and responses on the fly so older integrations keep seeing the shape of the API they were built against. Each version-change module declares which fields it touches, which lets Stripe auto-generate changelogs and apply changes in a defined, backwards-composable order. The piece walks through the mechanism (date-based versions, gating logic, transformation order) rather than just asserting the design works.</summary> <parameter name="what_they_built">A date-based API versioning system where every account is pinned to a version at signup, and backwards-incompatible changes are implemented as small, ordered, self-contained transformation modules ("version changes") that Stripe's gateway applies automatically to convert current API behavior back to whatever version a given caller is pinned to.
what_they_built: Stripe built a date-based API versioning system where users are automatically pinned to the API version active when they first integrate; breaking changes are encoded as "version change" code modules (each with a transformation function and list of eligible API resource types) that the API applies in sequence to walk a response back from the current version to whatever version a given request is pinned to, and this same version-change metadata is used to programmatically generate version-specific API documentation.
evidence: The article states this system has been running since 2011 and has been used to ship close to 100 backwards-incompatible API changes over six years without forcing existing integrations to break or update, with developers able to opt into newer versions per-request via a Stripe-Version header or upgrade in the dashboard.
why_it_matters: Shows a PM/engineering pattern for evolving a widely-integrated API surface (breaking changes as isolated, composable, declarative transformation units pinned per-consumer) rather than the more common all-or-nothing versioned-endpoint approach.

title: How we tested the conversion impact of global payment methods
summary: Stripe wanted to know whether dynamically surfacing locally relevant payment methods beyond cards actually improved conversion and revenue for businesses, across 50+ payment methods and 200 countries. They built a randomization scheme using a composite identifier (IP, user-agent, merchant ID, date) hashed to consistently assign payment-method combinations to sessions, ran the test through power analysis and A/A validation before a pilot, then used hundreds of causal-forest trees to estimate uplift across segments given the scale of combinations. The result: offering at least one additional relevant payment method produced a 12% average revenue increase and 7.4% conversion increase, with the biggest gains from popular local payment methods, digital wallets, and bank debits.
what_they_built: A checkout-level experimentation system that randomly (but session-consistently) withholds/surfaces payment methods and uses causal-forest models to estimate per-segment conversion and revenue uplift across thousands of country/payment-method combinations.
evidence: Measured outcomes: 12% average revenue increase and 7.4% average conversion-rate increase when at least one additional relevant payment method was dynamically surfaced beyond cards; biggest uplift concentrated in popular local payment methods, digital wallets, and bank debits.
why_it_matters: Shows how to run rigorous, statistically valid experimentation on a combinatorially large surface (many treatments x many segments) using causal-forest analysis instead of one experiment per variant.

title: How Stripe Radar responded to a new wave of card testing
summary: Stripe's Radar fraud team saw attackers shift from low-quality "enumeration" card-testing attacks to higher-quality "verification" attacks using stolen card data from sophisticated phishing dumps, which raised authorization rates for fraudulent test transactions and put customers at risk of disputes and card-network fraud-monitoring penalties. The team built a three-layer response inside Radar: manually ingesting known stolen-card dumps for direct blocking, automated internet monitoring to surface newly compromised card numbers, and probabilistic ML models to estimate compromise risk at scale for cards not yet directly observed. The controls run automatically within existing Radar infrastructure, so no integration changes were required from merchants.
what_they_built: A three-layer card-testing defense inside Stripe Radar combining manual stolen-card-data ingestion, automated internet monitoring for compromised cards, and ML models that estimate compromise risk probabilistically.
evidence: Stripe reports successful card testing attacks decreased by 80% even as global fraud rates rose 11%, and the system now blocks an additional 30 million high-risk transactions, which they say reduces dispute rates and keeps merchants out of card-network fraud-monitoring programs.
why_it_matters: Shows how a platform team can respond to an adversarial shift in attacker behavior by layering deterministic data feeds with probabilistic ML rather than relying on ML alone, and ship it transparently to users with zero integration burden.

title: Similarity Clustering to Catch Fraud Rings
summary: Stripe's Risk Intelligence team faced fraudsters mass-creating accounts by reusing bits of stolen or synthetic identity information (emails, cards, text patterns) across many signups, which is tedious for human analysts to spot one account at a time. They built a supervised similarity-learning system (using gradient-boosted trees to score pairwise account similarity, then connected-component analysis to form clusters) that surfaces likely fraud rings as a group rather than one account at a time, and hands analysts pre-grouped clusters instead of individual flagged accounts. The tool is deployed operationally alongside human review, not as a fully automated blocker, so analysts still make the final call but spend less time re-discovering connections manually. It also raises fraudsters' cost of attack since reused identity fragments become detectable.
what_they_built: A supervised graph-clustering system (XGBoost-based pairwise similarity scoring plus connected-component analysis on candidate account-similarity edges) that groups newly created accounts into likely fraud-ring clusters for risk analysts to review.
evidence: Reported to catch and block "hundreds of fraudulent accounts weekly with very few false positives"; described as letting analysts spend more time on sophisticated cases instead of re-tracing obvious connections manually, and as an iterative system whose accuracy improves as newly detected rings are shut down.
why_it_matters: Shows how to frame a trust-and-safety ML system as an analyst-productivity and adversary-cost lever rather than a pure accuracy metric, which is the argument PMs need when justifying investment in fraud/abuse tooling to non-ML stakeholders.

title: How we built it: Usage-based billing
summary: Stripe's billing team needed to support usage-based pricing at scale, where customers can generate huge, bursty volumes of metering events that must be aggregated accurately and fast enough to trigger billing alerts and prevent negative balances, while still producing correct invoices and revenue recognition data. They built an asynchronous event-processing pipeline: a stateless edge router feeds an event bus, which flows into an active-active Apache Flink deployment running in two regions simultaneously for reliability, with standardized event metadata enabling reconciliation across delayed or out-of-order events. A dual-path aggregation design splits fast, in-memory processing (for real-time alerts) from a slower disk-backed path (for invoicing and analytics). They also had to build new developer-facing observability (a real-time dashboard, validation-failure webhooks) to compensate for the reduced visibility that comes with async processing.
what_they_built: An asynchronous, dual-path event-aggregation architecture for usage-based billing, using an active-active Apache Flink deployment across two regions plus a stateless edge router and event bus.
evidence: The system processes up to 100,000 events per second per business, with P95 latency under 30 seconds for time-sensitive operations (e.g., balance/alert checks), and average end-to-end latency of about five minutes from event ingestion to fully rated output.
why_it_matters: Shows a PM-relevant tradeoff pattern for scaling a metered/usage-based pricing product: splitting "fast enough to act on" from "correct enough to bill on" into two deliberately different processing paths, and pairing an async architecture with new developer-observability surfaces so customers don't lose trust in a system they can no longer watch synchronously.

title: "Menus to Mmmmenus": A UX Case Study
summary: As Swiggy scaled from roughly 50 to 500 cities, restaurant menus in the app grew visually indistinguishable, hurting discovery and boring users despite thousands of new restaurants joining. A Swiggy designer visited 14 physical restaurants to study how they merchandise menus in person, then worked with a PM and researcher to reframe the problem around visual, intent-driven "drool decisions" rather than list-based browsing. The team iterated on the redesign extensively before piloting a new visual-menu treatment ("Top Noms") with select restaurants, and reported the pilot lifted engagement, conversion, and cart values at every restaurant tested, though exact figures were kept confidential.
what_they_built: A visual, image-driven menu redesign for Swiggy's restaurant pages ("Top Noms") replacing generic list-style menus with curated, intent-based visual sections.
evidence: Qualitative pilot result: "EVERY restaurant we piloted this with saw a tremendous increase in engagement, conversion, healthy cart values" — specific numbers described as confidential, backed by an internal graph the article shows but doesn't quantify in text.
why_it_matters: Shows how grounding a digital redesign in offline field research (studying how physical restaurants merchandise menus) can surface a reframing — from information architecture to visual/emotional decision-making — that a purely in-app data review might miss.

title: Swiggy's NYE War Room – The fight was on as the sun went down
summary: Swiggy describes how it prepared for New Year's Eve 2022, its highest-order night in India, by standing up a physical and virtual "war room" 40 days in advance. Roughly 23 engineering, analytics, data science and Prod Ops teams coordinated readiness (capacity forecasting via peak-orders-per-minute, Business Continuity Plans, Multi-AZ database migrations) and ran real-time incident response through a 950+ person Slack channel when a UPI payment outage and a restaurant ready-to-go signaling feature both failed under load. The analytics team built seven dashboards surfacing 46 near-real-time metrics after negotiating scope down from an initial request for "hundreds" of metrics and wireframing with 25 teams. The piece reports outcomes: orders were served across 540 cities/1,151 zones, and of the orders that received ratings, 90% were five-star.
what_they_built: A cross-functional NYE "war room" process — pre-event capacity/readiness planning (BCP, OPM forecasting, Multi-AZ migration) plus a live coordination model (physical war room, Slack #warroom channel, seven real-time dashboards with 46 metrics) for handling incidents during peak scale.
evidence: 23 engineering/analytics/DS/Prod Ops teams and 950+ Slack participants coordinated live; 7 dashboards with 46 near-real-time metrics were built after wireframing with 25 teams; two production incidents (UPI payment outage, ready-to-go signaling failure) were handled in real time; 90% of orders that received ratings were five-star, with 33,000+ positive comments.
why_it_matters: A concrete template for how a PM/program-management function can orchestrate cross-team incident readiness and real-time observability for a predictable peak-demand event.

title: Redesigning Swiggy's new user onboarding — A product design case study
summary: Swiggy's design team rebuilt the new-user onboarding (NUX) flow to align with the company's 2021 design refresh, fix usability problems, and surface non-food verticals like Instamart and Genie earlier. They benchmarked competitor apps, ran hallway testing across multiple flow permutations, and iterated on a contextual progress indicator, a welcome-offer placement that wouldn't confuse ineligible returning users, and a simplified locality-selection step. A key insight from testing was that many Indian users weren't familiar with or didn't readily have an email address, so the team made the email field optional. The redesigned flow was rolled out as an experiment from July to September 2022 and scaled to 100% of traffic by October 2022.
what_they_built: A redesigned mobile new-user onboarding/signup flow with a contextual progress indicator, restructured welcome-offer messaging, simplified locality selection, and an optional (rather than mandatory) email field.
evidence: Reported "an uptick in sign-up success rate, leading to higher incremental signups per month" after making the email field optional; specific percentage figures were not disclosed. The flow was validated via a phased experiment (Jul-Sep 2022) before scaling to 100% rollout by October 2022.
why_it_matters: Shows how a signup-form field that looks universally necessary (email) can be a region-specific literacy/familiarity barrier, and how phased experimentation can validate removing it without harming downstream email-dependent flows.

title: Data Apps: From Local to Live in 10 Minutes
summary: Talabat's Machine Learning Ops team faced a bottleneck: data scientists' Python scripts and models had no standardized way to become shareable, production-facing apps for business and product teams. They built a self-serve pipeline where a data scientist pushes code to a standardized GitHub repo, and Cloud Build automatically containerizes it with Docker, deploys it to Cloud Run, and publishes it to an internal Apps Directory Portal. The result is a Streamlit app going from a local script to a live, load-balanced, publicly accessible URL within about 10 minutes, with no infrastructure work required from the data scientist.
what_they_built: A standardized CI/CD pipeline (Streamlit + Docker + Google Cloud Build + Cloud Run, driven by a cloudbuild.yaml convention) that lets data scientists self-serve deploy internal data apps from a GitHub push, listed in an internal Apps Directory Portal.
evidence: Qualitative: the team states the process "has proven to be very effective in enabling our Data Scientists to deliver their Data Products seamlessly." Quantitative signal is limited to the stated deployment turnaround of roughly 10 minutes from push to live URL; no adoption counts or before/after metrics are given.
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete pattern for standing up a lightweight internal developer platform that removes infra friction for a non-engineering team (data scientists) without requiring bespoke deployment work per app.

title: Perspectives: talabat's Data Aggregation Framework
summary: Talabat's analytics teams had metric definitions duplicated inside every aggregation query, which caused inconsistent results across teams and made it hard to update a metric's logic globally. They built Perspectives, an internal framework that centralizes metric logic in YAML files, standardizes fact-to-dimension join logic via reusable SQL templates, and lets analysts declare an aggregation ("View File") that the system compiles into optimized SQL and auto-generates a scheduled Airflow DAG for. The post walks through the metric library, join-logic repository, view configuration format, and DAG generation mechanics.
what_they_built: Perspectives, a config-driven internal data platform tool: a YAML-based metric-definition library plus a join-logic template repository that auto-compiles analyst-authored "View File" configs into SQL and auto-generates scheduled Airflow DAGs.
evidence: Qualitative adoption claim only, no hard numbers given: "This has been widely adopted by our teams and has transformed [how] we define our aggregations," used by Data Analysts, Data Scientists, and metric owners.
why_it_matters: Shows how centralizing metric definitions as versioned, machine-readable config (rather than duplicated SQL) removes a common source of cross-team metric drift and manual-maintenance burden in a data platform.

title: Introducing Shield: talabat's Fraud Prevention Service
summary: Before Shield, talabat had no unified system to detect fraud across its multi-sided marketplace (customers, riders, vendors) — fraud logic was manual and fragmented across teams. The engineering team built Shield: a centralized platform combining a no-code rule engine (rules stored in GitHub, fetched every minute, testable in shadow mode before enforcement) with ML models for anomaly detection, fake-email detection, and behavioral fraud detection, backed by a real-time SNS-SQS-Lambda pipeline and a feature store on AWS Aurora PostgreSQL using recursive CTEs for graph-based entity linking. It also layered in fraud intelligence checks like mobile number validation, IP/email risk scoring, and device integrity checks including cloned-app detection on Android.
what_they_built: A centralized fraud-prevention platform (Shield) combining a configurable, no-code rule engine with shadow-mode testing and ML-based anomaly/behavioral/fake-account detection, fed by real-time and batch data pipelines.
evidence: Qualitative: the article states Shield replaced talabat's prior manual processes and fragmented fraud logic with one centralized system; no quantified before/after metrics (e.g. fraud rate reduction) are given in the retrieved text.
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete pattern for standing up a trust-and-safety platform inside a marketplace — separating a fast-iterating, analyst-editable rule layer (with shadow mode for safe rollout) from a slower ML layer, rather than hard-coding fraud logic into product flows.

title: Large-Scale Metric Computation in Online Controlled Experiment Platform
summary: WeChat's experiment platform needed to compute large numbers of metrics across tens of thousands of concurrent experiments without ballooning storage or CPU cost, and analysts wanted faster ad-hoc queries into experiment data. Tencent engineers built a metric-computation architecture around bit-sliced-index (BSI) arithmetic that compresses metric representations and speeds up both scheduled and ad-hoc computation. They report large reductions in storage footprint, pre-computation CPU hours, and query latency after deploying the system in production. The system is now used across WeChat as the default tool for experiment owners doing data-driven decision making.
what_they_built: A bit-sliced-index (BSI) based metric-computation engine for WeChat's online controlled experiment platform that replaces normal-format metric storage/computation with a compressed BSI representation supporting both scheduled pre-computation and fast ad-hoc queries.
evidence: For 105 core metrics over 29 days, BSI storage came to ~1.6 TB versus 4.1 TB in normal format; pre-computation CPU hours fell from 22,712 to 5,446 (about a 76% reduction); ad-hoc query latency dropped from 22.3s to 6.0s (about a 73% reduction). The paper also states the system is "widely used within WeChat as an important data-driven tool for decision making."
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete, measured pattern for scaling an internal experimentation/metrics platform's storage and query costs as experiment and metric volume grows — a governance/infrastructure problem most large product orgs eventually hit.

title: Improving Efficiency of Thumbtack's Online Marketplace
summary: Thumbtack's marketplace team faced supply-constrained markets where too few relevant pros were available to surface to customers, hurting customer experience. They shipped two interventions: personalized "mismatch insight" nudges showing pros the missed booking opportunities from preferences deemed negotiable, encouraging them to broaden what job types/locations they accept; and a conversion-modeled selective discounting mechanism that offers discounted bookings to budget-constrained pros only when it's predicted to improve customer outcomes. Both were validated with A/B tests and framed around a four-quadrant market health model (healthy, supply-constrained, demand-constrained, unhealthy). The piece is squarely about marketplace-mechanics product decisions rather than ML model internals.
what_they_built: Two marketplace-liquidity features: a weekly personalized "mismatch insights" nudge for pros to expand negotiable preferences, and a conversion-model-driven selective discounting system to add supply from budget-constrained pros in supply-constrained markets.
evidence: Both initiatives were validated via A/B tests, with the team stating they were able to "improve the customer experience by incentivizing pros to expand some of their preferences" within a few weeks; outcome is reported qualitatively as customer-experience improvement rather than a specific percentage.
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete, testable pattern for diagnosing and relieving two-sided marketplace supply constraints without brute-force supply acquisition, useful for any PM running a matching marketplace.

title: Arcturus — Inventory Processing System
summary: Tiki's legacy inventory system forced a tradeoff during peak flash-sale events: keep strict consistency and overload the system, or stay up and risk overselling, which led to manual disabling of hot SKUs and customer-compensated order cancellations. The engineering team rebuilt inventory processing as Arcturus, a single-threaded, non-blocking processor using a ring buffer (LMAX Disruptor), unified Kafka-based command queues treating warehouse updates and customer orders identically, and an eventual-consistency model with offset checkpoints across queue, cache, and database. Released in September 2019 ahead of the 10.10 sales event, it reportedly eliminated overselling and forced cancellations in subsequent major sales events. The post details throughput benchmarks at each layer of the stack.
what_they_built: Arcturus, a rearchitected inventory processing engine using Java 11, Kafka command queues, an LMAX Disruptor ring buffer for single-threaded non-blocking processing, Guava local caching, ZeroMQ for fast command responses, and MySQL persistence.
evidence: Reported benchmarks: 350,000 transactions/second in-memory throughput, 120,000 transactions/second full I/O throughput, 5,600 requests/second in staging, 99% average cache hit rate, and 0.3 requests/second database load; qualitative outcome that major sales events after the September 2019 release ran without overselling or order cancellations.
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete architectural pattern (unified command queue + ring buffer + eventual consistency) for resolving a consistency-vs-availability tradeoff in a high-throughput transactional system during demand spikes, relevant to any PM/platform team owning inventory, booking, or ticketing systems under flash-sale load.

title: TIKI @ Scale in 10 Years
summary: Tiki, a Vietnamese e-commerce company, traces ten years of platform evolution: replacing Magento with an in-house platform called Talaria built by a 5-person team in six months (2014-2015), then breaking the monolith into roughly 100+ microservices between 2016-2017 as the engineering org grew from 60 to 200+ people. The piece covers production incidents that forced architectural change (a 2013 flash-sale crash, the Magento-to-Talaria data migration) and a subsequent move to Google Cloud completed in under a month during peak season. It closes with current-state scale numbers: 700+ repositories, 500 active services, 20 million daily product-info sync events, and 40+ production deployments per day.
what_they_built: An in-house e-commerce platform (Talaria) replacing Magento, later decomposed into a ~500-service microservices architecture and migrated onto Google Cloud.
evidence: Reports 700+ code repositories, 500 active systems, 200+ engineers, 400 million monthly user interactions, 20 million daily product-info sync updates, and 40+ new deployments per day; also describes a "fastest in Asia-Pacific" sub-one-month cloud migration of hundreds of services.
why_it_matters: Shows how an internal platform team's architecture decisions (monolith-to-microservices, in-house build vs. vendor platform, cloud migration timing) get forced and validated by concrete production incidents and peak-traffic events, not roadmap planning.

title: Dynamic Content Moderation in Livestreams: Combining Supervised Classification with MLLM-Boosted Similarity Matching
publisher: arXiv (TikTok/ByteDance staff paper, accepted KDD 2026 ADS track)
summary: TikTok's trust and safety team needed to catch both known policy violations and novel, evolving forms of unwanted content in livestreams, where moderation must be fast and work across text, audio, and video. They built a hybrid production system: a supervised classifier for known violation types plus a reference-based similarity matcher for novel or subtle cases, with a multimodal LLM distilling knowledge into both pipelines to boost accuracy while keeping inference lightweight. The system is deployed at production scale, and large-scale A/B tests showed measurable reductions in user exposure to unwanted livestream content.
what_they_built: A hybrid livestream content-moderation framework combining a supervised classification pipeline with an MLLM-boosted reference-based similarity-matching pipeline, run in production on multimodal (text/audio/visual) livestream inputs.
evidence: In production, the classification pipeline achieves 67% recall at 80% precision, and the similarity pipeline achieves 76% recall at 80% precision; large-scale A/B tests showed a 6-8% reduction in user views of unwanted livestreams.
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete pattern for trust-and-safety teams -- pairing a static classifier with a similarity-based detector for novel abuse -- and how to evaluate governance systems with production precision/recall plus A/B-tested user-exposure metrics rather than offline model scores alone.

title: The Importance of User Distribution in Online Experiments (AB Testing)
summary: Tokopedia's engineering team explains why naive user-bucketing for A/B tests introduces biased allocation, selection bias, and memory effects across successive experiments, and why simple modulo-based assignment on user ID is not random enough to prevent users from landing in the same slot repeatedly. Their in-house Rollence experimentation platform (running since 2019) instead uses a double-hashing scheme: users are first hashed into 100 buckets with a constant prefix, then re-hashed per-experiment into 10,000 buckets using the experiment name plus user ID. Teams validate that this allocation is unbiased by running AA tests before launching a real experiment, checking that two identical treatment buckets show no statistically significant difference. The piece covers the full experimentation lifecycle Rollence manages: allocation, AA testing, AB testing, post-analysis, and rollout.
what_they_built: Rollence, Tokopedia's internal experimentation platform (live since 2019), using a two-step hashing mechanism to randomize user bucket assignment per-experiment and prevent bias/memory effects across tests.
evidence: Qualitative: the piece describes AA testing as the validation method — no statistically significant difference between two identical buckets is taken as evidence the allocation mechanism is free of bias; no specific pre/post metrics are quoted.
why_it_matters: Any PM running experimentation at scale needs to know that user-bucketing itself can silently bias results across sequential experiments unless allocation is re-randomized per test and validated with AA checks.

title: 신입 디자이너가 꼭 알아야 할 실험 설계 팁 (Experiment-design tips every junior designer should know)
summary: A Toss Bank product designer describes running a series of A/B tests on the non-member signup intro screen, facing three recurring problems: many possible drop-off points, a long history of prior experiments already run, and the difficulty of writing hypotheses that hold up under scrutiny. She used a "speed x impact" matrix to prioritize the intro screen over compliance-heavy steps, then mined past experiments for their problem framing rather than just their win/loss outcomes. The piece walks through one failed experiment and two successful follow-ups on the same screen, each with concrete before/after CTR and CVR figures.
what_they_built: A prioritization framework (drop-off-point mapping scored by speed and impact) plus a sequence of implemented A/B tests — a chatbot/consultant-persona variant, then copy and image-load optimizations, then scenario-based outcome-visualization copy — on Toss Bank's non-member signup intro screen.
evidence: First experiment (consultant persona with fewer options) dropped CTR over 10% and CVR over 3%. Second experiment (copy and image-load optimization) improved both CTR and CVR. Third experiment (outcome-visualization copy) raised CTR by 5% and improved CVR meaningfully.
why_it_matters: Shows a lightweight, transferable method for prioritizing which funnel step to test and for extracting reusable hypothesis-quality lessons from a team's prior experiment history, not just its win/loss scoreboard.

title: Beyond LTV: MTVi, a New Metric for Measuring Service Value
summary: Toss's data analytics team found that standard LTV couldn't answer whether a given service was actually worth building or promoting, since it conflates a user's natural value with the service's causal contribution. They built MTVi (Mid-term Value Incremental), a metric measuring the net incremental financial value a service creates over the following year, estimated via difference-in-differences comparing newly-activated users against a matched never-used control group rather than relying on impractical org-wide A/B tests. The metric splits into direct usage value plus cross-activation value (engagement with other Toss services it triggers), letting loss-leading services justify themselves via platform-level spillover. Toss describes using MTVi as a shared cross-team currency: capping CAC against MTVi and prioritizing investments across product, strategy, and finance.
what_they_built: MTVi, a causal-inference-based (difference-in-differences) financial value metric that isolates a service's true incremental contribution to user value, split into direct-use and cross-activation components.
evidence: Qualitative: Toss reports using MTVi operationally to cap marketing CAC against a service's incremental value and to guide cross-team prioritization decisions; gives the example of a loss-leading step-counting service whose negative direct-use value (A) is offset by positive cross-activation value (B), demonstrating platform-level profitability. No specific before/after numeric adoption metrics were given in the retrieved text.
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete pattern for building a decision-grade "incremental value" metric with a causal method (DID) when true experimentation isn't feasible across many product lines, and for getting product, marketing, and finance to share one number instead of arguing past each other.

title: 토스가 특허 낸 리서치툴, TNS (Toss Navigation Score) 제작기
summary: Toss's UX research and engineering teams found their existing tool, EVR (Entrance conVersion Rate), could only measure single-screen click accuracy and couldn't capture how users actually navigated multi-screen paths to find a feature. They built TNS (Toss Navigation Score), a patented in-app research tool that tags test participants with a TNS ID in the live app's logs so their navigation paths can be tracked separately from regular users, without duplicating the app environment. iOS and Android teams shipped a floating test UI across all app screens, and designers ran roughly 100 user tests to iteratively fix confusion (users mistaking the tool for an ad) via progressive, moment-of-need messaging. The resulting semiannual dashboard surfaces where users get lost and which services get confused with each other, and it directly surfaced that loan-home screens lacked an entry point to credit-score features (unlike the reverse), prompting a real navigation fix.
what_they_built: TNS (Toss Navigation Score): a patented, live-app UX research instrument that assigns test users a distinct log ID, overlays a floating in-app test UI across all screens (built by iOS/Android teams), and scores how quickly/directly users find a target feature, feeding a semiannual cross-team navigation-health dashboard.
evidence: Qualitative + process outcomes: patent filed on the scoring methodology; ~100 user-test sessions used to redesign the intro UI (reduced users mistaking it for an ad); TNS scores tracked semiannually across all Toss services and shared with teams scoring below average; concretely surfaced that the loan-home screen had no entry point to credit-score features (while the reverse existed), leading to a real product fix adding that navigation path.
why_it_matters: Shows how to instrument a live production app (not a synthetic clone) to measure real multi-step navigation behavior, and how to operationalize that signal into a recurring, cross-team dashboard that drives concrete UX fixes rather than a one-off research readout.

title: 진짜 A/B 테스트: 토스의 푸시 생태계를 데이터로 재설계한 방법 (A Real A/B Test: How Toss Redesigned Its Push Ecosystem With Data)
summary: As Toss's super-app expanded past 100 internal services, push notification volume ballooned and company-wide push CTR declined as users' response thresholds rose. A data analyst used EDA and SHAP feature analysis to find that users who fail to respond to N consecutive pushes of a similar purpose within N days come to see that push category as unnecessary, and used this to redesign the de-targeting logic. The team ran a two-month ABC test on 6% of users comparing a control against an aggressive and a conservative de-targeting variant, then rolled the safer variant out company-wide after results matched simulation.
what_they_built: A push de-targeting system that stops sending a given push category to a user after N consecutive non-responses within a recent window, tuned via an ABC test comparing an aggressive vs. conservative N threshold against a control.
evidence: Both variants achieved approximately +4 percentage points improvement in CTR with no significant decline in app-open AU, service AU, or revenue guardrails.
why_it_matters: Shows PMs a concrete pattern for using guardrail-metric-protected experimentation to cut notification fatigue without sacrificing revenue or engagement.

title: State of Android at Trade Republic — 2024 edition
summary: Trade Republic's Android guild faced slowing build times and mounting tech debt (a monolithic buildSrc config, an oversized :app module) as the mobile org and codebase grew. The Mobile Core & Architecture team responded with loose-coupled Gradle modules, a remote build cache, migration from buildSrc to project-specific convention plugins (build-logic), and further modularization of the app module. The post also covers the broader 2024 Android stack evolution: migrating from RxJava2 to Kotlin Coroutines/Flow, refining a ViewModel-contract MVVM pattern with a Delegate pattern for complex screens, and moving serialization from GSON to KotlinX Serialization.
what_they_built: A Gradle build-performance overhaul (loosely-coupled modules, remote build cache via Gradle Develocity, convention plugins replacing buildSrc, and app-module modularization) plus a coordinated migration to Kotlin Coroutines/Flow across the Android codebase.
evidence: Local clean build P90 time dropped from 22.7 to 13.94 minutes; with remote build cache, from 17.03 to 10.39 minutes (a 40% improvement). CI build P90 improved from 23.75 to 16.59 minutes (about 30%). The buildSrc-to-build-logic migration separately yielded an 8-second gain in clean builds, 4 seconds for core-module changes, 2 seconds for feature-module changes, and 2 seconds in the configuration phase.
why_it_matters: Shows a PM/platform lead how a focused internal developer-platform investment (build tooling, modularization) translates into measurable, defensible developer-experience wins that can be reported the same way as user-facing metrics.

title: Finding your next investment in less than 40 milliseconds
summary: Trade Republic's Recommendations team removed a user-agnostic gateway cache in front of their Instrument Search service in order to ship per-user personalization, since adding a user identifier to the cache key would have exploded cardinality and memory. This caused an 80% week-over-week traffic increase to the underlying AWS OpenSearch cluster and a much larger latency increase than expected. The team diagnosed two distinct causes -- minutely ElasticSearch refresh-interval spikes and search thread-pool exhaustion from an under-provisioned cluster -- and fixed them by widening the refresh interval to 5 minutes and vertically scaling the cluster (more vCPU, larger thread pool), which dropped high-percentile latency below pre-change levels.
what_they_built: A diagnosis-and-fix pass on their OpenSearch-backed Instrument Search service after deliberately removing a user-agnostic cache to enable personalization -- tuning ElasticSearch refresh interval and vertically scaling cluster nodes/thread pools.
evidence: 80% week-over-week increase in traffic to the search service after cache removal; minutely latency spikes traced to a 1-minute ElasticSearch refresh interval, resolved by widening it to 5 minutes; thread-pool queueing traced to an under-provisioned cluster, resolved by increasing thread pool size and vCPU per node, after which high percentile latencies dropped below pre-cache-removal levels.
why_it_matters: Shows a PM/platform team the real infrastructure cost of a personalization feature (removing a shared cache) and a concrete diagnostic playbook -- separating refresh-interval spikes from thread-pool exhaustion -- for search latency regressions.

title: From Architecture to Tech Radar: How Trade Republic uses Tech Radar and Architecture processes to innovate with security in mind
summary: Trade Republic, a ~280-engineer neobank organized into autonomous domain teams, needed a way to keep teams aligned on technology choices without slowing them down or compromising security/compliance. They adopted a ThoughtWorks-style Tech Radar (four rings: assess, trial, adopt, hold; four quadrants) fed by a formal RFC/architecture-decision-record review process, so every technology or technique's movement across the radar is backed by a documented decision. The post walks through the standard and shortened transition flows (e.g., skipping trial when deprecating yarn for npm/pnpm), the quadrant and engineering-function tagging scheme, and how RFCs escalate from team-level to wider-audience review before updating the radar.
what_they_built: An internal Tech Radar (Backstage-integrated, per later sections of the article) driven by an RFC/ADR review workflow that formally moves technologies and techniques through assess/trial/adopt/hold rings, tagged by quadrant and engineering function.
evidence: Qualitative: describes real transition examples (e.g., deprecating yarn for npm/pnpm using a shortened flow that skipped the trial ring) and explicit organizational lessons — that autonomy, agility, and alignment together let standards advance engineering practice "without harming velocity" across the ~280-engineer org, though no quantitative adoption/before-after metrics are given.
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete, lightweight governance mechanism (RFC-to-radar pipeline) a platform PM can adapt to keep many autonomous teams technically aligned without a heavyweight mandate process.

title: Traveloka Icon Kit: The Single Source of Icons
summary: Traveloka's engineers kept re-adding the same icons under different filenames, bloating app size and breaking asset caching, while the lack of design-system governance meant duplicate variants of the same icon (e.g. multiple chevrons) proliferated across the codebase. The team built TVLK Icon Kit, an Electron desktop app that lets designers submit icons through a review workflow gated by the design-system workgroup, then auto-publishes approved icons via GitHub Actions to npm as @traveloka/icon-kit-web (SVG/React) and @traveloka/district-icon (React Native). The tool became the single source of truth for icons across web and mobile engineering teams, with native iOS/Android integration still in progress at time of writing.
what_they_built: An Electron-based icon submission/review desktop app paired with a GitHub Actions pipeline that auto-publishes approved icons to two npm packages (web and React Native).
evidence: Qualitative adoption claim only: the solution "is currently well-received inside our organization and has been used by all web and React Native engineers," with no quantitative metrics (e.g. app-size reduction) given in the retrieved text.
why_it_matters: Shows how a lightweight internal contribution workflow plus automated publishing can turn a scattered design asset into a governed, single-source platform that teams actually adopt.

title: Building Traveloka Web Performance Culture
summary: Traveloka's frontend org faced inconsistent, undermanaged web performance across many product pages with no shared tooling or accountability. The Web Infrastructure team built Themis, a CLI tool that enforces performance budgets in CI and blocks PRs exceeding thresholds, plus Layar, a dashboard surfacing historical per-commit Technical Excellence Metrics (including Lighthouse scores) with leaderboards for debugging regressions. Adoption was driven collaboratively rather than mandated top-down, with product teams setting their own budgets, supported by workshops, bi-weekly syncs, and integration into performance reviews. By the time of writing, dozens of pages across multiple products had budgets registered and tracked.
what_they_built: A CI-integrated performance-budget gate (Themis) paired with a company-wide performance-metrics dashboard (Layar) that teams use to set and track Lighthouse-based budgets per page.
evidence: "As of today, 61 desktop & 51 mobile pages across five categories of various products had been registered to have their web performance (Lighthouse score) tracked and measured," and 8 products had set their own minimum performance budgets (e.g., mobile homepage set warning/fail thresholds at 85/80 Lighthouse score).
why_it_matters: Shows how a PM/eng org can drive adoption of a quality-gating internal platform not by central mandate but by giving teams ownership of their own budgets plus visibility tooling and review-process integration.

title: Accelerating experimentations through Simulations
summary: Trivago's Ranking and Auction teams relied on A/B tests that needed millions of user visits over several weeks to reach statistical significance, slowing iteration and risking revenue loss from negative experiments. To get faster signal, they built a Java/Reactor-based simulation service that replays sampled production requests through their Result List Composition (RLC) ranking service with algorithm changes toggled behind feature flags, capturing enriched responses to Kafka and persisting them in Hadoop for offline analysis. The tool doesn't model user click behavior, so a full A/B test is still needed before shipping, but it lets teams get quick directional reads on revenue and ranking-position impact before committing to a lengthy test. The post reports the tool proved useful internally and adoption within the team increased.
what_they_built: A reactive Java simulation service that replays sampled production traffic against feature-flagged ranking algorithm changes, plus a companion Python client for querying commonly-used metrics from the simulation output.
evidence: Qualitative: the team reports the tool "has proved to be useful for the team and its adoption has increased," and that it lets them estimate potential revenue impact and ranking-position dynamics before running a full A/B test — no hard percentage metrics are given.
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete pattern for cutting A/B-test cycle time on ranking changes by building an internal traffic-replay/simulation layer as a pre-filter before committing to a full experiment.

title: CSS at trivago — Part 1: Structure and ITCSS
publisher: Christoph Reinartz (team lead, UI/UX engineering, trivago) — personal Medium
summary: Trivago's frontend team faced sprawling, inconsistent CSS across the codebase, including dozens of near-duplicate greys and other ad-hoc colors accumulated over time. Under an internal initiative called Project Ironman, they refactored the first five layers of CSS using the ITCSS (Inverted Triangle CSS) methodology — Settings, Tools, Generic, Elements, and Objects — before addressing components. Adopting design-system variables platform-wide let them eliminate the color inconsistencies and structure specificity in a predictable, low-to-high order.
what_they_built: A seven-layer ITCSS-based CSS architecture (Settings, Tools, Generic, Elements, Objects, Components, Trumps) enforced via a refactoring initiative (Project Ironman) that layered in design-system variables ahead of component-level styling.
evidence: Qualitative before/after: the team reports they were able to "get rid of our 50 shades of grey and a lot of other color inconsistencies" once design-system variables were applied consistently, including to legacy CSS files; no quantitative metrics given.
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete, staged pattern (foundational layers before components) for retrofitting design-system discipline onto a large, already-inconsistent legacy codebase without a big-bang rewrite.

title: How We Cut Kafka Consumer Deployment Costs by 83%
summary: Trivago's PSE-kafka microservice, which forwards hotel price data to an external Ads service, suffered chronic high consumer lag, slow deployments, and low CPU utilization that had forced the team to run 60 oversized pods and still occasionally push expired prices. A backend engineer root-caused the problem to three compounding issues stacked on top of each other: a custom Kafka consumer that only committed offsets during poll(), a hardcoded 75-second in-memory delay bottlenecked by flatMap concurrency, and an undersized gRPC client thread pool. Fixing all three together (migrating to reactor-kafka, replacing the delay with a completion flag, and resizing the thread pool) eliminated the lag and let the team shrink the deployment from 60 pods to 6 while confirming the core business metric, Participation Rate, was unaffected.
what_they_built: A migration of a custom reactive Kafka consumer (KafkaReceiverFlux) to reactor-kafka, removal of a fixed in-memory processing delay in favor of an explicit completion flag, and a resized gRPC client thread pool, applied together to PSE-kafka.
evidence: Pod count dropped from 60 to 6 (83% cost reduction), consumer lag was eliminated and its P1 alerts stopped recurring, pod startup time fell from ~60s to ~10s, and Participation Rate plus requests to other teams were confirmed unchanged post-fix.
why_it_matters: A concrete reminder for PMs/platform owners that recurring infra incidents scaled away with more compute can mask multiple compounding root causes that only surface once you commit a focused investigation.

title: Large Scale CSS Refactoring at trivago
summary: trivago's hotel search frontend had accumulated inconsistent, desktop-first CSS with no shared design foundations, making it hard to keep the UI coherent while teams kept shipping features. An engineer led "Project Ironman," a four-week, 800-commit effort touching over 50,000 lines of SCSS to move the codebase to mobile-first media queries, adopt BEM naming and ITCSS layering, switch from reset.css to normalize.css with border-box sizing, and replace 47 image sprites with SVG icons (PNG fallback via Grunticon). The post reports concrete size/LOC/request reductions and reflects on coordinating the migration across multiple active feature teams without freezing development.
