evidence: Recommendation slots grew from 0 in 2020 to 10+ by 2023 via modeling and rule-based logic, each validated through experiments; the org accumulated a base of experiment-derived data enabling funnel-level hypothesis generation; experiment design and review "hurdles" (psychological and logistical) measurably dropped as more new joiners ran experiments and iteration speed increased. Outcomes are reported qualitatively/directionally rather than as a single headline KPI.
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete, replicable staged path (crawling to flying) and structural/tooling choices a PM can use to bootstrap an experimentation culture from scratch in an org with no prior A/B testing habit.

title: 나 4년 차 서버개발자, 배달의민족의 지리 체계를 뒤흔들다
summary: Baemin's geo data model was built on 행정동 (administrative divisions), which change frequently and drifted out of sync with reality, causing delivery-fee assignment errors and service-availability bugs. A backend engineer led a switch to 법정동 (legal divisions, stable since 1914) as the internal standard, evaluating seven external spatial-data vendors against explicit criteria (coastal/inland coverage gaps, data freshness, overlapping boundaries, cost, API availability, correction responsiveness) before selecting Ainavi Systems. The migration also required rebuilding the batch update pipeline — moving from repeated DB polygon queries to in-memory polygon data and grouping updates by division code — which cut a projected 40+ hour batch job to about 16 minutes.
what_they_built: A migration of Baemin's internal geo/delivery-zone data model from frequently-changing administrative divisions to legally-defined stable divisions, sourced from a newly vetted external data vendor and driven by a redesigned in-memory batch update pipeline.
evidence: Batch migration time dropped from an estimated 40+ hours to approximately 16 minutes, a reported ~150x performance improvement, achieved by loading polygon data into memory instead of repeated DB queries and grouping updates by division code via a MultiValueMap structure.
why_it_matters: Shows how a PM/engineering team should treat a foundational reference-data model (geographic boundaries) as a governed product decision — with explicit vendor evaluation criteria — rather than an incidental implementation detail, since drift there silently corrupts a customer-facing metric like delivery fees.

title: 한 번 성공하니 다음도 쉬울 줄 알았다: 최소주문금액바 4번의 A/B실험 (Minimum Order Amount Bar: Four Rounds of A/B Experiments)
summary: A Baemin quick-commerce PM walks through four sequential A/B experiments (Oct 2024 to May 2026) run on a "minimum order amount bar" UI component that shows shoppers how much more they need to add to hit the delivery minimum. Phase 1 (limited to Android, one page) cut cart abandonment and lifted overall order conversion; Phase 2 (full rollout) reproduced the effect but revealed average order value quietly dropped because customers anchored to exactly the minimum. Phase 3 added benefit/upsell nudges expecting to lift both conversion and AOV, but showed no significant change on primary metrics—until segment analysis showed the nudge worked only for users already exposed to coupon-based upsell conditions. Phase 4 tested low-cost product recommendations to close the remaining gap; conversion didn't move, but a deliberate reduction of nudge frequency in one variant showed nudges do protect AOV, and churn analysis found over half of exiting customers simply never reached the minimum threshold.
what_they_built: A "최소주문금액바" (minimum-order-amount progress bar) UI component in Baemin's grocery/quick-commerce app, iterated across four A/B-tested phases: baseline progress indicator, full-surface/OS rollout, benefit-nudge messaging, and low-cost product recommendations to close the order gap.
evidence: Phase 1: cart abandonment rate decreased and commerce-wide order conversion rate rose (Android/store-home only). Phase 2: abandonment fell and conversion rose across all surfaces/OS, but average order value dipped slightly as users ordered exactly to the threshold; benefit messaging raised click-through more for existing-intent (membership) users than first-time users. Phase 3: no significant lift in main conversion/AOV metrics overall, but among the subset of users actually exposed to coupon-based upsell messaging, cart size and conversion rose meaningfully. Phase 4: no significant conversion lift, but analysis found "이탈 고객의 절반 이상이 최소주문금액을 달성하지 못했다" (over half of churned customers never reached the minimum order amount), and deliberately reducing nudge exposure in one test group showed nudges do protect AOV.
why_it_matters: Shows a PM how to keep extracting decision-useful signal from repeated experiments that don't replicate a prior win—by digging into guardrail/secondary metrics and segment-level exposure rather than declaring simple success or failure.

title: '대략'이 아닌 '정확한' 데이터로!: 생산성 측정 지표 개발기 (Not 'roughly' but 'accurately': Building a Productivity Measurement Metric)
summary: Woowa Brothers' Sales Service Team, responsible for merchant-onboarding approval work, previously tracked worker productivity manually in Excel — a slow, error-prone, and inconsistent process. They built an automated metric-definition and measurement pipeline defining allocation rate, completion rate, labor input (converted to FTE via 8-hour standard), and a final productivity metric (processed requests divided by labor input). The system also automated attendance-based labor-input calculation and surfaced team/worker-level productivity for reallocation decisions, with the aggregation pipeline processing data in under 8 seconds and serving queries in under 200ms.
what_they_built: An automated productivity-measurement pipeline that defines and computes allocation rate, completion rate, labor input, and a final productivity metric per worker/team, replacing manual Excel-based tracking for merchant-onboarding approval teams.
evidence: Reported outcomes: roughly 5 FTE of annual labor cost saved from automating productivity compilation, roughly 1 FTE saved from automated attendance tracking, aggregation completing within 8 seconds with sub-200ms query latency, and a concrete before/after example where a worker's productivity rose from 77% of team average to 144% after team reassignment based on the new metric.
why_it_matters: Shows a PM how to convert a fuzzy, manually-tracked operational concept (worker productivity) into a precisely defined, automated metric that directly informs staffing and reallocation decisions.

title: 실험과 기능플래그를 위한 실험플랫폼 구축하기 (Building an Experimentation Platform for Experiments and Feature Flags)
summary: Baemin's teams needed a consistent way to run A/B experiments and control feature rollouts (canary releases, rollbacks, runtime toggles) without redeploying code, and without different concurrent experiments on the same screen contaminating each other's results. Woowa Brothers built an in-house experimentation platform with four server components (admin, group-assignment, event-ingestion, gateway) plus an Airflow-based data extraction/aggregation pipeline. Users are deterministically bucketed into 10,000 logical "slots" via hashing their identifier, and each experiment can be scoped to a sub-range of slots to prevent cross-experiment interference; results are evaluated with configurable confidence levels and Z-test p-values. The post walks through the admin UI, forced assignment for QA, targeting rules, event/goal definitions, and the ordered policy chain (force → status → target → slot assignment) that resolves each group-assignment request.
what_they_built: An internal experimentation and feature-flag platform at Baemin comprising an admin console, a group-assignment service using slot-based consistent hashing, an event-ingestion/gateway layer, and an Airflow-driven data aggregation pipeline for computing experiment results (conversion rates, p-values, confidence intervals).
evidence: Qualitative/architectural evidence rather than headline metrics: the article details the 10,000-slot hashing scheme guaranteeing stable bucketing for repeat users, the slot-range partitioning mechanism to avoid result contamination between concurrent experiments on the same surface, an ordered policy-chain implementation (ForceAssignmentPolicy, StatusPolicy, TargetPolicy, SlotAssignmentPolicy) in Kotlin, and the statistical methodology (Z-test, p-value vs. significance level) used to judge whether a result is trustworthy.
why_it_matters: Shows PMs and platform builders the concrete mechanics (consistent hashing, slot-range isolation, policy-chain resolution) needed to run trustworthy concurrent experiments at scale without them polluting each other.

title: Building a customer-focused Observability Maturity Model
summary: After Xero moved in 2017 from a centralized operations team to product teams that both build and run their services, observability expertise had to be rebuilt from scratch inside each team rather than living in one specialist group. Xero's engineering org responded with a customer-focused Observability Maturity Model spanning metrics, logs, traces, and SLOs (measured via latency, error rate, and synthetic-availability SLIs), backed by mandatory engineering standards such as SLO-based alerting and a minimum 2-hour post-release monitoring window for teams that haven't met baseline standards. The model defines staged maturity levels (base, beginner, intermediate, and beyond) each with concrete practices — from custom YAML-templated SLOs and a purpose-built internal SLO tool at the base level, to error-budget paging, distributed tracing, and synthetic tests at the beginner level, to team reliability reviews at the intermediate level.
what_they_built: An internal, vendor-agnostic Observability Maturity Model plus a custom SLO creation/management tool, paired with mandatory engineering standards that gate production releases on monitoring readiness.
evidence: Qualitative account rather than quantified metrics: the piece describes the staged capability levels teams progress through, the specific mandatory standards (SLO-based alerting, 2-hour post-release monitoring), and the reasoning behind them (symptoms of degradation and downstream alert delays take time to manifest), but does not report adoption percentages or before/after incident numbers.
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete, reusable pattern for how a platform/reliability team can shift observability ownership onto product teams via staged maturity levels, mandatory standards, and self-serve tooling rather than by mandate alone.

title: Driving Engineering Performance the Yassir Way
summary: Yassir's engineering org needed a way to measure and improve engineering performance without relying on gut feel or vanity metrics. They built a four-pillar framework: company OKRs focused on velocity and quality/reliability, a set of lag/lead/sentiment metrics (DORA metrics, a home-grown reliability score and tech-debt index, team composition and capacity allocation, and eNPS/psychological-safety surveys), a tech roadmap carved out alongside the product roadmap (20-30% capacity), and bi-annual individual performance reviews against four behavioral expectations. Running this system, they report large before/after swings in DORA metrics alongside improved developer sentiment.
what_they_built: A structured, four-pillar engineering performance-measurement and management system combining OKRs, DORA + custom reliability/tech-debt metrics, dedicated capacity allocation for tech work, and eNPS-based sentiment tracking.
evidence: Reported DORA outcomes: Lead time for change -30%, deployment frequency +100%, Mean Time to Restore -90%, plus increased developer satisfaction as measured by eNPS.
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete template for pairing OKRs with lag/lead/sentiment metrics so engineering performance management ties directly to measurable delivery and reliability outcomes, not just activity counts.

title: From Collaboration to System: The Birth of SEFAR
summary: Yassir's apps had grown independently on a basic, non-scalable UI kit, leaving designers and developers duplicating work and losing consistency across products. The team built SEFAR, a "system of systems" spanning UI components, a 200+ illustration library, and multilingual UX writing (English, French, Arabic and Arabic dialects), with color tokenization for rebrand flexibility and RTL/bidirectional support plus Latin-Arabic typography matching baked in from the start. They formalized governance around it: discovery workshops to set initial design decisions, a defined delivery workflow for component creation and maintenance, a 4-to-8-eye peer review principle for both design and developer handoff, and structured versioning and bug-reporting processes. The piece claims the system saw high adoption across Yassir's products but does not cite specific adoption metrics.
what_they_built: SEFAR, a company-wide design system covering UI components, illustrations, and multilingual UX writing, with tokenized theming and RTL/Arabic-aware components, governed by a formal discovery-to-handoff review workflow (including a 4-to-8-eye peer review step).
evidence: Qualitative only: the article states "the high adoption rate of SEFAR across our products speaks volumes about its success," but provides no percentages, team counts, or before/after metrics.
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete governance model (workshops, defined delivery workflow, 4-to-8-eye review, versioning, bug support) a PM/design-ops lead can copy to scale a design system across multiple product teams, especially where RTL/multilingual support is a first-class requirement.

title: How Yelp Built a Back-Testing Engine for Safer, Smarter Ad Budget Allocation
summary: Yelp's ad budget allocation system splits campaign spend across on- and off-platform inventory daily, with each day's decisions depending on prior outcomes, so small algorithm tweaks could cascade unpredictably and testing them live risked real advertiser money. The team built a Back-Testing Engine that mirrors production budgeting/billing code via Git submodules, replays historical Redshift campaign data day-by-day, uses CatBoost models to predict impressions/clicks/leads, and logs results to MLFlow, all orchestrated by a Scikit-Optimize Bayesian search over a YAML-defined parameter space. This let them filter and validate ideas offline before committing to slow, higher-risk A/B tests, shifting A/B testing from a discovery tool to a final-validation step. The post also candidly lists limitations: dependence on historical data and model quality, and risk of overfitting to past patterns.
what_they_built: An eight-component Back-Testing Engine (YAML-configured parameter search, a Scikit-Optimize Bayesian optimizer, production code mirrored via Git submodules, CatBoost models predicting outcomes, and MLFlow logging) that replays historical campaign data day-by-day to simulate proposed ad budget allocation algorithm changes before A/B testing or production launch.
evidence: Qualitative before/after: replaced reliance on slow, advertiser-level A/B tests (which took a month to measure and risked real advertiser budgets) with fast offline simulation used to filter candidates before A/B validation; team reports faster productionization (changes tested directly on git branches), improved scientist-engineer collaboration, and earlier detection of code bugs/edge cases across real data. No hard percentage/dollar metrics are given.
why_it_matters: Shows how a PM-adjacent team can shrink risky live experimentation to a smaller, more confident set of A/B tests by building a "digital twin" simulation layer on top of production code and historical data.

title: Android in Analytics Infra
summary: Yelp's Analytics Infra team owns two core Android SDKs — an experimentation SDK and a logging SDK — that feature teams across the company depend on to launch and measure features. Because these SDKs launch first at app startup and have no UI of their own, the team faces an unusually high blast radius (a startup crash hits 100% of affected devices) and an unusual validation problem: you can't easily use a tool to test the tool itself. Their answer is process, not tooling — sample apps, deliberately slow staged rollouts, and monitoring existing experiment data for anomalies to catch regressions indirectly. The post also covers why the SDKs can't share code with client repos (circular dependency risk) and the resulting on-call burden from being a widely-integrated core dependency.
what_they_built: Two Android infrastructure SDKs (experimentation and logging) built with Kotlin, Koin, and Retrofit that every Yelp feature team's app depends on for feature flagging/rollout and event logging.
evidence: Qualitative: the team reports no formal test harness exists for validating the SDKs themselves, so they rely on gradual rollouts and post-hoc anomaly detection in existing experiment data as an implicit proof the code works; they also note any startup-time crash propagates to 100% of affected devices, which is why rollouts are deliberately slow and cautious.
why_it_matters: Shows how a PM/platform team should reason about validating infrastructure that has no user-facing surface to test directly — lean on staged rollout and downstream signal monitoring instead of conventional QA.

title: Data Quality: How Yelp Stacks Up to the Competition
summary: Yelp wanted to know objectively how accurate its business listings were compared to competitors, since "accurate listings" is hard to self-assess at scale (50 million businesses, high churn, ambiguous edge cases). The team built a scoring rubric across seven data points — name, address, phone, website, photo presence, duplicate absence, and location accuracy — and manually audited roughly 1,000 businesses sourced from independent "Best Of" media lists, scoring Yelp against Google, TripAdvisor, and Yellowbook. They found Yelp and Google were closely matched and ahead of the rest, with Google stronger on website links (from web crawling) and Yelp stronger on photo content, while candidly flagging sampling and scoring flaws (uneven category/geography coverage, a scoring quirk that could inflate TripAdvisor's "no duplicates" score).
what_they_built: A manual, rubric-based data-quality benchmarking methodology (1 point per correct datum across 7 fields) applied to ~1,000 businesses to compare Yelp's listing accuracy against Google, TripAdvisor, and Yellowbook.
evidence: Qualitative-plus-partial-quantitative: "Yelp and Google are closely matched and ahead of the other competition"; Google led on website accuracy (crawling advantage), Yelp led on photo content; specific caveats given (e.g., 79 Dublin shopping listings vs. zero French listings, and a scoring flaw that likely inflated TripAdvisor's no-duplicates score).
why_it_matters: Shows a lightweight, honestly-caveated way to turn a fuzzy quality claim ("we have good data") into a comparable, defensible metric a PM can act on and communicate, flaws and all.

title: The Dream Query: How we scope projects with GraphQL
summary: Yelp engineers faced the usual friction of scoping a project before an underlying GraphQL schema exists: frontend and backend work couldn't parallelize, and data-shape mismatches surfaced late. They introduced the "Dream Query" practice — writing the ideal GraphQL query a page would need, during scoping, before any real application code exists — then reviewing it against schema guidelines and mocking it live with graphql-faker so frontend and backend can iterate in parallel. The write-review-implement process was adopted for feature planning across Yelp's engineering org. The piece is a first-person process writeup by a Yelp tech lead rather than a metrics-driven case study.
what_they_built: A planning-phase practice ("Dream Query") for scoping features against GraphQL, paired with graphql-faker to mock the theoretical schema so teams can query it live and iterate before real implementation begins.
evidence: Qualitative only: the author states the practice communicates data requirements earlier, encourages schema reuse, surfaces schema issues earlier, and enables chunked GraphQL migrations — no adoption counts, before/after metrics, or quantified iteration-time reduction are given.
why_it_matters: A lightweight, concrete technique any PM/eng team building on GraphQL (or any typed API) can borrow to de-risk scoping by writing the ideal client query first and mocking it before committing to implementation.

title: Using MOE, the Metric Optimization Engine, to Optimize an A/B Testing Experiment Framework
summary: Yelp's ad targeting team needed a faster way to tune A/B test parameters than naive uniform traffic splits, since sampling each configuration was costly. They built and open-sourced MOE (Metric Optimization Engine), which uses a Gaussian Process model to suggest new parameters with the highest Expected Improvement and can also dynamically reallocate traffic toward better-performing cohorts (a multi-armed-bandit approach) instead of a fixed 50/50 split. In a worked example, MOE converged to the globally optimal parameter value within three rounds of sampling, beating both the status quo and a local-maximum outcome. The post frames MOE as a general tool applicable anywhere sampling a metric is slow or expensive, not just ad targeting.
what_they_built: MOE, an open-sourced Bayesian-optimization / multi-armed-bandit engine that suggests optimal experiment parameters and dynamically allocates A/B test traffic instead of fixed splits.
evidence: Worked example shows MOE converging to the globally optimal parameter value within three sampling rounds, and the post states this approach "increase[s] the system CTR far beyond the initial status quo value, or even the local maxima" — though the example is presented as simulated/illustrative rather than a live production CTR readout.
why_it_matters: Shows a PM-relevant pattern for experimentation platforms: replacing fixed traffic splits with adaptive allocation can cut the time and traffic cost of finding a winning parameter set.

title: Search Query Understanding with LLMs: From Ideation to Production
summary: Yelp's search team needed to better match search results to what users actually meant, both by understanding structured intent within a query (segmentation with spell correction) and by showing users why a given business matched their query (LLM-generated review highlight snippets). They describe the full path from formulation through proof-of-concept to production scaling, using progressively smaller, cheaper models with fine-tuning and pre-computed caching to keep serving costs down. The review-highlighting feature was A/B tested in production and shown to lift engagement, with a stronger effect on less common (tail) queries.
what_they_built: An LLM-based query segmentation/spell-correction pipeline plus an LLM-generated review-highlight system that surfaces the phrase in a business's reviews most relevant to the user's search query.
evidence: A/B testing showed increased Session / Search CTR across Yelp's platforms after shipping the review-highlighting feature, with the impact noted as higher for tail (less common) queries.
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete pattern for shipping LLM features under cost constraints — prototype with a large model, then distill/fine-tune down to smaller cached models before A/B testing and full production rollout.

title: Yelp's Trust & Safety Report: How we protected our users in 2025
summary: Yelp faced growing volumes of AI-generated reviews, scam accounts, and fake business pages threatening the reliability of its review corpus. Its automated review recommendation software, which scores roughly 22 million annual reviews against hundreds of quality and reliability signals, was enhanced in 2025 to better catch AI-generated content and reviews lacking genuine consumer detail, layered with a human User Operations moderation team. The result: nearly 500,000 AI-flagged reviews filtered, 1.3 million accounts closed (up 138% year over year), and sharp increases in blocked fraud rings and fake business pages.
what_they_built: An automated review-recommendation and account-fraud detection system combining hundreds of quality/reliability signals with AI-generated-content detection, backed by a human moderation (User Operations) review layer.
evidence: Nearly 500,000 reviews filtered for AI-generated characteristics; of ~22M reviews, 70% recommended, 17% not recommended, 11% removed for policy violations; over 1.3 million accounts closed (+138% YoY), 889,800 for fake airline-support scams, ~2,000 for review-exchange rings (+49%), 50,700 suspicious business pages rejected (+29%), 1,340 deceptive lead-gen pages removed (+29%); User Operations team manually removed 193,700 reported reviews and proactively blocked 24,200 harmful reviews.
why_it_matters: Shows how a marketplace/platform PM can quantify and report trust-and-safety system performance with concrete, auditable enforcement metrics rather than vague policy statements.

title: How We Designed and Delivered Adaptive Fashion with Design Justice Principles in Mind
summary: Zalando's product design team describes how it built its Adaptive Fashion collection (launched October 2022) by applying design justice principles: rather than designing for people with disabilities, the team facilitated co-creation sessions with disabled community members and allies, in partnership with creative consultancy All is for All, and compensated participants for their input. The collection was integrated into the main shopping experience rather than segregated into a separate niche. Post-launch customer feedback on zipper durability led the team to switch fabric/zipper contractors for subsequent collections. The piece reports that the Adaptive range now outperforms Zalando's overall CSAT and has grown substantially in both catalog size and engagement.
what_they_built: An Adaptive Fashion product line and an accompanying co-creation design process that embeds disabled community members as paid collaborators in product decisions, including a switch of zipper suppliers driven by customer feedback.
evidence: CSAT for the Adaptive range exceeds overall Zalando CSAT; about a 90% increase in customers engaging with Adaptive journeys via dedicated filters/pages; the range has grown to roughly 600 distinct Adaptive styles, with brand partnerships (BOSS, Friboo) and a rising share of customers purchasing multiple Adaptive items annually.
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete mechanism (paid co-creation sessions plus a facilitator, not expert, posture) for turning an equity/inclusion commitment into measurable product and satisfaction gains, transferable to any PM building for an underserved segment.

title: Design Principles: Creating Shared Values for a More Inclusive Fashion Experience
summary: Ahead of launching its first adaptive fashion collection in October 2022, Zalando's design team ran customer interviews, desk research, and partnered with disability-inclusion experts, then brought 12 cross-functional teams (marketing, photography, digital development, assortment) through a facilitated three-step workshop — brainstorm keywords, mind-map customer implications, synthesize principles — to derive four shared design principles (e.g. "Style First, Not Functionality First," "Nothing About Us Without Us"). Those principles were embedded as onboarding material and a shared decision-making tool across the teams building the collection. The collection launched, sold out some items within the first week, drew press coverage in Vogue, Forbes, and Time, and expanded into hundreds of adaptive styles plus new adaptive-specific filtering on product pages.
what_they_built: A facilitated three-step cross-functional workshop process (brainstorm, mind-map, synthesize) that produced four adaptive-fashion design principles, used as onboarding material and a shared decision-making tool across 12 teams building Zalando's adaptive fashion collection.
evidence: Reported outcomes: some items sold out within the first week of launch; press coverage in Vogue, Forbes, and Time Magazine; expansion to hundreds of adaptive styles (private labels plus a Tommy Adaptive partnership); new adaptive-feature filters added to product pages.
why_it_matters: Shows a lightweight, repeatable workshop format (keyword brainstorm to mind-map to synthesized principles) for getting many cross-functional teams aligned on shared design values before a launch, with the principles then used operationally as onboarding and decision-making tools rather than left as a one-off deck.

title: Dead Ends or Data Goldmines? Investment Insights from Two Years of AI-Powered Postmortem Analysis
summary: Zalando's SRE org was sitting on thousands of postmortems from incidents affecting Postgres, DynamoDB, ElastiCache, S3, and Elasticsearch, but manual review (15-20 minutes per document, four per hour per reviewer) made systemic pattern-finding across two years of data impractical. They built a four-stage "map-fold" LLM pipeline (summarize, classify, analyze, find patterns) with human curation at each stage rather than relying on a single high-context model. The pipeline cut analysis time from days to hours, tripled reviewer productivity, and surfaced cross-incident patterns that informed concrete infrastructure investments, including an automated IaC change-validation tool that now shields against 25% of subsequent datastore incidents.
what_they_built: A multi-stage LLM pipeline (summarization, classification, root-cause analysis, and pattern synthesis) with human-in-the-loop review at each stage to mine two years of incident postmortems for infrastructure investment decisions.
evidence: Reduced postmortem analysis time from days to hours and tripled reviewer productivity; enabled processing a year's worth of postmortems in under 24 hours (Claude Sonnet 4 at ~30 seconds per document); a finding about AWS S3 misconfigurations led to automated IaC change validation that now prevents 25% of subsequent datastore incidents; early small (3B-12B parameter) models showed up to 40% hallucination probability, reduced substantially by moving to larger models, alongside a ~10% surface-attribution error rate even in advanced models; human curation shrank from 100% of output batches reviewed initially to 10-20% random sampling once quality stabilized.
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete pattern for using LLMs on messy internal document archives to drive real infrastructure investment decisions, while being explicit about where human review must stay in the loop and how that review burden shrinks over time as trust is earned.

title: Developing Zalando APIs: An Engineering Perspective
summary: Zalando runs a distributed system of 8,000+ active service applications built by 300+ delivery teams across six tech hubs, and needed a way to keep hundreds of independently-built APIs consistent and interoperable. Engineers describe the API-first process they actually run: draft the OpenAPI/Swagger spec before writing code, check the API Portal for existing overlapping services, lint the spec automatically against Zalando's open-source API Guidelines using their Zally tool, then submit it as a GitHub Enterprise pull request for peer and consumer review, with an API Guild reviewing significant APIs for guideline compliance and design quality. The piece frames this as a lightweight, scalable governance layer rather than a heavyweight review board. It also notes that Zally and the API Guidelines have been adopted externally by other companies, which the authors treat as validation of the approach.
what_they_built: An internal API-first workflow (spec-before-code, API Portal for discovery, Zally as an automated linter against published guidelines, GitHub PR review, and an API Guild for higher-stakes review) enforced across Zalando's engineering organization.
evidence: Scale figures given in the piece: 8,000+ active service applications built by 300+ delivery teams across six tech hubs and 1,200+ engineers, plus qualitative evidence that the Zally linter and API Guidelines have been adopted externally by other companies and open-sourced (200+ open source projects at Zalando overall). No before/after or defect-rate metrics are given — the evidence is organizational scale plus external adoption, not a controlled outcome comparison.
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete, low-ceremony pattern (spec-first + automated linting + lightweight PR review + a guild for edge cases) for keeping API quality consistent across hundreds of autonomous teams without a centralized approval bottleneck.

title: An Introduction to the Zalando Design System
summary: Zalando needed a design system that let a central team maintain visual and code consistency while dozens of feature teams kept autonomy to ship business logic quickly. They built a five-layer architecture (design tokens, electrons/CSS grammar, atoms, molecules, organisms) as a monorepo of individually versioned npm packages, with GitHub code owners enforcing that the central team owns tokens/CSS/atoms/molecules while feature teams own organisms. After more than two years in production, adoption reached close to 100% and the system was described as running relatively smoothly, though the piece also names ownership fragmentation and deployment coupling as ongoing pain points.
what_they_built: An atomic-design-based design system delivered as a monorepo of independently versioned component packages, with a design-tokens layer shared across web and mobile and a code-owners-enforced split between centrally owned primitives and team-owned business components.
evidence: Adoption rate close to 100% after more than 2 years in production, per the team's own account, with the system reported as running relatively smoothly over that period.
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete governance pattern — code-owners-enforced split between platform-owned primitives and team-owned business components — for balancing central design consistency against feature-team autonomy at scale.

title: Navigating complex design system updates with Figma tools
summary: Zalando's Design System team had a few weeks to redesign the product-card component across a system of 200+ components and 70 styles to comply with an EU regulation (Omnibus) requiring transparent price-discount disclosure, without breaking 50+ consuming teams. They used two Figma features — boolean properties to simplify toggling states and branching to isolate and review changes before merging into the main library — to rebuild a dynamically adjusting product card. They validated the rollout through structured, templated bug-bash sessions with the wider design community across devices and configurations before merging to the shared library.
what_they_built: A redesigned, dynamically adjusting product-card component built with Figma boolean properties and developed on a Figma branch, plus a templated bug-bash testing process for validating design-system changes before merge.
evidence: Toggling a sale price on the component dropped from seven clicks to one; the team reports more design variants than before but a significant improvement in usability, validated through structured bug-bash testing with the design community before merging the branch into the main library (200+ components, 70 styles, 50+ teams affected).
why_it_matters: Shows how a platform/design-systems team can absorb a hard regulatory deadline without breaking downstream teams by using branching and componentization features to isolate, test, and merge risky shared-library changes.

title: Theming the Zalando Design System
summary: Zalando's design system team faced a proliferation of ad-hoc "hacky" theming hacks as the business needed visual variations (e.g. brand accent color changes) while keeping the system maintainable. They built a semantic design token architecture — distinguishing which properties are themable, naming tokens by semantic role rather than literal value, and introducing three color schemes (default, monochrome-light, monochrome-dark) to handle accessibility and dynamic backgrounds. Implementation differs by platform: CSS custom properties scoped via classes on web, generated JSON parsed by a ThemeManager on iOS, and XML/JSON resources feeding both the View system and Compose on Android. The post is candid about unresolved friction, including CSS variable-resolution specificity issues requiring extra "scope" classes when variables reference other variables.
what_they_built: A semantic design-token-based theming architecture spanning Web, iOS, and Android, supporting three color schemes with platform-specific token generation and application pipelines.
evidence: Qualitative: names concrete unresolved problems the architecture still faces (CSS variable-resolution specificity requiring extra "scope" classes, governance decisions about which properties are themable) rather than reporting adoption/performance metrics.
why_it_matters: Shows PMs/design-system owners the concrete tradeoffs (governance boundaries, token semantics, cross-platform parity) involved in taking theming from ad-hoc hacks to a maintained system-level capability.

title: Bringing more character to the Zalando Design System with theming
summary: Zalando's design system had product teams independently overriding styles to create visual variations, producing scattered local implementations and duplicated components that required manual updates across touchpoints. The design team built a semantic (not primitive) token architecture across eight categories -- colour, typography, font family, border, ratio, size, spacing, grid/breakpoints -- deliberately kept small to stay manageable, and layered a "Colour Schemes" concept on top so colour variants didn't require full new themes. They implemented this in Figma using Variables (Collections and Modes) to build full Base and Designer theme collections plus a three-scheme colour collection, avoiding third-party plugins to keep it usable for their 200+ designer community.
what_they_built: A component-level design token architecture plus a Figma Variables system (Collections/Modes) supporting two themes -- Base and a luxury "Designer" theme -- and a separate Colour Schemes layer (Default, Monochrome Light, Monochrome Dark for Base; one monochromatic scheme for Designer) to avoid combinatorial explosion of theme requests.
evidence: Qualitative: the team reports the Colour Schemes approach "reduced component variants significantly" versus building full new themes per colour variation, and documents explicit process lessons (define terms collaboratively, involve engineers early, document decisions, invest in ongoing token-adoption training) rather than quantitative metrics.
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete pattern -- semantic component-level tokens plus a separate "scheme" layer distinct from "theme" -- for scaling a design system to support brand variants without multiplying components or fielding endless one-off style requests.

title: More Editorial Content, please.
summary: Zalando's legacy landing-page tooling required engineering involvement for every content change, creating a two-week lead time bottleneck for content managers across 25 markets. The team built a new CMS on top of Contentful, a headless content management system, giving non-technical stakeholders the ability to create and publish landing pages independently while keeping web and app content in sync from a single source. The platform also supports extensibility, letting other teams add new modules such as a Sustainability Certificate component. Adoption resulted in dramatically faster publishing and no engineering involvement required for routine page creation.
what_they_built: An internal content platform built on Contentful (headless CMS) that lets non-engineering content/editorial teams independently create, localize, and publish landing pages across web and app for 25 markets, replacing legacy engineering-dependent tooling.
evidence: Time-to-live for landing pages reduced from 2 days to 4 hours; the prior 2-week lead time was removed entirely; no engineering involvement now needed to create and publish landing pages; 82% increase in the number of landing pages published year-over-year.
why_it_matters: Shows how investing in a self-serve internal content platform can remove engineering as a bottleneck for a high-frequency, non-technical workflow, with measurable gains in speed and publishing throughput.

title: Ensuring Even Ad Spend on the Zalando Homepage: How Our New Bidding Algorithm Maximizes Value for Advertisers and Shoppers
summary: Zalando's on-site advertising system was underspending advertiser budgets unevenly across the day, hurting both advertiser value and inventory efficiency on the homepage. The team built a closed-feedback-loop bidding algorithm that forecasts hourly traffic, tracks actual spend against an even-spend target, and dynamically raises or lowers bid prices to smooth budget delivery across the campaign period. They validated the new approach against the previous strategy via A/B testing with a budget split, checking both advertiser-facing metrics and homepage customer experience. The new strategy improved ad views, cost-per-view, clicks, and click-through rate without degrading the shopper experience.
what_they_built: A dynamic bid-adjustment algorithm using a closed feedback loop — comparing actual spend to a forecasted even-spend target each hour and rescaling bid prices via the formula (1 - spent_t)/(1 - spent_t^even) — to evenly pace advertiser budget spend across the Zalando homepage.
evidence: A/B test results: "10% increase in views compared to the previous approach; with a linear drop of CPV of around 10%... the absolute number of clicks on ads increased by 23%. Enhanced click-through rate (CTR): The ratio between clicks and views improved by 11%," with no significant negative impact on overall homepage customer experience metrics.
why_it_matters: Shows how a PM can frame a monetization/pacing optimization as a controlled experiment that protects the core product experience while improving marketplace-side metrics for a second-order stakeholder (advertisers).

title: Experimentation Platform at Zalando: Part 1 - Evolution
summary: Zalando traces how its internal experimentation platform, "Octopus," evolved through three phases: an initial 2015 "Crawl" phase built to fix inconsistent, siloed A/B testing across teams; a 2016-2020 "Walk" phase that standardized statistical methods, built company-wide experimentation culture, and fixed data-quality problems from inconsistent tracking schemas; and a 2020-onward "Run" phase pushing toward more advanced causal-inference methods and faster test cycles. Along the way they found systemic data-quality issues (sample ratio mismatch affecting at least 20% of A/B tests) and worked to reduce median test runtime from about three weeks. The piece is an organizational/platform retrospective on building trustworthy experimentation infrastructure at scale, not a model or algorithm paper.
what_they_built: An internal experimentation platform ("Octopus") that moved from ad hoc, team-run A/B tests to a centralized system with standardized statistical testing (two-sided t-tests at 5% significance), design audits, consultation hours, automated data-quality checks, and Overall Evaluation Criteria for choosing KPIs.
evidence: Reports that sample ratio mismatch affected at least 20% of A/B tests before quality controls were added, that median test runtime was roughly three weeks going into the "Run" phase, and that concurrent A/B test capacity grew over time (with a 2020 dip attributed to coordinated product initiatives and COVID-19 pauses) — evidence is a mix of quantified data-quality findings and qualitative organizational lessons.
why_it_matters: Shows a PM/platform team how to sequence the build-out of internal experimentation infrastructure — from fixing basic trust and data-quality problems before ever investing in advanced statistical methods.

title: How We Redesigned Online Fashion Browsing
summary: Zalando's design team found that customers browsing the catalog fell into two mindsets — "hunting" for something specific versus "exploring" for inspiration — and that about two-thirds relied only on generic functional filters (brand, price, size) rather than fashion-specific attributes, leaving most of the assortment effectively invisible. Nearly half of customers also reported feeling overwhelmed by the choice on offer, and struggled to describe fashion concepts in the standard filter terminology. Over two months of daily design reviews, rapid prototyping, and four rounds of qualitative user testing, the team validated that visual cues helped customers compare and learn fashion attributes better than text filters. They shipped three features — single-attribute visual entry points, multi-attribute visual entry points, and suggested filters surfaced at the top of catalog pages — rolled out fully in September 2022.
what_they_built: A visual-filtering redesign of the fashion catalog browsing experience: image-based single- and multi-attribute entry carousels plus algorithmically suggested filters surfaced at the top of category pages, replacing reliance on text-only functional filters.
evidence: Post-rollout, "the new experience has more than halved the number of customers who find shopping at Zalando 'overwhelming,'" down from the pre-redesign baseline of roughly half of customers reporting that feeling.
why_it_matters: Shows how segmenting users by underlying intent (hunting vs. exploring) rather than by demographics can directly inform which UI mechanism (visual vs. textual filtering) solves a discoverability problem, and how a tight two-month research-to-ship cycle with recurring qualitative testing can validate that choice before full rollout.

title: Closing the Data-Quality Loop
summary: Zalando's Dublin Fashion Content Platform team had originally bootstrapped their English-language fashion classifier's training data from the now-defunct DMOZ directory, assuming it was "good enough." To actually validate the classifier's accuracy, they built a properly quality-controlled "Golden Standard" labeled corpus using the CrowdFlower crowdsourcing platform rather than relying on slow, expensive researcher-led manual labeling. Running their DMOZ-derived "Fashion" links through this new pipeline revealed that roughly 22% of them were mislabeled as fashion, exposing significant noise in data they had been treating as ground truth. The finding justified the investment in a repeatable, quality-controlled labeling process for future model evaluation.
what_they_built: A crowdsourced, quality-controlled "Golden Standard" data-labeling pipeline built on the CrowdFlower platform, used to validate and correct the ground-truth training data for Zalando's fashion content classifier.
evidence: Quantified prior training-data noise: "Roughly 22% of all our DMOZ 'Fashion' links are not fashion," demonstrating that the original bootstrap dataset was significantly mislabeled and validating the effort of building the new corpus.
why_it_matters: Shows a PM/platform builder why "good enough" bootstrap data needs an independent, quality-controlled validation loop before it's trusted as ground truth for downstream product decisions.

title: Search Quality Assurance with AI as a Judge
summary: Zalando's search team needed to validate relevance before launching search in new markets (Luxembourg, Portugal, Greece) without relying on slow, expensive manual annotation. They built an automated pipeline that clusters incoming queries using Named Entity Recognition tags, then runs a multimodal LLM judge (using product data and images) to score result relevance against defined criteria, with automated translated test-case generation and orchestration on Apache Airflow. The framework surfaced concrete issues -- NER recognition failures, product attribute gaps, and category discoverability problems -- before those markets went live, at roughly $250 and 3-5 hours per full evaluation run versus multiple days for manual human evaluation.
what_they_built: An automated, multimodal LLM-as-a-judge pipeline that clusters search queries via NER and scores search relevance at scale, replacing manual annotation for pre-launch quality checks across new-market rollouts.
evidence: Reports concrete operating figures: about $250 per full evaluation run, 3-5 hours per run (versus days for manual human evaluation), and specific search-quality issues (NER recognition gaps, missing product attributes, poor category discoverability) caught before launching search in Luxembourg, Portugal, and Greece.
why_it_matters: Shows PMs a concrete pattern for using an LLM judge to scale quality evaluation of a ranking/relevance system ahead of a market or feature launch, with real cost and time tradeoffs versus manual review.

title: Paper Announcement: Leveraging Multimodal LLMs for Large-Scale Product Retrieval Evaluation
summary: Zalando's search team faced the classic bottleneck of relevance evaluation: human annotation of search-result quality is accurate but slow and expensive, especially across languages and at production scale. They built a multimodal-LLM-as-judge pipeline that scores search results as "highly relevant," "acceptable substitute," or "irrelevant" using both text and product images, following a four-step process from query extraction through LLM-generated annotation guidelines to continuous monitoring. They validated the framework directly against human annotators on 20,000 real production, multilingual search queries and analyzed where each disagreed with ground truth. The framework matched human-level accuracy while running dramatically faster and cheaper, and the team published the methodology and error analysis in an accompanying paper.
what_they_built: A multimodal LLM-as-judge evaluation pipeline that automatically scores search-result relevance against LLM-generated annotation guidelines and stores results for continuous production monitoring.
evidence: The pipeline assessed 20,000 query-product pairs in around 20 minutes versus weeks for human annotators, and MLLM-based assessments are reported as "up to 1,000 times cheaper than human labor"; humans and LLMs made comparable error rates but with different error patterns (humans erred more on brand mismatches/category errors, LLMs on stricter judgments/query misinterpretation).
why_it_matters: Shows PMs a concrete, evidenced pattern for replacing slow human-in-the-loop quality evaluation with an LLM judge validated against human agreement rates before trusting it as a production metric.

title: Parallel Run Pattern - A Migration Technique in Microservices Architecture
summary: Zalando's Returns team needed to retire monolith return-processing logic and cut over to a new microservice without risking production regressions. They built a "parallel run" pattern using the Skipper proxy: the monolith continued serving clients as source of truth while asynchronously forwarding the same requests to the new microservice's consistency-checks endpoint for comparison. Responses were classified as matched, unmatched, or failed, giving the team real production traffic as a live test bed and a per-endpoint rollout/rollback lever via proxy config rather than redeploys. Once confidence was established, they deleted the parallel-run scaffolding, removing roughly 700 lines of code and 1,300 lines of tests.
what_they_built: A dual-write/shadow-traffic consistency-checking mechanism, routed through the Skipper proxy, that compares monolith and microservice responses per endpoint before a full cutover.
evidence: Reports concrete cleanup metrics after retiring the pattern (~700 lines of parallel-run code and ~1,300 lines of unit/component tests removed) and describes tracking matched/unmatched/failed consistency outcomes per endpoint during rollout, using production traffic rather than synthetic test cases.
why_it_matters: Gives PMs and platform leads a concrete, low-risk playbook for retiring legacy system logic behind a new service using real traffic and reversible proxy-level rollout instead of a risky big-bang cutover.

title: Inside the strategic shift to implement our new loyalty programme
summary: Zalando's design team took on redesigning the Plus loyalty programme, converting it from a subscription model to a points-based rewards system where customers unlock benefit levels through engagement, on an eight-month timeline the original strategy team had considered close to impossible. Without a dedicated product manager bridging design and engineering, the team improvised a cadence of twice-weekly design consultation hours to keep decisions moving amid ambiguity and constantly shifting branding and engineering requests. They protected delivery velocity by scoping a minimum viable product and working semi-independently in the final weeks, then relied on cross-functional trust to absorb the technical and design debt left by imperfect early releases. The programme launched on schedule and was rolled out to 13 markets.
what_they_built: A redesigned Zalando Plus loyalty programme moving from a flat subscription to a points-based, tiered benefits system, including interactive engagement features like raffles and giveaways.
evidence: Launched on time within the eight-month deadline and expanded to 13 markets; outcomes are otherwise reported qualitatively (improved customer sense of recognition, new cross-functional processes) rather than with quantitative metrics.
why_it_matters: Shows how a design-led team can substitute structured cross-functional rituals (regular consultation hours, explicit MVP scoping) for a missing PM role to hit a hard deadline on a business-model change.

title: The Day Our Own Queries DoS'ed Us: Inside Zalando Search
summary: Zalando's Search & Browse team describes a self-inflicted denial-of-service on their Elasticsearch cluster: an internal application, during a maintenance procedure, began sending 50x its usual query volume—only 20-100 requests per second in absolute terms, but composed of expensive faceting aggregations on high-cardinality fields like SKU. Because the queries were syntactically legitimate and came from a trusted internal service, they went undetected until the cluster became "hot" and normal customer search traffic starved across two markets. The team traces the detection gap, applies immediate mitigations (cache tuning, node scaling, market cluster isolation, ingestion throttling), and ships longer-term prevention: per-client request tagging via X-Opaque-Id, per-client slow-query dashboards, application-side query limiting, and updated incident runbooks.
what_they_built: Per-client query tagging (X-Opaque-Id), monitoring dashboards for per-client slow query rates, application-side query rate limiting, and revised incident runbooks to prevent recurrence of internally-generated search DoS.
evidence: Reports the internal service was sending 50x normal query volume (20-100 req/s absolute) with expensive high-cardinality faceting; documents the specific mitigations applied (extended cache TTLs, node scaling, market cluster splitting, replica reduction) and the concrete detection/prevention tooling shipped afterward.
why_it_matters: Shows PMs and platform builders that internal, "trusted" traffic can be as dangerous as external abuse, and that per-client observability (not just aggregate metrics) is a prerequisite for catching self-inflicted capacity incidents before they degrade the customer experience.

title: Sunrise: Zalando's developer platform based on Backstage
summary: Zalando's platform team built Sunrise, an internal developer portal on top of Spotify's Backstage, to give engineers a single hub for teams, applications, APIs, CI/CD pipelines, infrastructure accounts, and costs. Since 2021 they've grown it to 40,000+ registered entities and 30 in-house plugins spanning 27 tools, with 2,000 PRs merged into the platform's repos. Because their goal was for Sunrise to be builders' daily starting point, they defined weekday and weekly active-user share among Builders as their primary adoption metrics rather than raw signups, and drove adoption partly by deprecating legacy internal tools it replaced.
what_they_built: An internal developer portal (Sunrise) built on Backstage, extended with 30 custom plugins integrating 27 internal tools and services.
evidence: Over 40,000 registered entities, 30 plugins across 27 tools, 2,000 PRs merged, and adoption tracked via weekday/weekly active share of Builders as the primary success metric; at least 10 plugins are owned by other Zalando teams and one plugin (Zally API Linter) was open-sourced.
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete, non-vanity adoption metric (weekday/weekly active share of the target user population, not raw registrations) for internal platform products, plus using legacy-tool deprecation as a deliberate adoption lever.

title: A Systematic Approach to Reducing Technical Debt
summary: A Zalando Merchant Orders team accumulated a technical-debt backlog of over 70 items, some dating to 2018, that had been growing for three straight years. When Gregor Ulm joined as engineering lead in autumn 2020, he introduced a weekly technical-debt rotation where each engineer spends at least one day per week resolving debt tickets or surfacing new issues via code review, applying Theory Y intrinsic-motivation management rather than top-down directives. The process reduced weekly AWS hosting costs by $500 through resource-requirement review, shipped over ten features/improvements within eight weeks, and reversed the backlog's multi-year growth trend for the first time.
what_they_built: A recurring weekly rotation in which engineers take turns dedicating time to identifying and fixing technical debt, backed by ticket triage and code-review-driven discovery of unknown issues.
evidence: Reduced weekly AWS hosting fees by $500 via resource-requirement evaluation; shipped 10+ features/improvements in eight weeks (four sprints); the technical-debt backlog began declining for the first time after three years of continuous growth.
why_it_matters: Shows a PM/eng-lead a lightweight, low-overhead process (a rotating weekly slot, not a dedicated team) that can reverse chronic technical-debt growth with measurable cost and velocity outcomes.

title: How Zapier rolled out AI org-wide and drove 97% adoption
summary: In March 2023, when GPT-4 launched, Zapier's leadership declared a company-wide "Code Red" to force urgent, org-wide adoption of AI rather than waiting for a polished strategy. Over roughly two years they ran a five-phase process (Curiosity, Code Red, Foundations, Habits, Reinvention) combining top-down guardrails with bottom-up experimentation — hackathons, internal AI champions, and continuous reinforcement — to get every team using AI daily. The Support team built Sidekick, an internal ticket summarizer, which cut average handle time in half. Daily AI usage climbed from 63% (late 2023) to 77% (end of 2024) to 97% most recently.
what_they_built: A structured, five-phase internal change-management program for org-wide AI adoption, plus specific internal AI tools like Sidekick, a support-ticket summarizer.
evidence: Daily AI usage grew from 63% (late 2023, six months after "Code Red") to 77% (end of 2024) to 97% most recently; Sidekick "slashed average handle time...in half" for the support team.
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete, phased playbook and measurable adoption curve for driving a company-wide capability change, not just a tooling rollout.

title: A Lesson in Creating and Using Niche Business DSLs at Scale
summary: Zerodha built Veto, an internal order-validation engine that lets its non-engineering Risk Management team author dynamic business rules to reject risky trades before they hit the Order Management System. Their first version used a govaluate-based expression DSL styled like Excel formulas, but operators struggled to write complex rules (bracket-matching, no variables, boolean-only output) and developers still had to be pulled in to review or extend it, defeating the point of a self-service DSL. The team benchmarked five alternatives (govaluate, Yaegi, Rego/OPA, Hashicorp plugins, native Go plugins) on a real production rule, found native Go plugins fastest, and rebuilt Veto v2 so operators write plain Go rule files—complete with a required self-test map per rule—that get compiled and hot-loaded as plugins.
what_they_built: Veto, a hot-reloading order-validation reverse proxy in front of Zerodha's Order Management System, whose business rules moved from a govaluate expression DSL to plain-Go code distributed as native Go plugins.
evidence: Reports actual benchmark numbers on a production rule: govaluate 907 ns/op, go-plugins 503 ns/op, yaegi 11091 ns/op, rego 13796 ns/op, with Hashicorp plugins discarded as slower; also details qualitative usability failures of the DSL approach (unreadable expressions, no variables, boolean-only results, developer bottleneck) that motivated the redesign.
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete, benchmarked case where a business-facing DSL failed on usability grounds despite technical soundness, and how giving the target users a full language with proper tooling beat a constrained DSL abstraction.

title: Zomato Draft Red Herring Prospectus — Food Delivery Unit Economics
summary: Zomato's Draft Red Herring Prospectus, filed with India's securities regulator SEBI ahead of its 2021 IPO, discloses how the company's food-delivery unit economics evolved as its customer base matured. The filing attributes a decline in advertising and sales-promotion expense as a share of total income to a rising proportion of organic repeat orders from customers first acquired via paid promotion, evidenced by a "GOV retention by cohorts" chart and multi-year expense-ratio data. It ties this trend explicitly to underlying platform-level order economics (delivery, packaging, discounting, and customer/delivery-partner incentive costs per order) for Fiscal 2020 and the nine months ended December 2020.
what_they_built: Not a product build — a regulatory financial disclosure (DRHP) presenting Zomato's audited historical unit-economics and marketing-spend-efficiency metrics across FY2018-FY2020 plus a stub period, framed as evidence of a shift from paid-acquisition-led to retention-led growth.
evidence: Advertising and sales-promotion expense as a percentage of total income moved from 16.66% (FY2018) to 88.43% (FY2019) to 48.80% (FY2020) to 22.44% (nine months ended Dec 31, 2020), a self-reported and audited trajectory tied to increasing repeat-customer share.
why_it_matters: Gives a PM a rare, audited, company-disclosed dataset showing how retention/repeat-purchase growth can quantifiably reduce paid-acquisition spend as a share of revenue over multiple fiscal years, useful as a benchmark for building or defending a similar unit-economics narrative.

title: Our learnings from the biggest night of the year
summary: Zomato's engineering, SRE, mobile, payments, logistics, and customer experience teams recap how they prepared for New Year's Eve 2021, historically their highest-traffic night. Over 15 days of cross-team load testing pushed infrastructure to 10x expected load by replaying amplified, randomized production traffic against a production clone. The teams describe concrete mechanisms used on the night — feature toggles, kill switches, circuit breakers, manual demand throttling when delivery-partner or kitchen capacity was constrained, and a failover to an alternate provider when a third-party call service went down — alongside the resulting scale: nearly double the prior year's orders-per-minute and 2.5 million orders serviced globally.
what_they_built: A NYE-specific scale-readiness process spanning SRE, mobile (Android/iOS), payments, logistics, customer support, and restaurant-partner engineering — including load testing at 10x expected traffic via replayed/amplified production data on a production clone, automated and manual demand-throttling controls, and kill switches/circuit breakers across services.
evidence: Went from 4,000 OPM (orders per minute) on NYE 2020 to almost double that in 2021; serviced over 2.5 million orders globally that night; mitigated a third-party call provider outage by switching to an alternate provider with minimal order impact.
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete, multi-team playbook for preparing a consumer product org for a predictable, extreme-scale demand spike, with named mechanisms (traffic replay at 10x, kill switches, provider failover) a PM elsewhere can borrow for peak-event readiness.

title: A Tale of Scale: Behind the Scenes at Zomato Tech for NYE 2023
summary: Ahead of NYE 2023, Zomato's SRE team faced the recurring challenge of validating 300+ microservices against extreme, unpredictable holiday traffic without risking production. They built an in-house Benchmarking Platform to safely load-test over 100 services with gradual ramp-up, automatic abort on critical alerts, and isolation from production, combined with chaos testing (injected latency via their Kuma service mesh, kill-switch drills, cache-miss injection) and prescaled EC2/ECS fleets. On the night, order rate peaked at 8,400 orders/minute against 80.92 million requests/minute of traffic, with detailed throughput reported across Kafka, DynamoDB, MongoDB, ElastiCache, and RDS, all without a service outage.
what_they_built: An in-house, production-isolated Benchmarking Platform plus a chaos-testing/kill-switch/degraded-mode program used to load-test 100+ microservices ahead of a predictable annual traffic spike.
evidence: 14,000 minutes of benchmarking across 100+ services, peak benchmarking throughput of 50 million requests/minute, over 30 billion requests fired in the benchmarking phase (storing ~400 TB of benchmark requests) with zero service outages; on NYE itself, order rate hit 8,400 orders/minute, traffic peaked at 80.92 million requests/minute, DynamoDB peaked at 12.3M RCUs/5.2M WCUs per minute, MongoDB read throughput peaked at 470,000 ops/s, and Kafka handled 450+ million messages/minute at 5.6 GBps.
why_it_matters: Shows how a platform team turns a recurring predictable-peak event into a forcing function for building reusable internal load-testing and chaos-engineering infrastructure, not just one-off war-room heroics.

title: All new Search and Discovery on Zomato
summary: Zomato's app had accumulated multiple separate search bars for different use-cases (online ordering, Gold, etc.), each with its own UI, APIs, and analytics, making the experience fragmented and costly to maintain. The team rebuilt this as a single unified search/discovery surface organized around an entity-intent model, combining text-match, popularity, and intent signals into one relevance score used to rank and select "leader" results. They paired this with a server-configurable UI component system (Sushi) for staged rollouts without app updates, and a universal interaction-tracking scheme (search_id/previous_search_ids chains) that let them run funnel analysis and A/B tests across the whole discovery surface rather than one screen at a time.
what_they_built: A unified, entity-intent-based search and discovery platform for the Zomato app, replacing multiple fragmented search bars with one relevance-ranked surface, backed by server-configurable UI modules and end-to-end interaction tracking for experimentation.
evidence: The combined relevance score/leader-detection mechanism identified a clear "leader" result in roughly 65% of queries at the time of publication, with an explicit internal target of 90%; the team also reports that pairing server-configurable UI with universal tracking enabled rapid staged-rollout experimentation across the new surface.
why_it_matters: Shows a concrete pattern for consolidating fragmented in-app search/discovery surfaces into one platform with a shared ranking model, configurable UI, and unified experiment tracking -- transferable to any PM facing multiple redundant search entry points.
